It remains highly valuable for Marcionite use because it preserves the same Pauline spirit, opposition to legalism, and proclamation of the Father’s gracious salvation that later Marcionite theology would articulate more systematically.

This interpretation is further strengthened by the judgment of many scholars that the final two chapters of the received text represent later interpolations. These chapters diverge significantly in both literary style and theological emphasis, reflecting trends that align more closely with post-Marcionite orthodoxy. Their addition appears to have been intended to bring the original work into greater conformity with the evolving doctrinal mainstream.

Tertullian, one of Marcion’s most vocal critics, appeals to a letter written by Marcion as evidence that Marcion’s faith had once agreed with that of the Roman church. The Marcionite Church identifies this otherwise unspecified letter with the Homily to Diognetus.

Despite being labeled an epistle, the structure of the text does not resemble that of a letter. It lacks standard epistolary elements such as a salutation, signature, or sender. Rather, it reads as a public theological discourse, an instructional homily delivered in a church setting.

Historical inscriptions from the ancient city of Smyrna identify a figure named Diognetus, a local aristocrat active during the time of Polycarp. Smyrna, located in Anatolia, was one of the cities where Marcion evangelized and established congregations. Notably, a Marcionite bishop named Metrodorus, who was later martyred, is known to have served in Smyrna.

It is likely that Diognetus, a curious pagan, visited a Marcionite congregation and questioned Marcion directly about his beliefs. The content of the Homily to Diognetus appears to be Marcion’s public response to these inquiries, later transcribed and circulated throughout the Christian world. Tertullian’s reference to the homily as a “letter” likely reflects a misclassification.

At some point in its transmission, the text was anonymized. All attribution to Marcion of Sinope was removed, and the work was circulated under no authorial name. Eventually lost, the manuscript was rediscovered centuries later and recognized for its historical and theological significance.

The edition of the Homily to Diognetus included in the Homileticon was revised through a comparison of the surviving Greek text with several established English translations. J. B. Lightfoot’s rendering in The Apostolic Fathers supplied the principal base text, while the Roberts–Donaldson translation in the Ante-Nicene Fathers and H. G. Meecham’s critical edition and translation were used alongside the Greek to evaluate disputed or flexible terms and clauses. Kirsopp Lake was consulted as a supplementary witness.

Where the Greek permitted more than one philologically defensible rendering, preference was given to language that cohered more closely with Marcionite theology without departing from the underlying text. Particular attention was given to preserving Pauline vocabulary, distinguishing God the Father from the Word as the mediating Artificer, emphasizing that the Father is without wrath, and avoiding unnecessarily creation-centered renderings where the Greek allowed alternatives such as “operation,” “fashioner,” or “dispensation.” The resulting language was then conformed to the style and cadence of the King James Version, without introducing theological paraphrases unsupported by the Greek.

See also: Question 15.02, “Why does the Church observe the Lord’s Day rather than the Jewish Sabbath?”

questions.question: What is the Synaxicon?
questions.answer:
The name Synaxicon comes from Greek synaxis (“gathering, assembly”) joined to the book-title ending -ikon, whose κ (kappa) becomes c in Latin, yielding -icon and meaning “the book that gathers together.” Marutha of Martyropolis records that Marcionites possessed a book called the Saka—Syriac for “Sum,” “Summary,” or “End”—and its Greek equivalent would indeed be the Synaxicon, typically a compendium of the lives of saints and martyrs.

Numerous scholars and theologians—including Hermann Detering, Joseph Turmel, Alfred Loisy, and Roger Parvus—have argued that the seven epistles attributed to Ignatius of Antioch were originally composed within a Marcionite Christian context. The Marcionite Church builds upon this scholarly hypothesis and holds that these epistles were authored by Marcion of Sinope himself shortly before his martyrdom in Rome in 154 C.E. These letters were compiled into a single codex, referred to in Syriac as the Saka and in Greek as the Synaxicon, and were later added to the broader Marcionite textual tradition after Marcion’s death.

Following his excommunication from the Roman Church, Marcion returned to the East, where he continued leading congregations throughout Anatolia and Syria. Each of the epistles is addressed to churches within Anatolia, Marcion’s native region. The author of the epistles refers to himself as the bishop of Syria, based in Antioch. Marcion assumed this episcopal role in Antioch after failing to become bishop of Rome. Antioch was a major center of early Pauline Christianity and is where followers of Jesus were first called “Christians.” These epistles also contain the earliest written usage of the term “Christianity.”

Strikingly, extant lists of bishops of Antioch reveal vacancies and overlapping dates between the 140s and 160s, aligning precisely with the period during which Marcion could have occupied this position. Antioch, like Rome, maintained an episcopal lineage that claimed apostolic succession through Paul the Apostle. Additionally, patristic sources describe Syria as a stronghold of Marcionite Christianity, and archaeological evidence—such as the discovery of a Marcionite church in the region—corroborates this assessment.

The epistles in question frequently quote or allude to the Odes of Solomon, originally Marcionite hymns now preserved in the Psalmicon. Irenaeus recorded that Marcion encountered his rival Polycarp in Rome around 154 C.E., during which Polycarp famously denounced him as “the firstborn of Satan.” Polycarp is independently known to have visited Rome in 155 C.E. to discuss the Easter controversy with Bishop Anicetus, suggesting that this confrontation likely occurred as Marcion returned to Rome for what would be his final days.

The notion that the author of the epistles was transported to Rome for execution is unusual, as local punishment was more common. However, Marcion’s situation was unique: having caused considerable division within the Roman Church, it would have been fitting for Roman authorities to summon him back to the city where his controversy had originated.

This theory also explains the conspicuous absence of any mention of a bishop of Rome in the epistle addressed to the Roman congregation—consistent with the fact that no Marcionite bishop presided over the Roman community at that time. The epistles focus primarily on ecclesiastical concerns, which aligns with the context of Marcion attempting to secure succession and doctrinal continuity for his churches in anticipation of his martyrdom.

The episcopal structure outlined in these letters mirrors the succession model described by Adamantius for the Marcionite bishops. The presence of multiple versions and redactions of the epistles suggests that they were heavily interpolated. According to historian William Dool Killen, the Catholic Bishop of Rome, Callixtus I, reworked these epistles around 220 C.E., readdressing and expanding them to promote the authority of the monarchical episcopate. In doing so, he reattributed the letters from Marcion to the earlier martyr, Ignatius of Antioch.

Callixtus altered the identifying title “Nauclerus” (meaning shipmaster), commonly used for Marcion due to his occupation and sea voyage to Rome, to “Theophorus,” meaning “God-bearer.” Like Marcion, the newly imagined Ignatius was said to be a disciple of the Apostle John and a follower of the Apostle Paul, having been ordained as the first Bishop of Antioch by Paul.

This reattribution also forced the date of composition back to 108 C.E., a claim based solely on the account of Eusebius of Caesarea in the fourth century. Scholars such as Richard Pervo suggest that Eusebius had a vested interest in creating the appearance of an early and continuous succession of orthodox leaders from the Apostolic age.

Paul Foster and other modern scholars place the writing of the epistles closer to the early 150s C.E., with 154 C.E.—the year R. Joseph Hoffmann argues Marcion died—being a more plausible composition date. This timing is reinforced by internal evidence suggesting the epistles engage with ideas from Ptolemy the Gnostic, an adversary of the Marcionites who became active only in the 150s.

Callixtus also reassigned one epistle originally addressed to a Marcionite bishop in Smyrna—Metrodorus—to Polycarp of Smyrna. Ironically, both Metrodorus and Polycarp were later martyred together in 156 C.E., despite representing rival Christian communities.

The epistles exhibit stylistic similarities with Pauline letters, contain numerous anti-Judaic themes, and include what is likely the earliest argument for observing the Lord’s Day on Sunday instead of the Jewish Sabbath—an innovation widely attributed to Marcionite communities.

While the Marcionite Church does not regard these epistles as divinely inspired scripture, they are honored as valuable patristic texts. They offer insight into theology, liturgy, church governance, and the historical development of the Marcionite tradition.

The reconstruction received by the Church draws principally upon the textual research of Roger Parvus and the translations of J. B. Lightfoot. These sources are used critically as aids to recovering the Marcionite form of the letters, not as authorities equal to the canon.

questions.question: What is the Dialekticon?
questions.answer:
The Dialekticon is an ecclesiastical book of the Church, consisting of Marcionite dialogues and disputations. Dialogue and disputation were ancient forms of Christian literature, used for instruction, controversy, catechesis, and the orderly expression of theology. The Church does not receive the Dialekticon as canonical Scripture or as Deuterocanon, but as an ecclesiastical book useful for teaching doctrine, defending the faith, and showing how Marcionite theology answers error.

At present, the Church has reconstructed one such dialogue: the Dialogue of Megethius Against Hermogenes Concerning Matter, set in 185 C.E. This dialogue presents Megethius as the Marcionite teacher and Hermogenes as the opposing disputant concerning matter, evil, creation, and the origin of the visible world-order. As is common in ancient Christian dialogues, the Church does not require that the disputation happened literally in the exact form presented here, nor does the Church claim that every speech records a stenographic historical exchange. The dialogue form is a teaching instrument: it sets opposing doctrines side by side, tests them by reason and faith, and instructs the Church through ordered disputation.

Hermogenes is used as the opposing disputant because he was a fitting historical and theological interlocutor. He was a near contemporary of several known Marcionite teachers and advanced a doctrine concerning matter that ancient heresiologists themselves compared and contrasted with Marcionite doctrine. Hermogenes taught that matter was uncreated and eternal, whereas the Church confesses that matter is created, morally neutral, and subordinate to God. By the time of the later Dialogue of Adamantius, however, Hermogenes and his school were no longer a living or prominent stream of controversy. For this reason, the older Hermogenean dispute over matter appears to have been displaced, confused, or reapplied to other heretical opponents, especially Valentinians. The Dialekticon restores Hermogenes as the more natural disputant for this controversy.

The reconstruction also helps explain the confusion among heresiologists concerning Marcionite belief about matter. Some witnesses speak as though Marcionites held a distinct principle of matter, while others do not describe Marcionite doctrine in that way. The Dialogue of Megethius clarifies the distinction. The Church does not teach eternal matter, evil matter, or matter as a rival god. Rather, it teaches that matter is created and morally neutral, while the present visible world-order is fashioned mediately by lower powers and is subject to corruption, bondage, and death. This distinction could easily be misunderstood or distorted by hostile witnesses who treated every separation between God the Father, matter, and the lower world-order as though it were a doctrine of co-eternal principles.

The reconstruction follows two principal lines of recovery. First, the Church receives the theory of J. Rendel Harris that a section preserved in Methodius’ On Free Will originally belonged to the opening of a Marcionite treatise or disputation. Second, the Church adds Stephan Huller’s theory that the fragments attributed to Maximus concerning matter were originally connected with Megethius. The names themselves support the possibility of confusion or substitution: Megethius and Maximus are semantically related, both bearing the sense of greatness, one in Greek form and the other in Latin form. Later witnesses after Eusebius, namely Jerome and Epiphanius, attribute Maximus to an otherwise obscure bishop of Jerusalem; the Church judges that this may reflect a later process by which originally Marcionite material was disguised, displaced, or reattributed.

Both streams of material eventually appear to have entered the Dialogue of Adamantius, though in altered, shortened, rearranged, and reattributed form. The Philocalia is also important, as Huller notes, because it helps connect part of the Maximus material with the later Adamantius tradition. The Church therefore restores the Methodius material, following Harris’ theory, and the Maximus/Megethius material, following Huller’s theory, arranging both into one flowing dialogue on matter and evil.

The English form of the Dialogue of Megethius is therefore not a translation from one complete surviving manuscript. It is an ecclesiastical reconstruction and translation, rendered in the scriptural style of the Testamentum, from the surviving ancient witnesses and parallels. The Methodius portion is drawn from the material preserved in Methodius’ On Free Will. The Maximus portion is drawn chiefly from the fragments concerning matter and the origin of evil preserved by Eusebius, especially in the Praeparatio Evangelica, together with the related notices and parallels by which this material was later connected to the Adamantius tradition and the Philocalia. Public-domain English renderings of these ancient materials were consulted where useful. The Church then restored, harmonized, and arranged the material into a single flowing disputation, recast it into KJV/Jacobean English, and applied the chapter-and-verse structure for liturgical, catechetical, and ecclesiastical use.

The Church judges that later Catholic editors and interpolators did not wish Marcionites to appear reasonable, philosophically careful, or theologically detailed in their arguments concerning matter. Therefore, in later forms of the material, the Marcionite side was made to appear heretical or confused, while the original position of Megethius was transferred to an allegedly orthodox speaker. The Dialekticon restores this material to its proper Marcionite setting.

The basic argument of Megethius is that matter is not eternal, uncreated, or co-equal with God. If matter is compound, it must depend upon simpler principles and therefore cannot be without beginning. If matter is made a second uncreated principle beside God, then God is no longer confessed as supreme over all. If matter is placed within God, then disorder and evil are placed within God. If God is placed within matter, then God is contained and circumscribed by matter. Therefore the doctrine of co-eternal matter fails.

Megethius also teaches that evil is not a substance. Evil is not matter itself, nor is matter wicked by nature. Evil belongs to disorder, corruption, bondage, passion, misuse, false rule, and sinful action. Thus the Church rejects both the Hermogenean error that matter is eternal and the Manichaean error that matter is evil. Matter is created and morally neutral; God the Father gives being from nothing through the Logos; the lower powers fashion the visible world-order from created matter; and the corruption of this world-order does not make God the Father the author of evil.

For this reason, the Dialekticon is valuable to the Church. It strengthens the doctrine of created matter, guards against dualism, explains evil without making God its author, and teaches the faithful to distinguish matter, the visible world, the world-order, and the kingdom of God.

Because the Dialekticon is an ecclesiastical reconstruction, it is read as a teaching book of the Church and not as canonical Scripture, not as Deuterocanon, not as a verbatim transcript, and not as a translation of one complete surviving manuscript.

See also: Question 9.04, “What is the origin of matter?”; Question 9.05, “Does the Church believe matter is eternal?”; Question 9.06, “Does the Church believe in creation out of nothing?”; Question 9.07, “Why does the Church reject the doctrine of co-eternal matter?”; Question 9.08, “Is evil a substance?”; Question 9.10, “What is deferred demiurgy?”

questions.question: What is the Litourgicon?
questions.answer:
Litourgicon comes from the Greek leitourgía (“public worship” or “liturgy”) joined to the book-title ending -ikon, whose κ (kappa) becomes c in Latin, yielding -icon and signifying “the liturgical book” or “service book.”

The Litourgicon is the liturgical service book of the Marcionite Church. It gathers the ordered forms by which the Church celebrates the Holy Mysteries, administers the Holy Rites, and conducts its common life of prayer. It contains the Liturgy of Holy Baptism, the Liturgy of Holy Chrismation, and the Divine Liturgy, thereby providing the liturgical forms for all three Holy Mysteries. The Divine Liturgy also incorporates Holy Footwashing, Holy Veiling, the Holy Lovefeast, the Holy Kiss, and the Holy Sign. Together, these orders contain all three Holy Mysteries and five of the nine Holy Rites.

No complete order of the ancient Marcionite Eucharist has survived. The present Divine Liturgy therefore does not claim to reproduce word for word the rite celebrated by Marcion or by every congregation of the ancient Church. It is a reconstruction intended to provide a simple and basic Pauline liturgy in keeping with the earliest surviving pre-Nicene forms of Christian worship. Its ancient elements have been selected, arranged, and adapted according to the Evangelicon, Apostolicon, Psalmicon, the Church’s discipline concerning the Holy Mysteries and Holy Rites, and the theology of the Marcionite Church.

The Divine Liturgy is intentionally foundational rather than maximal. It supplies the regular order for the proclamation of the Gospel and Apostle, psalmody, homily, intercession, confession of faith, Holy Communion, thanksgiving, dismissal, and the Holy Lovefeast. It may be celebrated in a church, chapel, or private home wherever a celebrant and at least two communicants are gathered.

Because it is a basic form and model, the Divine Liturgy may be built upon. Appropriate psalms, hymns, prayers, intercessions, readings, and ceremonial actions may be added according to the season, feast, needs of the congregation, or local custom, provided that they remain consistent with the canon, the ecclesiastical books, the Church’s teaching concerning the Holy Mysteries, Holy Rites, and liturgical worship, and the orderly and reverent character of Christian worship. Nothing should be added merely for ornament, novelty, or imitation of another tradition.

The reconstruction preserves characteristic Marcionite practices including westward prayer, the Eucharistic fast, ritual washing and footwashing, the mixed chalice, open communion for fully initiated Christians, the bowing of the head at the name of Jesus, and the Holy Lovefeast as a Holy Rite of charity.

questions.question: What is the significance of the -icon suffix?
questions.answer:
The books gathered within the modern Testamentum use a family of titles that end in -icon. This suffix carries a clear linguistic and historical logic that binds the complete Church codex together. The choice to Latinise the original Greek -ikon also suits the fact that we are writing in English and therefore already employ the Latin alphabet.

In Greek, -ικόν (-ikon) is the neuter singular of an adjective that can stand on its own as a noun, “the book pertaining to…” Thus τὸ Εὐαγγελικόν (Evangelikon), “the Gospel book,” and τὸ Ἀποστολικόν (Apostolikon), literally “the Apostle book” (the collected letters of the Apostle Paul), provided early Christians with a ready template. When such titles passed into Latin manuscripts, the hard κ became c, turning -ikon into -icon. Because the modern complete collection bears the Latin title Testamentum, retaining the Latinised spelling for each constituent book is both historically faithful and visually cohesive.

Several of our titles—Evangelicon, Apostolicon, Litourgicon, and Homileticon—are already well attested in scholarly, heresiological, and liturgical literature. The remaining names are modern but strictly grammatical coinages, chosen not only for symmetry but to flag their distinctively Marcionite content:

Antilegicon, “the book of disputed writings,” rooted in the patristic term antilegomena / antilegomenon (“works spoken against”), gathers letters whose authority was historically contested.

Psalmicon designates “the psalm book,” arranging those hymns read in Marcionite worship.

Synaxicon, literally “the book which brings together the epistles,” borrows the form of a traditional Synaxarion—typically a summary of martyrs’ and saints’ lives—but here it gathers only Marcion’s seven epistles composed before his own martyrdom, highlighting his witness rather than presenting a general hagiography.

Dialekticon designates “the dialogue or disputation book,” gathering reconstructed dialogues and disputations useful for instruction, controversy, and the orderly expression of theology.

Adopting unique titles where precedent is lacking is more than a stylistic flourish: it marks the distinct Marcionite theological, liturgical, historical, and ecclesiastical composition of the modern Testamentum while maintaining the uniform -icon ending that identifies each constituent book as part of one ordered corpus. In this way, the collection remains stylistically coherent, linguistically transparent, and unmistakably our own.

questions.question: Does the Church recognize agrapha?
questions.answer:
By agrapha we mean dominical sayings that circulate outside Scripture as independent, extra-scriptural authorities—and we do not recognize that category as binding.

The closest case people often raise is “Be ye approved money-changers.” But in our tradition this is not treated as a free-floating, non-scriptural saying. Apelles explicitly appeals to it as something Christ “said in the gospel.” Epiphanius preserves Apelles’ claim in exactly that form: “he said in the gospel, ‘Become approved money-changers’” (Greek: γίνεσθε δόκιμοι τραπεζῖται).

In other words, what later writers and collectors handled as an “agraphon” is better explained as a textual dislocation: during the long process of editing, copying, and re-forming the Evangelicon, a line that had circulated as Gospel text could become detached from its original context and then circulate independently—thus appearing “agraphal” after the fact.

“Be ye approved money-changers, rejecting some things, but holding fast that which is good.”

Evangelicon 4:48

questions.question: What is Marcionite priority?
questions.answer:
Much of what is known about Marcion and his theological work comes to us through the writings of his detractors. Notably, every major critique of Marcion’s teachings was written posthumously—after his death, when he could no longer defend himself or refute the accusations leveled against him. Among his most vocal opponents was the early ancient Catholic Church Father Tertullian, who alleged that Marcion had “mutilated” the Gospel of Luke, selectively removing passages that did not align with his beliefs. However, modern scholarship increasingly supports the theory of Marcionite Priority—the view that Marcion’s Evangelicon is the earlier text, and that what we now know as the Gospel of Luke is a later, expanded version edited to reflect emerging Catholic theology.

Some scholars suggest that this editorial transformation was undertaken by Theophilus, bishop of Antioch, around 169 C.E. According to this view, Theophilus reworked and altered the original Evangelicon, adding new material and removing other material, and retroactively named the result the Gospel of Luke.

Crucially, there is no surviving documentary evidence of a pre-Marcionite Gospel of Luke or pre-Marcionite Pauline Epistles. This strongly suggests that Marcion’s canon was in use in Rome before 144 C.E., and that the manuscripts he employed predate the oldest known Pauline manuscripts, such as the Egyptian papyri dating to around 200 C.E. Thus, the Pauline texts in Marcion’s original Testamentum are likely closer to the original apostolic writings than the later, edited versions adopted by the emerging Catholic Church.

Moreover, the direction of textual change supports Marcionite Priority. Catholic redaction concentrated on the Pauline epistles, where concise letters were expanded and interpolated to align with emerging doctrine. By contrast, in the Evangelicon, editors altered wording, removed passages, and redistributed verses to manufacture the fourfold Gospel. The structure and content of Marcion’s texts suggest not a Catholic abridgment, but rather earlier, more primitive versions that were subsequently reshaped to conform to Catholic orthodoxy.

questions.question: Who wrote the Evangelicon?
questions.answer:
In the Marcionite Church, the Gospel is received as the work of the Apostle Paul, written and proclaimed by him as a message given directly by the risen Christ through revelation, not as a tradition derived from men. Paul states this plainly: “the gospel which was preached of me is not after man … but by the revelation of Jesus Christ” (Galatians 1:11–12). He further ties that commission to the risen Son’s disclosure within him: “To reveal his Son in me, that I might preach him among the heathen” (Galatians 1:16).

Accordingly, Paul treats the Gospel as a received deposit that he then transmits authoritatively to the churches: “I have received of the Lord that which also I delivered unto you” (I Corinthians 11:23). Likewise, “I declare unto you the gospel … For I delivered unto you first of all that which I also received” (I Corinthians 15:1–3), immediately setting forth the Gospel’s substance—Christ’s death, burial, and rising again (I Corinthians 15:3–4).

Paul also designates this revealed proclamation as “my gospel” in the sense of stewardship and entrusted jurisdiction, warning that rival “gospels” are perversions: “Which is not another …” (Galatians 1:7). He even describes the final judgment as proceeding “according to my gospel” (Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 2:7).

This Pauline authorship claim is also explicitly voiced in the Dialogue of Adamantius. When Adamantius asks from whom scripture confirms there is only one Gospel, the Marcionite spokesman answers, “Christ.” When pressed about who wrote of the crucifixion and resurrection, he replies, “The Apostle Paul imparted this … He himself wrote the Gospel.” In that exchange, the speaker does not quote the phrase “by the revelation of Jesus Christ,” but he does ground the one Gospel in Christ and then assign its writing and impartation to Paul—precisely the point Paul makes directly in Galatians.

Claims that Marcion “wrote” the Gospel, or “edited” it into existence, confuse preservation with authorship. Marcion was not the author of the Gospel, nor was he its inventive redactor. His role was custodial and missionary: he transcribed what he had received, translated it into Latin for wider proclamation, disseminated it through evangelistic missions, and circulated it together with Paul’s epistles in a single codex—preserving the Gospel alongside the Pauline letters received in the Apostolicon.

questions.question: Did Apelles use a distinct Gospel text?
questions.answer:
Writers within a century of Apelles do not name a separate “Gospel of Apelles.” When Tertullian addresses Apelles, he points back to his case against Marcion and “his own favourite gospel,” which is consistent with Apelles continuing to rely on the Evangelicon used in Marcionite circles.

Pseudo-Tertullian reports that Apelles “uses one only apostle” (that is, the Apostolicon) and drew on private readings he called “Manifestations” from the prophetess Philomena. These Manifestations were prophetic notes, not a narrative life of Jesus. The first explicit notice that names an Evangelium Apellis appears only with Jerome in the late fourth century, in his preface to Matthew, where he lists it among apocrypha. Later compilers echo him, but modern scholars treat the notice as a mistake, likely a muddled reference to the Manifestations or a cataloguer’s way of tagging the Evangelicon as used among Apelleans.

In short, Apelles did not compose a new gospel. He continued to preach from Marcion’s Evangelicon, and references to an “Apellean gospel” likely arise from confusion over Philomena’s Manifestations or over verses within the Evangelicon.

questions.question: Do the canonical scriptures of the Testamentum use a Western text-type?
questions.answer:
The Evangelicon and Apostolicon cannot be classified simply as a Western text-type in the strict technical sense.

In New Testament textual criticism, “Western,” “Alexandrian,” “Byzantine,” and (more debatably) “Caesarean” are manuscript-based groupings. The Evangelicon is not preserved as a continuous manuscript tradition that can be classified the way a codex or versional family can. What we possess is a reconstruction derived chiefly from patristic witnesses, so it is methodologically imprecise to label it a “Western text-type” as though it were a manuscript.

That said, the Evangelicon can show occasional agreements with readings later characteristic of the so-called Western stream (especially Old Latin-related readings, and certain shorter Lukan forms sometimes discussed as “Western non-interpolations”). Those overlaps are one reason some scholars have described the Evangelicon’s underlying text as “Western” in tendency. The difficulty is consistency: the Evangelicon does not behave “Western” across the board, and it does not exhibit the broader Western habit of expansive paraphrase as a stable pattern.

A further reason the Evangelicon can appear “Western” is the mediation of the polemical witnesses themselves. Writers such as Tertullian were operating in a Latin milieu and likely compared Marcion’s Gospel against the Gospel text available to them in their own ecclesial setting, which would have had a Western-style Latin complexion. In other words, part of the “Western feel” may arise not from the Evangelicon alone, but from the textual baseline and phrasing habits of the critic doing the comparison, as well as the inevitable distortions introduced when Greek is reported, paraphrased, or rebutted in Latin.

It is also possible that Western connections were strengthened through early Latin transmission. Some have proposed that Marcion (or Marcionite circles) were early movers in rendering Gospel materials into Latin, and that such translations could have influenced later Latin forms. Whether or not that stronger claim is accepted, it remains true that the Western tradition is heavily mediated through Latin witnesses, so any early Latin pathway—Marcionite or otherwise—can create points of contact that make the Evangelicon look “Western-adjacent” when filtered through later comparison.

Compared to the other text-types, the Evangelicon is least like the Byzantine text, which is typically characterized by later smoothing, harmonization, and expansion. The Evangelicon often lacks later accretions and does not exhibit the fully developed Byzantine editorial profile. It can sometimes resemble the Alexandrian tendency toward shorter, less harmonized forms, though it does not consistently reproduce Alexandrian readings either. As for the “Caesarean” category, many textual critics treat it as unstable or overly mixed even for manuscript classification; it is therefore especially unhelpful to label a reconstructed text like the Evangelicon “Caesarean,” even if individual readings occasionally resemble those sometimes placed in that orbit.

A related issue is the so-called “Western non-interpolations.” This term is used for a small cluster of passages—especially in Luke—where Western witnesses (most notably Codex Bezae and some Old Latin forms) preserve a shorter text that lacks material found in the majority tradition. Some textual critics have argued that these shorter readings are “non-interpolations,” meaning they may reflect an earlier form of the Gospel before later scribes expanded it; others argue the Western tradition simply omitted or abbreviated. The Evangelicon enters this discussion because, in a few of the classic Lukan loci (often cited in Luke 22 and Luke 24), reconstructions of Marcion’s Gospel are sometimes closer to the shorter form. Even where that is true, it should be handled with caution: the Evangelicon is reconstructed rather than manuscript-attested, and some “Western” coloration may reflect the Latin baseline of polemical witnesses such as Tertullian. The most defensible claim is therefore limited—Western non-interpolations can illuminate why certain Evangelicon readings appear early or non-Byzantine, but they do not by themselves establish a Western text-type classification or decide originality in every disputed passage.

So the best answer is: the Evangelicon is not formally a Western text-type, but it can show Western/Old Latin points of contact and can look more Western when viewed through Latin polemical witnesses such as Tertullian. Overall, it is better described as an early, pre-Byzantine Lukan-like text-form with mixed affinities, rather than a clean member of any one later-defined text family.

Finally same caution applies to the Apostolicon. “Western,” “Alexandrian,” and “Byzantine” are primarily shorthand for manuscript relationships, but the Apostolicon is likewise reconstructed from patristic reporting rather than preserved as a continuous Marcionite manuscript line. Moreover, the Pauline letters do not track the Gospel text-types in a one-to-one manner; their transmission history is different, and “Western” traits in Paul are often mediated through Latin witnesses and the polemical baselines of the critics themselves. Accordingly, it is more accurate to say that the Apostolicon sometimes exhibits early, non-Byzantine affinities in particular readings, while resisting any clean, universal classification as a single later-defined text family.

questions.question: What are the Marcionite prologues?
questions.answer:
The Prologues to the Pauline Epistles—brief introductory statements akin to those found in modern Bible study guides—are not part of the biblical text itself, but have traditionally been so named because they are widely believed to have originated within Marcionite Christian circles. These prologues appear in several manuscripts of the Latin Vulgate, most notably in the Codex Fuldensis.

Contemporary scholarship generally acknowledges a Marcionite origin for these prologues, attributing their presence in the Latin tradition to the activity of Marcion or Marcionite Christian teachers in Rome during the second century, who are believed to have translated the Greek epistles of Paul into Latin for use in their communities.

In contrast, several early Latin codices preserve Anti-Marcionite Prologues to the Gospels, which appear to have been composed as direct responses to Marcionite teachings and to assert a rival interpretive framework within the emerging Catholic tradition.

questions.question: How were the Marcionite prologues reconstructed?
questions.answer:
We reconstructed the Marcionite prologues by collating the surviving Latin witnesses of the prologue tradition and then producing a maximal composite that preserves the fullest, most concrete form of each notice. Multiple versions of the Marcionite prologues exist in Latin: some are longer and some shorter; some name couriers and others omit them; some specify a city of composition while others give only a region (or remain silent); and some explicitly note imprisonment while others do not.

Because these prologues are paratexts rather than Scripture, we did not treat any single wording as inviolable. Instead, we adopted a more liberal reconstructive method: we combined the distinct Marcionite variants into a single expanded form that includes, wherever attested, the elements most useful for historical reconstruction—namely (1) the courier who carried the epistle, (2) any named secretary or writing agent, (3) the city (or at least region) of composition, and (4) whether the Apostle wrote in prison or while at liberty.

Our aim was not merely stylistic completeness, but historical clarity. By preserving the prologues’ concrete logistical data—messengers, writing locations, and imprisonment notices—we can more accurately recover the chronological and geographical sequence implied by the prologue tradition, and thus outline the Apostle’s missionary journeys as the Marcionite corpus itself frames them.

Where variants conflicted on couriers or places of composition, we followed two governing principles. First, we generally preferred the more specific witness (a named city rather than a vague region, a named courier rather than an omission). Second, when specificity competed, we preferred the more constrained or “arrested” version—i.e., the one that explicitly situates Paul under custody or in prison—because these details supply a firmer chronological anchor and typically reflect a more developed provenance tradition rather than a later abbreviation.

Two epistles require special note because their prologues necessarily involve a more explicit scholarly reconstruction. The prologue to Laodiceans was assembled by synthesizing the different Laodiceans variants that survive in the Marcionite Latin stream, while also drawing on an otherwise unused prologue tradition transmitted for Ephesians, given the long-standing identification of Laodiceans with the catholic “Ephesians” letter and the way the manuscript tradition appears to have cross-pollinated their paratexts. Likewise, the prologue to Alexandrians was recreated by adapting a Latin prologue to Hebrews, reflecting the widespread identification of “Alexandrians” with the epistle known in the catholic canon as Hebrews and the fact that the most substantial surviving prologue material for that letter is preserved under the Hebrews title.

questions.question: When did the Apostle Paul write his epistles?
questions.answer:
The list below provides not only the estimated dates for the composition of the Apostle Paul’s epistles, but also the city of composition and the courier by whom each letter was carried (where the tradition specifies one), as preserved in the Marcionite Latin prologues:

Galatians — ~49 C.E. (written from Ephesus; sent by Titus)

First Corinthians — ~50 C.E. (written from Ephesus; sent by Timothy)

Second Corinthians — ~51 C.E. (written from Troas; sent by Titus)

Romans — ~52 C.E. (written from Corinth; courier not specified)

First Thessalonians — ~53 C.E. (written from Athens; sent by Tychicus and Onesimus)

Second Thessalonians — ~54 C.E. (written from Athens; sent by Tychicus and Onesimus)

Laodiceans (commonly identified as Ephesians) — ~55 C.E. (the first epistle written while imprisoned; written from Ephesus; sent by Tychicus the deacon)

Colossians — ~56 C.E. (written from Ephesus, in prison; sent by Tychicus and Onesimus)

Philemon —~ 60 C.E. (written from Rome, in prison; sent by Onesimus)

Philippians — ~61 C.E. (written from Rome, in prison; sent by Epaphroditus)

Titus — ~62 C.E. (written from Nicopolis, after he had left prison; courier not specified)

First Timothy — ~63 C.E. (written from Laodicea; sent by Tychicus)

Second Timothy — ~64 C.E. (written from Rome, during his second imprisonment; courier not specified)

Alexandrians (commonly identified as Hebrews) — ~66 C.E. (written by Tertius in Italy; sent by Phoebe)

questions.question: What is the Proto-Evangelicon?
questions.answer:
The Proto-Evangelicon is the Marcionite Church’s title for the short Marcionite prologue to the Evangelicon preserved under the ancient designation Pro-Evangelium. In this usage, proto- means “before” or “prefatory to” the Gospel; it does not denote a hypothetical earlier edition of the Evangelicon.

An anonymous Syriac anti-Marcionite Exposition of the Gospel, formerly attributed to Ephrem, preserves the following sentence at the beginning of what it calls Marcion’s Pro-Evangelium:

“O wonder beyond wonders, rapture, power, and amazement is it, that one can say nothing at all about the gospel, nor even conceive of it, nor compare it with anything.”

The Syriac witness was edited and translated by Joseph Schäfers. Adolf von Harnack regarded the sentence as the probable opening of the Antitheses and described it as the only substantial sentence preserved word for word from Marcion. Joseph B. Tyson likewise notes both the designation “Proevangelium” and its probable connection with the Antitheses. Sebastian Moll emphasizes that the identification remains uncertain and argues that the name may instead refer to Marcion’s Gospel itself or to an otherwise unknown work. (Anonymous Syriac Exposition of the Gospel 1; Adolf von Harnack, Marcion: The Gospel of the Alien God, 59; Joseph B. Tyson, “Anti-Judaism in Marcion and His Opponents,” 198–199; Sebastian Moll, At the Left Hand of Christ, 169–170.)

Tertullian says that the Antitheses served as a “dowry” supporting Marcion’s Gospel and elsewhere speaks expressly of “prefixing” the Antitheses to it (Tertullian, Against Marcion 4.1; 4.6). This supports treating the surviving encomium as an introductory frame placed before the Evangelicon, even though its precise ancient literary classification cannot now be established with certainty.

The Marcionite Church therefore receives the Proto-Evangelicon as a paratext rather than as a verse or chapter of the Evangelicon itself. It is treated in the same manner as the Marcionite prologues to the Pauline Epistles: an ancient introductory witness that frames a canonical book but is not itself canonical Scripture. It may accordingly be placed immediately before the Evangelicon while remaining subordinate to the Gospel text that it introduces.

questions.question: What are the anti-Marcionite prologues?
questions.answer:
The influence and widespread circulation of the Marcionite Evangelicon, or Gospel of the Lord Jesus Christ, was so profound in the early centuries that the emerging Catholic Church felt compelled to preface its own gospel texts with explicitly anti-Marcionite introductions.

These introductory texts, known as the anti-Marcionite prologues, appear at the beginning of the Gospels of Mark, Luke, and John. It remains uncertain whether a similar prologue was ever composed for the Gospel of Matthew. The term anti-Marcionite prologues was coined by biblical scholar Donatien de Bruyne, who argued that their purpose was to counter the theological influence of Marcion and to reinforce the authority of the Catholic gospel tradition. Of these texts, only the prologue to the Gospel of John contains direct reference to Marcion of Sinope by name.

Although originally composed in Greek, the surviving prologues to Mark and John are preserved only in Latin manuscripts. The earliest complete manuscript containing all three extant prologues dates to the eighth century, though scholars believe they were initially circulated independently. Their composition is generally dated between the second and fourth centuries.

These prologues served to affirm the traditional church views on gospel authorship and to bolster ecclesiastical claims of continuity and orthodoxy in contrast to the Marcionite canon. Their existence highlights both the enduring popularity of Marcion’s gospel and the broader effort by Catholic authorities to define—and defend—their emerging scriptural canon.

questions.question: What is interpolation?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite versions of the Pauline Epistles were markedly shorter than the expanded forms found in later canonical Bibles. However, a growing body of scholarly research has concluded that the Marcionite texts more accurately reflect the original writings of the Apostle Paul. In contrast, the conventional versions appear to have undergone substantial expansion through interpolations—additions that introduced later theological constructs foreign to Paul’s original message.

Among the doctrines absent in the Marcionite corpus but present in the versions preserved in the later Catholic canon are teachings concerning misogyny, legalism, the doctrine of original sin, deterministic predestination (which implies divine causation of sin), anti-Semitic rhetoric, eternal damnation, and polemics against pagan cultures. Marcion, by contrast, preached a message of salvation through faith alone, unburdened by the legalistic framework later embraced by the emerging Catholic Church.

In the Roman literary world, it was not uncommon for scribes or disciples to write in the name of revered figures from the past. While such practices would today be classified as forgery, they were then regarded as acts of humility and homage. This cultural reverence for tradition often led writers to frame new theological or philosophical ideas under the authority of an established name to lend credibility and acceptance.

One particular method used to alter texts was interpolation—the insertion of new material into an existing work. A scribe might pause while copying a manuscript, insert a passage of their own composition, and then resume transcription. This method was not unusual and is observable in the surviving works of authors such as Josephus and Homer. Yet, despite its prevalence in antiquity, the possibility that such interpolations also affected the biblical text is rarely confronted with due seriousness.

These insertions reflect a broader process of literary accretion—where texts, once considered complete, are gradually expanded over time. This process not only altered individual passages but also contributed to the growth of the scriptural canon itself. As more writings were appended to the original corpus, the theological character of the tradition shifted accordingly.

As a general rule, the most authentic and original form of any ancient text subject to accretion is likely to be the shortest version. In this light, the brevity and theological clarity of the Marcionite epistles may point not to deficiency, but to fidelity to the apostolic source.

questions.question: By what principles are the Church’s texts reconstructed and translated?
questions.answer:
The original manuscripts of the Marcionite canon have not survived physically into modern times. Following the consolidation of Catholic political power in the fourth century, Marcionite scriptures were subjected to suppression, confiscation, and destruction, and no manuscript of the original Marcionite canon is known to have survived intact. Nevertheless, the canon of the Marcionite Church can be meaningfully reconstructed through extensive references found within patristic sources. Several early Christian authors—particularly Tertullian in his five-volume Adversus Marcionem, Epiphanius in the Panarion, and Adamantius in De recta in Deum fide—quoted extensively from Marcionite scriptures and provided detailed comparisons between the original Testamentum and texts that later became part of the Catholic canon. These authors frequently engaged in theological critiques of Marcionite texts, thereby preserving significant portions of them within their criticisms.

The resulting text is neither a paraphrase nor a devotional rewriting. It is a disciplined textual reconstruction founded upon patristic quotations and descriptions, early Latin and Syriac evidence, ancient manuscript traditions, and modern critical scholarship.

By analyzing these quotations and descriptions, scholars have successfully reconstructed substantial segments of Marcionite scriptures. While not every verse is directly attested, ancient critics focused their attention primarily on passages that diverged theologically from texts later adopted by the emerging Catholic Church. In instances where no direct commentary or quotations exist, it is reasonable to assume that the Marcionite version closely matched, or was identical to, the corresponding conventional text. Thus, standard biblical texts often serve as reliable proxies for these uncontroversial passages.

This reconstruction adheres to a maximalist methodology, particularly for the Evangelicon and Apostolicon, favoring a comprehensive approach aimed at restoring the complete form and narrative continuity of the original Marcionite canon. The absence of mention by patristic authors such as Tertullian or Epiphanius is not considered evidence of nonexistence. Conversely, speculative interpolations are excluded unless supported by primary evidence or indicated by manuscript variations suggesting textual evolution. Hypothetical interpolations are accepted only when grounded in verifiable ancient textual divergences. Unsupported conjectures are entirely excluded.

In reconstructing the Evangelicon, attested verses absent from the Gospel of Luke are included only if they lack a parallel in Luke. The full pericope containing such verses is preserved for narrative coherence and inserted at the most contextually suitable location. When a verse noted by ancient sources as having been excised from the Evangelicon, which is not present in Luke either directly or in the form of a parallel, the verse itself is excluded, but the remainder of the pericope is retained. If an attested variant verse not from Luke exists, only that variant verse is included rather than the entire pericope. The fullest available version of any pericope is utilized to maintain narrative continuity and clarity.

The Pericope Adulterae, traditionally located in John 7:53–8:11, is included in this reconstruction as a conjecturally restored Lucan tradition because a small group of medieval Greek manuscripts belonging to Family 13 place it after Luke 21:38 and because the narrative exhibits notable Lukan affinities.

Material attested from the Gospel of Apelles—the gospel supposedly used by Marcion’s disciple—is incorporated where relevant, reflecting the belief that Apelles continued to preserve and transmit the Evangelicon.

The identification of gospel parallels and pericopes primarily follows the scholarly works of Kurt Aland (Synopsis Quattuor Evangeliorum, 1985) and Lorin L. Cranford (A Study Manual of the New Testament, 1981).

In contrast, the reconstruction of the Antilegicon—those Pauline writings whose authenticity was historically disputed—adopts a minimalist approach. Only verses directly cited, alluded to, or referenced by early patristic writers, particularly those before the Council of Nicaea in 325 C.E., are included. Clement of Alexandria serves as the primary source, supplemented by catenae and references from other second-century authors. Exceptions are limited to verses cited in the Marcionite prologues or essential for textual continuity.

The English translation of the canonical and deuterocanonical scriptural books within the Testamentum is based on the King James Version, chosen for its formal equivalence, literary elegance, and compatibility with prior reconstruction efforts. Where the surviving evidence supports distinct Marcionite language, the reconstruction preserves that language on its own terms rather than silently conforming it to later Catholic terminology.

The Evangelicon version employed in this project draws from James Hamlyn Hill’s 1891 edition, itself based on August Hahn’s 1823 reconstruction. Further revisions have incorporated the scholarship of Daniel Jon Mahar, David Inglis, Stephan Huller, Gustav Volkmar (Das Evangelium Marcions, 1852), Theodor Zahn (Geschichte des n.t. Kanons, 1888), Charles B. Waite (History of the Christian Religion to the Year C.E. 200, 1881), and André Wautier (Comment Naquit Le Christianisme, 1980).

Reconstructions of the Pauline Epistles in the Apostolicon principally rely on David Inglis’s scholarship, integrating material from W. C. van Manen (Marcions Brief van Paulus aan de Galatiers, 1887), Adolf von Harnack (History of Dogma, 1894), Paul-Louis Couchoud (La Première Edition de St Paul, 1928), and Jason BeDuhn (The First New Testament: Marcion’s Scriptural Canon, 2013).

The Antilegicon texts predominantly draw from Maegan C.M. Gilliland’s work (Text of the Pauline Epistles and Hebrews in Clement of Alexandria, 2016), with supplementary reference to Philip Schaff (Ante-Nicene Fathers, 1885).

The Psalmicon was reconstructed through comparison of the surviving Syriac, Greek, and Coptic witnesses with the translations and textual work of J. Rendel Harris and Alphonse Mingana, James H. Charlesworth, and Samuel Zinner’s translation, edited by Mark M. Mattison. The collection is understood as a body of hymns gathered by Marcion from different Pauline churches, including Greek- and Syriac-speaking communities, and ordered for common liturgical use rather than composed entirely by Marcion himself. Where multiple renderings were textually defensible, the edition followed the principles stated in Question 3.22, including preservation of Marcionite distinctions concerning the heavenly and visible orders, immortality as a gift received through Christ, spiritual resurrection, and mediated fashioning of the physical world.

Liturgical rites compiled in the Litourgicon, including the Divine Liturgy, are based on translations by James Donaldson (Ante-Nicene Fathers, 1886).

The Homileticon is based principally upon J. B. Lightfoot’s text and translation in The Apostolic Fathers, revised against the surviving Greek and the translations of Roberts–Donaldson, H. G. Meecham, and Kirsopp Lake according to the principles stated in Question 3.23. The translation of the Synaxicon likewise relies principally upon Lightfoot’s Apostolic Fathers (1891).

The Synaxicon preserves the Marcionite letters traditionally transmitted under the names of Ignatius and the communities to which they were addressed. The reconstruction of these epistles, together with the identification and location of Johannine parallels included in the Evangelicon, draws from Roger Parvus’ research (A New Look at the Letters of Ignatius of Antioch and Other Apellean Writings, 2008).

Overall, this reconstruction embodies a commitment to historical accuracy, theological coherence, and textual integrity, utilizing surviving historical evidence to restore the Marcionite canon in its most authentic and defensible form.

questions.question: How does the Church use heresiological witnesses?
questions.answer:
The Church does not fully trust the heresiologists. We consider the reconstructed scriptures of the Marcionite tradition to be the highest-priority evidence for Marcion’s actual theology and the faith of the early Marcionite Church.

This also explains why their stock accusations—such as dualism, docetism, and the like—do not stand. Furthermore, the heresiologists were incentivized to claim that Marcion edited and abridged the Gospel of Luke. This bias led them to concentrate on Lukan-like verses when commenting on the Evangelicon and to ignore or explain away passages that were not Lukan in style or content. Had they acknowledged that the Evangelicon contained substantial non-Lukan material, they would have implicitly conceded that it was earlier, larger, and the fountainhead of the later canonical gospels. Even so, they occasionally slipped and admitted, directly or indirectly, that non-Lukan material stood within the Evangelicon, and those admissions further erode their credibility.

questions.question: Which English translation does the Church use for the scriptural books of the Testamentum?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church affirms the use of English translations that adhere to the principle of formal equivalence. For the reconstruction of the canonical and deuterocanonical scriptural books within the Testamentum, the King James Version was selected as the translation base.

A formal equivalence, or literal, translation seeks to remain as faithful as possible to the original language of the text, minimizing interpretive influence from the translator. This approach emphasizes a word-for-word rendering rather than a thought-for-thought paraphrase. The underlying rationale is that the more literal the translation, the less risk there is of distorting or altering the intended meaning of the original message.

questions.question: How does the Church interpret scripture?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church affirms literal interpretation as essential to rightly dividing the word of truth—but only when applied to authentic, uncorrupted Scripture. We make a necessary distinction between the false scriptures of the Hebrew Bible and the true Gospel preserved in the canonical books of the Testamentum, the Evangelicon and Apostolicon.

We read the Hebrew Bible literally, not to redeem it, but to expose it. Its plain sense reveals a god of wrath, vengeance, and cruelty—a false deity unworthy of worship. When Yahweh commands slaughter, establishes slavery, or delights in sacrifice, we do not spiritualize those actions into metaphors. We take them at face value. And for that reason, we reject him.

Literal reading, rightly applied, reveals the moral failure of the False Covenant.

Many literalists argue that Scripture must be read at face value because it is divinely inspired. But this claim collapses when the source is false. The Hebrew Bible was not inspired by the Father of Jesus Christ, and no theory of inspiration can rescue it. Literalism, in this case, serves not to sanctify but to expose.

We agree with literalists who argue that the plain sense of the text preserves its clarity and protects against allegorical abuse. Allegory has long been used to twist evil into virtue, making genocide into spiritual warfare and blood sacrifice into hidden mercy. These distortions corrupted the early Church, which sought to preserve the Hebrew Bible by reading it through the lens of allegory rather than truth.

What is less understood, however, is that Marcion—recognized as a prominent Christian leader in Antioch before his break with Rome—helped establish the exegetical clarity that later came to define the Antiochene school. Long before Diodore of Tarsus or Theodore of Mopsuestia, Marcion had already applied the Antiochene principle of grammatical-historical interpretation—but with greater consistency. He did not merely reject allegory; he rejected the false scriptures that allegory was invented to defend.

The Marcionite Church affirms the true principle of rightly dividing the word of truth (II Timothy 2:15). But we understand that this division is not merely about proper interpretation—it is about discerning between the word of the Father and the word of a false god. The gospel must not only be read plainly; it must be read purely. And that requires separating it from all that is foreign, false, and forged.

The Protestant Reformers, while reviving literal interpretation, were ultimately misguided. They rejected Catholic allegory but applied literalism indiscriminately—even to interpolated, corrupted, and spurious texts in what they called the “New Testament.” They read the Hebrew Bible literally and still tried to claim it as Christian scripture. Worse still, they failed to cleanse their canon of forged epistles, redactions, and anti-Pauline material.

Literalism is not a safeguard if the foundation is flawed. It serves truth only when the text itself is pure—free from scribal adulteration, theological interpolation, and false attribution. That is why the Marcionite Church recognizes only the Evangelicon and Apostolicon as the canon: the unadulterated Gospel of Christ and the apostolic writings of Paul, rightly divided and preserved in their integrity within the modern Testamentum.

We read the Hebrew Bible literally—to expose the false god who authored it.

We read the canonical scriptures of the Testamentum literally—to receive the Gospel of the true God, revealed by Christ.

We do not blend the two, and we do not put new wine into old bottles.

As the Lord declares in the Evangelicon, “No man putteth new wine into old bottles.”

We heed that word. The gospel of the Father cannot be poured into the skins of the Law.

Finally, Adamantius preserves this same literalist instinct on the lips of the Marcionites themselves: when Adamantius attempts to blunt the antitheses by retreating into figurative explanation, the Marcionite refuses the escape-route—“Which here is a simile? … Nothing is altogether a simile,” and again, “It is not a simile”—insisting that the text must be taken as it stands, in its plain and moral sense, rather than dissolved into allegory. And when Adamantius tries to import proof from outside the Evangelicon, the Marcionite objects, “Such is not written in our Gospel,” thereby binding doctrine to the written words themselves rather than to later harmonizations or interpretive inventions.

See also: Question 2.03, “What is the hierarchy of authority in the Marcionite Church?”; Question 3.15, “Is the Hebrew Bible part of the Christian canon?”

questions.question: Why do Jesus and Paul refer to the Hebrew Bible?
questions.answer:
Jesus frequently taught through parables, allegories, and metaphors—methods intended to reveal spiritual truths in symbolic or illustrative form. In the canonical scriptures of the Testamentum, references to the Hebrew Bible are didactic in nature. A citation or allusion does not imply that the referenced text is divinely inspired; rather, it may simply serve as a familiar point of contact for the audience.

One can acknowledge that a text contains ethical or moral insights without attributing divine authority to it. In the rare instances where Jesus or the Apostle Paul reference the Hebrew scriptures, it is typically either to correct or challenge their claims, or to acknowledge limited truths within them.

Adamantius preserves this very Marcionite distinction in debate: when the judge presses why the Apostle Paul “makes use of the words of the prophets,” the Marcionite Megethius refuses to treat the Apostle Paul’s quotations as a grant of divine precedence, answering instead that Paul is speaking of “the law of Moses and not of God.” In other words, the Marcionite claim is that Paul cites the Hebrew scriptures as a disputational instrument—turning their familiar language against them for the sake of correction—rather than as an appeal to a higher, binding authority.

Given that Jesus primarily preached to Jewish and Samaritan audiences, and that Paul addressed both Jews and Gentile proselytes familiar with Jewish teachings, the Hebrew Bible served as a useful rhetorical and pedagogical tool. By invoking scriptures familiar to his listeners, Jesus and Paul were able to communicate profound theological truths in a manner that resonated with their audience—without affirming the divine inspiration of the Hebrew texts themselves.

See also: Question 3.15, “Is the Hebrew Bible part of the Christian canon?”; Question 4.14, “How does the Church interpret scripture?”

questions.question: Which translations of the Hebrew scriptures were historically used for refutations?
questions.answer:
Marcionite polemic did not treat the Hebrew Bible as a neutral or self-authenticating text; when it was cited, it was typically handled as evidence against the false covenant, and therefore it mattered which form of the text was being quoted. In practice, that meant privileging Greek witnesses that were more exact, more transparent, and less smoothed-over than popularized renderings—because a tighter translation leaves less room to “explain away” contradictions or morally indefensible portrayals of the false deity.

A key ancient testimony on this point concerns Theodotion. Ancient writers report that Theodotion was a Marcionite Christian proselyte from Ephesus, who learned Hebrew and produced a comprehensive Hebrew-to-Greek translation of the Hebrew Scriptures. In the Marcionite understanding, this mattered because they did not trust the prevailing text-traditions—Hebrew, Aramaic, or earlier Greek witnesses—to be handled honestly by their custodians; they believed those traditions were frequently interpreted through allegory and ecclesiastical gloss in ways that softened the plain sense of the text and hid its moral and doctrinal problems.

What made Theodotion’s version different, in that context, was its method: it aimed to track the Hebrew more closely and render it more literally into Greek, with fewer interpretive liberties. This enabled a more thorough literalist reading of the texts, bringing forward the plain claims, commands, and depictions of the false deity without the usual softening layers of harmonization or allegory. For that reason, it is widely believed that such more accurate and transparent translations were well-suited—indeed, likely preferred—for possibly Marcion’s Antitheses and more likely for Apelles’ Syllogisms.

Unfortunately, no complete, independent copy of this translation (in the form Marcionites would have circulated and controlled) survives to us today. In practical terms, the loss of those more severe, literal textual witnesses made it easier for later centuries to drift toward a more Judaized Christianity—one increasingly reconciled to the Hebrew Bible by interpretive smoothing, harmonization, and appeals to the very textual conventions Marcionites rejected.

It is also worth noting that an earlier Greek translation current is likewise associated with Marcion’s home region: Aquila of Sinope produced a famously literal Greek rendering of the Hebrew Scriptures. Given Aquila’s provenance and the timeline, Marcion likely knew of, had access to, or at least benefited from the same Pontic translation milieu—and such a translation would have served the same basic Marcionite purpose: a strict, literal presentation of the false covenant’s text that could be set plainly against the Gospel in antithetical argument.

questions.question: How long did Jesus’ ministry last?
questions.answer:
The earthly ministry of Jesus Christ is understood to have lasted a little more than four years, reckoned from His descent and manifestation to the Passion.

The Evangelicon contains at least three distinct references to separate Passover observances, namely His first journey to Jerusalem, His second pilgrimage there, and finally the occasion of the Last Supper. Additionally, the Feast of Levi the Publican is widely interpreted as a fourth reference to a Passover meal, further substantiating the chronological framework.

By correlating these Passover markers with the foundational dates held in Marcionite reckoning, namely Christ’s descent into Capernaum on either December 31, 28 C.E. or January 1, 29 C.E., and His crucifixion on April 3, 33 C.E., we arrive at a ministry duration of four years, three months, and three days (from December 31) or four years, three months, and two days (from January 1).

For reference, the reconstructed Jerusalem Passover dates (Nisan 14, Passover eve and preparation, in the Julian calendar) for these years are as follows:

29 C.E.: April 18

30 C.E.: April 7

31 C.E.: March 27

32 C.E.: April 13

33 C.E.: April 3

questions.question: What is the role of John the Baptist in the Evangelicon?
questions.answer:
John the Baptist is the first true prophet of Christ’s testament and the divinely sent forerunner who prepares, bears witness, and then yields the stage to Christ. He is not a prophet of the false deity known as Yahweh, and his witness is completely independent of Judaic prophecies, rites, rituals, and scriptures. The Evangelicon presents him as the earthly witness to the heavenly descent: “There was a man sent from God… He was not that Light, but was sent to bear witness of that Light” (Evangelicon 1:6–8); “John bare witness of him” (Evangelicon 1:15); and “Jesus descended out of heaven into the Galilean city of Capernaum” (Evangelicon 2:3). In the Evangelicon there is no nativity, no genealogy, and no baptism of Jesus by John; John and Jesus are not related, they never meet, and John is not connected with Elias. Jesus appears and acts on the Father’s authority, set forth in a triad of witness: His self-testimony—“I am the light of the world” (Evangelicon 2:23)—His works and mission from the Father—“Though I bear record of myself… I and the Father that sent me” (Evangelicon 2:25; Evangelicon 2:27–29)—and His appeal to a “greater witness than that of John,” namely His works and the Father’s testimony (Evangelicon 7:34–35). John’s role is honored—“a burning and a shining light”—yet Christ adds, “I receive not testimony from man” (Evangelicon 7:31–33; Evangelicon 7:32).

John’s ministry is real and weighty. He calls to repentance—“O generation of vipers… the axe is laid unto the root… every tree… is hewn down, and cast into the fire” (Evangelicon 2:81–84)—and the response divides the public: “All the people, and the publicans, justified God… but the Pharisees and lawyers rejected the counsel of God” (Evangelicon 5:29–30). The transition from forerunner to Christ occurs through practice rather than a baptismal rite: fasting gives way to the Bridegroom’s presence and new wine (Evangelicon 3:53–57); the disciples ask, “Lord, teach us to pray, as John also taught his disciples,” and Jesus gives the prayer (Evangelicon 10:1); the narrative marks a turning point “until John,” after which the kingdom is proclaimed (Evangelicon 14:17). As John’s course ends, he sends two disciples from prison—“Art thou he that should come? or look we for another?”—and Jesus answers with deeds—“the blind see… the dead are raised… to the poor have good tidings been announced”—then adds the beatitude and publicly calls John “more than a prophet,” while declaring “he that is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he” (Evangelicon 5:19–23; Evangelicon 5:22; Evangelicon 5:26; Evangelicon 5:28). In the Evangelicon this prison inquiry functions as the baptism-replacement: John always knows whom he is witnessing, may be tested under imprisonment, but sends his disciples to Christ and concludes his work (Evangelicon 5:19–28).

Christ then leverages John’s public credibility to expose hostile leaders: “The baptism of John, was it from heaven, or of men?” When they refuse, He replies, “Neither tell I you by what authority I do these things,” warning that the stone will break or grind those who stumble (Evangelicon 18:4–9). John’s witness is finally sealed in martyrdom: the court’s perplexity—“John have I beheaded: but who is this?”—precedes the beheading narrative (Evangelicon 8:7–9; Evangelicon 8:10–22). In sum, the Evangelicon’s presentation is clear and consistent: John is the Father’s prophet, not Yahweh’s; he is not tied to Hebrew prophecies or Second Temple frameworks; he does not inaugurate Jesus’ ministry by baptism, nor share kinship, nor meet Him. He prepares, bears witness to the heavenly descent, hands his disciples to Christ, and departs; Jesus stands vindicated by His works and the Father’s testimony (Evangelicon 7:31–35; Evangelicon 2:3).

questions.question: How does the Church regard the original Twelve Apostles?
questions.answer:
We regard the original Twelve Apostles as true but flawed and imperfect apostles of Christ. They were witnesses to his earthly ministry and were called by him, but they did not receive the fullness of the gospel in the same way Paul did. For this reason, we hold to the doctrine of Pauline primacy: the teaching that Paul, not the Twelve, was the chief apostle of the true Gospel because he received the fullness of his revelation directly from Christ. Paul alone among the apostles was endowed with the gift of prophecy.

The Twelve were therefore secondary to Paul. They believed in Christ, but they often failed to understand the true meaning of his descent, his grace, and his deliverance from the law and the powers of this world. Peter especially stands as the clearest example of this failure. Though he confessed Christ, he later wavered, compromised, and was rebuked by Paul because he “walked not uprightly according to the truth of the gospel” (Galatians 2:14). His weakness helped preserve the influence of James, Jerusalem, and the law, which contributed to the later corruption of the Church and the obscuring of the true gospel.

This does not mean that the Twelve were unbelievers or enemies of Christ. Rather, they were incomplete and unstable witnesses. They knew Christ according to his appearance among men, but Paul knew him according to the higher revelation of the gospel. The failure of the Twelve, especially Peter’s failure to stand firmly against Judaizing corruption, allowed the Church to drift away from the gospel of grace and toward the false covenant, ritualism, and legalism.

John and Andrew stand out as partial exceptions. According to Marcionite tradition, John was the teacher of Marcion in Ephesus and received the Pauline epistles, showing that he came to recognize the superior authority of Paul’s revelation. Andrew likewise is remembered as having ordained Marcion’s father as bishop of Sinope, who was himself a disciple of Paul. These traditions show that at least some among the apostolic generation moved closer to the truth during their earthly lives than Peter did, turning in a more Pauline direction and separating themselves from the Jerusalem-centered influence of James and Peter.

Thus, we honor the Twelve as early Christian witnesses, but we do not place them above Paul. They were called by Christ, but Paul was chosen to reveal the fullness of Christ’s gospel. The Twelve saw the beginning; Paul received the mystery. The Twelve bore witness imperfectly; Paul preached the gospel in its purity.

questions.question: Who were the Seventy Disciples?
questions.answer:
The Seventy Disciples were early emissaries appointed and commissioned by Jesus Christ, as recorded in the Evangelicon. They were sent out in pairs on specific missions to prepare the way for His arrival in various cities and regions.

“And after these things the Lord appointed other seventy also, and sent them two and two before his face into every city and place, whither he himself was about to come.”

Evangelicon 9:1

Below is the Marcionite Church’s compilation of the Seventy. Our method is twofold: it is scripturally grounded in the Evangelicon and the Apostolicon; and it cross-checks those names against traditional rosters transmitted through history, retaining customary honorifics and traditional locales where appropriate.

Also, unlike many inherited rosters, this list includes women named in Scripture and received in the tradition. It also notes that among the Seventy is Philologus of Sinope, recognized as the father of Marcion.

This list reflects the wide geographical spread of the early Church and the diversity of leaders commissioned by Christ to carry His message to the nations.

questions.question: Who replaced Judas Iscariot as an Apostle?
questions.answer: The Marcionite Church affirms that the Apostle Paul was personally appointed by the risen Jesus Christ and thereby elevated to apostolic authority, taking the place of Judas Iscariot as one of the true Twelve Apostles.

questions.question: Who were the “false apostles” that Paul mentions?
questions.answer:
The Apostle Paul employed the term “false apostles” primarily in reference to the Judaizers—a faction of Jewish Christians who insisted that adherence to the Mosaic Law, including circumcision, was necessary for salvation and for the acceptance of Gentile converts into the Christian faith.

Paul was unwavering in his opposition to the Judaizers, regarding their teachings as a fundamental distortion of the Gospel of Christ. He saw them not only as a threat to the universality of the Christian message but as disseminators of grave doctrinal error. Throughout his epistles—as preserved in the Apostolicon within the Testamentum—Paul offers a sustained and vigorous refutation of the Judaizing position, denouncing both their theology and their divisive influence within the early Church.

Paul’s commitment to the Gospel of grace led him even to publicly confront the Apostle Peter, whom he accused of compromising with the Judaizers. Paul rebuked Peter for showing outward support for their practices in certain contexts while privately distancing himself from their doctrines. This confrontation underscores Paul’s belief that the Gospel must be free from the legalism and ritualism of the Mosaic covenant.

All evidence within Paul’s writings suggests a complete rejection of any attempt to integrate Judaism into Christianity. His stance was later echoed by figures such as Barnabas, Basilides, Cerdo, and Marcion, as well as by various Gnostic Christians—all of whom opposed the inclusion of the Hebrew Bible within the Christian canon.

As Paul warned:

“For such are false apostles, deceitful workers, transforming themselves into the apostles of Christ.”

II Corinthians 11:13

questions.question: Why did Jesus rename Simon to Peter?
questions.answer:
From the standpoint of the Marcionite Church, Jesus did not rename Simon “Peter” in order to make him the infallible foundation of the Church or to authorize the later claims of the emerging Catholic Church. Rather, the new name is a prophetic and judgment-laden sign. “Peter” means “rock,” and in a Marcionite reading that rock symbolizes hardness, heaviness, and an earthbound attachment to the old and false order—specifically to the Law of the Hebrew Bible and to Yahweh’s false covenant.

As “Simon,” he is the one who hears: a disciple who has truly responded to Christ, but who stands between one old and false covenant and one new and true covenant. By calling him “Peter,” Jesus identifies what Simon will become: the leading figure of a Judaizing community that tries to merge the new and true covenant of the one true God, God the Father, with Yahweh’s false covenant. Peter thus becomes the “rock” on which a mixed religion is built—a religion that confuses the one true God with a false deity and binds the gospel back under the Law.

In contrast, the Marcionite Church looks to the Apostle Paul, not Peter, as the true foundational witness. Paul proclaims a gospel that is free from the entanglements of the Hebrew Bible and from the claims of Yahweh’s false covenant.
