Jesus’ renaming of Simon to Peter is therefore read as a warning sign in the gospel itself: a revelation that Peter’s name and legacy would underwrite an institutional, Judaizing church, while the true Church of God the Father would be grounded instead in the heavenly revelation entrusted to Paul.

questions.question: Who are the Four Evangelists?
questions.answer:
Marcionites do not use the phrase “the four evangelists” to mean four competing Gospel authors (Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John). Our canon has one Gospel, and when the Apostle Paul speaks of “evangelists,” he is saying in the Pauline sense of authorized Gospel preachers and emissaries, not in later attributions of separate written gospels. This is precisely how the Marcionite spokesman Megethius argues in the Dialogues of Adamantius: when pressed on “four evangelists,” he rejects the category and insists that Paul’s language about “evangelists” refers instead to Paul’s own co-labourers, naming Silvanus and Timothy as the relevant referents.

Following that Pauline usage, the Marcionite tradition identifies the “four evangelists” as the four principal Pauline emissaries who carried, guarded, and proclaimed the Gospel alongside the Apostle in the churches: Silvanus, Timothy, Epaphroditus, and Tychicus. This is not a claim about four authored “Gospels,” but a claim about four evangelical ministers, the living messengers through whom the one Gospel was announced and the Pauline mission was sustained.

The later Paulicians, whom we treat as Neo-Marcionites in many respects, preserve this exact Pauline definition in the medieval record. Byzantine abjuration formulae explicitly accuse the Paulicians of “accepting” Paul’s disciples as types of the four evangelists, and in the same sequence they name the Pauline figures associated with that evangelic role, including Silvanus, Timothy, Epaphroditus, and Tychicus.

questions.question: What is the significance of Naaman the Syrian in the Evangelicon?
questions.answer:
“And many lepers were in Israel in the time of Elisha the prophet; and none of them was cleansed, saving Naaman the Syrian.”

Evangelicon 3:12

In this passage, Jesus allegorically references the story of Naaman the Syrian as a teaching tool—illustrating that divine grace and healing are not reserved for the religiously privileged or ethnically chosen, but extended even to those whom society deems unworthy. Naaman, a Gentile and the commander of the Aramean army that had warred against Israel, would have been viewed by the Jewish audience as both an outsider and an enemy. Yet, in the story, it is he—not an Israelite—who receives healing from leprosy.

Jesus invokes this account not to affirm the historical accuracy or divine inspiration of the Hebrew Bible, but to challenge the exclusivist mindset of His audience. His point was clear: God’s mercy is not bound by ethnic or religious boundaries.

At the time, Galilean Jews were living under Roman occupation, and the mere suggestion that God might favor Gentiles—especially over Jews—was met with intense hostility. This hostility is precisely what unfolds in Nazareth following Jesus’ remarks:

“And they in the synagogue, when they heard these things, were filled with wrath, And rose up, and thrust him out of the city, and led him unto the brow of the hill whereon their city was built, that they might cast him down headlong. But he passing through the midst of them went his way to Capernaum.”

Evangelicon 3:13–15

Jesus’ reference to the story of Naaman was not an endorsement of the Hebrew Bible’s historical reliability. Rather, it was a rhetorical use of a familiar narrative to convey a deeper spiritual truth—that God’s grace often reaches those least expected, and that divine favor is not determined by ancestry or religious observance.

questions.question: Why does the Evangelicon open with the Hymn to the Word?
questions.answer:
In Against Heresies, Irenaeus quotes what is commonly known today as the Hymn to the Word in the Gospel of John and uses it to criticize the alleged theological positions of Marcion and his followers. He quotes the line, “He was in this world, and the world was made by Him, and the world knew Him not,” and then says that, according to Marcion, “neither was the world made by Him; nor did He come to His own things, but to those of another” (Irenaeus, Against Heresies 3.12.2).

Scholar Adolf von Harnack suggested that this may show only that Marcion knew the Hymn, not necessarily that he included it in his canon. But the argument can be reversed. If Irenaeus is using the Hymn in a polemic against Marcionite Christians, that may indicate that Marcionites knew, used, or at least had to answer that text. Heresiologists often argued from texts their opponents were known to respect, because those texts had force inside the controversy.

A similar pattern appears in Tertullian. His repeated method of argumentation follows the formula: “The Gospel says X, but Marcion teaches Y,” or “Paul says X, yet the Marcionites assert Y.” These rhetorical constructions are not always grounded in accurate representation of Marcionite scripture. They are polemical contrasts meant to make Marcionite theology appear absurd. Irenaeus’ use of the Hymn should be read in the same way: not as proof that the Hymn is anti-Marcionite, but as evidence that the Hymn was useful in the second-century controversy over Christ, creation, matter, and the world.

The Hymn is also not hostile to Marcionite theology when read carefully. It begins not with the Hebrew creation myth, but with the eternal Word: “In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.” This is not a six-day creation narrative. It is a cosmic and spiritual confession of Christ’s preexistence, His relation to God the Father, and His authority over the heavenly and invisible powers.

The line “All things were made by Him; and without Him was not any thing made that was made” does not require the belief that God the Father directly fashioned the visible world in the manner described in Genesis. Rather, it accords with the Apostle’s teaching that all things “that are in heaven and invisible,” including “thrones,” “dominions,” “principalities,” and “powers,” stand beneath Christ and consist in Him. The Hymn speaks of ultimate dependence upon the Word; it does not deny mediated formation, subordinate powers, or deferred demiurgy.

This is exactly the logic of deferred demiurgy. Deferred demiurgy means that creation is mediated, not direct. The visible, fleshly, and corruptible world was not fashioned immediately by God the Father in the fullness of His perfection, but mediately through lower principalities, powers, thrones, dominions, and angelic rulers beneath Him. The Hymn defends this hierarchy by placing Christ before all things and above all things, while leaving room for the lower order through which the visible world was fashioned.

The Hymn also supports the Marcionite view of matter. Matter itself, called by the Apostle “the elements of this world,” is not identical with the world-order fashioned from it. Matter is the underlying substance of the visible order. Hippolytus says that Marcion and Cerdo taught “three principles of the universe,” namely “good, just, and matter,” and that the lower world-fashioning power “made all things out of subjacent matter” (Hippolytus, Refutation of All Heresies 7.17).

Read in that light, the Hymn’s statement that nothing made exists apart from the Word means that matter itself is not an independent eternal rival to God. Matter ultimately depends upon God the Father through the Word. But the visible, fleshly, corruptible world-order was fashioned from matter by the ‘god of this world.’ The bronze comes from God; the statue is shaped by the lower craftsman.

This also explains the Hymn’s language of light and darkness. “The light shineth in darkness; and the darkness comprehended it not.” The lower world is real, but darkened. Matter is real, but not evil in itself. The visible order is real, but fleshly, corruptible, and unable to know the Father apart from Christ. When Jesus descends, the Light enters the lower realm and exposes its ignorance.

Irenaeus tried to use the Hymn against Marcionite Christianity because he assumed that “the world was made by Him” must mean that the visible world was directly fashioned by the same God proclaimed in the Hebrew Bible. But that is precisely the assumption Marcionite Christians reject. The Hymn does not say that Genesis is Christian scripture. It does not say that the Father fashioned the world in six days. It does not say that the lower principalities and powers played no role in the visible order. It says that all things ultimately depend upon the Word, and that the world did not know Him.

That last point is deeply Marcionite. “The world knew Him not” is the language of alienation. Christ descends into a world that does not recognize Him because He comes from above it. He is not merely a prophet of the lower order, nor a servant of the ‘god of this world.’ He is the Light from the Father, entering the darkness of the visible realm to reveal the unknown God and rescue souls from bondage.

For these reasons, the reconstructed Evangelicon as preserved by the Marcionite Church opens with the Hymn as its first chapter. It is a declaration of Christ’s cosmic preexistence, His supremacy over the heavenly and invisible powers, His role as the source through whom all things ultimately consist, and His descent as Light into the darkness of the lower world.

See also: Question 9.11, “How does the Church understand Logos-mediated creation in relation to Marcionite deferred demiurgy?”; Question 9.16, “What form of the creation hymn does the Church receive from Colossians?”

It is also noteworthy that Tatian’s Diatessaron, an early gospel harmony, likewise begins with the Hymn. Modern scholarship notes that Tatian begins with John 1:1-5, placing the whole gospel narrative under the theological framework of “In the beginning was the Word.” This gives additional historical support to opening a gospel text with the Hymn rather than with a birth narrative or genealogy.

Thus, the Hymn does not undermine Marcionite Christianity. It strengthens it. It teaches that Christ is from above, that He precedes the visible world, that all things ultimately depend upon Him, that darkness did not comprehend Him, and that His descent into the world was the revelation of the Father whom the world did not know.

questions.question: What is the distinction between Pauline and Petrine Christianity?
questions.answer:
Controversy took root in Christianity from its earliest days. When the Apostle Paul converted, he did not seek to reform Judaism but to leave it behind entirely. Paul believed that he was led by the indwelling Spirit of Christ and that his gospel was not of human origin, but received through divine revelation from the risen Jesus. For Paul, Christ was the universal Savior of all humanity—Jew and Gentile alike.

Throughout the first and second centuries, a profound theological divide emerged between the followers of Paul and those aligned with the Apostle Peter. Pauline Christians held that the Gospel was a new, universal covenant—open to all, independent of Jewish law. In contrast, Petrine Christians insisted that Christianity was an extension of Judaism, requiring adherence to Jewish customs such as circumcision. In their eyes, uncircumcised converts were illegitimate Christians, and bishops who had not come from the Jewish priesthood were not valid clergy. Some extreme groups, such as the Ebionites, took this position even further, demanding strict adherence to Mosaic law and denying the legitimacy of Paul’s apostleship altogether.

This division manifested in ecclesiastical conflicts as well. Catholic canon law, said to be derived from apostolic tradition, later insisted that there could be only one bishop per city. Yet in Antioch—one of the earliest Christian centers—records show the simultaneous presence of two bishops: Evodius, ordained by Peter, and Ignatius, ordained by Paul. This dual succession reflects the deeper theological and institutional schism between the Pauline and Petrine streams of early Christianity.

A similar tension appears in the early episcopate of Rome. Ancient sources suggest that Linus, recognized as the first bishop of Rome, was ordained by Paul. He was followed by Anacletus (or Cletus), also said to have been appointed by Paul. Clement, traditionally regarded as ordained by Peter, is listed as third or even fourth in some episcopal lists. However, in other traditions—especially those emphasizing Petrine primacy—Clement is portrayed as the first legitimate bishop, as if the Roman church only attained apostolic legitimacy once a successor of Peter took office. Jerome himself remarked on this ambiguity, writing: “Clement… the fourth bishop of Rome after Peter, if indeed the second was Linus and the third Anacletus, although most of the Latins think that Clement was second after the apostle [Peter].”

This complex and overlapping episcopal lineage strongly suggests that, as in Antioch, Rome too had parallel jurisdictions—one tracing its authority to Paul, the other to Peter. This dual succession helps explain the difficulty historians face in establishing clear dates and order for the earliest bishops of Rome. Rather than a single, unified episcopacy, early Christianity appears to have operated under competing apostolic lineages, shaped by profound theological differences that persisted well into the development of the institutional Church.

questions.question: Who was Philologus of Sinope?
questions.answer:
Philologus of Sinope, the father of Marcion of Sinope, is traditionally identified as one of the Seventy Disciples of Jesus Christ. According to early ecclesiastical tradition, he was consecrated as Bishop of Sinope by the Apostle Andrew.

Philologus is also believed to have been a companion of the Apostle Paul during his time in Rome. He is mentioned in the Epistle to the Alexandrians, where the text reads:

“Salute Philologus, and Julia, Nereus, and his sister, and Olympas, and all the saints which are with them.”

Alexandrians 1:15

This passage attests to Philologus’s early role in the formation of the Pauline communities and his close association with apostolic leadership.

The Menologion of Emperor Basil II (tenth century) is the earliest surviving source to name Bishop Philologus as Marcion’s father. Byzantine compilers had no motive to glorify a condemned heretic by inventing such a pedigree—the Apostle Andrew ordained Philologus, he was counted among Christ’s Seventy disciples, and he was a devoted companion of the Apostle Paul, who even mentioned him in one of his epistles. Falsely attributing so distinguished a lineage would only have bolstered Marcion’s authority; in the absence of any reason to lie, this claim must be accepted as authentic.

questions.question: Did Philologus expel Marcion?
questions.answer:
The claim that Philologus expelled Marcion—the rumor repeated centuries later by both Tertullian and Epiphanius—can be traced to hostile polemical tradition rather than reliable Marcionite memory.

This natural and orderly succession was later twisted by Catholic polemicists into a tale of scandal. Tertullian, writing in Adversus Marcionem, asserted:

“Marcion, the heretic, was disinherited by his own bishop-father, and cast out of the Church.” — Adversus Marcionem 1.1

Epiphanius, writing more than two centuries later in the Panarion, embellished the accusation with a morally charged fabrication:

“When he was young, he seduced a virgin and was excommunicated by his own father, who was a bishop.” — Panarion 42.1–3

These claims are entirely unsubstantiated by contemporary evidence and follow a familiar pattern of rhetorical slander employed by heresiologists to discredit theological opponents. According to Marcionite tradition, the truth is far different: Marcion was not a disgraced rebel, but a faithful heir to the Pauline mission, appointed after years of spiritual preparation and apostolic training. The narrative later crafted by Rome was not a record of history, but an act of institutional propaganda, designed to undermine Marcion’s legitimacy and obscure his apostolic inheritance.

questions.question: Who were the Cerdonians?
questions.answer:
The Cerdonians were a proto-Marcionite group founded by Cerdo, a Syrian Christian teacher who came to Rome before Marcion’s public ministry there. They were one of the immediate forerunners of the Marcionite Church: Pauline in disposition, sharply anti-Judaizing, and already committed to the distinction between God the Father revealed by Christ and the lower ‘god of this world’ known through the Law, judgment, and the material order.

Cerdo was excommunicated in 138 C.E., several years before Marcion’s own rejection in 144 C.E. Yet Cerdo’s response was different from Marcion’s. He did not establish a separate church of his own. Instead, he repeatedly sought to regain communion with the Roman church, only to fall back again into the same confession that had first brought him under suspicion. Later heresiologists mocked him for this pattern, portraying him as a man who was continually leaving the church and then reentering it.

The Cerdonians appear to have shared a common scriptural foundation with the later Marcionites, centered on the Gospel and the Pauline epistles, rather than the Hebrew Bible or the later catholic expansion of the canon. Their theology was not yet as fully organized as Marcion’s, but it already moved in the same direction: the Gospel of Christ revealed a previously unknown Father, while the world below was ruled by an inferior power marked by ignorance, judgment, contradiction, and negative demiurgy.

The existence of Cerdo and the Cerdonians also shows that Pauline Christianity had already spread widely and retained real strength before the formal organization of the Marcionite Church. Marcion did not invent this current out of nothing. He inherited, clarified, and organized a living Pauline movement already present in Rome, while Cerdo’s Syrian origin suggests that this same Pauline disposition remained strong in Syria as well. The Cerdonians therefore stand as evidence that the Gospel of Paul continued to move through the churches before Marcion gave it institutional form.

At the same time, the Cerdonians seem to have retained some gnosticizing influences. Ancient opponents describe Cerdo’s teaching in language that overlaps with wider second-century dualist and anti-cosmic currents. Yet the movement was not simply “gnostic” in the later technical sense. Its heart was Pauline: salvation came through the revelation of Christ, apart from the Law, apart from the world-ruling powers, and apart from the righteousness claimed by the lower order.

When Marcion arrived in Rome, the Cerdonians welcomed him as the figure capable of giving their movement clearer doctrine, discipline, liturgy, canon, and public form. They supported Marcion’s candidacy for the bishopric of Rome and saw in him the rightful organizer of the apostolic Gospel against the Judaizing party. After Marcion’s rejection in 144 C.E., the Cerdonian circle did not continue as a lasting rival body. It folded into the Marcionite Church, becoming part of the broader communion that carried Marcion’s Evangelicon, Apostolicon, and antithesis between God the Father and the ‘god of this world’ across the Roman worl

questions.question: Did Marcion seek the bishopric of Rome?
questions.answer:
Around the year 142 C.E., following the death of Hyginus, the Church of Rome stood at a defining crossroads. Marcion of Sinope, bishop of Sinope and a devoted follower of the Apostle Paul, presented himself as a candidate for the episcopal office in Rome.

According to Marcionite tradition, the first legitimate bishop of Rome was Linus, ordained by Paul, and succeeded by Anacletus, the second Pauline bishop. The Catholic faction, however, traced its authority through Peter, and claimed Clement—the first Petrine bishop of Rome—as the founder of their episcopal succession. After Anacletus’s death, the Pauline line weakened, and the Petrine succession gained exclusive control, establishing a single episcopal line that would come to dominate the Roman Church. This shift set a precedent of two competing apostolic jurisdictions—Pauline and Petrine—vying for the soul of the Church.

To support his effort, Marcion donated 200,000 sesterces to the Roman Church. His gospel message was radical and clear: the God revealed by Jesus Christ was a God of perfect mercy and goodness, wholly distinct from the false god Yahweh of the Hebrew scriptures—a god of wrath, judgment, and legalism. Alongside him, Valentinus of Egypt also sought influence, promoting a mystical Gnostic worldview. Yet both men were ultimately rejected in favor of Pius (later Pius I), who upheld the Catholic project of merging Jewish scripture with Christian revelation.

Marcion initially remained within the Roman community, leading a growing reformist faction. But on the Ides of July, 144 C.E., Bishop Pius excommunicated him, returned his donation, and expelled him from communion. According to Marcionite tradition, the following day Marcion stood before the Roman clergy and solemnly declared:

“I will divide your Church and cause within her a division, which will last forever.”

Two years earlier, in 142 C.E., Marcion had already been ordained bishop of Antioch, succeeding Cornelius, a Petrine-aligned bishop. Upon assuming the post, he resigned his episcopacy in his native Sinope, where he had previously led a small but faithful Pauline community. From Antioch—a city that at the time rivaled Rome in power and prestige—Marcion began organizing a new ecclesiastical order, founded entirely on the gospel of Paul, the Apostolicon, and the Evangelicon. Anatolia, his homeland, quickly became the heartland of Marcionism, where his message of freedom from the law and rejection of Yahweh found a devoted and expanding audience. Many eastern bishops were in closer communion with Antioch than with Rome, aligning themselves with Marcion’s gospel of grace.

Like Rome, Antioch had a history of dual apostolic lineage. According to Marcionite understanding, its first Pauline bishop was Ignatius, a disciple of Paul who upheld the primacy of grace. In contrast, Catholic tradition traced its Petrine succession in Antioch back to Evodius, regarded as the first Petrine bishop of Antioch, allegedly ordained by Peter himself. This legacy of duality set the stage for the schism that followed.

Marcionite tradition holds that, in response to Marcion’s rising influence and consolidation of the Pauline faction, the emerging Catholic Church installed Heron II as a parallel Petrine bishop in Antioch shortly after Marcion’s excommunication in 144 C.E. Heron II represented the institutional wing rooted in apostolic succession through Peter and theological allegiance to the Hebrew scriptures. Antioch thus became a divided episcopate, with Marcion and Heron II embodying two rival theological visions: one grounded in law and prophetic continuity, the other in grace and the radical freedom of Christ revealed apart from the law.

It was also in Antioch, around 169 C.E., that the Catholic bishop Theophilus began to edit the Evangelicon, reworking and altering Marcion’s gospel by adding new material and removing other material so as to align it with the Catholic view of scripture and doctrine. This reworked version would later circulate as the Gospel of Luke, stripped of its original anti-Judaic and Pauline clarity. It marked the beginning of Rome’s efforts to co-opt and neutralize Marcion’s scriptural legacy.

Following his excommunication, Marcion formally established a separate Church, declared himself Archbishop—a title later twisted by his Catholic opponents who mocked him as the “Archheretic”—and ordained trusted disciples to key episcopal seats across the Christian world. Among the most significant were:

Apelles, ordained bishop of Alexandria, who would later succeed Marcion as Archbishop after his martyrdom.

Lucanus, ordained as the Marcionite bishop of Rome, continued the Pauline witness in the heart of Catholic power.

Onesimus, ordained bishop of Ephesus, a church founded by Paul and long faithful to its Pauline heritage. It was in Ephesus that Marcion had originally served as a disciple of the Apostle John.

Damas, ordained bishop of Magnesia on the Maeander, a city eager to embrace Marcion’s gospel over Rome’s legalism.

Polybius, ordained as bishop of Tralles, an Anatolian city that openly rejected Petrine ecclesiology.

Metrodorus, ordained bishop of Smyrna, where he led the Marcionite community in open and ongoing rivalry with the Petrine faction headed by Polycarp, one of Marcion’s most relentless adversaries. It was in Smyrna that Marcion had earlier delivered his famed Homily to Diognetus.

These churches—Alexandria, Rome, Ephesus, Magnesia, Tralles, Philadelphia, and Smyrna—stood at the forefront of Marcion’s ecclesiastical rebellion. Each declared its loyalty to the God revealed by Christ alone, rejected the authority of the Hebrew scriptures, and severed communion with the Roman and Petrine hierarchy. In these cities of Anatolia, the gospel of Paul took deep root, as Marcionite bishops took their place beside, or in place of, their Catholic counterparts.

In 154 C.E., Marcion returned to Rome, not to debate, but to face martyrdom. He had been condemned by Roman authorities for fomenting division among the Christian communities of the capital. During this final journey, he encountered his lifelong adversary, Polycarp of Smyrna, who, upon seeing him, famously declared:

“Yea, I know thee as the first-born of Satan.”

Unmoved by scorn or condemnation, Marcion was led into the Colosseum, where he was martyred for the gospel he believed had been revealed through Paul alone—a gospel of grace, liberty, and the worship of the true God, wholly distinct from the false deity of the law.

Though the later ancient Catholic Church would enshrine figures like Pius and Polycarp as saints and defenders of orthodoxy, it was Marcion’s legacy that forced the early Church to define its canon, its creeds, and its institutional authority. The division he foretold became real—and in many ways, has never been healed.

questions.question: How did the Catholic Church alter the teachings of Paul?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church confesses that the emerging Catholic Church—founded by followers of the Apostle Peter—and the Marcionite Church—established in the tradition of the Apostle Paul—were, from the beginning, divided over the true meaning of the Gospel.

After Marcion’s death, Catholic authorities reportedly destroyed the original Pauline texts preserved by the Marcionite Church. In doing so, they ensured that only their redacted versions would survive, allowing them to retroactively claim apostolic authenticity for texts that had been altered to conform to Catholic theology.

The Acts of the Apostles was similarly crafted with the purpose of recasting Paul as a loyal Catholic missionary, harmonizing his mission with Petrine authority. Scholarly consensus holds that the same author who penned the Gospel of Luke also authored Acts—a narrative shaped more by ecclesiastical politics than historical fact. Indeed, many of the events described in Acts lack any corroborating historical record. As the scholar Hermann Detering observed: “The great majority of historical statements made in Acts about the life and person of the Apostle Paul are legendary in character and thus are to be enjoyed only with great caution.” The figure presented in Acts is not the radical Apostle Paul of the original Gospel, but a domesticated “Catholic” version—one created to absorb Paul into a broader ecclesial unity.

Rather than continuing to oppose Paul, as was initially the case, the emerging Catholic Church ultimately co-opted him—constructing a sanitized, orthodox version of the apostle and casting Marcion as a heretic for faithfully preserving Paul’s original gospel.

The accusation that Marcion “founded” his own church was a strategic invention of the early Catholic hierarchy. In truth, Marcion did not establish a new religion, but sought to preserve the authentic, Pauline Church rooted in the teachings of the Apostle to the Gentiles. To undermine this claim, Catholic tradition began depicting Paul and Peter as close allies—appearing together in church iconography, often as friends or even brothers in ministry.

This revisionist portrayal was designed to reinforce Catholic authority and gradually eclipse the widespread influence of the Marcionite Church. Through theological appropriation, institutional power, and historical suppression, the emerging Catholic Church eventually gained the upper hand, but only by rewriting the legacy of Paul and marginalizing the church that had most faithfully preserved his message.

questions.question: Who were the disciples of Marcion?
questions.answer: Among the known disciples and early successors of Marcion of Sinope were Apelles of Alexandria, Lucanus or Lucianus of Rome, Syneros, Potitus, and Basilicus. These names are preserved chiefly by hostile heresiological writers. Rhodo, whose testimony is quoted by Eusebius, names Apelles, Potitus, Basilicus, and Syneros, and attempts to classify them according to their supposed “principles” or “natures.” Pseudo-Tertullian separately names Lucanus as a follower of Marcion. These reports should not be read as proof that Marcion’s successors abandoned the Marcionite confession, which always upheld the belief in the one unbegotten god, God the Father. Rather, their disputes concerned how to describe the lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions beneath Him, especially the being called in the Apostolicon ‘god of this world.’

questions.question: Who were the notable Marcionite Christian martyrs?
questions.answer:
Eusebius of Caesarea records that among the various early Christian sects, the Marcionite Christians produced the greatest number of martyrs. His testimony highlights the widespread devotion and steadfastness of the Marcionite faithful in the face of imperial persecution.

He names two martyrs in particular. The first is Metrodorus, Bishop of Smyrna, who was burned alive alongside Polycarp in 156 C.E., during the wave of Christian persecutions under Emperor Antoninus Pius. Metrodorus is remembered for his unwavering commitment to the Gospel preserved in the original Testamentum.

The second is Asclepius, Bishop of Eleutheropolis, who suffered martyrdom during the Diocletianic Persecution. He was burned alive at Caesarea on January 10, 310 C.E., sharing the pyre with Peter Apselamus, another Christian martyr.

Eusebius also makes mention of an unnamed Marcionite woman—possibly a deaconess—who was executed in the arena of Caesarea around 257 C.E., during the reign of Emperor Valerian. Though her name is lost to history, her witness remains a testament to the courage and endurance of the early Marcionite Church.

questions.question: Who was Apelles of Alexandria?
questions.answer:
Apelles of Alexandria began his ministry under Marcion’s guidance in Rome and later continued his theological work in Alexandria. Apelles remained active well into the reign of Emperor Commodus, which lasted from 180 to 193 C.E.

Apelles stands out as the most prominent and influential of Marcion’s disciples, and should be regarded as the true theological successor of Marcion. The Apellean school was not a corruption of Marcionism, nor a departure from Marcion’s original doctrine, but a direct continuation of Marcion’s own teaching. Despite the accusations of the heresiologists, Apelles preserved the original Marcionite confession: God the Father, revealed by Jesus Christ and proclaimed by the Apostle Paul. Rhodo identifies Apelles as a Marcionite teacher who confessed one highest God, though Rhodo reports this through the language of hostile controversy. Pseudo-Tertullian likewise calls Apelles “a disciple of Marcion” and says that he taught one God in the infinite upper regions, together with subordinate powers and angels beneath Him.

Apelles developed his own school of thought while maintaining fidelity to the foundational principles of Marcionite Christianity. The Apelleans did not abandon Marcion’s faith or introduce a foreign dualism. Nor did Apelles accept the false accusation that Marcionite Christianity denied the real manifestation of Christ. Against the heresiological charge of docetism, Apelles affirmed that Christ truly appeared and was truly manifested, while still refusing to identify the Savior with the material order governed by the lower powers. He taught, as Marcion did, that there is one supreme God, God the Father. The Apellean distinction concerned the identity and role of the lower power called in the Apostolicon ‘god of this world.’ The Apelleans understood this lower power as a begotten, subordinate being, identified with Satan, not as a second co-eternal, co-equal god.

Apelles was also the first to advance a distinctly Marcionite theory of the making of the physical world. He attributed the making of the physical world to a lower angelic power, a form of deferred or indirect demiurgy that preserved the transcendence of God the Father while explaining the world’s mixture of order and disorder. Pseudo-Tertullian says Apelles taught that the supreme God made many powers and angels, while another angelic power, called Lord, fashioned the world in imitation of a superior world. This lower fashioner of the physical world was not evil in the absolute sense. Like Marcion, Apelles understood the lower power as just, limited, and inferior, not as equal to God the Father.

This point is crucial. Traditional Apelleans did not view the physical world as evil. They viewed it as inferior, mixed, limited, and governed by a lower just power. The fashioner of the physical world could impose law, judgment, and order, but he could not reveal the alien mercy of God the Father. Thus the Apellean position remained Marcionite: one highest God, one saving Christ, one Gospel, and a lower fashioner of the physical world whose justice is surpassed by the grace of the Father.

Apelles was also known for his Syllogisms, a work of argumentative proofs directed against the Hebrew Bible. Pseudo-Tertullian says Apelles wrote books called Syllogisms in which he sought to prove that what Moses wrote about God was false. These were not mere polemics, but a structured program of critique that pressed opponents on consistency, causality, and divine character, aiming to clear space for the singular Gospel proclaimed by Christ and Paul.

From Alexandria, Apelles engaged currents of philosophical exegesis and employed rigorous logical method. His Syllogisms marshaled contradictions and moral difficulties in the Hebrew Bible to argue that it could not be the pure revelation of God the Father as proclaimed by Jesus Christ and the Apostle Paul. Apelles’ Alexandrian associations also suggest contact with schools that prized textual criticism and philosophical theology, which helped shape his distinctive combination of biblical argument, moral reasoning, and spare Christ-centered proclamation.

Later reports connect Apelles with the prophetess Philumena, but even there his emphasis remained on a simple rule of faith oriented to God the Father and Jesus Christ. He also expanded the canon to include the Epistle to the Alexandrians and the Pastoral Epistles, I and II Timothy and Titus. Moreover, Apelles did not teach works-based justification, but rather treated good works as the necessary evidence and fruit of genuine faith. Rhodo reports that Apelles taught that those who trusted in the Crucified would be saved, and that such faith would be shown by their continuance in good works.

questions.question: Did Apelles preserve Marcion’s doctrine?
questions.answer:
Apelles taught essentially as Marcion did.

Apelles lived long enough to witness the first heresiological treatises and attacks that proliferated after Marcion’s death, and he directly repudiated the two accusations that became the standard calumnies against Marcionite Christians: docetism and dualism. We know this most clearly from his conversation with Rhodo, where Apelles does not “revise” Marcion so much as rebut hostile caricatures of him. And he likely did the same in his own major work, the Syllogisms, a kind of sequel to Marcion’s Antitheses, extending the same antithetical critique by systematic argument.

Only later, after Apelles’ death, did heresiologists increasingly feel compelled to frame him as a “heretic of Marcion.” This was not because Apelles had actually founded a new theology, but because their broader narrative required it: if Apelles remained plainly Marcionite while publicly refuting their stock accusations, then the web of polemical claims they had spun about Marcion would begin to unravel. Recasting Apelles as a deviant offshoot preserved their storyline.

The false heresiological claim that Apelles broke with Marcion because he lusted after a woman belongs to the same genre as the equally false claim that Marcion’s own father expelled him for seducing a virgin. Both stories follow a familiar polemical pattern. When heresiologists wished to discredit theological opponents, they often supplied a sexual scandal to explain what was, in reality, a doctrinal conflict. These tales should be read not as sober biography, but as fabricated motifs designed to castigate dissenting teachers and make their theology appear to arise from moral corruption rather than from scriptural conviction.

The more straightforward historical reality is that Apelles is the first clearly “historic” Marcionite figure for whom we have sustained notice, and there are no reliable reports demonstrating that he materially departed from Marcion’s theology or from the Marcionite canon. Where later writers insist on differences, their reports are best read as retrospective damage control, not neutral description.

questions.question: Did Apelles have any disciples?
questions.answer:
Early Christian writers attest that Apelles of Alexandria had at least one prominent later follower, Severus. Epiphanius of Salamis (Panarion 45.1.3–4), Augustine (De Haeresibus 55), and Theodoret of Cyrrhus (Compendium of Heretical Fables 1.25) name Severus as a successor to Apelles and as the leader of the Severians.

Severus should be understood as representing a later development within the broader Apellean stream of Marcionite Christianity. Like Apelles, the Severians preserved the simpler Apellean structure: God the Father, above all; and beneath him the lower power called in the Apostolicon “the god of this world” and called in the Evangelicon “the prince of this world.” Like the traditional Apelleans, the Severians identified this lower maker of the physical world with Satan, while still refusing to make him a second co-eternal, co-equal god.

The key difference between the traditional Apelleans and the Severians was moral and ascetic. Traditional Apelleans, preserving the original teaching of Marcion and Apelles, understood the lower maker of the physical world as just, limited, and inferior, but not evil in the absolute sense. The Severians sharpened this critique. They treated the lower maker of the physical world not merely as just and inferior, but as evil. This led them toward a more ascetic and world-denying approach to life, and to a stronger rejection of much of the material world. Even so, the Severians remained within a Marcionite cosmological hierarchy, not a system of dualism, because they did not make Satan equal to God the Father.

The sources also report that Severus and his followers repudiated the Hebrew Bible and its prophetic books. Epiphanius says they “revile the Law and the Prophets” (Panarion 45.1.4), and Augustine notes that they “receive neither the Law nor the Prophets” (De Haeresibus 55). This rejection of the Hebrew Bible’s authority ties Severus directly back to Marcion and Apelles, both of whom distinguished the Gospel of Christ from the law, prophets, and scriptures of the lower maker of the physical world. The Severians therefore did not represent a foreign religion, but a stricter ascetic development within the Apellean branch of Marcionite Christianity.

In addition, the sources attest that Philomena the Prophetess was closely associated with Apelles. She originally served as a Marcionite deaconess and, according to Jerome, went to Rome ahead of Marcion to herald his arrival (Epistle 133.4). Apelles confirmed her prophetic gifts, and her revelations were recorded in his now-lost Manifestations (Phanerōseis), which preserved her prophecies and visions.

Philomena’s role is important because it shows that the Apellean school was not merely philosophical or argumentative. It included prophecy, ecclesial ministry, and charismatic authority within the Marcionite Church. Apelles’ association with Philomena did not represent a departure from Marcion’s original teaching, but belonged to the living continuation of Marcionite Christianity: the Gospel, the Apostle, the original Testamentum, and the witness of prophetic revelation ordered beneath God the Father.

According to Marcionite tradition, Philomena was martyred in Rome, and her tomb is identified in the Catacombs of Priscilla, where her remains were discovered on May 24, 1802. Three tiles sealing the tomb read Pax tecum Filumena — “Peace be unto you, Philomena” — from which her name, Filumena, Greek philouménē, meaning “beloved,” is rendered in English as Philomena. The two anchors, three arrows, the palm, and the ivy leaf on the tiles were interpreted as symbols of her martyrdom.

We maintain that, after miracles were associated with the tomb and remains, the Roman Catholic Church attributed to her a constructed history unrelated to her true identity. Later, on February 14, 1961, the Roman Catholic Church ordered her name removed from all liturgical calendars, most likely when it was recognized that she was the Marcionite Philomena.

Writers who mention Apelles’ prophetess include Tertullian (De Carne Christi 6; cf. Against Marcion 4.17), Hippolytus (Refutation of All Heresies 7.26), and the Latin Adversus Omnes Haereses 6. Later notices occur in Epiphanius (Panarion 44–45) and Theodoret (Compendium 1.25). These testimonies preserve the memory of Philomena as a prophetic figure within the Apellean continuation of Marcionite Christianity, and of Severus as the founder of a later Severian development within that same broader stream.

questions.question: Who was Lucanus of Rome?
questions.answer:
Lucanus, also called Lucanus of Rome, appears as another important disciple of Marcion. Pseudo-Tertullian says that after Marcion arose “a certain Lucanus,” whom he calls a follower and disciple of Marcion. Lucanus became associated with a later Marcionite school that distinguished more sharply among the lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions beneath God the Father.

Lucanus’ teachings included the doctrine of the transmigration of souls, a form of reincarnation, and the immateriality and immortality of the soul. More importantly for the history of Marcionite schools, the Lucanist tendency distinguished the ‘god of this world’ from Satan, treating the former as just and directly responsible for the making of the physical world, and the latter as evil.

questions.question: What were the principal Marcionite schools?
questions.answer:
The principal Marcionite schools were the Apellean, Lucanist, and Severian schools. These were internal tendencies within the broader Marcionite Church rather than independent religions or formal schisms. They remained united by the Evangelicon, the Apostolicon, the apostolic primacy of Paul, and the confession of God the Father revealed through Jesus Christ.

Apelles of Alexandria founded the Apellean school, with Potitus and Basilicus associated with that stream. Lucanus of Rome founded the Lucanist school, to which Syneros belonged. Severus gave his name to the later Severian school. Their theological distinctions are treated separately below.

See also: Question 6.17, “How were the Marcionite schools theologically distinguished?”

questions.question: What was the Apellean School?
questions.answer:
The Apellean school, founded by Apelles of Alexandria, was the direct continuation of Marcion’s original teaching. Apelles did not create a new religion, nor did he replace Marcion’s doctrine with a foreign system. He preserved the original Marcionite confession of God the Father, revealed by Jesus Christ and proclaimed by Paul. The later heresiologists tried to represent Apelles as a deviation from Marcion, but this was polemical distortion. In substance, Apelles taught exactly as Marcion did: there is one supreme God, and beneath him are lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions. Apelles also pushed back against the false accusations of docetism and crude dualism leveled against the Marcionites, affirming the real manifestation of Christ and clarifying that the lower maker of the physical world was a subordinate power rather than a second co-eternal, co-equal god.

The Apelleans understood the lower power called in the Apostolicon “the god of this world” and called in the Evangelicon “the prince of this world” as a subordinate, begotten power. This lower power was identified with Satan, but he was not a second co-eternal, co-equal god. He was one of the lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions beneath God the Father.

The Apelleans believed that this lower power played a role in the making of the physical world. Pseudo-Tertullian reports that Apelles taught one supreme God in the infinite upper regions, who made many powers and angels, and that another angelic power originated the world in imitation of a superior world. This is why Apelles may be described as teaching a deferred or indirect demiurgy. The making of the physical world is mediated through a lower power, while creation still ultimately flows from God the Father.

This is not dualism. The Apelleans did not posit two eternal gods. They did not teach that Satan was equal to God. They taught a cosmological hierarchy: God the Father, above all; Christ sent by him; and beneath them the lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions associated with the governance and making of the physical world.

Traditional Apelleans did not regard the physical world as evil. They regarded it as inferior, mixed, limited, and governed by a lower just power. Nor did they regard the maker of the physical world as evil in the absolute sense. Like Marcion, they regarded this lower power as just, limited, and inferior. The maker of the physical world could impose law, judgment, and order, but he could not reveal the alien mercy of God the Father. His justice was surpassed by the grace of the Father revealed in Jesus Christ.

Within this Apellean stream belong Apelles, Potitus, Basilicus, Marcus, Philomena the Prophetess, and Severus. Potitus and Basilicus are named by Rhodo among Marcionite teachers whom hostile writers described in terms of a “two-principle” view. Within a Marcionite reading, this should not be understood as belief in two co-eternal, co-equal gods. Rather, they are best treated as Apellean because their framework preserves the simpler distinction between God the Father, and the lower powers associated with the making and governance of the physical world. Marcus, in the Dialogue of Adamantius, likewise belongs in the Apellean stream because he preserves the simpler Marcionite structure that identifies the lower maker of the physical world with the hostile power called in the Apostolicon “the god of this world” and called in the Evangelicon “the prince of this world.” Both Philomena the Prophetess and Severus were direct disciples of Apelles, though Severus would ultimately found his own school, distinct from the Apellean stream.

questions.question: What was the Lucanist School?
questions.answer:
The Lucanist school, founded by Lucanus or Lucianus of Rome, developed Marcionite cosmology in a different direction. Pseudo-Tertullian explicitly calls Lucanus a follower and disciple of Marcion. The Lucanists still confessed God the Father. They did not teach that Satan was equal to God, nor did they teach two co-eternal gods. Rather, they distinguished between the “god of this world” and Satan.

For the Lucanists, the “god of this world” was the just lower power directly responsible for the making of the physical world. Satan, by contrast, was an evil third entity. This produced a threefold cosmological hierarchy: God the Father, above all; the “god of this world” as the just maker of the physical world beneath him; and Satan as a hostile and evil lower power beneath or against that order.

Traditional Lucanists, like traditional Apelleans, did not view the physical world as evil in itself. They viewed it as inferior, limited, and governed by lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions. The Lucanists differed from the Apelleans not because they were more dualistic, but because they distinguished the just “god of this world,” who made the physical world, from Satan, who was evil. The world was therefore not the product of an evil co-eternal god, but of a lower just power beneath God the Father.

This also is not dualism. It is not a doctrine of two equal gods. It is a hierarchy of lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions beneath God the Father. Even when the making of the physical world is mediated through the “god of this world,” and even when Satan is distinguished as an evil power, all being still ultimately depends upon the one true God.

Within this Lucanist stream belong Lucanus, Syneros, Megethius, and Prepon the Assyrian. Syneros is associated by Rhodo with “three natures,” a hostile formulation that fits the Lucanist tendency to distinguish the just “god of this world” from Satan as another lower power. Megethius, in the Dialogue of Adamantius, likewise belongs in the Lucanist stream because he preserves a threefold cosmological scheme, distinguishing the good, the just, and the evil. Finally, Prepon belongs naturally in the Lucanist camp because Hippolytus says he introduced a doctrine in which “what is just” constitutes a third principle, placed between what is good and what is bad. Within Marcionite theology, none of this should not be treated as three co-eternal gods, but as an attempt to describe the order of lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions beneath God the Father.

questions.question: What was the Severian School?
questions.answer:
The Severian School was a later and more radical ascetic movement associated with Severus and his followers, the Severians. Although heresiologists often used this school as a stand-in for “Marcionism” writ large, this identification is misleading. The Severians were relentlessly mocked and attacked by hostile writers for their extreme asceticism, rigid discipline, hatred of the world, and severe rejection of ordinary material life. Because of this, many ancient accusations against “Marcionites” in general are likely exaggerated through a Severian lens.

The Severian School was not founded by a direct disciple of Marcion. For this reason, its distinctive views are not accepted as normative by the modern Marcionite Church. This differs from the Apellean and Lucanist streams, which are recognized as legitimate Marcionite positions because Apelles and Lucanus were both disciples of Marcion and therefore stand within the immediate Marcionite succession.

Theologically, Severus represents a later development from within the broader Apellean stream. His followers still identified Satan with the being called in the Apostolicon “the god of this world” and called in the Evangelicon “the prince of this world.” They therefore preserved the simpler Apellean structure rather than the threefold Lucanist structure. However, the Severians departed from traditional Apelleans by treating this lower world-fashioning power not merely as just, limited, and inferior, but as evil. This led them toward a far more severe rejection of the physical world.

This is the key distinction between traditional Apelleans and Severians. Traditional Apelleans, like Marcion, viewed the lower ruler of the physical world as just and inferior, not evil. Severians, while preserving the Apellean identification of Satan with the “god of this world,” sharpened the critique and treated the lower world-fashioning power as evil. As a result, they adopted a more world-denying, ascetic, and rigid posture.

Thus, the Severian School should be understood as a later, extreme, and non-normative branch associated with the Marcionite world, not as the standard form of Marcionite Christianity. Its testimony is historically useful, especially for understanding how hostile writers caricatured Marcionites, but its distinctive theology is not received as authoritative by the Marcionite Church.

questions.question: How were the Marcionite schools theologically distinguished?
questions.answer:
The main division within Marcionism was not between monotheism and dualism. All schools confessed God the Father. The difference concerned the order of the lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions beneath him. These differences did not result in formal schisms from the Marcionite Church. They reflected distinct schools of interpretation within one ecclesial body, united by the same Gospel, the same Apostle, and the same confession of God the Father.

The traditional Apelleans held a two-fold structure:

God the Father, above all; the lower “god of this world”, identified with Satan, beneath him; this lower power being just, limited, and inferior, but not a second co-eternal, co-equal god.

The Severians held a stricter ascetic version:

God the Father, above all; the lower “god of this world”, identified with Satan, beneath him; this lower power being evil, leading to a more ascetic rejection of much of the material world.

The Lucanists held a threefold structure:

God the Father, above all; the just “god of this world,” maker of the physical world, beneath him; and Satan as an evil third power.

Neither the traditional Apellean nor the Lucanist position was dualist in the strict sense. Neither taught a second co-eternal, co-equal god. Both preserved a Marcionite cosmological hierarchy, and both viewed the physical world as inferior and limited rather than evil in itself. The Severians moved in a more ascetic and world-denying direction by identifying the maker of the physical world with evil, but even there they did not make him equal to God the Father. These differences did not result in formal schisms from the Marcionite Church, but remained internal schools within the broader Marcionite tradition. All these schools sought to explain the lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions beneath the sovereignty of God the Father, while remaining united by the singular Pauline Gospel.

questions.question: Who were the first Marcionite bishops, presbyters, and deacons?
questions.answer:
Marcion of Sinope, in his preserved epistles contained within the Synaxicon, names a wide circle of Marcionite deacons, presbyters, and bishops who accompanied or supported him during his captivity. Together, these figures reveal both the breadth of his influence across second-century Christian communities and the well-defined leadership structure already in place within the Marcionite Church.

Deacons:

Philo of Cilicia – Deacon of Tarsus, mentioned in the letters to the Smyrnaeans and Philadelphians; he travelled with Marcion toward Rome, highlighting Cilicia’s strong Marcionite presence.

Rhaius Agathopus – A Syrian deacon who joined Marcion at Troas and remained with him throughout the journey.

Burrhus of Ephesus – Deacon delegated by the Ephesian assembly; he met Marcion early on the route and carried messages on their behalf.

Crocus of Rome – Roman deacon who hosted and ministered to Marcion after his arrival in the capital.

Zotion of Magnesia – Deacon of the Magnesian church who assisted Bishop Damas in greeting the prisoner on his way to Smyrna.

Presbyters:

Apollonius of Magnesia – One of two presbyters sent by the Magnesian congregation to visit Marcion, offering encouragement and logistical aid.

Bassus of Magnesia – Fellow presbyter of the same church, partnering with Apollonius and Damas to ensure Marcion’s letters reached their destinations.

Bishops:

Damas of Magnesia – Encountered Marcion while Roman soldiers escorted the latter toward Smyrna; Marcion entrusted him with a letter addressed to the Magnesian flock.

Metrodorus of Smyrna – Bishop of Smyrna who received and hosted Marcion during his stop in the city, convened the local assembly, forwarded Marcion’s letters, safeguarded his personal effects, and acted as liaison with neighbouring churches. His steadfast leadership made Smyrna a rallying point for Marcionite believers, and he was burned alive in 156 C.E. during persecutions carried out under Emperor Marcus Aurelius, sealing his witness in martyrdom.

Polybius of Tralles – Described in Marcion’s Epistle to the Trallians as a man of solemn bearing and gentle spirit, he travelled to Smyrna to stand in solidarity with Marcion during the final stages of the journey.

These bishops, presbyters, and deacons form a vivid portrait of committed Marcionite leadership—men who stood beside their founder in faith and service and whose cooperation attests to the vigor and organization of the Church he established.

questions.question: Who are some other notable Marcionite Christians?
questions.answer:
Among other notable Marcionite Christians preserved in the historical record are Prepon the Assyrian, Paul of Lebaba, Pitho, Megethius, Marcus, Theodotion, Ambrose of Alexandria, and Blastus of Rome. These figures show the continued development, spread, and influence of Marcionite Christianity after Marcion himself. Some belonged to the major theological schools within Marcionism, while others bear witness to the geographic and ecclesial reach of the Marcionite Church.

Prepon the Assyrian belongs naturally within the later Lucanist stream of Marcionite theology. He is remembered for composing an epistle, now lost, to Bardaisan or to Bardaisan’s followers, in which he defended the theological principles of the Marcionite tradition. According to Hippolytus, in this lost epistle, Prepon articulated the threefold distinction between what is good, what is just, and what is evil.

Paul of Lebaba, a Marcionite presbyter, is renowned for establishing a church in the village of Lebaba in Syria in 318 C.E. The inscription found within this church remains the oldest known surviving inscribed reference to Jesus Christ. It reads:

“The meeting-house of the Marcionites, in the village of Lebaba, of the Lord and Saviour Jesus the Good — Erected by the forethought of Paul a presbyter, in the year 630 [Seleucid era].”

This archaeological discovery affirms the enduring presence of Marcionite Christianity in Syria and serves as a powerful witness to their Christological devotion. It also shows that Marcionite communities were not merely speculative schools or literary circles, but organized ecclesial bodies with presbyters, meeting-houses, and public inscriptions.

Megethius and Marcus, both Marcionite presbyters, are cited in De Recta in Deum Fide, also known as the Dialogue of Adamantius, a late-third or early-fourth-century dialogue opposing various theological views. Their inclusion demonstrates the continued activity and influence of Marcionite leaders in theological discourse well into the post-Marcion era.

Within the framework of the two major Marcionite schools, Marcus should be identified with the Apellean stream, while Megethius should be identified with the Lucanist stream.

Pitho also belongs among the later Marcionite figures preserved in the historical record. Although little survives concerning his individual teaching, he appears within the broader stream of Marcionite leadership and succession. His importance lies chiefly in showing the continuity of Marcionite ecclesial life beyond the first generation of disciples.

Theodotion, the translator of the Hebrew Bible into Greek, is recorded by Epiphanius of Salamis as having once been affiliated with the Marcionite Church before abandoning Christianity entirely and embracing Judaism. Whether Epiphanius’ report is complete or polemically shaped, it nevertheless shows that heresiological writers remembered Marcionite Christianity as a movement with influence beyond its own congregations and with contact even among figures later associated with Jewish textual traditions.

Similarly, Jerome notes that Ambrose of Alexandria had been a Marcionite Christian prior to his conversion under the influence of Origen. This testimony is significant because it places Marcionite Christianity within the intellectual and theological world of Alexandria, where disputes over scripture, canon, allegory, and the character of God were especially active. Because Apelles himself was associated with Alexandria, Ambrose’s Alexandrian connection makes him a likely former Apellean before becoming an Origenist, though the surviving sources do not explicitly label him as such.

Blastus is also remembered in later sources as having originally been associated with Marcionite Christianity in Rome before aligning with other Christian movements. Theodoret of Cyrrhus identifies him as a figure later connected with Montanism and Quartodeciman practice. His case shows that Marcionite Christianity stood within the larger network of second- and third-century Christian disputes over prophecy, discipline, ecclesial authority, and the proper observance of the Christian calendar.

Taken together, these figures reveal the wide reach of Marcionite Christianity across Syria, Rome, Alexandria, and the wider Greek-speaking world. They also show that Marcionism did not dissolve into unrelated sects. Its internal differences, including the Apellean and Lucanist schools, did not result in formal schisms from the Marcionite Church. Rather, they represented distinct schools of interpretation within one broader ecclesial body.

questions.question: Which later Christian groups descended from or were influenced by the Marcionites?
questions.answer:
Some scholars, including historian Joseph Turmel, have noted striking theological parallels between the Marcionite Christians and the early Johannine community responsible for the Gospel of John and the Johannine Epistles. According to certain traditions, Marcion of Sinope began his ministry in Anatolia as a disciple of the Apostle John, suggesting a potential lineage of influence between these early Christian streams.

Numerous later Christian movements appear to have inherited theological elements from the Marcionite tradition. If the Cerdonians can be described as proto-Marcionites, then the Paulicians can best be described as neo-Marcionites: not identical to the original Marcionite Church, but a later Pauline revival that received, adapted, and reworked major elements of the Marcionite inheritance.

Among these later movements, the Paulicians stand out as the most prominent successors. Confined primarily to the territories of the Eastern Roman Empire, the Paulicians first flourished in Armenia, where they preserved a strongly Pauline and Marcionite foundation. Over time, however, this inheritance began to diverge as the Paulicians encountered and absorbed Manichaean and other dualist influences. These influences gradually pulled portions of the movement farther away from their original Marcionite and Pauline roots and contributed to the development of a more syncretic theology.

Like the Marcionites, the Paulicians held the Apostle Paul in the highest regard, considering him the foremost interpreter of the Gospel. The founder of the Paulician movement, Constantine the Armenian, hailed from a Marcionite church in Mananalis, near Samosata. Around 657 C.E., he began to teach a new religious message based on the Marcionite canon. According to the account of Petrus Siculus, a Byzantine chronicler who lived among the Paulicians in Tibrike, Constantine received the Evangelicon and Apostolicon from a Marcionite deacon in Syria and distributed them among his followers, who initially preserved these texts as their authoritative scriptures.

In their earliest form, then, the Paulicians appear to have stood very near to the Marcionite tradition. Their foundation was Gospel and Apostle, not the Hebrew Bible. Their religious identity was Pauline, anti-Judaizing, and hostile to the later catholic expansion of the canon. But as the movement developed, especially in Armenia and the eastern borderlands of the empire, Manichaean dualism increasingly shaped Paulician doctrine and practice. This later Manichaean overlay explains why the Paulicians eventually departed from Marcionism proper while still retaining unmistakable Marcionite elements.

Over time, the Paulicians appear to have expanded and modified Marcion’s original Testamentum. They continued to use only the Gospel of Luke and retained most of Paul’s interpolated epistles, while rejecting the Epistles of Peter. Like the Marcionites, they entirely rejected the Hebrew Bible, rejected the resurrection of the body, embraced amillennialism, and refused to venerate Mary.

Despite their shared Pauline foundation, the Paulicians eventually diverged significantly from Marcionite theology. They came to embrace dualism, docetism, iconoclasm, and a non-Trinitarian understanding of God, positions that brought them closer to later dualist and gnosticizing traditions than to Marcionism proper. These divergences should be understood not as the essence of their original Marcionite inheritance, but as later developments caused in large part by the merging of Paulician Christianity with Manichaean influences.

Between 747 and 970 C.E., the Byzantine emperors deported large numbers of Paulicians to Thrace, in modern-day Bulgaria, as part of imperial resettlement and religious control efforts. These transplanted Paulician communities gradually evolved into a new movement: Bogomilism, which flourished throughout Bulgaria.

Bogomilism, in turn, gave rise to Catharism, a dualist Christian movement that spread through southern France between the twelfth and fourteenth centuries. As political and religious pressures intensified in the region, many of the remaining Paulicians and Bogomils eventually converted to Roman Catholicism, Eastern Orthodoxy, or Islam during the period of Ottoman conquest. Ethnic groups such as the Banat Bulgarians and the Pomaks trace their ancestry to these historical communities.

Those Paulicians who remained in Armenia and were not deported gradually gave rise to the Tondrakian movement by the tenth century, a Christian movement that persisted in Armenia until as late as the 1820s.

If one accepts the theory of baptismal successionism, the belief that valid Christian faith communities can be traced through their rejection of infant baptism, then a theological lineage may be drawn from the Marcionites through the Paulicians, Bogomils, and Cathars, ultimately culminating in the emergence of modern Baptist traditions. Each of these historical movements consistently upheld the doctrine of credobaptism, baptism upon profession of personal faith.

questions.question: What is the Chronicon?
questions.answer:
The Chronicon is the historical record of the Marcionite Church, tracing its foundations in Jesus Christ and the Apostle Paul, the life and ministry of Marcion of Sinope, and the development, persecution, and endurance of Marcionite communities throughout the ancient world.

Its name is drawn from the Greek chronikon, meaning a chronicle or ordered account of events. The Chronicon arranges the history of the Church in chronological form, preserving dates, persons, ministries, councils, persecutions, schools, migrations, and surviving notices from the descent of Christ and the apostolate of Paul through the last explicit notice of living Marcionite communities in 987 C.E., followed by later medieval witnesses preserving the historical memory of Marcionism through 1286 C.E.

The Chronicon is an ecclesiastical historical work rather than canon or deuterocanon. It preserves the memory and continuity of the Church, distinguishes received Marcionite tradition from hostile heresiological claims, and orders the surviving evidence into a coherent account. Like every subordinate ecclesiastical work, it is received only insofar as it accords with the Gospel revealed by Christ and preached by the Apostle Paul.

questions.question: What is theology?
questions.answer:
Theology is the ordered confession and explanation of the revelation of God the Father through Jesus Christ. It begins with the Gospel, is governed by the canonical scriptures of the Testamentum and the Rule of Faith, and uses reason, tradition, and historical evidence in subordination to that revelation.

“O the depth of the riches both of the wisdom and knowledge of God! how unsearchable are his judgments, and his ways past finding out!”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 9:1

Marcionite theology therefore seeks neither speculation detached from the Gospel nor submission to later councils as independent authorities. Its purpose is to understand, defend, and faithfully proclaim what Christ revealed and Paul preached.

questions.question: How does the Church understand Holy Wisdom?
questions.answer:
Holy Wisdom is the divine Wisdom of God manifested through Jesus Christ and communicated to the faithful by the Holy Spirit. Holy Wisdom is not a separate goddess, a fallen Aeon, or an independent divine being alongside God the Father.

The Apostle expressly identifies Christ as “the power of God, and the wisdom of God,” and declares that Christ is made unto us wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption. The Holy Spirit likewise grants the word of wisdom, reveals spiritual truth, and enables the faithful to discern the things of God. The Church may therefore speak of Holy Wisdom both christologically, because Christ is the Wisdom of God manifested, and pneumatologically, because the Holy Spirit communicates that Wisdom within the Church.

“But unto them which are called, both Jews and Greeks, Christ the power of God, and the wisdom of God.”

I Corinthians 1:24

“But of him are ye in Christ Jesus, who of God is made unto us wisdom, and righteousness, and sanctification, and redemption.”

I Corinthians 1:30

“For to one is given by the Spirit the word of wisdom.”

I Corinthians 12:8

The Church rejects the Valentinian myth that Sophia fell from the divine fullness and produced the lower world through error. Divine Wisdom does not fall into ignorance. Christ descends to overcome ignorance, and the Holy Spirit leads the faithful into the Wisdom revealed through Him.

questions.question: How does the Church distinguish revelation, scripture, tradition, and reason?
questions.answer:
Revelation is the self-disclosure of God the Father through Jesus Christ. Scripture is the authoritative written witness to that revelation in the Evangelicon and Apostolicon. Tradition is the Church’s received transmission of doctrine, worship, and discipline, while reason is the faculty by which the faithful examine, order, and defend what has been received.

These are not equal authorities. Revelation is fulfilled in Christ; scripture bears supreme written witness to it; tradition serves its preservation; and reason serves its understanding. None may be used to contradict the Gospel.

questions.question: What are the Antitheses?
questions.answer:
According to Marcion of Sinope, God the Father—who is revealed through Jesus Christ—had no prior interaction with the world before Christ’s manifestation. He was entirely unknown until that moment, transcendent and untainted by the material realm. In his now-lost work the Antitheses, Marcion presented a series of stark contrasts between the Hebrew Bible and the Gospel, between the god of the false covenant and the true God revealed in the Evangelicon and Apostolicon, between law and grace, judgment and mercy. He depicted Christianity not as a continuation of the Hebrew tradition but as a completely new and independent revelation, one that abrogated and superseded all that came before it.

In our chronology, we date the composition of the Antitheses to 139 C.E., aligning it with the Chronicle of Edessa’s notice (Seleucid year 449) that Marcion “forsook the emerging Catholic Church.” That Edessan datum functions as the best external chronological peg for the moment when Marcion’s Gospel-versus-Law program is remembered as taking a written, public form.

Tertullian, one of Marcion’s fiercest critics, preserved a single quote from the Antitheses that illustrates Marcion’s approach:

“Whereas David in old time, in the capture of Sion, was offended by the blind who opposed his admission into the stronghold, so, on the contrary, Christ succored the blind man, to show by this act that He was not David’s son, and how different in disposition He was—kind to the blind, while David ordered them to be slain.”

Marcion’s critique was shaped by Hellenistic philosophical principles, especially Platonism, and he employed moral reasoning to contrast the inconsistency and cruelty of the Hebrew Bible deity with the compassion and righteousness of the God proclaimed by Christ. Marcion argued that the original Testamentum and the Hebrew Bible are irreconcilable. Where Moses taught “an eye for an eye,” Jesus nullified this code of retaliation. Marcion highlighted Isaiah’s disturbing claim—“I make peace and create evil; I, the Lord, do all these things”—and contrasted it with Jesus’ statement, “A good tree cannot bring forth evil fruit.” Marcion emphasized the incompatibility between the fruits of the two trees: one bitter, one sweet.

He cited examples of moral divergence: in the Hebrew Bible, the prophet Elisha curses children, and bears maul them. Jesus, by contrast, says, “Let the little children come unto me.” Joshua halts the sun to prolong the massacre of his enemies, while Paul, quoting Christ, teaches, “Let not the sun go down upon your wrath.” The Hebrew Bible allows for divorce and polygamy; the canonical Evangelicon and Apostolicon forbid both. Moses imposed strict Sabbath regulations and ceremonial law; Jesus deconstructs and transcends both.

Marcion also pointed to internal contradictions within the Hebrew Bible itself. The deity commands that no work be done on the Sabbath, yet instructs the Israelites to march around Jericho seven times on the Sabbath. Though he forbids graven images, he directs Moses to create a bronze serpent. Marcion questioned how an omniscient deity could ask, “Adam, where art thou?”—a clear sign, in Marcion’s view, of ignorance.

Even more strikingly, in Genesis, Jacob is said to have wrestled with the deity of the Hebrew Bible and prevailed—a blasphemous notion if applied to the true God. Likewise, before destroying Sodom and Gomorrah, the deity says, “I will go down now, and see whether they have done altogether according to the cry of it… and if not, I will know.” Marcion saw this as further proof that the god of the Hebrew Bible was neither omnipresent nor omniscient, but a limited, fallible being unworthy of worship.

Through these comparisons, Marcion advanced the case for a radical theological reformation: the rejection of the false covenant and its god, and the full embrace of the true covenant revealed by Jesus Christ through the Gospel and the writings of Paul.

questions.question: What are the Syllogisms of Apelles?
questions.answer:
Apelles, a notable disciple of Marcion, authored a substantial multi-volume work entitled Syllogisms—comprising at least thirty-eight volumes—in which he offered a systematic critique of the Hebrew Bible and its depiction of God (Ambrose explicitly notes Apelles’ arguments “in the thirty-eighth tome” of that work). The title itself suggests that Apelles intended to build upon Marcion’s earlier work, the Antitheses, which had contrasted the false deity of the Hebrew Bible with the true God revealed in the original Testamentum, emphasizing the irreconcilable differences between law and Gospel, justice and mercy, wrath and grace.

Though the original text of Syllogisms has not survived, several fragments have been preserved through the polemics of later Catholic theologians. Ambrose of Milan, in the fourth century, directed a number of rebuttals against Apelles in his treatise De Paradiso. It is through such responses that we possess rare direct quotations from Apelles’ lost work.

In one preserved fragment, Apelles addresses the inconsistency of divine foreknowledge in the Genesis account:

“Did God know that Adam would transgress His commandment, or knew He it not? If He knew it not, how then declareth He Himself to be Almighty? But if He knew, and yet gave commandment concerning that which He foresaw would not be obeyed, then did He speak in vain. But God doeth nought in vain, neither commandeth He that which is superfluous. Yet lo, He gave unto Adam, the first-formed man, a statute which He knew should not be observed. Wherefore, this writing proceedeth not from God, for the Most High doth nothing without purpose.”

Another excerpt questions the plausibility of the flood narrative and the Ark:

“By no means could it have been possible to bring aboard the Ark so great a multitude of beasts, and the provision for their sustenance for the space of a whole year, in so short a time. For if the unclean beasts are said to have entered two and two, that is, two males and two females of each kind, and the clean beasts seven and seven, that is, seven pairs, how then could the space, as it is written, have contained even four elephants alone? Verily, it is manifest that the tale is feigned. And if the tale be false, then surely this scripture proceedeth not from God.”

In a third passage, Apelles critiques the theological logic of the Eden narrative:

“How is it that the tree of life doth seem to confer more life than the very breath of God?”

On the supposed perfection of Adam:

“If God made man not perfect; yet if every man by his own diligence acquireth the perfection of virtue, doth it not appear that man obtaineth more unto himself than God bestowed upon him?”

On the knowledge of death:

“And if man had not tasted of death, surely he could not know that which he had not tasted.”

These fragments show Apelles continuing the Marcionite tradition of moral and philosophical opposition to the Hebrew Bible, exposing what he perceived to be contradictions, absurdities, and ethical failings in the text. Like Marcion, Apelles maintained that such scriptures could not have proceeded from the Father revealed by Christ, but rather from a lesser, false, and ignorant deity associated with the false covenant. His work represents a further refinement of the Marcionite conviction that Christianity stands in absolute distinction from Judaism—not as its fulfillment, but as its repudiation.

questions.question: How is Marcionite theology related to Hellenistic philosophy?
