questions.answer: Marcion and the early Marcionites engaged with Hellenistic philosophy to sharpen and defend their theological convictions, particularly drawing from Platonism, Stoicism, Cynicism, and to a lesser extent, Epicureanism, always holding revelation above reason. From Platonism, they embraced the idea that the highest reality is spiritual and that God is wholly Good, immutable, benevolent, and free from violence or contradiction; and they also operated comfortably within the wider Greco-Roman “soul-language” that included metempsychosis (the transmigration of souls) as a conceptual tool for describing the soul’s chastening within the material order and its eventual ascent. This helped articulate the radical moral and ontological distinction between the true God revealed by Christ and the morally deficient false god depicted in the Hebrew Bible. In the same orbit belongs the Marcionite recognition of a division of souls, that souls are not all “the same” in their condition or orientation, but can be spoken of as differing in nature and destiny; Origen explicitly notes that those “from the school of Marcion” (alongside Valentinus and Basilides) were associated with the claim that there are “souls of different natures,” precisely as a way of accounting for radically different births and outcomes. Stoic influence is reflected in Marcion’s personal asceticism and moral seriousness, though asceticism was not universally mandated among Marcionite believers. Cynicism, with its contempt for religious hypocrisy and institutional corruption, resonated with the Marcionite critique of both Jewish legalism and the authoritarian tendencies of the emerging proto-Catholic church. Even Epicureanism could contribute by reinforcing the premise that a truly good God cannot be the author of suffering, injustice, or fear. This perspective sharpened their theological rejection of the false god as depicted in the Hebrew Bible. In all, Hellenistic philosophy provided the Marcionites with tools to express their faith in the supreme goodness of the Father and the uniqueness of the Gospel, without ever eclipsing the authority of divine revelation.

questions.question: How does the Church read other Christian theologians?
questions.answer: The Marcionite Church reads other Christian theologians critically and selectively. Marcionite Christians hold in high regard the centuries of thought produced by theologians outside our own tradition. Many ancient Church Fathers and later Christian thinkers ironically shared views with Marcion on a variety of issues, and we can appreciate their arguments and reasoning even as we dispute other points of doctrine. We encourage every Marcionite believer to engage these writings with an open mind and a discerning spirit. In many areas, such as philosophical reasoning, moral instruction, liturgical development, and historical analysis, the insights of non-Marcionite scholars harmonize with our own convictions and can deepen our understanding of the Gospel. When their conclusions align with the testimony of the Evangelicon, the Apostolicon, and the witness of the Apostle Paul, they are worthy of study and reflection. Where their assertions contradict the core principles handed down through Marcion and our received Scriptures, particularly doctrines that elevate the Hebrew Bible’s deity as the Father of Jesus Christ, we respectfully set those portions aside. By reading broadly yet critically, Marcionite Christians gain both the benefits of the wider Christian intellectual heritage and the clarity that comes from remaining faithful to the true message of Christ preserved in the canonical Evangelicon and Apostolicon.

questions.question: Why does the Church reject the ecumenical councils?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church rejects all ecumenical councils and recognizes only the Apostolic Council of Jerusalem held in 48 C.E., the sole council recorded in the Apostolicon and convened under apostolic authority.

This council affirmed that followers of Jesus were not bound to observe the Mosaic Law, including circumcision, Sabbath regulations, or ritual fasting. However, it did retain a limited set of ethical and ritual instructions, now known as the Apostolic Decree. These include abstaining from food offered to idols, from blood and meat from strangled animals, and from idolatry and sexual immorality.

The Marcionite Church holds that this decree, issued by the Apostles, remains the only legitimate and binding conciliar directive in the history of the faith. This same anti-conciliar posture was later preserved by the Paulicians, whom we regard as Neo-Marcionites: the medieval Paulician adjuration formulae explicitly accuse them of rejecting the ecumenical councils, confirming that they did not receive the post-apostolic conciliar system as authoritative.

Notably, the first non-apostolic ancient church councils only began to occur over 10 years after Marcion of Sinope’s excommunication and one year after his martyrdom, the first of these being the Council of Rome in 155 C.E.

The rejection of the Council of Nicaea does not imply denial of the divinity of Jesus Christ. The Church confesses the Son as truly divine, but rejects the council’s attempt to define His divinity by identifying God the Father with Yahweh and by placing an imperially convened assembly above the apostolic canon.

No post-apostolic council may create a new rule of faith, bind the Church to the Hebrew Bible, or revise the Gospel revealed by Christ and preached by Paul. Councils may be studied as historical witnesses, but they possess no authority over the Evangelicon and Apostolicon.

questions.question: How does the Church approach Christian unity?
questions.answer: The Marcionite Church remains open to Christian unity, although its theological convictions differ significantly from the dominant doctrines of most other Christian traditions.

questions.question: How does the Marcionite Church understand dispensationalism?
questions.answer: Marcionite Christians affirm a form of dispensationalism in which God’s actions in history are understood through distinct periods or revelations. In the Marcionite view, the singular and defining dispensation began with the sudden and unexpected descent of Jesus Christ in 29 C.E. Prior to this event, God the Father had never revealed Himself to humanity nor engaged with the world in any way. All divine revelation begins, and is fulfilled, with Christ alone.

questions.question: Who is Jesus Christ?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church confesses that Jesus Christ is one Lord, truly divine and truly embodied, possessing real flesh and a real human mode of life, yet descending from above rather than being generated through ordinary human generation. Later theological vocabulary may be used descriptively to clarify this confession, but it is not received as conciliar authority.

“Which he wrought in Christ, when he raised him from the dead, and set him at his own right hand in the heavenly places, Far above all principality, and power, and might, and dominion, and every name that is named, not only in this world, but also in that which is to come.”

Laodiceans 1:20–21

Marcion of Sinope upheld both the unity of Christ’s person and the reality of His dual mode of existence. His teachings emphasized that the Lord Jesus was not a mere apparition or a being of divided substance, but truly both God and man. As preserved in Marcion’s Epistle to the Ephesians 8:5, Marcion writes:

“There is one only physician, of flesh and of spirit, generate and ingenerate, God in man, true Life in death, Son of Man and Son of God, first passible and then impassible, Jesus Christ our Lord.”

This confession reflects the Marcionite understanding of Christ, not as a mere apparition, nor as a confusion of divine and human realities, but as the one Lord sent by God the Father to redeem souls from the bondage of this world.

questions.question: Does the Church believe in the pre-existence of Christ?
questions.answer:
The Church confesses the pre-existence of Jesus Christ. The Son did not begin when He descended into Capernaum, nor was He generated by this world. He came from above, from God the Father, and His descent was His manifestation in the visible world for the salvation of souls.

The Evangelicon teaches that the Word was with God in the beginning, that the Word was God, and that the Word was made flesh and dwelt among us. It also teaches that none knoweth the Father but the Son, and he to whomsoever the Son wishes to reveal Him. Thus Christ is not a mere prophet who arose from below, but the heavenly Son who reveals the otherwise unknown Father.

“In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.”

Evangelicon 1:1

“And the Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us, and we beheld his glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth.”

Evangelicon 1:14

“Who, being in the form of God, thought it not robbery to be equal with God: But made himself of no reputation, and took upon him the form of a servant, and was made in the likeness of men.”

Philippians 2:6-7

The pre-existence of Christ therefore grounds the Church’s doctrine of descent and kenosis. Christ’s appearing was not His creation, but His voluntary self-emptying and saving entrance into the corruptible world-order.

See also: Question 8.01, “Who is Jesus Christ?”; Question 8.03, “What does it mean that Christ descended?”; Question 8.04, “How does the Church understand kenosis?”

questions.question: What does it mean that Christ descended?
questions.answer:
Christ’s descent means that the Son came from the heavenly and invisible order into the physical, fleshly, and visible world without being generated by that world. His appearing was a true entrance into human history, not merely a change in human awareness or an allegorical manifestation.

The descent reveals both His heavenly origin and His saving mission. He came from God the Father to make the Father known, overthrow the bondage of the lower powers, and deliver souls through His teaching, suffering, death, and resurrection.

questions.question: How does the Church understand kenosis?
questions.answer:
Kenosis is the voluntary self-emptying or humiliation of Jesus Christ. It does not mean that the Son ceased to be divine, surrendered His Sonship, or became a mere man. It means that He descended from the heavenly and invisible order, took the form of a servant, entered the condition of human weakness, and submitted Himself to suffering and death for the salvation of souls.

“Let this mind be in you, which was also in Christ Jesus: Who, being in the form of God, thought it not robbery to be equal with God: But made himself of no reputation, and took upon him the form of a servant, and was made in the likeness of men: And being found in fashion as a man, he humbled himself, and became obedient unto death, even the death of the cross.”

Philippians 2:5–8

Christ’s kenosis was real because His embodiment, suffering, bloodshed, and death were real. His body was not generated through ordinary human birth, but neither was it a phantom. The Son entered the visible world-order ruled by the lower powers and accepted the condition of those whom He came to liberate.

Kenosis is therefore both humility and redemptive descent. He who was above the powers placed Himself beneath their violence; He who was life entered death; and He who came from God the Father answered the cruelty of this world with obedience, mercy, and forgiveness. His self-emptying reveals not the loss of divinity, but the humility of divinity.

The faithful are called to possess the same mind: renouncing pride, domination, vainglory, and fleshly bondage, while serving others in love. Christian kenosis is not self-hatred or the denial of one’s being, but voluntary humility in imitation of the Lord.

See also: Question 8.03, “What does it mean that Christ descended?”; Question 8.07, “Why is Marcionite Christology not docetic?”; Question 8.08, “How can Christ possess real flesh if He was not born of Mary?”; Question 11.03, “How does Christ save?”

questions.question: What does Christ’s descent into Capernaum signify?
questions.answer:
Why did Jesus choose to descend into Capernaum, a modest fishing village in Galilee, rather than any other place on Earth? While we, as finite beings, cannot fully comprehend the workings of the divine, we can attempt to reason through the possibilities with the light we have been given.

It may be that Jesus chose Galilee—and, by extension, Samaria and Judea—because it was one of the few regions in the world at that time where some form of monotheism was practiced. This theological framework, though flawed and distorted, may have prepared the people there to more readily grasp the truth of the God revealed through Christ. Their belief in an omnipotent, omniscient, and incorporeal deity aligned more closely with the nature of the true Father than the polytheism found elsewhere.

Another possible reason is the ministry of John the Baptist, who preached repentance and prepared the hearts of many for the coming of Christ. Jesus himself acknowledged John’s unique role:

“For I say unto you, Among those that are born of women there is not a greater prophet than John the Baptist: but he that is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he.”

Evangelicon 5:28

This may suggest that Jesus chose to enter the region west of the Jordan River not only to fulfill a mission of salvation, but to affirm and build upon the groundwork laid by John.

The Jews and Samaritans, despite their many errors, did acknowledge many attributes of God the Father—his eternal nature, his spiritual essence, and his sovereign authority. It was during the Transfiguration that the true Father was fully revealed through Christ, making this region the place where spiritual blindness could begin to be undone.

Additionally, some Marcionite Christians believe that Yahweh, the deity worshipped in the Hebrew Bible, is in fact Satan masquerading as God. In this view, Jesus’ choice to descend into a land devoted to that false god was a deliberate act of confrontation—to overturn the false covenant and expose its author. As Jesus declared:

“Think not that I am come to fulfil the law, or the prophets: I am not come to fulfil, but to destroy.”

Evangelicon 14:16

While these reflections provide insight, they remain conjecture. The true answer to why Jesus descended where he did may never be fully known in this life. What matters above all is not the geography of his appearance, but the authority of his teaching and the salvation he brought.

questions.question: Why did Christ not descend sooner?
questions.answer:
The Apostle Paul declares that the sending of Christ came at the appointed season of God. “But when the fulness of the time was come, God sent forth his Son” so that we might receive adoption (Galatians 4:4–5). “For when we were yet without strength, in due time Christ died for the ungodly” (Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 4:6). God made known “the mystery of his will” to unite all things in Christ “in the dispensation of the fulness of times” (Laodiceans 1:9–10). This gospel is “the mystery which hath been hid from ages and from generations, but now is made manifest to his saints” (Colossians 1:26). Christ “gave himself a ransom for all, to be testified in due time” (I Timothy 2:6), and God “hath in due times manifested his word through preaching” (Titus 1:2–3).

Marcion’s Homily to Diognetus teaches the same truth in another key. God acted according to a long-kept, great, and unutterable plan. While that counsel was concealed, He only seemed to neglect us. Once it was revealed through His Son, its purpose became clear.

God permitted humanity for a season to follow its passions so that, convicted by our own deeds as unworthy of life, we would learn that we could not attain it by our own works. Then, when our iniquity was filled up and the time appointed by God arrived, He sent His Son. He came gently, as a king might send his son, to persuade and to summon rather than to coerce, for force is no attribute of God.

Thus, the timing reveals the sweet exchange: the holy for the lawless, the just for the unjust, so that the righteousness of One should justify many. Having first demonstrated our inability, God displayed His power to save, granting what we could never achieve on our own.

questions.question: Why is Marcionite Christology not docetic?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christianity does not affirm docetism. Docetism is a heterodox doctrine that holds Jesus’ bodily existence—particularly His human form—as a mere illusion or appearance, denying that He truly suffered or died in the flesh.

There is no credible evidence that Marcion of Sinope taught or supported this view. The primary source for the accusation that Marcion believed Christ to be a phantom is Tertullian, who presents it within Adversus Marcionem. However, these claims appear to function more as a reductio ad absurdum—an exaggerated rhetorical device—than a faithful representation of Marcion’s own Christology. Ironically, even within Tertullian’s own polemic, there are remnants suggesting that Marcion affirmed Christ’s possession of real flesh—flesh capable of suffering and dying on the cross. Tertullian simply dismissed these details as inconsistent and irrational rather than reassessing his accusations. Modern scholarship has increasingly rejected Tertullian’s caricature, particularly given that nearly all other early sources from the second and third centuries are silent regarding any association between Marcion and Docetism.

The doctrine of Docetism finds no support in the canonical Evangelicon and Apostolicon, and it is explicitly refuted by Marcion’s foremost disciple, Apelles. According to Rhodo, an early Christian writer, Apelles rejected Docetism outright, affirming that Jesus truly possessed human flesh—though not born of human parents. Apelles taught that Jesus descended directly from Heaven and appeared in human form, possessing real flesh, but not through natural birth. This belief aligns with the textual structure of the Evangelicon, which contains no nativity or birth narrative, omits any mention of the Virgin Mary, and begins with Christ’s sudden appearance in Capernaum. Apelles upheld the Marcionite view that Jesus entered the world fully formed, not through human generation, but by divine descent.

Misunderstandings about Marcion’s theology are largely due to the fact that nearly all surviving accounts were written by hostile Catholic heresiologists intent on discrediting him. In Evangelicon 23:36–42, Jesus explicitly demonstrates the reality of His body after the resurrection:

“But they were terrified and affrighted, and supposed that they had seen a phantom. And he said unto them, Why are ye troubled? and why do thoughts arise in your hearts? Behold my hands and my feet, that it is I myself: for a spirit hath not flesh and bones, as ye see me have. And when he had thus spoken, he shewed them his hands and his feet. And while they yet believed not for joy, and wondered, he said unto them, Have ye here any meat? And they gave him a piece of a broiled fish, and of an honeycomb. And he took it, and did eat before them.”

This passage affirms both the physicality and continuity of Christ’s risen body—explicitly refuting any notion that Marcionite theology denied Christ’s true humanity.

It is likely that the association of Marcion with Docetism arose from confusion with another figure: Marcian of Rhossus, a contemporary of Marcion who led a Docetist sect believed to have authored or used the Gospel of Peter. Since Marcion rejected Peter and upheld Paul alone as the true apostolic witness to Christ, it is implausible that he and this Marcian were the same individual.

Furthermore, while one of Marcion’s disciples—Lucanus—is reported to have embraced some Docetist beliefs, including denial of the physical body of Christ and the immortality of the soul, these views were not representative of the broader Marcionite Church, which consistently upheld the teachings of Marcion and the authority of the canonical Evangelicon and Apostolicon.

Finally, some accusations of Docetism may have stemmed from Marcion’s eschatological position, which aligns with that of the Apostle Paul—namely, that it is the soul or spirit that is raised in the resurrection, not flesh and blood. This view, focused on spiritual resurrection for believers, may have led critics to falsely extend the belief to Christ, erroneously assuming that the Marcionite Church denied His physical resurrection. Yet, as the Evangelicon affirms, Jesus not only appeared in the flesh but suffered, died, and rose bodily—refuting the charge of Docetism entirely.

See also: Question 8.01, “Who is Jesus Christ?”; Question 8.09, “Why does the Church reject the virgin birth?”

questions.question: How can Christ possess real flesh if He was not born of Mary?
questions.answer:
Christ possessed real flesh because embodiment does not require generation from ordinary human generation or birth from Mary. The Church teaches that He descended from above and assumed a true human body suited to His mission in the world. His flesh was tangible, capable of suffering, and truly given unto death.

The rejection of the virgin birth is therefore not a denial of embodiment. The Church rejects an earthly genealogy for Christ, not His real body, real suffering, real death, or bodily appearances after the resurrection.

Christ’s body was therefore real in substance and capable of hunger, weariness, pain, bloodshed, and death, yet it was divinely given for His saving mission rather than produced through ordinary human generation. His heavenly origin and His true embodiment are complementary, not contradictory.

See also: Question 8.07, “Why is Marcionite Christology not docetic?”; Question 8.09, “Why does the Church reject the virgin birth?”; Question 10.01, “What is the soul?”

questions.question: Why does the Church reject the virgin birth?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians reject the doctrine of the virgin birth. The belief is so widely accepted within mainstream Christianity that questioning it is often treated as heretical. Yet this doctrine is absent from the earliest Christian texts and traditions foundational to Marcionite Christianity.

The Evangelicon contains no birth narrative. There is no manger, no Bethlehem infancy, no Mary cradling a child, no star, no shepherds, no magi, no census, no flight to Egypt, and no genealogy. The Gospel begins not with Christ’s birth, but with his descent from heaven into Capernaum:

“Jesus descended out of heaven into the Galilean city of Capernaum, a city near the sea, in the borders of Zabulon and Nephthalim. Having taken on the appearance of a man, he appeared thirty years of age and was teaching in the synagogue on the Sabbath days.”

Evangelicon 2:3–4

This opening is central to Marcionite Christology. Christ does not enter the Gospel as an infant born into a human bloodline. He descends from heaven, takes on the appearance of a man, appears thirty years of age, and begins teaching with authority. The silence about birth is not an accidental omission. It is theological. The Evangelicon refuses to anchor Christ in the lower order of flesh, genealogy, tribal descent, or Hebrew prophecy.

In Marcionite doctrine, descent from above does not mean illusion. Christ’s flesh was real, His suffering was real, and His death was real; what the Church rejects is not embodiment, but generation through ordinary human generation.

Most biblical scholars regard Mark as the earliest of the four gospels of the later Catholic canon. Mark also contains no birth or childhood narrative. It begins with Jesus’ public ministry. The Gospel of John, typically dated later than the Synoptics, likewise contains no nativity account. This absence shows that the virgin birth was not essential to the earliest proclamation of Christ. The birth narratives appear only in Matthew and Luke, and even there they differ significantly.

Matthew and Luke do not give one consistent nativity tradition. Matthew traces Jesus’ genealogy through Solomon, while Luke traces it through Nathan, another son of David. Matthew includes magi, Herod’s massacre of infants, and the flight to Egypt. Luke includes a census under Quirinius, shepherds, and a presentation at the temple. Matthew has no shepherds or temple presentation; Luke has no magi, massacre, or flight to Egypt. These are not minor differences. They are distinct theological constructions designed to attach Jesus to Davidic descent, Bethlehem, and Hebrew Bible expectation.

Both Matthew and Luke also contain a basic tension: they assert a miraculous conception by the Holy Spirit while also providing genealogies through Joseph, whose biological connection to Jesus is denied by the same birth narratives. This contradiction raises serious questions about the coherence and purpose of the virgin birth doctrine.

The Apostle Paul never teaches a virgin birth. He never mentions Mary as Christ’s mother, never appeals to Bethlehem, never grounds Christ’s identity in Davidic biology, and never makes the Gospel depend on a nativity. In the Apostolicon, Paul’s language is instead the language of sending, descent, likeness, and manifestation.

“For what the law could not do, in that it was weak through the flesh, God sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh, and for sin, condemned sin in the flesh.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 7:3

The phrase “in the likeness of sinful flesh” is crucial. It does not mean Christ was unreal. It means his body was real and capable of suffering, but not generated by the lower biological order. Christ truly appeared in flesh, truly suffered, truly died, and truly rose. But his origin was from God the Father, not from a womb.

Paul makes the same point in his hymn of Christ’s descent:

“Who, being in the form of God, thought it not robbery to be equal with God: But made himself of no reputation, and took upon him the form of a servant, and was made in the likeness of men: And being found in fashion as a man, he humbled himself, and became obedient unto death, even the death of the cross.”

Philippians 2:6–8

The language is form, likeness, and fashion. It describes a real manifestation from above, not ordinary biological generation below. Christ’s body was not an illusion, phantom, or costume. It was real. But it was differently originated.

The Evangelicon also preserves a direct challenge to the claim that Christ had a biological mother and brothers:

“To find out if he was birthed, they set a trap for him. And it was told him by certain which said, Thy mother and thy brethren stand without, desiring to see thee. And he answered and said unto them, Who are my mother and my brethren? None but they who hear my words and do them.”

Evangelicon 6:19–21

In the Marcionite reading, this is not a casual remark about family priorities. It is a Christological declaration. Christ does not affirm biological descent and then merely subordinate it to spiritual kinship. He refuses to define himself by earthly motherhood, fleshly lineage, or family claim. His origin is the Father.

This rejection of the virgin birth is not docetism. Marcionite Christianity does not teach that Christ was a ghost, illusion, or mere appearance. Apelles, the foremost disciple of Marcion, pushed back against the false accusation of docetism and affirmed the real manifestation of Christ. Christ’s body was real. His suffering was real. His death on the cross was real. What Marcionites deny is not the reality of Christ’s body, but its generation through the lower order of flesh, conception, gestation, and birth.

The virgin birth, therefore, appears to be a later theological development rather than an apostolic teaching. Its purpose was to graft Jesus onto the prophecies, geography, and lineage of the Hebrew Bible. It made him the Davidic Jewish Messiah expected by the Jewish scriptures. Marcionite Christians reject that framework. Jesus was not the Jewish Messiah foretold by the Hebrew prophets. He was the divine Son sent from God the Father to reveal the previously unknown Father and rescue souls from bondage to the lower world-order.

The importance of Jesus Christ lies not in the manner of a supposed earthly birth, but in the divine mission he accomplished through his descent, teaching, death, and resurrection. The foundation of the Christian faith is not the nativity. It is the revelation of the Father, the Gospel, the cross, and the empty tomb.

“Without father, without mother, without descent, having neither beginning of days, nor end of life; but made like unto the Son of God; abideth a priest continually.”

Alexandrians 8:3

This verse encapsulates the Marcionite understanding: Christ came not through human lineage, but directly from the Father, outside the constraints of earthly descent and Judaic expectation.

This rejection did not disappear with Marcion’s immediate school. It persisted among later neo-Marcionites, such as the Paulicians. The Byzantine adjuration formulae condemn those who say the Lord “brought his body from above” and require converts instead to confess that the Son of God was truly “born in the flesh of holy Mary” and “incarnate of her substance.” In plain terms, the adjurations treat the Paulician position as a denial that Christ was genuinely conceived and born of the Virgin, and they require its formal repudiation as a condition of reconciliation.

Thus, Marcionite Christians reject the virgin birth not because we deny Christ’s reality, but because we affirm his higher origin. Christ was not produced by the lower order. He was sent by God the Father, manifested in real flesh, and entered the world to rescue souls from bondage. He had no need of Bethlehem, Davidic genealogy, or Mary’s womb. His origin is the Father, and his mission is salvation.

See also: Question 8.04, “How does the Church understand kenosis?”; Question 8.08, “How can Christ possess real flesh if He was not born of Mary?”; Question 8.05, “What does Christ’s descent into Capernaum signify?”

questions.question: How did Jesus pass through the angry crowd in Nazareth with a real body?
questions.answer:
“But he passing through the midst of them went his way to Capernaum.”

Evangelicon 3:15

From a Marcionite perspective, this event does not mean Jesus became incorporeal or ceased to have a real body. We confess that He truly assumed a body — not from the mythological virgin birth, but miraculously given by God the Father. In this scene, that body remained fully tangible, yet He was able to pass unharmed through a hostile crowd.

The text does not require that He vanish or lose materiality. Rather, He moved through them by the force of His will, the awe of His authority, and possibly the aid of God the Father. The crowd, though enraged, could not seize Him because His time had not yet come.

This moment at Nazareth shows that even before the resurrection, Jesus’ body was fully subject to His divine will and could not be taken except on His own terms. It was a real, tangible body, yet His mission and authority ensured that no opposition could prevail until the appointed hour.

questions.question: How did Jesus disappear and reappear after the resurrection?
questions.answer:
“And their eyes were opened, and they knew him; and he vanished out of their sight.”

Evangelicon 23:30

From a Marcionite perspective, this disappearance is fully consistent with the reality of the Lord’s post-resurrection body. We confess that Jesus truly assumed a body — not from the mythological virgin birth story, but miraculously given by God the Father. After His resurrection, this body was glorified and entirely subject to His will.

In that glorified state, He was touched, He ate, and He was seen. Yet He was no longer bound by the ordinary limitations of mortal flesh. His appearances were physical and real, but His glorified nature allowed Him to manifest and withdraw from sight at the time and place of His choosing.

At Emmaus, the disappearance was not a trick of perception, but the deliberate conclusion of His visible presence after revealing Himself in the breaking of bread — signaling that His followers would now know Him, not by constant physical presence, but through faith and the proclamation of His word.

The vanishing at Emmaus affirms both that the risen Christ bore a real, tangible body and that the resurrection transformed it into a glorified body, perfectly responsive to His divine will.

questions.question: Why is Christ called the Son of Man?
questions.answer:
In the Dialogue of Adamantius, the Marcionite speaker Megethius is directly questioned about the title “Son of Man.” Adamantius asks how Christ can be called “Son of Man” if he is truly the Son of God and did not come by ordinary human generation. Megethius answers that Christ used the phrase “as a parable,” and when Eutropius asks whether this should be understood spiritually rather than historically, Megethius answers: “Spiritual.”

The witness is hostile, but valuable: it preserves a distinctly Marcionite way of reading the title.

Therefore, when Christ calls himself the “Son of Man,” he is not confessing descent from Adam, birth from the lower order, or subjection to the false deity of the Hebrew Bible. He is speaking according to his manifestation among men. He appeared in the likeness of men, took the form of a servant, and was found in fashion as a man, yet his origin remained heavenly.

This agrees with the Evangelicon, where Christ descends from heaven into Capernaum, having taken on the appearance of a man. It also agrees with Paul, who says that God sent his own Son “in the likeness of sinful flesh” and that Christ was “made in the likeness of men.” The phrase “Son of Man” therefore refers to Christ’s real manifestation in the human condition, not to ordinary human generation.

It is not a denial of his true body. Christ was not a phantom, illusion, or mere symbol. He truly appeared in flesh, truly suffered, truly died, and truly rose. But his flesh was divinely given from above, not produced by the fleshly system governed by the ‘god of this world.’

Thus, “Son of Man” means that the Son of God humbled himself and appeared among men for our salvation. It is the title of his condescension, manifestation, and redemptive solidarity with those he came to rescue. It does not make him a son of Adam. It reveals the mercy of the heavenly Christ who entered the condition of mankind without originating from its bondage.

questions.question: What is the significance of the name ‘Jesus’?
questions.answer:
If Jesus was not born to Mary and Joseph, then why did he bear the name “Jesus”? The name Jesus is derived from the Hebrew Yeshua, meaning “to rescue,” “to save,” or simply “salvation.” These meanings are entirely consistent with his divine mission: he came to bring salvation to all people, transcending nations and borders.

Though the precise mysteries of the divine may remain beyond our full comprehension as mortal beings, it is reasonable to conclude that Jesus chose a name that would be both accessible to those he ministered to and rich in symbolic meaning. His name, therefore, was not merely a cultural reference point but a direct reflection of his purpose—to redeem and to save.

In the Evangelicon narrative, the name “Jesus” does not first appear as a birth-name spoken by Mary or Joseph, but as an address thrust upon Him at the outset of His public manifestation: it is the devil-possessed man in the synagogue of Capernaum who first cries out “Jesus,” and he immediately couples the name not with any genealogy or earthly origin, but with a divine title—“the Holy One of God.” This detail is telling. The Evangelicon presents the name as part of His revealed identity in ministry, recognized (even unwillingly) by hostile spirits, rather than as a merely domestic or familial designation anchored in nativity traditions.

questions.question: Was James the Just Christ’s biological brother?
questions.answer:
James the Just was not the biological brother of Jesus Christ. According to early Christian tradition, he was one of the Seventy Disciples and the first bishop of Jerusalem.

“And when James, Cephas, and John, who seemed to be pillars, perceived the grace that was given unto me, they gave to me and Barnabas the right hands of fellowship; that we should go unto the heathen, and they unto the circumcision.”

Galatians 2:9

Paul also refers to James as “the Lord’s brother”:

“But other of the apostles saw I none, save James the Lord’s brother.”

Galatians 1:19

And again, James is named among those to whom the risen Christ appeared:

“And that he was buried, and that he rose again the third day according to the scriptures: And that he was seen of Cephas, then of the twelve… After that, he was seen of James; then of all the apostles.”

I Corinthians 15:4–7

In all of these cases, the term “brother” comes from the Koine Greek word adelphos, which often denotes spiritual kinship rather than blood relation. This is evident throughout Paul’s epistles, where he frequently uses the term to describe fellow believers and co-laborers in the faith.

For example:

Paul calls Apollos “our brother”: “As touching our brother Apollos…” (I Corinthians 16:12)

He refers to Timothy as “our brother”: “Paul, an apostle… and Timothy our brother…” (II Corinthians 1:1)

He describes Titus as “my brother”: “…because I found not Titus my brother…” (II Corinthians 2:13)

He calls Tychicus a “beloved brother”: “…Tychicus, a beloved brother and faithful minister in the Lord…” (Laodiceans 6:21)

He refers to Onesimus likewise: “…Onesimus, a faithful and beloved brother…” (Colossians 4:9)

He describes Epaphroditus as “my brother”: “…Epaphroditus, my brother, and companion in labour…” (Philippians 2:25)

And he calls Sosthenes “our brother”: “…Sosthenes our brother.” (I Corinthians 1:1)

These consistent usages demonstrate that “brother” in Paul’s writings does not signify a literal family relationship but a spiritual or communal bond in Christ.

Neither Catholic, Orthodox, nor Lutheran traditions today insist on a biological brotherhood between Jesus and James, instead suggesting he may have been a cousin, step-brother, or some other familial relation. However, the Marcionite Church rejects any physical or biological kinship between James and Jesus, affirming instead that James was a spiritual brother—one of the early leaders of the Church, not of Christ’s household, but of his mission.

The honorific title “the Lord’s brother” was used by Paul to emphasize James’ importance in the early Christian community, particularly in Jerusalem, and his role as a witness to the risen Christ—just as Paul himself was. But this title in no way suggests biological relation. Rather, it affirms the spiritual unity shared by all who follow Christ in truth and faith.

questions.question: Why does the Church deny that Christ is the Jewish Messiah?
questions.answer:
The Jewish Messiah, as envisioned in the Hebrew scriptures, is described as a militant leader—a bearer of arms and skilled in warfare. By contrast, our Lord Jesus Christ came in peace, without sword or spear, offering salvation to all nations rather than conquest for one people.

The prophecies of Isaiah regarding the Jewish Messiah do not align with the life or mission of Jesus. Isaiah’s Jewish Messiah is said to be named Emmanuel (Isaiah 7:14), and to plunder Damascus and Samaria before the king of Assyria (Isaiah 8:4). These descriptions do not suit Jesus Christ, who neither bore that name nor participated in military conquest or nationalistic violence.

Our Lord Jesus Christ was not foretold in the Hebrew Bible. His suffering and death on the cross contradict the expectations of a Jewish Messiah and are in fact condemned by the Hebrew scriptures themselves. According to the Law, anyone hung on a tree is accursed of the false god:

“And if a man have committed a sin worthy of death, and he be to be put to death, and thou hang him on a tree: His body shall not remain all night upon the tree, but thou shalt in any wise bury him that day; (for he that is hanged is accursed of God;) that thy land be not defiled, which the Lord thy God giveth thee for an inheritance.” —Deuteronomy 21:22–23

This passage only reinforces the conclusion that the god of the Hebrew Bible—the lawgiver who curses the crucified—is a false deity and not the Father revealed by Jesus.

Marcion taught that Christ was the sudden savior, who descended from Heaven in the form of a fully mature man. He came to rescue believers from bondage to the Law of the false god and returned to Heaven after accomplishing his mission. He was not the Jewish Messiah of Judaic expectation, nor did he fulfill any of the prophecies claimed by the Jewish tradition. Neither Christ nor his Apostles, especially Paul, subjected themselves to the Law or its ordinances. Marcion’s original Testamentum made no attempt to reconcile the Gospel with the Hebrew scriptures, for it recognized no continuity between the two. Christ was not the fulfillment of Judaism, but its rejection—and the revelation of a new and true God.

questions.question: How does the Church understand the names Christos and Chrestos?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians would not have used the word Christ—Christos in Greek—in the sense of affirming Jesus as the Jewish Messiah. While the term Christos literally means “anointed,” Marcionite Christians likely understood it in a more general, symbolic sense, referring to Jesus’ spiritual mission rather than any fulfillment of Hebrew prophecy. The reference to Jesus being anointed with oil before his passion, as found in the Evangelicon, supports this interpretation:

“My head with oil thou didst not anoint: but this woman hath anointed my feet with ointment.”

Evangelicon 5:46

This anointing of the feet, rather than the head as in Israelite tradition, marked a key divergence from Jewish messianic customs and indicated that Jesus’ kingship was not of this world. While none of the ancient critics of Marcion—including Tertullian and Epiphanius—accused the Marcionite Christians of using an alternate word in place of Christos, Tertullian does note that Marcionites practiced anointing newly baptized believers with oil, reflecting a continuation of this spiritual symbolism.

An important historical artifact sheds further light on Marcionite terminology. On October 1, 318 C.E., Paul of Lebaba, a Marcionite presbyter, dedicated a church in the Syrian village of Lebaba. The surviving inscription is the oldest known inscribed reference to Jesus and uses a related term, Chrestos—meaning “good” or “righteous”—rather than Christos. The inscription reads:

“The meeting-house of the Marcionites, in the village of Lebaba, of the Lord and Saviour Jesus the Good — Erected by the forethought of Paul a presbyter, in the year 630 Seleucid era.”

This phrase, “Jesus the Good,” was likely a devotional title emphasizing his moral perfection and divine benevolence, not a doctrinal replacement for Christ. The canonical scriptures of the Testamentum refer to Jesus by many titles—Lord, Saviour, Son of God, and Son of Man—reflecting his manifold office in Marcionite theology.

Even in the Septuagint—the Greek translation of the Hebrew Bible—the term Christos is not reserved for a singular Jewish messianic figure. It is applied to Israelite kings, high priests, prophets, patriarchs, and even foreign rulers like Cyrus the Great. Thus, the Marcionite use of the term Christos would have aligned with its more general meaning: one who is consecrated, not necessarily one who fulfills Jewish nationalist expectations.

Marcionite Christians affirmed that Jesus’ anointing was spiritual, not political, and that his mission had nothing to do with the restoration of Israel under the law of the false god. Instead, Jesus was anointed to proclaim the Kingdom of God—a heavenly, universal reign in which he now sits at the right hand of the true God, revealed only through the Gospel.

This understanding also helps explain the significance of the Chi Rho symbol within Marcionite tradition. The Chi Rho, formed by the overlapping Greek letters chi (Χ) and rho (Ρ), stands for both Christos (anointed) and Chrestos (good). It represents the dual truths of Jesus’ divine mission: that he is both the anointed emissary of God the Father and the embodiment of perfect goodness.

In the Evangelicon itself, it is striking that the first figure to address Jesus by name is not a disciple, nor a friendly confessor, but the devil-possessed man in the synagogue of Capernaum—who cries out “Jesus” and identifies him only as “the Holy One of God,” before Jesus has offered any public self-designation that would invite a reading of Him as the Jewish Messiah. Only much later in the ministry does Peter apply the title “the Christ”; and even then, Jesus appears to treat that term as a practical label of recognition rather than a programmatic claim to Jewish Messiahship—employing it sparingly, and without the prophetic-nationalist content that the false covenant attached to it, as though he adopted the title only as convenience required, while continuing to be known chiefly by truer names: Lord, Saviour, Son of God, Son of Man, and Holy One.

questions.question: Was Christ Melchisedec?
questions.answer:
Melchisedec is not to be identified with Jesus Christ. Rather, he functions as a literary foreshadowing or typological figure within the Apostle Paul’s Epistle to the Alexandrians.

Melchisedec appears only briefly in the Book of Genesis, where he offers a symbolic sacrifice of bread and wine and receives tithes from Abraham. The Hebrew Bible provides no account of his ancestry, birth, or death, leading some to interpret him as an eternal priest without origin or conclusion. The name “Melchisedec” itself is not a proper name but a title, meaning “King of Righteousness.”

In the Epistle to the Alexandrians, the Apostle Paul does not equate Melchisedec with Christ but states clearly that Melchisedec resembles the Son of God. This resemblance lies in his portrayal as timeless and in his offering of bread and wine—elements that Christ himself fulfills in the true, spiritual sense. Paul’s usage is allegorical, not literal; Melchisedec serves as a figure by which to explain Christ’s superior and eternal priesthood.

The Apostle Paul never defines Melchisedec’s identity because the purpose of referencing him was pedagogical, not doctrinal. Paul is instructing the Alexandrian believers—many of whom were familiar with the Hebrew scriptures—by reinterpreting their literary heritage in light of the Gospel of the Lord Jesus.

“Whither the forerunner is for us entered, even Jesus, made an high priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec.”

Alexandrians 7:11

questions.question: Why does the Church use the Chi Rho rather than the Cross?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians seek to restore the original and most authentic form of Christianity. In doing so, we adopt the Chi Rho as our principal symbol—a Christogram formed by overlaying the first two capital letters of the Greek word ΧΡΙΣΤΟΣ (Christos), chi (Χ) and rho (Ρ), so that the vertical stroke of the rho intersects the center of the chi. Unlike other Christian traditions, the Marcionite Chi Rho is never flanked by the Alpha and Omega symbols, as these are derived from the non-canonical Book of Revelation, which we do not recognize as scripture.

Our preference for the Chi Rho over the traditional cross distinguishes us from other Christian denominations and reflects historical accuracy. There is minimal evidence of the cross as a Christian symbol in the second century, and it is likely that early Christians avoided it due to its gruesome association with public execution. For the first three centuries of the Church, the cross played a relatively minor role in Christian iconography.

Nevertheless, we do not reject or forbid the use of the cross or crucifix. The only guideline for Marcionite Christian imagery is that it must be grounded in the canonical scripture contained in the Testamentum. The Chi Rho remains our preferred symbol because it emphasizes the central truth of our faith—that the resurrection of Jesus is more essential than the manner of His death.

questions.question: How does the Church understand christocentrism?
questions.answer:
Christocentrism refers to a theological orientation within Christianity in which the person and work of Jesus Christ serve as the central focus around which all doctrine, worship, and interpretation are ordered.

The Apostle Paul articulates the foundation of this approach:

“For Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 8:7

Marcion of Sinope extended and systematized this principle by asserting that the revelation of Christ constitutes the highest and final authority in matters of faith. For Marcion, the person of Jesus—His incarnation, death, and resurrection—superseded and transcended the authority of the Hebrew scriptures. The Gospel of Christ, preserved in the original Testamentum, became the inviolable standard by which all else must be measured.

In responding to those who demanded validation from Hebrew texts, Marcion drew a sharp distinction:

“For I heard certain persons saying, ‘If I find it not in the charters, I believe it not in the Gospel.’ And when I said to them, ‘It is written,’ they answered me, ‘That is the question.’ But as for me, my charter is Jesus Christ, the inviolable charter is His cross and His death and His resurrection, and faith through Him.” Philadelphians 8:6–8

This bold affirmation encapsulates the Christocentric heart of Marcionite Christianity: all truth and authority flow from the revelation of Jesus Christ alone.

questions.question: What is Marcionite cosmology?
questions.answer:
Marcionite cosmology adheres to both deferred demiurgy and negative demiurgy.

Deferred demiurgy means that creation is mediated, not direct. The visible, fleshly, and corruptible world was not fashioned immediately by God the Father in the fullness of His perfection, but mediately using subjacent matter by lower principalities, powers, thrones, dominions, and angelic rulers beneath Him.

Negative demiurgy means that the ‘prince of this world’, as known in the Evangelicon, and the ‘god of this world’, as known in the Apostolicon, was initially ignorant of God the Father. He ruled and fashioned the lower world without knowing the Father above him. He thought himself God because he knew no higher God. But when Jesus descended, the hidden Father was made known, and the lower powers perceived that their kingdom was being invaded, judged, and overthrown by One greater than themselves.

questions.question: How does the Church understand creation?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians are creationists in the theological sense: all being ultimately depends upon God the Father. Yet the Church does not receive the Genesis creation account as Christian scripture, nor does it teach that God the Father directly fashioned the visible world in six literal days.

The Church distinguishes ultimate origin from mediate formation. God the Father is the source of all being; the visible and corruptible world-order was fashioned below Him through lower powers from matter, which the Apostle calls “the elements of this world.” Therefore the Father is not the immediate craftsman of the present bondage, decay, and false worship that mark this age.

Where the Testamentum does not define questions of natural history, cosmology, biology, or the age of the universe, the Church uses reason and observation. Creation is confessed theologically, not reduced to a literal reading of the Hebrew Genesis narrative.

See also: Question 9.03, “What is matter?”; Question 9.04, “What is the origin of matter?”; Question 9.10, “What is deferred demiurgy?”; Question 9.11, “How does the Church understand Logos-mediated creation in relation to Marcionite deferred demiurgy?”

questions.question: What is matter?
questions.answer:
Matter is the underlying substance or elemental material from which the physical, fleshly, and visible world was fashioned. The Apostle calls it “the elements of this world.” Matter is not God, not the ‘god of this world’, not an angelic power, and not the world-order imposed upon visible creation.

“Even so we, when we were children, were in bondage under the elements of the world.”

Galatians 4:3

Matter is morally neutral in itself. It is distinct from the fashioned world and from the corrupt system of bondage governing that world. The question of its ultimate origin is treated separately.

The Dialogue of Megethius confirms this distinction. Matter is not an eternal rival to God, nor is it the cause of evil as though matter were a wicked substance. Matter belongs to the created order; evil belongs to disorder, bondage, action, corruption, and false rule.

See also: Question 9.04, “What is the origin of matter?”; Question 9.09, “What is the difference between matter, the world, and the world-order, and are they evil?”

questions.question: What is the origin of matter?
questions.answer:
Matter itself, called by the Apostle “the elements of this world,” was not made by the ‘god of this world.’ The ancient witnesses distinguish matter from the power that fashioned the visible, fleshly world. Hippolytus says that Marcion and Cerdo taught “three principles of the universe,” namely the good, the just, and matter, and that the ‘god of this world’ formed all things from “subjacent matter” (Hippolytus, Refutation of All Heresies 7.17). However, the heresiologists inaccurately depicted matter as though it were a distinct principle within a cosmological hierarchy. It was not. Matter was not a ruler, power, angel, or rival deity. Matter ultimately originates from God the Father, but it was taken up, shaped, and arranged by the lower world-ruling power into the present physical order.

The Dialogue of Megethius strengthens this doctrine by rejecting the claim that matter is uncreated. Megethius argues that if matter is compound, it depends upon prior elements and therefore came into being; and if it came into being, it cannot be uncreated. Matter therefore cannot stand beside God as a second eternal principle.

In other words, matter is not identical with the ‘god of this world’, nor is it simply the same thing as the visible world he formed. Matter is the underlying substance from which the present physical order was fashioned, but its ultimate source is God the Father. The physical world fashioned from matter is likewise not evil merely because it exists. It contains real goods, but it is also flawed, mixed, corruptible, and subject to bondage, decay, sin, and death.

This distinction is important. The ‘god of this world’ is not the absolute maker of being itself. He is the fashioner, arranger, and ruler of this lower world. Matter is like bronze in the hand of a craftsman. A sculptor may fashion a bronze statue, shape it, polish it, and give it visible form, but he did not thereby create the bronze itself. So also the ‘god of this world’ fashioned the physical, fleshly, and visible world from matter, but he did not originate matter as God the Father does.

Tertullian, though writing as a hostile witness, preserves the same basic distinction when he argues against Marcion’s teaching concerning God, the lower world-fashioning power, and matter. Even in Tertullian’s account, matter is treated as something underlying the formation of the world, not simply as the same thing as the lower ruler himself (Tertullian, Against Marcion 1.15).

Therefore, we do not say that matter is evil in itself. Matter is morally neutral. Nor do we say that the physical world is pure evil. It contains life, beauty, order, and many real goods, but these goods are unstable and perishing because the present world-system is flawed, fleshly, corruptible, and ruled by lower powers. The evil lies not in matter as such, but in the corrupt order imposed upon it by the ‘god of this world’, who fashioned a visible realm in imperfect imitation of the higher heavens. Matter belongs ultimately to God the Father, but the present world-system is the work of the lower ruler who shaped it. The bronze is not evil; the idol made from it is.

questions.question: Does the Church believe matter is eternal?
questions.answer:
No. The Church does not believe that matter is eternal, uncreated, self-existent, or co-eternal with God. God the Father alone is unbegotten and without beginning. Matter is created, derivative, subordinate, and dependent upon Him.

The Dialogue of Megethius rejects the opinion of Hermogenes that uncreated matter must be placed beside God in order to explain evil. Megethius argues that if matter is compound, it depends upon prior elements and therefore came into being; and if it came into being, it cannot be uncreated.

Thus the Church confesses neither eternal matter nor evil matter. Matter ultimately belongs to the created order, while the present world-order is the result of lower fashioning, bondage, and corruption.

See also: Question 9.04, “What is the origin of matter?”; Question 9.06, “Does the Church believe in creation out of nothing?”; Question 9.07, “Why does the Church reject the doctrine of co-eternal matter?”; Dialogue of Megethius 7:1-20; Dialogue of Megethius 11:1-11.

questions.question: Does the Church believe in creation out of nothing?
questions.answer:
The Church teaches that matter is not eternal, uncreated, self-existent, or co-eternal with God. God the Father alone is unbegotten and without beginning. All things beneath Him are derivative, dependent, and brought into being by His will through the Word.

Creation out of nothing does not mean that the present corrupt world-order is the direct and perfect work of God the Father. Rather, it means that no eternal matter stands beside God as a rival principle. Matter itself is created and morally neutral; the lower powers then fashioned the visible world-order from created matter, in imitation of the higher heavens and under the limits of their own inferiority.

God the Father gives being from nothing through the Logos; the lower powers fashion the visible world-order from created matter. Thus the Church rejects both errors: we do not say that matter is eternal, as Hermogenes taught; nor do we say that the corruptible world-order is equal to the Kingdom of God. God gives being; the lower powers fashion, order, govern, and corrupt what is beneath them.

The Dialogue of Megethius supports this doctrine by arguing that if qualities can be made where they did not previously exist, then they are made out of nothing; and if such qualities may be made out of nothing, matter itself cannot be treated as an eternal limit upon God.

See also: Question 9.04, “What is the origin of matter?”; Question 9.05, “Does the Church believe matter is eternal?”; Question 9.07, “Why does the Church reject the doctrine of co-eternal matter?”; Question 9.10, “What is deferred demiurgy?”; Question 9.11, “How does the Church understand Logos-mediated creation in relation to Marcionite deferred demiurgy?”; Dialogue of Megethius 8:1-31.

questions.question: Why does the Church reject the doctrine of co-eternal matter?
questions.answer:
The Church rejects co-eternal matter because it introduces a second uncreated principle beside God, weakens divine supremacy, and fails to explain evil. If matter is separate from God, then it becomes a rival eternal substance. If matter is in God, then God becomes the receptacle of disorder and evil. If God is in matter, then God is contained and circumscribed by matter.

Megethius therefore argues that the doctrine of uncreated matter cannot preserve the greatness of God. It either collapses God and matter into one uncreated substance, multiplies uncreated substances, or makes God dependent upon a realm beneath Him. None of these errors may be confessed by the Church.

The true doctrine is simpler and more reverent: God the Father alone is unbegotten; matter is created and subordinate; evil is not explained by making matter eternal, but by recognizing disorder, false rule, bondage, corruption, and sinful action within the lower world-order.

See also: Question 9.05, “Does the Church believe matter is eternal?”; Question 9.06, “Does the Church believe in creation out of nothing?”; Question 9.08, “Is evil a substance?”; Dialogue of Megethius 7:1-20.

questions.question: Is evil a substance?
questions.answer:
Evil is not a substance, not matter, and not a created nature. Evil belongs to disorder, corruption, privation, false rule, sinful will, destructive action, and bondage under the lower powers. Matter can be used wrongly, ordered wrongly, or ruled corruptly, but matter itself is not evil.

The Dialogue of Megethius is especially useful here. Megethius presses the question whether evil things are substances or actions and qualities. The argument shows that evil things do not possess independent substantial being; they arise in actors, actions, passions, corruptions, and disordered qualities.

Therefore the Church does not blame matter as though matter were a wicked god or an evil substance. The evil of this age lies in the corrupt world-order, in the lower powers who govern it, and in sinful wills that serve bondage rather than the Gospel.

See also: Question 9.09, “What is the difference between matter, the world, and the world-order, and are they evil?”; Question 11.05, “How does the Church explain moral evil?”; Question 9.20, “How does the Church explain natural evil?”; Dialogue of Megethius 9:1-18; Dialogue of Megethius 10:16-24.

questions.question: What is the difference between matter, the world, and the world-order, and are they evil?
questions.answer:
The Church distinguishes matter, the world, and the world-order. Matter is the underlying substance from which the physical, fleshly, and visible world is fashioned. The world is visible creation in its present corruptible form, marked by weakness, decay, suffering, and death. The world-order is the system of bondage, domination, false worship, and lower rule operating within it.

Matter is not evil in itself, nor is bodily existence intrinsically sinful. The visible world is corruptible and inferior to the heavens above, yet it contains real goods, beauty, love, mercy, order, and created life. The world-order, however, is the system by which the lower powers enslave souls and turn the good things of creation into idols.

This distinction preserves the rejection of both material worship and hatred of the body. The faithful do not adore matter as though the visible world were the Kingdom of God, nor do they despise embodiment as though the body were intrinsically sinful. The body is disciplined as a temporary instrument of obedience, while Christ liberates the soul from the corrupt world-order.

Most Marcionite schools held this distinction. The Severians adopted a stricter and more world-denying position, but their teaching did not define Marcionite Christianity as a whole.

See also: Question 9.03, “What is matter?”; Question 9.04, “What is the origin of matter?”; Question 9.19, “Why do beauty, love, and goodness exist in this world?”; Question 10.09, “Does the Marcionite Church believe the body is evil?”

questions.question: What is deferred demiurgy?
questions.answer:
Deferred demiurgy explains how this lower order came to be. The physical world was fashioned mediately through lower powers. This is why Marcionite Christianity speaks in the Apostle’s language of “principalities,” “powers,” “thrones,” and “dominions,” and of things “in heaven and invisible.” God the Father is above all. Christ is sent from Him. Beneath them are the lower powers. Matter itself is not one of these powers, nor another rung in their hierarchy. It proceeds ultimately from God the Father, while the physical world-order is the result of lower fashioning from matter.

Deferred demiurgy does not mean Hermogenean eternal matter. The lower powers fashion the visible world from matter, but matter itself is created and subordinate. The Dialogue of Megethius is important here because it rejects the attempt to explain evil by placing uncreated matter beside God.

The ancient heresiologists preserve the angelic character of these lower powers most clearly in their reports about Apelles and the Apelleans. Apelles taught that the one who fashioned the world was an angelic power beneath the supreme God. Pseudo-Tertullian says Apelles taught that the world was made by a lower angelic power “in imitation of a superior world,” but imperfectly. Tertullian likewise reports that the Apelleans spoke of “a certain angel of great renown” as having made this world and then repented of his work. (Pseudo-Tertullian, Against All Heresies 6; Tertullian, On the Flesh of Christ 8.)

Tertullian, arguing against Marcion’s use of the Apostle, also discusses “princes and powers,” “fallen angels,” and the devil in connection with the powers that did not know the Lord of glory. (Tertullian, Against Marcion 5.6.) This supports the wider Marcionite reading of the lower powers as angelic or fallen angelic rulers, though the clearest explicit testimony belongs to the Apelleans.

Marcionite cosmology, therefore, has a clear hierarchy: God the Father above all; Christ sent from Him; beneath them the lower principalities, powers, thrones, dominions, and angelic rulers; and the visible, fleshly, corruptible world fashioned from matter through deferred demiurgy. Matter itself is not a separate ruler or divine principle in this hierarchy. Matter ultimately originates from God the Father, but the present world-system is the result of matter being shaped and governed by lower powers.

This is why Marcionite salvation is not the glorification of the present world-order, but rescue from it. Christ comes from God the Father into the world governed by lower powers. He reveals the unknown Father, exposes the ignorance of the ‘god of this world’, defeats the dominion of sin, death, Satan, and the lower powers, and rescues souls from bondage. The Gospel is therefore not a law for improving the lower order, but a revelation from above that calls souls out of corruption and into the kingdom of God.

Thus, Marcionite cosmology is neither materialist nor Gnostic dualist. It is a hierarchical cosmology of God, Christ, souls, angelic powers, matter, and the physical world. Matter is real, but morally neutral. Matter ultimately comes from God the Father, not from the ‘god of this world.’ The world is real, but flawed and inferior. The body is real, but fleshly and corruptible. The lower powers are real, but subordinate. Satan is real, but defeated. God the Father alone is supreme, and Christ alone is the way of deliverance from the present evil age.

See also: Question 9.12, “Who is called the ‘god of this world’?”; Question 9.04, “What is the origin of matter?”; Question 9.16, “What form of the creation hymn does the Church receive from Colossians?”

questions.question: How does the Church understand Logos-mediated creation in relation to Marcionite deferred demiurgy?
questions.answer:
Creation through the Logos is the ancient Christian teaching that God the Father brought creation into being through his pre-existent Word, or Logos. Ancient Christian writers who taught some form of this doctrine included Justin Martyr, Theophilus of Antioch, Athenagoras of Athens, Irenaeus of Lyons, Tertullian, Origen, and Athanasius of Alexandria. They did not all possess identical Christologies, but they agreed that the Logos existed before the world and served as the divine agent, instrument, pattern, or mediator through whom creation was ordered.

Justin Martyr wrote that the Word was begotten before the works of creation and that God “created and arranged all things by Him” (Second Apology 6). Theophilus likewise declared that “God by His own Word and Wisdom made all things” (To Autolycus I.7), while Athenagoras described the Son as “the Logos of the Father,” after whose pattern and through whose agency all things were made (Plea for the Christians 10).

Irenaeus taught that God’s own Word was sufficient for “the formation of all things,” including the material world (Against Heresies II.2.5). Tertullian similarly distinguished the Father, “by whom” all things were made, from the Word, “through whom” they were made (Against Praxeas 21). Origen maintained that nothing was made apart from the Logos, although he explored distinctions between things made “through” him and things made “by” him (Commentary on John II). Athanasius later expressed the developed Nicene form of the doctrine by teaching that all things were created by the Father through the Son, while the Son himself was begotten rather than created (Statement of Faith 2).

Marcionite deferred demiurgy accepts the fundamental principle that creation proceeds from God the Father through the Logos, but it distinguishes between the direct creation of the heavenly order and the subsequent formation of the material world. The Logos directly brought forth the spiritual and invisible creation, including the thrones, dominions, principalities, and powers described in Colossians. These lower spiritual powers thereafter fashioned the visible and ordered world.

Matter itself should not therefore be regarded as inherently evil or as necessarily independent of God. On the Marcionite understanding presented here, the Logos likely brought matter into being as an unformed or elemental substance, while the lower spiritual order later arranged, shaped, and governed it as the physical cosmos. The distinction is between the creation of matter and spiritual being through the Logos and the secondary formation of matter into the ordered world by subordinate powers.

The difference may be represented as follows:

Ordinary Logos-Mediated Creation:

God the Father → the Logos → the entire visible and invisible creation

Marcionite Deferred Demiurgy:

God the Father → the Logos → matter and the heavenly spiritual powers → the lower powers fashion matter into the ordered visible world

Deferred demiurgy may therefore be understood as a stratified or extended form of Logos-mediated creation. God the Father remains the ultimate source of all existence, and the Logos remains the universal foundation through whom creation proceeds. Nevertheless, the Logos need not be regarded as the immediate artificer of every material form, creature, or structure within the physical cosmos.

This distinction permits the Church to affirm that “all things were made by him; and without him was not any thing made that was made,” while recognizing different degrees of causation. Nothing exists independently of the Logos, because matter itself, together with the lower powers that ordered it, ultimately derives from him. The Logos is consequently the ultimate cause and sovereign of the entire creation, while the immediate fashioning of the material cosmos is deferred to the spiritual powers that he brought into being.

The Dialogue of Megethius supports this by showing that matter cannot be treated as uncreated or co-eternal. Matter belongs to the created order, while the lower powers are responsible for the secondary fashioning of the corruptible world-order.

See also: Question 9.10, “What is deferred demiurgy?”; Question 9.16, “What form of the creation hymn does the Church receive from Colossians?”; Question 9.04, “What is the origin of matter?”

questions.question: Who is called the ‘god of this world’?
questions.answer:
In the traditional Apellean and Marcionite understanding, Satan, the ‘prince of this world’, is called the ‘god of this world’. The Lucanist school distinguished the lower just ruler from Satan, but likewise regarded him as a subordinate angelic power rather than a true or coequal god.

“In whom the god of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel of Christ, who is the image of God, should shine unto them.”

II Corinthians 4:4

In the traditional Apellean reading, which preserves Marcion’s original teaching, the phrase “god of this world” refers to the fallen angel known as Satan, the ‘prince of this world’, and the ruler of the present age. This title identifies him as a real lower power, principality, throne, or dominion, but not as a second co-eternal, co-equal god.

This distinction is essential. The “god of this world” is mockingly called “god” only in relation to this world, this age, and the unbelieving order he blinds and governs. He is not God in the true sense. He is not unbegotten. He is not equal to God the Father. He is a subordinate and begotten angelic power whose authority is limited, derivative, and temporary.

All Marcionite schools that speak of these lower powers understand them as angelic beings, not as rival deities. The Apelleans identify the “god of this world” with Satan, understood as a fallen angelic power. The Lucanists distinguish the “god of this world” from Satan, but still regard both as lower angelic powers beneath God. The Severians identify Satan with the “god of this world” and take a stricter view of his evil, yet even they do not make him an unbegotten or co-equal god. Across all positions, the dispute concerns how the lower angelic hierarchy is ordered, not whether any lower power rivals God the Father.

The Apelleans taught that this lower angelic power played a role in the fashioning of the physical world. Apelles described the ‘prince of this world’ as a fiery angel who attempted to imitate the higher realm but failed to reproduce its perfection. This explains why the physical world contains order, law, judgment, beauty, and structure, but also corruption, bondage, decay, and death. The world is not evil in itself, but it is inferior, mixed, and governed by a lower just angelic power that cannot reveal the alien mercy of God the Father.

Thus, when Paul says that the “god of this world” blinds the minds of unbelievers, he is not teaching dualism. He is describing the activity of a lower angelic power beneath God. Satan’s dominion operates through blindness, accusation, law, judgment, fear, false religion, worldly philosophy, and the systems of the present age. His influence permeates the values and assumptions of the unbelieving world, preventing men from beholding “the light of the glorious gospel of Christ, who is the image of God.”

Elsewhere, this same hostile dominion is described through related Pauline language. In Laodiceans, Satan is called “the prince of the power of the air,” a title that emphasizes his sway over the spiritual atmosphere of the present world. This title does not imply ultimate or unrestrained authority. It describes the scope of his permitted and temporary influence among those who remain outside the light of Christ.

Paul also speaks of the “princes of this world”:

“Howbeit we speak wisdom among them that are perfect: yet not the wisdom of this world, nor of the princes of this world, that come to nought… which none of the princes of this world knew: for had they known it, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory.”

I Corinthians 2:6–8

These “princes of this world” are not merely earthly rulers. They are lower angelic powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions operating behind the visible structures of worldly authority. Deceived by their own blindness, they failed to recognize Christ and became unwitting instruments of their own defeat. Their reign is temporary. They “come to nought.”

The Lucanist school developed this cosmology differently. Lucanists distinguished the “god of this world” from Satan. In their view, the “god of this world” was a just lower angelic power directly responsible for the fashioning of the physical world, while Satan was an evil angelic adversary. This created a threefold hierarchy: God the Father, above all; the just angelic ruler of the physical world beneath him; and Satan as a hostile and evil angelic power. Even here, however, the doctrine is not dualism, because neither the “god of this world” nor Satan is co-eternal or co-equal with God the Father.

The Severians later took a stricter ascetic position. Like the traditional Apelleans, they identified Satan with the “god of this world.” Unlike traditional Apelleans, however, they regarded the ‘prince of this world’ as evil rather than merely just, limited, and inferior. This led the Severians toward a more world-denying approach. Yet even the Severians did not make Satan equal to God the Father. He remained a fallen angelic power, not a rival god.

Therefore, when the Apostolicon declares that the “god of this world” blinds unbelievers, it must be understood within a hierarchy of lower angelic powers, not a system of rival gods. His dominion extends over the present age and the unbelieving world. Those who belong to Christ are delivered from his rule and brought into the light of the Gospel.

In the Evangelicon, Jesus declares:

“The prince of this world cometh, and hath nothing in me.”

Evangelicon 24:16

This statement reveals the singular hostility of the ruler of the present age. He approaches Christ, but he has no claim over him. Christ does not belong to the physical world’s bondage, law, judgment, or corruption. He comes from God the Father, and stands utterly apart from the lower angelic powers that govern this age.

Thus, in the traditional Apellean and Marcionite reading, the “god of this world” is Satan, the ‘prince of this world’, and the angelic ruler of the present age. In the Lucanist reading, the “god of this world” is distinguished from Satan, but remains a subordinate angelic power beneath God. In the Severian reading, Satan is again identified with this title, but viewed more severely as evil. None of these readings teaches that the lower power is the true God. None makes him eternal. None makes him equal to the Father. All understand him within the lower angelic order of powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions beneath God the Father, whose sovereignty alone is absolute.

questions.question: What is negative demiurgy?
questions.answer:
Negative demiurgy explains the ignorance of the lower ruler. The ‘god of this world’ began in ignorance of God the Father. He knew his own law, judgment, dominion, and visible realm, but he did not know the invisible Father whom Jesus came to reveal. This is why the Apostle says:

“Which none of the princes of this world knew: for had they known it, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory.”

I Corinthians 2:8.

Tertullian preserves the Marcionite use of this passage when he says that Marcion’s side argued from Paul that the “princes of this world” did not know the Lord of glory. Tertullian rejects the Marcionite conclusion, but his objection preserves the claim: the lower powers were ignorant before Christ’s revelation. He also says that the evil spirit in the Gospel knew Jesus as “the Holy One of God,” and that once the stronger One had come upon the strong man, the lower ruler “could not have been ignorant any longer.” (Tertullian, Against Marcion 5.6.)

This is exactly what the Evangelicon shows at the beginning of the Lord’s manifestation:
