“And in the synagogue there was a man, which had a spirit of an unclean devil, and cried out with a loud voice, Saying, let us alone; what have we to do with thee, thou Jesus? art thou come to destroy us? I know thee who thou art; the Holy One of God.”

Evangelicon 2:7-8.

By this time, the devils knew. The lower principalities, powers, thrones, and dominions had begun to perceive that Jesus was not beneath them, not sent by them, and not subject to their order. He came from above them. His descent revealed that their authority was not ultimate.

Ancient witnesses differ in describing the lower ruler’s response to this disclosure, but they preserve the central claim that God the Father was not known before Christ revealed Him. His ignorance concerned revelation and worship, not the ultimate source of being.

questions.question: How does the Church distinguish physical and visible creation from spiritual and invisible creation?
questions.answer:
The ancient heresiologists themselves preserve this distinction as part of their accusation against Marcion. Tertullian says that Marcionites divided “things visible and invisible,” while Jerome likewise reports that Marcion separated the creation of the physical, fleshly, and visible world from the spiritual, heavenly, and invisible realm.

Jerome preserves the same accusation in his commentary on Ephesians/Laodiceans. Commenting on Ephesians 3:9, he asks: “Where are Marcion, Valentinus and the other heretics who say that there is one creator of the visible world and another creator of the invisible?” This is one of the clearest hostile summaries of the Marcionite distinction between the visible and invisible orders.

This distinction is also grounded in the Apostle’s own language:

“While we look not at the things which are seen, but at the things which are not seen: for the things which are seen are temporal; but the things which are not seen are eternal.”

II Corinthians 4:18

The “things which are seen” are temporal, fleshly, and corruptible. The “things which are not seen” are eternal, heavenly, and spiritual. The lower visible order is therefore not identical with the higher invisible kingdom revealed by Christ.

However, this distinction does not mean that matter is eternal, evil in itself, or independent of God the Father. Ultimately, all creation ties back to the Father, for Paul says:

“But to us there is but one God, the Father, of whom are all things, and we in him; and one Lord Jesus Christ, by whom are all things, and we by him.”

I Corinthians 8:6

God the Father is the ultimate source of all things. He made matter itself, from which the physical, fleshly, and visible world was later fashioned, and he also made the ‘prince of this world’. The lower ruler is therefore not eternal, equal to God, or self-existing. He is a subordinate power who fashioned the physical, fleshly, and visible world from what ultimately proceeds from the Father.

Therefore, we confess a real distinction between the lower visible order and the higher invisible kingdom, without making matter a second eternal principle. The physical, fleshly, and visible world is temporal and subject to corruption. The spiritual, heavenly, and invisible world is eternal and belongs to God the Father, Christ, and the kingdom of God. All things ultimately proceed from the Father, but the lower world was fashioned through the ‘prince of this world’ and the powers beneath him.

This cosmology also explains the condition of souls. Souls are not products of the physical body. They preexist embodiment and come from God the Father. The body belongs to the visible and corruptible realm shaped through deferred demiurgy, but the soul belongs to the higher and invisible order. The pre-existant soul descends into embodiment, becomes subject to bondage under the elements of this world, and must be awakened and liberated by Christ.

The soul’s descend and salvation mirrors Christ’s own movement. Christ descends from above into the lower world, enters the realm of flesh and death, defeats the powers that govern it, and ascends again to the Father. So too the soul, once liberated by Christ, is not left in the lower order. It is rescued from bondage, drawn upward from the visible to the invisible, from the fleshly to the spiritual, from corruption to incorruption, from the present age into the kingdom of God.

This distinction between visible and invisible creation does not imply two eternal substances or two supreme gods. The Dialogue of Megethius rejects any doctrine that would make matter an uncreated rival beside God.

See also: Question 18.05, “Why is Marcionism not dualism?”; Question 9.04, “What is the origin of matter?”

questions.question: What are angels?
questions.answer:
Angels are spiritual beings belonging to the invisible order. They are begotten and subordinate powers, not gods, and none is co-eternal or co-equal with God the Father. The Apostle speaks of angels, archangels, principalities, powers, thrones, and dominions, showing that the spiritual world contains many ranks and authorities beneath God.

Some angels serve the purposes of God, minister to the faithful, and rejoice in the salvation of souls. Others are ignorant, rebellious, hostile, or bound to the government of this present world. Satan, demons, and the lower world-ruling powers are therefore angelic beings, but not all angels are evil, nor should every spiritual power be identified indiscriminately with Satan.

The Church worships no angel. However great a power may appear, it remains a creature beneath God the Father and subject to the Lord Jesus Christ. The Apostle’s declaration that the saints shall judge angels confirms that angelic powers are not sovereign over the children of God:

“Know ye not that we shall judge angels? how much more things that pertain to this life?”

I Corinthians 6:3

questions.question: What form of the creation hymn does the Church receive from Colossians?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church receives the creation hymn from Colossians in the following form:

“Who is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of every creature: For by Him were all things created that are in heaven and invisible, whether they be thrones, or dominions, or principalities, or powers: And He is before all things, and by Him all things consist.”

Colossians 1:15-17

This form is not a denial of creation through Christ. It is a confession of the proper order of creation. The hymn speaks of Christ as the image of the invisible God, the firstborn before every creature, and the one through whom the heavenly and invisible orders were established. The things named are not the earthly, physical, and visible fabric of this world, but the spiritual ranks above it: thrones, dominions, principalities, and powers.

This is why the hymn still allows for a true cosmological hierarchy. God the Father is the invisible and supreme God. Christ is the image of the invisible God and is before all things. Beneath Christ stand the heavenly and invisible powers. Beneath them stands the physical, fleshly, and visible world, fashioned within the lower order from matter and ruled by the ‘god of this world.’ The hymn therefore does not collapse all reality into one undifferentiated creation. It distinguishes the heavenly and invisible order from the earthly and visible order.

See also: Question 9.10, “What is deferred demiurgy?”; Question 9.12, “Who is called the ‘god of this world’?”

This comports with the Marcionite teaching of deferred demiurgy. God the Father is the source of the spiritual and invisible order revealed in Christ. The physical and visible world proceeds through subordinate powers and belongs to the lower cosmological order. Thus all things may still be ordered under Christ, without making the Father directly responsible for the fashioning of the fleshly and visible world.

The hostile witnesses provide evidence that this distinction was central to Marcionite interpretation. Tertullian says the Marcionites divided reality into visible and invisible classes, “to divide things into the two classes of visible and invisible, with two gods for their authors, and so to claim the invisible for their own, (the supreme) God” (Tertullian, Against Marcion I.16). Though Tertullian rejects the doctrine, his accusation supports the conclusion that Marcionite teaching separated the invisible things belonging to the supreme God from the visible things belonging to the lower world.

Tertullian also addresses this very passage in Colossians. He argues that Marcion removed certain constituent parts from the hymn, asking, “what fullness is that which is not composed of the constituents which Marcion has removed from it” (Tertullian, Against Marcion V.19). He then identifies those constituents as the things “created in Christ, whether in heaven or on earth,” the things “visible and invisible,” and the ranks of “thrones and dominions and principalities and powers” (Tertullian, Against Marcion V.19). Tertullian does not give a clean transcript of Marcion’s Apostolicon, nor does he tell us with precision which exact words stood in Marcion’s text and which did not. His testimony shows that this passage was disputed precisely because it bore upon the distinction between heaven and earth, invisible and visible, and the spiritual ranks of the higher order.

This is important for defending the reconstruction. Tertullian’s complaint should be read as polemical and collective, not as a word-for-word report of Marcion’s text. When he lists the “constituents” of the passage, he is naming the categories needed for his own argument against Marcion: heaven and earth, visible and invisible, thrones, dominions, principalities, and powers. But this does not prove that every item in Tertullian’s list was absent from Marcion’s Apostolicon. It may mean that Marcion’s text, or Marcionite interpretation of the text, excluded the earthly and visible categories while preserving the heavenly and invisible hierarchy.

The retained angelic-rank list also fits the Marcionite sense of the passage. Thrones, dominions, principalities, and powers are not ordinary earthly objects. They are precisely the kind of heavenly and supra-cosmic orders that belong naturally to the invisible hierarchy. Retaining them allows the hymn to confess Christ’s supremacy over the spiritual order without making Him the immediate fashioner of the physical, fleshly, and visible world.

Jerome witnesses to the same controversy in his Commentary on Laodiceans, the epistle commonly called Ephesians. Speaking against Marcion and Valentinus, he asks, “Ubi sunt Marcion et Valentinus, et omnes haeretici, qui alterum mundi, id est, visibilium, et alterum asserunt invisibilium conditorem?” (Jerome, Commentary on Laodiceans/Ephesians, on Ephesians 1:9). That is, Jerome accuses them of teaching one source of the world, that is, of visible things, and another of invisible things. Jerome writes as an opponent, but his testimony supports the same distinction: Marcionite exegesis separated the heavenly, spiritual, and invisible creation from the earthly, physical, and visible world.

The reconstruction is therefore not arbitrary. The received text says, “in heaven and in earth, visible and invisible.” The Marcionite form preserves the conceptual structure, but gives it its proper theological sense: “in heaven and invisible.” It removes exactly the two categories the hostile witnesses say Marcionite theology separated from the supreme God: earth and visible. The result is shorter, coherent, and theologically intelligible.

Colossians 1:17 also supports this reading: “And He is before all things, and by Him all things consist.” This does not require that Christ directly fashioned the physical world. The phrase “all things” is governed by the immediate context: the heavenly and invisible orders just named, including thrones, dominions, principalities, and powers. Christ is before all these things, and by Him these things consist. The verse therefore confesses Christ’s priority, supremacy, and sustaining power over the invisible hierarchy, while remaining compatible with deferred demiurgy.

This reconstruction also avoids the opposite error of over-editing. If Marcion had simply removed the entire hymn, Tertullian’s detailed engagement with its categories would be harder to explain. Tertullian does not merely say that Marcion deleted the passage. He argues that Marcion removed or emptied those parts which, in Tertullian’s reading, would make the hymn support the direct fashioning of all visible and earthly things by the supreme God. That fits a partial Marcionite form better than a total omission.

Therefore, our form of Colossians 1:15-17 does not erase cosmology. It clarifies it. Christ is confessed as preeminent over every heavenly and invisible power, while the physical and visible world remains the domain of matter, flesh, and the lower ruler. The hymn is therefore a confession of Christ’s supremacy within a real hierarchy of being, not a confession that the true God directly fashioned the lower world.

For this reason, the reconstruction is not proven word-for-word by the surviving hostile witnesses, but it is disciplined, cautious, and patristically defensible. It preserves Colossians 1:15 and 1:17, which Tertullian’s argument presupposes, and it narrows Colossians 1:16 at precisely the point where Marcionite cosmology required distinction: heaven from earth, invisible from visible, and spiritual hierarchy from the physical world.

See also: Question 9.14, “How does the Church distinguish physical and visible creation from spiritual and invisible creation?”; Question 9.17, “What is the locus classicus in Laodiceans?”

questions.question: What is the locus classicus in Laodiceans?
questions.answer:
“And to make all men see what is the fellowship of the mystery, which from the beginning of the world hath been hid in God, who created all things by Jesus Christ.”

Laodiceans 3:9

Tertullian’s well-known complaint about Laodiceans, commonly called Ephesians, turns on a remarkably small textual point. He alleges that Marcion’s text lacked the preposition “in” in the phrase “hid in God.” He then argues that this omission forces the reader to understand the clause as though the mystery had been hidden “from” the God who created all things. But that is a polemical amplification, not a secure textual demonstration of an intentional Marcionite editorial program.

The absence of a short particle like ἐν is exactly the sort of mechanical omission scribes routinely introduce, especially in densely copied theological prose, without any doctrinal motive. Text-critically, the “missing ἐν” reading is also not uniquely Marcionite. The broader manuscript tradition contains witnesses that likewise omit ἐν at Ephesians 3:9, showing that this variant can arise and circulate independently of Marcionite transmission. That alone substantially weakens the inference: “Marcion deleted it, therefore Marcion intended to teach dualism.”

Grammatically, Tertullian’s theological conclusion is larger than the textual difference he claims. In Greek, removing ἐν typically leaves a dative construction, τῷ θεῷ, that does not naturally and automatically yield “hidden from God” without further interpretive pressure. Tertullian’s “from God” construal works more cleanly in Latin rhetoric than it does as a strict inference from the underlying Greek syntax. This suggests that we are seeing a controversial reading strategy, an adversary leaning hard on an awkward or ambiguous line, rather than reliable evidence that Marcion deliberately recast the verse to teach a metaphysical dualism.

The theological issue must also be stated carefully. Marcionite Christianity does teach deferred demiurgy: the physical world was made mediately through lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions beneath God the Father. In the traditional Apellean reading, the lower maker of the physical world is the being called in the Apostolicon “the god of this world” and called in the Evangelicon “the prince of this world.” But this is not dualism in the classical sense. The lower maker is not a second co-eternal, co-equal god. He is subordinate, begotten, limited, and derivative.

Therefore, even if Tertullian is accurately reporting a Marcionite textual variant, the conclusion he draws is still too large. The variant would not prove that Marcionite Christianity taught two equal gods, nor would it prove that Marcion deliberately altered Paul to establish a dualist metaphysics. At most, it would show that Tertullian found a textual ambiguity useful for arguing that Marcion separated God the Father from the maker of the physical world.

Nor does this verse erase the distinction between Apellean and Lucanist readings. Traditional Apelleans preserved the original teaching of Marcion by identifying the lower maker of the physical world with Satan, while typically treating Yahweh as a false and fabricated national deity rather than automatically identifying him with the “god of this world.” Some later Lucanists, by contrast, did identify or closely associate the ‘god of the Jews’ with the just ‘god of this world,’ the lower maker and governor of the physical world. But even in that Lucanist framework, the ‘god of this world’ remains subordinate to God the Father and is not a second eternal principle.

Finally, if this verse truly functioned as a decisive locus classicus for proving “Marcionite dualism,” it is notable that Epiphanius does not make it a centerpiece in his anti-Marcionite cataloguing in the way one might expect from an author eager to stockpile prooftexts and textual “smoking guns.” Epiphanius often pounces on what he takes to be distinctive Marcionite corruptions. His failure to spotlight this specific verse, or to underline the alleged prepositional variant, is at least consistent with the judgment that Laodiceans 3:9 was not a stable, widely recognized Marcionite signature, but a minor textual fluctuation inflated by later polemic.

For these reasons, Laodiceans 3:9 should not be pressed into service as evidence that Marcion deliberately edited Paul to promote crude dualism, nor should it be treated as a textual foundation for the claim that Marcionite Christianity is reducible to two rival gods. The Marcionite doctrine is subtler: God the Father is supreme; the physical world is made mediately through lower powers; the lower maker of the physical world is subordinate and derivative; and salvation is the revelation of the Father through Christ, who rescues souls from bondage to the present world-order.

At most, Tertullian’s argument shows how anti-Marcionite writers tried to frame Marcion’s text. A small, readily explicable scribal omission was leveraged into a sweeping theological accusation that the underlying textual evidence does not compel. Laodiceans 3:9 may be important for later anti-Marcionite controversy, but it should not be allowed to override the more precise Marcionite confession: not dualism, not two coequal gods, but deferred demiurgy beneath the sovereignty of God the Father.

questions.question: What are the three heavens?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians confess a threefold division of heaven: the visible heavens, the second heaven of the lower powers, and the third heaven, which is Paradise and the Kingdom of God.

The first heaven is the visible heaven: the sky, firmament, stars, and ordered heavens seen by bodily eyes. This is the heaven of the visible cosmos, bound to the material and fleshly order of this world. Paul distinguishes the earthly and heavenly orders when he writes:

“There are also celestial bodies, and bodies terrestrial: but the glory of the celestial is one, and the glory of the terrestrial is another.”

I Corinthians 15:40

And again:

“Now this I say, brethren, that flesh and blood cannot inherit the kingdom of God; neither doth corruption inherit incorruption.”

I Corinthians 15:50

The second heaven is the higher cosmic realm of the ‘god of this world’ and the princes, powers, thrones, dominions, and rulers who govern the present age. Paul speaks of this lower ruler when he writes:

“In whom the god of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel of Christ, who is the image of God, should shine unto them.”

II Corinthians 4:4

Paul also speaks of heavenly powers and rulers:

“To the intent that now unto the principalities and powers in heavenly places might be known by the church the manifold wisdom of God,”

Laodiceans 3:10

And again:

“For we wrestle not against flesh and blood, but against principalities, against powers, against the rulers of the darkness of this age, against spiritual wickedness in heavenly places.”

Laodiceans 6:12

This second heaven is the realm of cosmic government beneath God the Father. It is the dwelling-place of the lower powers who rule the present world-order. It is also the realm where pre-embodied souls dwelt before their descent into flesh, embodiment, trial, and eventual salvation.

The ancient heresiologists preserve a related teaching from Apelles, the disciple of Marcion. Pseudo-Tertullian says that Apelles taught that the lower world was formed “in imitation of a superior world,” but not with the same perfection as that higher realm. This supports the Marcionite understanding that the ‘god of this world’ did not create the visible order as an original kingdom of his own, but fashioned it as a lower, defective imitation of the higher cosmic pattern.

The third heaven is the heaven of God the Father: Paradise, the Kingdom of God, and the realm revealed by Jesus Christ. This is the true heavenly kingdom beyond the dominion of the ‘god of this world.’ Paul writes:

“I knew a man in Christ above fourteen years ago, whether in the body, I cannot tell; or whether out of the body, I cannot tell: God knoweth; such an one caught up to the third heaven. And I knew such a man, whether in the body, or out of the body, I cannot tell: God knoweth; How that he was caught up into paradise, and heard unspeakable words, which it is not lawful for a man to utter.”

II Corinthians 12:2–4

For Marcionite Christians, this passage is central. Paul was swept into the third heaven, into Paradise itself, and there received the direct revelation of Christ for the Gospel. This agrees with Paul’s own declaration:

“But I certify you, brethren, that the gospel which was preached of me is not after man. For I neither received it of man, neither was I taught it, but by the revelation of Jesus Christ.”

Galatians 1:11–12

The ancient heresiologists also show that Marcionite and Marcion-adjacent schools distinguished between the highest God, the lower powers, and the fashioned world. Tertullian, while arguing against Marcion, connects the dispute over Paul’s ascent with Paradise and the question of whether the higher God has a Paradise of his own. Rhodo, preserved by Eusebius, also reports that Marcionite schools debated one, two, and three principles or natures, showing that these heavenly distinctions belonged to the wider Marcionite theological world.

Therefore, when we speak of three heavens, we do not mean three equal realms or three gods. We mean three ordered levels of reality:

The first visible heaven of the material and fleshly world;

The second heaven of the ‘god of this world’, the princes, powers, and pre-embodied souls, after which the visible world was defectively patterned;

The third heaven, Paradise, and the Kingdom of God, where God the Father, Christ, and the souls that have obtained salvation dwell.

questions.question: Why do beauty, love, and goodness exist in this world?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church does not hate the world, the flesh, or created beauty. The world is flawed, corruptible, and inferior to what is above, yet it still contains real beauty, love, and goodness because it was fashioned as an imitation of a higher order.

Apelles, the disciple of Marcion, taught that the lower world was formed in imitation of a superior world, but not with the same perfection as that higher realm. The Church receives this Apellean witness as valuable testimony to the wider Marcionite cosmology: this world is not the Kingdom of God, but neither is it pure evil or hateful in itself.

The visible world bears fractured reflections of the second heaven, and the second heaven itself is a lower reflection of the third heaven, Paradise and the Kingdom of God. Earthly beauty, familial love, friendship, mercy, art, music, order, and the goodness found in creatures are therefore not to be despised. They are signs, shadows, and fragments of what is greater.

The faithful may admire beauty, love, and goodness in this world with gratitude and discernment, while remembering that the present world-order is not the Kingdom of God. We do not worship the world, the body, or fleshly beauty, nor do we mistake a fragment for the fullness. We receive every good thing as a faint witness to the greater incorruptible good that is to come.

Because matter is not evil in itself, the goods of this world are not illusions. They are real but partial, unstable, and inferior reflections of the higher order. The Dialogue of Megethius therefore helps the Church admire created beauty without worshipping the world and reject world-hatred without denying corruption.

See also: Question 9.09, “What is the difference between matter, the world, and the world-order, and are they evil?”; Question 9.10, “What is deferred demiurgy?”; Question 9.18, “What are the three heavens?”; Question 10.09, “Does the Marcionite Church believe the body is evil?”

questions.question: How does the Church explain natural evil?
questions.answer:
Natural evil refers to suffering that occurs independently of direct human action. Earthquakes, droughts, famines, congenital diseases, plagues, and natural disasters fall into this category. Unlike moral evil, which is rooted in human choice and disobedience, natural evil appears indiscriminate and morally neutral, yet it inflicts real pain and hardship upon humanity.

The Dialogue of Megethius helps the Church avoid two false answers: that evil comes from God the Father directly, or that evil comes from matter as though matter were itself wicked. Natural evil belongs to the instability, corruption, and lower ordering of the present world, not to the Father’s perfect will or to matter as an evil substance.

Many have viewed natural evil as a serious objection to belief in a good and sovereign God. They ask: if it would be wrong for a person to allow such suffering, why is it not wrong for God? Does a benevolent God permit devastation that no decent human being would ever cause?

Marcionite Christianity answers this question by beginning with the true order of reality. There is God the Father, revealed by Jesus Christ and proclaimed by the Apostle Paul. Beneath him are lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions. The physical world was not made directly by God the Father in the fullness of his perfection. It was made mediately through a lower, imperfect being. This is the doctrine of deferred demiurgy.

According to this doctrine, the maker of the physical world is not a second co-eternal, co-equal god. He is a subordinate lower power. Traditional Apelleans, preserving the original teaching of Marcion, identified this lower maker of the physical world with the being called in the Apostolicon “the god of this world” and called in the Evangelicon “the prince of this world.” Apelles described this lower maker as a fiery angel who attempted to imitate the higher realm but failed to reproduce its perfection. The result is the physical world we inhabit: ordered but unstable, beautiful but corruptible, lawful but violent, capable of life yet always shadowed by decay and death.

Natural evil, then, is not a defect in God the Father. It is a defect in the lower physical order. This world was made imperfectly because it was made by a lower imperfect being. Its earthquakes, diseases, famines, and disasters belong to the instability of a creation that reflects the limitations of its maker. The physical world is not evil in itself, but it is inferior, mixed, fragile, and subject to corruption.

This is why Marcionite Christianity does not answer the problem of natural evil by making God the Father the direct author of every disaster. The Father revealed by Christ is not the author of disease, catastrophe, decay, and death. These belong immediately to the lower order of the physical world, governed by lesser powers. Yet neither does Marcionite Christianity teach that the maker of the physical world is equal to God. The lower maker is subordinate, limited, and derivative. His world exists beneath the sovereignty of God the Father.

This does not mean that God the Father is absent from the question of natural evil. All being ultimately depends upon God the Father, even when the physical world is made mediately through lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions. God permits the lower physical order to continue for a time, not because it is perfect, but because he is using even this corruptible world as the arena in which the Gospel is preached, souls are rescued, judgment is revealed, mercy is shown, and the children of God are called out of bondage into the liberty of Christ.

Why, then, does God the Father keep the physical world this way?

He permits it because the present age is not the final kingdom. God the Father has not chosen to perfect the lower creation by force from within its own system. Instead, he has revealed a higher salvation through Jesus Christ. He allows the present world to continue for a time so that the Gospel may be preached, so that souls may be rescued from bondage, and so that the children of God may be called out from the dominion of the lower powers into the liberty of Christ.

The Apostle Paul gives voice to this truth:

“For the creature was made subject to vanity, not willingly, but by reason of him who hath subjected the same in hope. Because the creature itself also shall be delivered from the bondage of corruption into the glorious liberty of the children of God. For we know that the whole creation groaneth and travaileth in pain together until now.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 7:20–22

This passage is central to the Marcionite answer. Creation is subject to vanity. It is in bondage to corruption. It groans and travails in pain. That is exactly what we see in the natural order. The world is not already the perfected kingdom of God. It is a lower, suffering, corruptible order awaiting deliverance.

Natural evil, therefore, is both a symptom and a revelation. It reveals the inferiority of the physical world and the insufficiency of the lower maker. It shows that the present age cannot save itself. It exposes the limits of law, nature, flesh, and worldly wisdom. The disasters of this world are not evidence against the goodness of God the Father. They are evidence that this physical order is not his final and perfect kingdom.

Natural evil may also function, in part, as judgment, chastening, exposure, or summons to repentance. This does not mean that every disaster should be simplistically assigned to a particular sin, nor that every sufferer is more guilty than another. Rather, it means that suffering within the lower physical order reveals the condition of a world estranged from its true hope. It exposes the vanity of the present age and reminds mankind that life in this world is fragile, temporary, and unable to save itself.

At the same time, natural evil also becomes a stage upon which the depth of human character is revealed. In the wake of disaster, we often witness courage, compassion, sacrifice, mercy, and love. We also see cowardice, cruelty, exploitation, and indifference. Natural tragedy exposes not only the fragility of the world, but also the moral condition of mankind.

Morally accountable humanity stands under the shadow of sin:

“For all have sinned, and come short of the glory of God.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 3:23

That any of us are granted life, mercy, rescue, and the promise of eternal inheritance is not something owed to us by the physical world. It is the gift of God the Father through Jesus Christ. The present world gives suffering, decay, and death. Christ gives life, freedom, and incorruption.

To those who demand that God justify himself before man, Marcionite Christianity gives a sober reply: God does not answer to man. Man answers to God.

“So then every one of us shall give account of himself to God.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 12:12

It is wrong for human beings to take life because we are not its authors. We are creatures, not sovereigns. But all life ultimately belongs to God the Father. Even where death comes through the instability of the lower physical order, man still gives account to God, not God to man. The divine prerogative belongs to God alone, while human beings are judged by the law of love, mercy, and righteousness revealed through Christ.

The existence of natural evil therefore does not place God the Father in the dock of human judgment. Rather, it reminds us that we are mortal creatures living in a lower and corruptible order. We are not the authors of life. We are not the masters of creation. We are those in need of rescue.

Ultimately, the only lasting hope in a world marked by natural suffering is Jesus Christ. He does not promise that his followers will be untouched by the hardships of this life. The children of God still live for now within the lower physical order, and therefore they still encounter sickness, loss, disaster, and death. But Christ offers something greater than temporary exemption from suffering. He offers deliverance from the whole dominion of corruption.

In the Gospel, God the Father does not merely explain natural evil. He overcomes it. He sends Christ into the world governed by lower powers, and Christ conquers without belonging to it. The prince of this world has nothing in him. The lower powers cannot claim him. Death cannot hold him. Through him, those who believe are rescued from the present evil age and promised a kingdom beyond decay.

In that coming reality, moral and natural evil alike will be no more. The bondage of corruption will end. The groaning creation will be delivered. The children of God will be transformed into the image of Christ, free from sin, sorrow, death, and the tyranny of the lower powers.

Until then, natural evil does not prove divine apathy. It reveals the condition of a lower world made imperfectly by a lower imperfect being. It reveals a creation still groaning for redemption. It exposes the vanity of the present age, summons mankind to repentance, and points beyond itself to the mercy of God the Father, who is already at work through the Gospel of Jesus Christ, calling his children out of corruption and into eternal life.

See also: Question 11.05, “How does the Church explain moral evil?”; Question 12.06, “What is annihilationism?”; Question 12.07, “What is the eternal sin?”

questions.question: Why does God the Father permit the present world to continue?
questions.answer:
God the Father permits the present world to continue for a time so that the Gospel may be preached, souls may repent and be rescued from bondage, and the children of God may be gathered into the liberty of Christ.

This permission does not signify approval of evil or suffering. The present age is not the final Kingdom of God, but the temporary arena in which mercy is revealed, judgment is exposed, repentance is summoned, and redemption is offered.

Therefore, the evil, corruption, decay, violence, and instability of the present world should not be attributed directly to God the Father. The world is imperfect because it was made by a lower imperfect power. It is ordered, but unstable; beautiful, but corruptible; capable of life, but subject to death. The physical world is not evil in itself, but it is inferior, mixed, and governed by lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions beneath God.

This lower maker of the physical world is not a second co-eternal, co-equal god. He is subordinate, limited, and derivative. Traditional Apelleans, preserving the original teaching of Marcion, identified him with the being called in the Apostolicon “the god of this world” and called in the Evangelicon “the prince of this world.” He is a lower power, not the true God. His making of the physical world explains why the present order contains both structure and suffering, law and death, beauty and bondage.

Why, then, does God allow this world with evil to continue?

He allows it because the present age is not the final kingdom. God the Father has not chosen to perfect the lower creation by force from within its own system. Instead, he has revealed a higher salvation through Jesus Christ. He permits the present world to continue for a time so that the Gospel may be preached, so that souls may be rescued from bondage, and so that the children of God may be called out from the dominion of the lower powers into the liberty of Christ.

This does not mean that God delights in evil or suffering. It means that he is using even this corruptible world as the arena in which mercy is revealed, judgment is exposed, repentance is summoned, and redemption is offered. The evil of this world is not the final word. It is the condition from which Christ rescues his own.

What if God had made souls incapable of evil? A world without choice might contain no rebellion, but it would also lack genuine love, worship, obedience, mercy, repentance, and faith. Forced obedience is not righteousness. It is compulsion. The true God revealed through Jesus Christ seeks children, not machines. He desires willing hearts, not coerced behavior.

Freedom entails risk, but it also makes possible the glory of redemption. The present world reveals what law, flesh, nature, and lower powers can and cannot do. It exposes the vanity of the present age. It reveals mankind’s need for rescue. And it provides the arena in which the mercy of God the Father is revealed through Jesus Christ.

Thus, Marcionite Christians do not believe that this present world is the final expression of God’s goodness. It is not the perfected kingdom. It is a lower, corruptible order awaiting deliverance. The Apostle Paul writes:

“For I reckon that the sufferings of this present time are not worthy to be compared with the glory which shall be revealed in us.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 7:18

This is the promise that sustains us: not that we will be spared all pain in the present age, but that the suffering of this lower world is not the end of the story. God the Father has revealed in Christ a kingdom beyond corruption, beyond death, beyond the lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions that govern this age.

In that kingdom, evil will not merely be restrained. It will be overcome. The children of God will be delivered from the bondage of corruption and transformed into the image of Christ. The question, then, is not why this lower world is already perfect. It is not. The question is why God allows it to continue for a time. Marcionite Christianity answers: so that Christ may rescue his own from the present evil age and bring them into eternal life through God the Father.

questions.question: How does the Church understand Satan?
questions.answer:
Satan is a fallen angel, a hostile lower power, and the adversary of mankind. He is not a second co-eternal, co-equal god. He is not equal to God the Father. He belongs among the lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions that govern and afflict the present age.

Jesus makes it unmistakably clear that Satan is a fallen angel:

“And he said unto them, I beheld Satan as lightning fallen from heaven.”

Evangelicon 9:18

This declaration follows the return of the seventy disciples, who rejoiced that even the demons submitted to them in Jesus’ name. But Jesus reminded them that Satan had already been cast down. His fall was not merely a future event, but a present reality. He had no power over Christ, and therefore no ultimate power over those sent out in Christ’s authority.

Jesus assured his disciples that he had granted them authority over the hostile powers of this age:

“Behold, I give unto you authority to tread on serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy: and nothing shall by any means hurt you.”

Evangelicon 9:19

Yet Jesus also made clear that spiritual authority, while extraordinary, is not the highest cause for joy. Far greater is the assurance of salvation, that our names are written in the heavens:

“Notwithstanding, in this rejoice not, that the spirits are subject unto you; but rather rejoice, because your names are written in the heavens.”

Evangelicon 9:20

In the traditional Apellean reading, which preserves the original teaching of Marcion, Satan is the same lower power called in the Apostolicon “the god of this world” and called in the Evangelicon “the prince of this world.” He is the lower maker of the physical world, but he is not the true God. He is begotten, subordinate, limited, and derivative. Apelles described this lower maker as a fiery angel who attempted to imitate the higher realm but failed to reproduce its perfection. This is why the physical world is ordered yet unstable, beautiful yet corruptible, capable of life yet subject to decay and death.

Traditional Apelleans, like Marcion, did not regard Satan as a second divine principle equal to God. Nor did they treat the physical world as evil in itself. Rather, they understood Satan as a lower just power: inferior, limited, and unable to reveal the alien mercy of God the Father. He could impose law, judgment, and accusation, but he could not reveal grace.

The Lucanists understood Satan somewhat differently. They distinguished Satan from the “god of this world.” In their threefold structure, God the Father, stands above all; the just “god of this world” is the maker of the physical world beneath him; and Satan is an evil third power. This Lucanist distinction does not create dualism, because Satan still remains a lower power, not a co-eternal or co-equal god.

The Severians took a stricter ascetic view. Like the traditional Apelleans, they identified Satan with the “god of this world” and the “prince of this world.” Unlike the traditional Apelleans, however, they regarded this lower maker of the physical world as evil rather than merely just, limited, and inferior. This led them toward a more world-denying and ascetic posture. Yet even the Severians did not make Satan equal to God the Father.

Until Christ fully overthrows the powers of darkness, believers remain engaged in spiritual struggle. The Apostle Paul reminds us that our conflict is not against flesh and blood, but against lower spiritual powers:

“Finally, my brethren, be strong in the Lord, and in the power of his might. Put on the whole armour of God, that ye may be able to stand against the wiles of the devil. For we wrestle not against flesh and blood, but against principalities, against powers, against the rulers of the darkness of this world, against spiritual wickedness in high places. Wherefore take unto you the whole armour of God, that ye may be able to withstand in the evil day, and having done all, to stand. Stand therefore, having your loins girt about with truth, and having on the breastplate of righteousness; And your feet shod with the preparation of the gospel of peace; Above all, taking the shield of faith, wherewith ye shall be able to quench all the fiery darts of the wicked. And take the helmet of salvation, and the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God: Praying always with all prayer and supplication in the Spirit, and watching thereunto with all perseverance and supplication for all saints.”

Laodiceans 6:10–18

This passage places Satan within the broader hierarchy of hostile lower powers, principalities, and rulers of the darkness of this world. He acts through accusation, blindness, fear, false religion, worldly systems, and spiritual deception. But his power is not absolute. He is already fallen, already judged, and already defeated in Christ.

Tertullian, though writing against Marcion, still preserves an important admission. He calls Satan “the angel,” “which both we and Marcion know him to be,” thereby conceding that Marcion also recognized Satan as a fallen angel. This is consistent with the Marcionite confession: Satan is real, hostile, and powerful within the present age, but he is not God. He is a fallen lower power beneath God the Father.

Christ has already triumphed over Satan’s dominion. Through Christ, believers are equipped not only to resist evil but to stand firm in the light of divine truth, clothed in righteousness and strengthened by the hope of eternal life. Satan may blind the unbelieving world, but he has no claim over Christ, and he cannot overcome those whose names are written in the heavens.

questions.question: How does the Church understand pagan deities?
questions.answer: Marcionites hold that the deities of the pagans were not all mere inventions. Some were real lesser spirits—angels or devils—who held authority in this present world, though identifying which ones is difficult. Discernment rests on their fruits, the bondage their cults produced, and how they shrink before the Gospel. With the advent of Christ their sway was unmasked and greatly diminished. Similar views appear in other early Christian thinkers who equated pagan deities with hostile spirits, including Justin Martyr (1 Apol. 5; 2 Apol. 5), Athenagoras (Plea 24), Theophilus of Antioch (Ad Autol. II.31), Tertullian (Apol. 22; De Idol.), Clement of Alexandria (Protr. 2), Origen (C. Cels. V.4), Lactantius (Div. Inst. II), and Augustine (City of God VIII–IX).

questions.question: Who is the ‘god of the Jews’?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christianity teaches deferred demiurgy: the physical world was fashioned mediately through lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions beneath God the Father. The name Yahweh is a modern scholarly vocalization of the Hebrew divine name YHWH. Ancient Marcionites, as quoted by heresiologists, usually speak instead of the ‘god of the Jews.’

In the traditional Apellean reading, which preserves the original teaching of Marcion, the lower fashioner of the physical world is the being called by the Apostle the ‘god of this world.’ He is subordinate, begotten, limited, and derivative, not a second eternal God. Apelles described him as an angelic power who attempted to imitate the higher realm but failed to reproduce its perfection. This explains why the physical world is ordered yet unstable, beautiful yet corruptible, lawful yet violent, capable of life yet subject to decay and death.

The Apelleans typically viewed the god of the Jews, Yahweh, as a false and fabricated deity, comparable to other mythological and national gods invented throughout human history, such as Zeus, Thor, Baal, or Marduk. On this reading, the god of the Jews is not the lower fashioner of the physical world himself, but a counterfeit religious identity projected onto the lower order by human tradition, priestcraft, fear, and false revelation. The deity portrayed in the Hebrew Bible is therefore not God the Father, not the source of the Gospel, and not worthy of Christian worship.

This Apellean attitude is clearest in the Syllogisms of Apelles. Ancient testimony says Apelles composed the Syllogisms to expose the Hebrew writings as inconsistent, false, self-contradictory, and mythical. Eusebius preserves Rhodo’s report that Apelles said the prophecies “refuted themselves, because they have said nothing true; for they are inconsistent, and false, and self-contradictory,” and further says Apelles wrote many things against the law of Moses, being zealous for its “refutation and overthrow” (Eusebius, Ecclesiastical History 5.13). Origen likewise says Apelles treated the writings of the Jews as fables (Origen, Against Celsus 5.54).

The Syllogisms were therefore not merely a criticism of isolated passages. They were a direct refutation of the existence and authority of the god presented in the Hebrew myths. The Apellean position was that the Hebrew Bible did not reveal a true lower god worthy of worship, but a fabricated and contradictory religious fiction.

This means that, in the Apellean stream, the ‘god of this world’ and the god of the Jews should not automatically be collapsed into one figure. The ‘god of this world’ is the lower world-fashioning angel, identified with Satan, while the god of the Jews is typically treated as a false national deity and fabricated claimant to divine authority. The Hebrew Bible is rejected not because it reveals a true rival god, but because it falsely attributes divine authority to a counterfeit deity and binds its readers to law, violence, jealousy, tribal election, and failed prophecy.

A minority position among later Lucanists, however, described the ‘god of this world’ more directly as the ‘god of the Jews.’ This was not the universal Marcionite position, nor the traditional Apellean position. It appears instead as a later Lucanist tendency, likely among communities that may not have been well catechized in the older distinction between the lower world-fashioning power and the fabricated deity of the Hebrew Bible.

The clearest evidence for this appears in the Dialogue of Adamantius. In that work, Megethius, the Marcionite spokesman whom we identify with the Lucanist stream, distinguishes God the Father, the ‘god of this world’, and Satan. He explicitly identifies the lower just ruler with the god of the Jews, saying: “This is one, who handed down the Law, the God of the Jews,” and again, “The God of the Jews is one, [the demiurge], but the Other is not his son” (Dialogue of Adamantius 1.9-10).

This fits the Lucanist pattern. The Lucanists distinguished three principles: good, just, and evil. God the Father is the good God above all. The ‘god of this world’ is the just lower ruler beneath Him. Satan is the evil third power. Because the Lucanists distinguished the just lower ruler from Satan, some later Lucanists could identify the just lower ruler with the god of the Jews, the lawgiver and governor of the lower order, while still treating Satan as a separate evil adversary.

Epiphanius preserves similar hostile testimony. In the Panarion, he reports that Marcionites fasted on the Sabbath because it was the rest day of “the god of the Jews who made the world.” Elsewhere, he describes Marcionite teaching in terms of the prophets speaking by inspiration of the archon who fashioned the world, and refers to the god of the Jews as the lower world-fashioning power. These reports show that some heresiological witnesses associated Marcionite doctrine with the formula: god of the Jews equals the lower world-ruler (Epiphanius, Panarion 42).

Even these witnesses must be read carefully. They are hostile reports, not neutral Marcionite catechisms. They often compress different Marcionite schools into one polemical summary. The existence of these reports provides evidence that some Marcionites, especially later Lucanists, identified or closely associated the god of the Jews with the just ‘god of this world.’ It does not establish that this was the universal Marcionite doctrine.

The difference between the Apellean and Lucanist readings is therefore clear. In the Apellean framework, the lower world-fashioning angel is identified with Satan, while the god of the Jews is more typically regarded as a false and fabricated deity of the Hebrew Bible. In the Lucanist framework, the just ‘god of this world’ is distinguished from Satan, and some later Lucanists identified that just lower ruler with the god of the Jews.

Thus, Marcionite Christians reject both errors: we do not worship the god of the Jews as God, and we do not elevate him into a second eternal principle. The god of the Jews is not God the Father. He is not equal to God. He is not the source of the Gospel. In the traditional Apellean view, he is a false and fabricated deity of the Hebrew Bible, refuted by Apelles’ Syllogisms as part of the mythology of the Jews. In the later minority Lucanist view, he was identified with the just ‘god of this world’, the lower ruler of the visible order. But neither view makes him the true God, and neither view compromises the Marcionite confession that God the Father, revealed by Jesus Christ, alone is supreme.

questions.question: How does the Church regard astrology?
questions.answer:
The Church distinguishes the recognition of heavenly signs from divination, fatalism, and the worship of celestial powers. The Marcionite Church affirms that the heavens can bear real signs permitted by God, and that these signs can be read as public testimony to divine action in history and as warnings of coming judgment. As the Evangelicon teaches: “And there shall be signs in sun, and moon, and stars; and upon the earth distress of nations, with perplexity; the sea and waves roaring;” (Evangelicon 19:22).

At the same time, we explicitly couch any astrological sympathies with Paul’s warnings against returning to religious calendarism and the “elements” (στοιχεῖα) of the world. The Apostle cautions against being brought back under ritualized observances that enslave rather than liberate: “Ye observe days, and months, and times, and years” (Galatians 4:10), and he warns against captivity through merely human systems and elemental powers: “Beware lest any man spoil you through philosophy and vain deceit, after the tradition of men, after the rudiments of the world, and not after Christ” (Colossians 2:8). For the Marcionite Christian, this means: signs are not fate, and the heavens are never a substitute providence.

This distinction also clarifies the famous polemical charge. The oft-cited claim that “the Marcionites are very strongly addicted to astrology” comes from Tertullian in Against Marcion 1.18, where it functions as a taunt rather than a documented Marcionite rule of faith or liturgical directive. There is no surviving Marcionite creed, canon rule, or liturgical instruction that commands believers to practice horoscope-casting or astral divination.

A more plausible reason the slur circulated is calendrical. Marcionite Christians historically distinguished themselves in how they fixed feast days: rather than anchoring Easter to the Jewish Passover (as some early communities did) or relying primarily on later ecclesiastical convention, Marcionite observance has often emphasized historically remembered dates and widely discussed celestial phenomena—something opponents could easily (and unfairly) caricature as “astrology.”

That is how the Marcionite Church treats the heavens in practice: not to foretell fortunes, but to remember and to confess. For example, in Marcionite Christian practice, Good Friday is fixed as the first Friday in April, historically anchored (in Marcionite tradition) to Friday, April 3, 33 C.E., and associated with a lunar eclipse tradition. This is commemoration, not divination: we do not consult the heavens to predict personal outcomes, but to anchor the Lord’s passion to a remembered historical horizon.

Further, later Christian writers explicitly appealed to non-Christian chroniclers when discussing the crucifixion darkness and “eclipse” tradition. They report that Thallus, a pagan historian, tried to explain the darkness as an eclipse (a notice preserved only through later quotation, especially in Julius Africanus), and that Phlegon of Tralles recorded an extraordinary eclipse and earthquake in the time of Tiberius (cited by Origen and also carried in the chronographic tradition associated with Eusebius and Jerome). Whatever one concludes about these reports, they are invoked precisely as external witnesses to a public portent—again, sign language, not horoscope practice.

So our answer is “yes,” with precision: we affirm heavenly signs (Evangelicon 19:22), and we deny astrology as fate-craft and fortune-telling—lest anyone be returned to “days, and months, and times, and years” (Galatians 4:10) or to the “rudiments of the world” rather than Christ (Colossians 2:8).

questions.question: What is the soul?
questions.answer:
The soul is the living, personal principle of human identity that thinks, wills, remembers, suffers, and is capable of receiving the revelation and salvation of God the Father through Christ. It is distinct from the physical body and continues beyond the death of the body.

“For which cause we faint not; but though our outward man perish, yet the inward man is renewed day by day.”

II Corinthians 4:16

The soul is not divine by nature and does not save itself. It remains dependent upon grace, judgment, correction, and liberation through Jesus Christ.

questions.question: What is the relation between the soul and the body?
questions.answer:
The soul and body are joined during earthly life, but they are not identical. The body belongs to the physical, fleshly, and visible order and is subject to corruption, while the soul is the enduring personal subject capable of faith, repentance, and salvation.

The body is not evil in itself. It may become an instrument of righteousness through prayer, fasting, charity, Holy Baptism, and Holy Communion. Nevertheless, salvation concerns the liberation and transformation of the soul rather than the eternal preservation of corruptible flesh.

The body consequently possesses a temporary sacred use during earthly life. It is not the soul’s final destiny, but it is the vessel through which the faithful worship, serve, suffer, and grow in holiness.

“What? know ye not that your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost which is in you, which ye have of God, and ye are not your own? For ye are bought with a price: therefore glorify God in your body, and in your spirit, which are God’s.”

I Corinthians 6:19–20

See also: Question 10.09, “Does the Marcionite Church believe the body is evil?”; Question 10.05, “What is the transmigration of souls?”; Question 11.01, “What is salvation?”

questions.question: What are the different natures of souls?
questions.answer:
The early patristic record explicitly associates Marcionites with the doctrine of a “different nature” of souls. Origen, in his Commentary on Romans, says the Marcionites taught that souls differ by nature, and he treats this as a defining feature of their anthropology.

At the same time, this development did not arise in a vacuum. It draws on patterns already present in the Apostolicon. The Apostle Paul repeatedly distinguishes between the “natural” and the “spiritual” man: “the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God… but he that is spiritual judgeth all things” (I Corinthians 2:14–15). He contrasts a “natural body” with a “spiritual body,” insisting that “there is a natural body, and there is a spiritual body” (I Corinthians 15:44–46). He also divides humanity into those who are “after the flesh” and those who are “after the Spirit” (Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 7:5–6), and prays that the believers’ “whole spirit and soul and body be preserved blameless” (I Thessalonians 5:23), implying a real qualitative differentiation within human life. In receiving and reworking some Valentinian-style language about different soul-orders, later Marcionites were developing their own theology in direct dialogue with these Pauline distinctions.

For these reasons, we can say that there is scriptural warrant for speaking of a real distinction—often described, in later language, as a “different nature” of souls—between those who remain merely “natural” and those who, by the grace of God the Father revealed in Christ, participate in a truly “spiritual” life. While we reject any crude fatalism, we acknowledge with the Apostle Paul that not all souls stand in the same order, orientation, or destiny, and that the epistles of the Apostolicon themselves provide the categories of “natural” and “spiritual” that later Marcionites developed, in conversation with certain Gnostic currents, into a more explicit doctrine of differing soul-natures.

In the Dialogue of Adamantius, the Marcionite Marcus presses this distinction to its sharpest edge: only “spiritual” souls, that is, the spiritual element in man (the pneuma), are saved. This means that souls that have not attained spirituality through faith in Christ are not saved. In Marcus’s framing, salvation belongs properly to what is spiritual, not to the material order, and not to the merely natural constitution of man as such.

questions.question: What is the preexistence of souls?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church affirms the preexistence of souls: souls exist prior to embodiment. This doctrine coheres naturally with the Apostle’s “before” language and with the Marcionite account of embodiment within the physical, fleshly, and visible world.

“He hath chosen us in him before the foundation of the world…”

Laodiceans 1:4–5

And again:

God’s saving purpose and grace were given “before the world began.” Titus 1:2

In Marcionite theology, the soul is not merely a product of the flesh, nor is it generated by the material body. The soul precedes embodiment and enters the lower physical world, where it becomes subject to bondage, forgetfulness, judgment, temptation, and the powers of the present age. This is why salvation concerns the awakening and liberation of the soul, not the permanent vindication of corruptible flesh.

This doctrine also shows that souls were created directly by God the Father. Because souls are non-physical and preexist embodiment, they cannot be explained as products of the lower physical order or as creations of the lower maker of the physical world. The body belongs to the corruptible realm shaped through deferred demiurgy, but the soul belongs to the higher order. Souls come from God the Father, descend into embodiment, and are liberated by Christ so that they may return to the Father’s kingdom.

In the Apellean tradition, which preserves the original teaching of Marcion, this preexistence is described through a concrete descent narrative. Tertullian reports that Apelles taught that souls were enticed down from heavenly abodes by a lower power, the being called in the Apostolicon “the god of this world” and called in the Evangelicon “the prince of this world.” Apelles described this lower power as a fiery angel, the lower maker of the physical world, who attempted to imitate the higher realm but failed to reproduce its perfection. In this account, souls are drawn downward into the lower world and enclosed within flesh.

This doctrine is not dualism. The lower maker of the physical world is not a second co-eternal, co-equal god. He is a subordinate lower power, principality, throne, or dominion beneath God the Father. The physical world is therefore real, ordered, and meaningful, but also inferior, mixed, corruptible, and subject to decay. Embodiment is not the soul’s origin. It is the soul’s condition within the present world-order.

This same framework also makes the other Marcionite soul teachings cohere rather than conflict. If souls exist prior to embodiment and are not produced by the body, then it is intelligible that Marcionites spoke of a division or differentiation among souls. Hostile writers often summarized this as a distinction between different “natures” of souls. In Marcionite terms, this is better understood as the distinction between natural souls and spiritual souls.

Natural souls remain bound to the physical world of the flesh and may pass through remedial embodiment until they encounter the Gospel in due season, provided they have not committed the Eternal Sin. Spiritual souls, by contrast, are awakened by the revelation of God the Father through Jesus Christ. Having received the Gospel and not hardened themselves against the Holy Spirit, they are not destined for repeated embodiment but for ascent into the Father’s kingdom.

Origen explicitly targets those who “introduce souls of different natures,” a polemical engagement widely understood to include Marcionite claims about differentiated souls and destinies. His criticism preserves the fact that Marcionite Christians did not treat all souls as identical in condition, reception, or destiny. Yet this distinction should not be misread as fatalism. The key question is not merely what condition a soul begins in, but whether it receives the Gospel, repents, and is awakened by Christ.

The doctrine of preexistent souls also explains the Marcionite teaching on transmigration of souls, or metempsychosis. If embodiment is a condition the soul can enter, rather than the event that creates the soul, then the soul can also pass from body to body within the lower world-order. Epiphanius attributes to Marcion the claim that there are “reincarnations of souls” and “transmigrations from body to body.” Within Marcionite theology, this does not mean endless return for all souls without distinction. It is a remedial process for natural souls who have not truly heard the Gospel, or who remain imperfect and in bondage but have not committed the Eternal Sin.

Preexistence, the differentiation of souls, and transmigration therefore form one coherent doctrine: natural souls may remain bound to the present world-order until they encounter the Gospel, while souls awakened through Christ ascend into the Kingdom of God. Those who knowingly commit the Eternal Sin face annihilation rather than further remedial embodiment.

This framework also clarifies the relationship between preexistence and the harrowing of hell. Christ’s descent broke the absolute dominion of death, Hell, Satan, and the lower powers over human souls. It did not erase the soul’s need for repentance, awakening, and deliverance. Rather, it opened the way for souls to be rescued from bondage, whether through direct reception of the Gospel or, for natural souls still capable of repentance, through remedial passage within the lower world-order.

Finally, it is worth noting that some form of preexistence was debated beyond Marcionite circles. Origen himself developed a major early Christian account of rational souls’ prior existence and descent in his theological system, even while opposing the Marcionite claim that souls differ by nature. This shows that the preexistence of souls was not an alien or impossible concept in early Christian thought. In Marcionite Christianity, however, it serves a distinctly Gospel-centered purpose: to explain the soul’s divine origin, its bondage in the lower world, its need for Christ, and its liberation by the one true God revealed through Jesus Christ.

See also: Question 9.04, “What is the origin of matter?”; Question 10.05, “What is the transmigration of souls?”

questions.question: What is the transmigration of souls?
questions.answer:
The Church affirms transmigration of souls (also called metempsychosis) as a real feature of the soul’s journey through the present world-order—but not as a blanket guarantee of universal salvation.

In the patristic record, this belief is explicitly attributed to Marcion and the Marcionites as part of their broader insistence that salvation pertains to the soul rather than to the permanent vindication of fleshly bodies. Epiphanius, for example, says that Marcion “assigns salvation” to souls, and “claims that there are reincarnations of souls, and transmigrations from body to body.”

This teaching is also associated, in the same heresiological stream, with Marcionite schools after Marcion. Epiphanius identifies Lucian (Lucanus) as an early associate of Marcion who founded the Marcionite school known as the Lucanists, whose doctrines he describes as “like Marcion’s in every way,” even while admitting that the sect was hard to track because it was ancient and short-lived.

Irenaeus reports that Christians, including Marcionites, who taught transmigration, appealed to this saying in the Gospel, treating “prison” as embodiment and “release” as the soul’s eventual liberation after completing what is lacking.

Evangelicon 11:70-71

“When thou goest with thine adversary to the magistrate, as thou art in the way, give diligence that thou mayest be delivered from him; lest he hale thee to the judge, and the judge deliver thee to the officer, and the officer cast thee into prison. I tell thee, thou shalt not depart thence, till thou hast paid the very last mite.”

In this reading, the Lord’s image is not merely a warning about earthly courts, but a spiritual description of the soul’s remedial passage through the realm of judgment until its debt is exhausted. Crucially, this remedial logic is aimed at souls who remain capable of repentance and reception—souls that have not shut themselves against the Spirit in the way Christ calls eternal and unforgivable.

This framework also clarifies the Marcionite doctrine of the division of souls: the heresiological claim that there are differing kinds of souls coheres naturally with transmigration. On this reading, natural souls remain bound to the physical world of the flesh and therefore transmigrate from body to body until they encounter the Gospel in due season—so long as they have not committed the Eternal Sin. Spiritual souls, by contrast, are awakened by the revelation of God the Father, having received the Gospel and not hardened themselves against the Holy Spirit; they are not destined for repeated embodiment but for ascent into the Father’s kingdom. In short: natural souls transmigrate; spiritual souls ascend.

The Marcionites were not alone in being accused of (or known for) transmigration.

Carpocratians. Irenaeus says the Carpocratians taught that souls must undergo “transmigration from body to body” so they can experience every form of life and action. He adds that, on their scheme, “in this way also all souls are saved,” including those who are “set free” by passing “from body to body.” This is an important parallel: transmigration is explicitly presented as a salvation-explaining doctrine—though we do not follow the Carpocratian claim that this mechanism guarantees salvation for all without exception.

Basilides and the Basilideans. Clement of Alexandria reports Basilidean teaching in which the soul is punished in the present life for sin “in another life,” and he explicitly raises the question “whether the soul is changed to another body.” This is not merely moral progress; it is a theory of prior lives and bodily change used to explain suffering and purification.

So when critics claim transmigration is an alien concept imported into Christian discourse, the historical record shows multiple early Christian or quasi-Christian movements using it to address the same pressure point: how divine justice and mercy can be true when human lives are radically unequal in opportunity and knowledge.

Transmigration of souls provides a coherent answer to a moral problem that every serious theology must face: How can those who never heard the Gospel be saved? If a soul can pass through successive lives, then no one is “finally lost” simply because they were born in a place, time, or circumstance where the Gospel was never encountered. Transmigration becomes the mechanism by which grace can reach such souls in due season—so long as they have not committed the Eternal Sin, which Christ sets apart as uniquely unforgivable and therefore beyond remedial restoration.

This is also one more place where Marcionism shows its Hellenistic intellectual pedigree. The Marcionite movement emerged in a Greco-Roman milieu saturated with Platonic and Middle-Platonic talk about the soul’s imprisonment in the material order, its purification, and its ascent toward the divine; and the older philosophical tradition (Plato and the Pythagoreans especially) already had a vocabulary for metempsychosis as a way of explaining moral formation, recompense, and cosmic pedagogy. Our confession of transmigration is therefore not an embarrassment or a novelty, but a consistent example of Marcionism translating the Evangelicon’s sharp antithesis between fleshly bondage and spiritual deliverance into the conceptual language the ancient world actually spoke—pressing philosophy into service as handmaid, not master, of the Gospel of God the Father.

In short: metempsychosis safeguards the breadth of God the Father’s saving mercy toward those who lacked Gospel light, while still taking seriously the soul’s real need for repentance, awakening, and deliverance from the world’s bondage—and it does so without denying Christ’s own boundary: that there is an Eternal Sin which “shall not be forgiven,” and thus is not included within remedial transmigration.

See also: Question 12.06, “What is annihilationism?”; Question 12.07, “What is the eternal sin?”

questions.question: How does the Marcionite Church understand original sin?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church understands original sin not as inherited guilt, but as bondage within the corruptible world-order, and therefore rejects the doctrine developed by the emerging Catholic tradition and later inherited by much of Western Christianity. That doctrine taught that all humanity inherited guilt and condemnation from Adam’s disobedience in the Garden of Eden. Rooted in the mythological creation story of Genesis, it portrayed humanity as fallen by nature and deserving divine wrath from birth. For Marcionites, such a doctrine slandered the character of God the Father and obscured the Gospel of Jesus Christ.

The Western doctrine of original sin eventually came to rest on several claims: that Adam committed a historical transgression in Eden; that the guilt of that transgression was transmitted to all his descendants; that this inherited guilt was sufficient to condemn even infants before any personal act; and that a ritual or forensic remedy was required to remove that inherited charge. Marcionite Christianity rejects this entire structure.

This does not mean that Marcionites are soft on the human condition. We reject inherited guilt, but we affirm the reality of bondage. Humanity is born into the lower world-order, a physical order shaped through deferred demiurgy and governed by lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions. Souls enter a realm marked by flesh, law, corruption, violence, death, and temptation. The problem is not that every soul is legally guilty of Adam’s mythical transgression. The problem is that souls are bound within a corruptible order from which they cannot free themselves.

In contrast to the Catholic doctrine of inherited guilt, Marcion and his followers taught that sin arises because souls possess freedom and live within a world shaped by corruption, law, and violence. It is not guilt that is transmitted biologically from one generation to the next, but the ongoing condition of bondage and the capacity for moral failure within the lower order. Each soul is responsible for its own choices, not for the imagined rebellion of a mythical first man.

The Apostle Paul’s language supports this distinction. The condition of mankind is not best described as inherited guilt, but as slavery: slavery under the elements of the world, the law of sin and death, and the powers of the present age. A slave is not guilty merely for being born inside the master’s house. He is captive. What he needs is not condemnation for another man’s crime, but rescue from bondage.

This is why the Gospel is deliverance. Christ does not come to prosecute Adam’s descendants for inherited guilt. He comes to rescue souls from the present evil age. He liberates them from sin, death, Satan, and the lower powers. Salvation is therefore not the satisfaction of inherited legal wrath, but ransom, purchase, and liberation from bondage through Jesus Christ.

Romans 7 likewise supports this understanding. Sin works in the flesh through commandment and law. The agent of death is not inherited biological descent, but the law of sin and death operating within the lower world-order. Paul describes the soul’s captivity within flesh and law, and then gives the answer: freedom in Christ. There is no condemnation for those in Christ Jesus, because the law of the Spirit of life has freed them from the law of sin and death.

Nor can the Hebrew Bible be used to establish original sin for Christians. The Hebrew Psalms and Genesis are not part of the canon or deuterocanon of the Testamentum and are not the doctrinal foundation of the Gospel. Their exclusion from the scriptural rule is not altered by the broader codicological use of Testamentum for the Church’s complete modern collection. Whatever the Hebrew Bible says about Adam, conception, guilt, or inheritance belongs to the covenant and literature of the lower order. Christian doctrine must be read from Christ and the Apostle, not from Genesis mythology.

The same applies to the common appeal to the phrase “all have sinned.” Marcionite Christianity affirms that all morally accountable persons sin personally, because all who come to discernment live within the present corruptible order and are subject to moral failure. But this is not the same as saying that infants, babies, or young children before moral discernment are born legally guilty of Adam’s sin before they have done anything at all. Universal personal sin among the morally accountable is not inherited ancestral guilt.

This doctrine matters most at the cradle. Original sin has been used for centuries to terrify parents with the thought that infants are born guilty, condemned, and liable to punishment before any personal act. Marcionite Christianity rejects that cruelty. An infant is not born guilty. No transgression, ancestral or personal, is imputed to the infant. God the Father is not the prosecutor of Adam’s race. He is the rescuer of souls through Jesus Christ.

This also comports with the Marcionite doctrine of souls. Souls preexist embodiment and come from God the Father. They are not products of corruptible flesh, nor are they guilty because of descent from Adam. Babies, infants, and young children who die before moral discernment are not judged as sinners, nor sent into corrective transmigration for personal guilt, but are received by God the Father into the kingdom of God. Natural souls may pass through remedial embodiment until they encounter the Gospel in due season, so long as they have not committed the Eternal Sin. Spiritual souls, awakened by Christ, ascend through him into the Father’s kingdom.

The Catholic doctrine of original sin served to bind humanity under law and to justify the legalistic authority of the institutional church. The Marcionite Gospel offers a radically different vision. God the Father, revealed through Jesus Christ, does not impose inherited guilt. He extends mercy, awakens souls, and calls them out of bondage into freedom. The problem is not an ancestral debt. The problem is captivity. The answer is not condemnation. The answer is rescue.

In this, Marcionites proclaim freedom over bondage, grace over inherited guilt, and truth over myth: a Gospel that liberates rather than condemns.

questions.question: How does the Marcionite Church understand free will?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church understands free will as a sacred gift from the true God, enabling each soul to choose faith, obedience, and love for Christ. Without free will, worship would be hollow and salvation meaningless.

We are not born guilty. Marcionite Christians reject the doctrine of original sin and hold that accountability begins when a person can discern right from wrong.
