As the Apostle Paul writes:

“Servants, be obedient to them that are your masters according to the flesh, with fear and trembling, in singleness of your heart, as unto Christ.”

Laodiceans 6:5

Such obedience must be freely given, not coerced. It is by the voluntary turning of the heart that one enters into the liberty of the Gospel.

Thus, free will is essential to the Christian life and to the transformative grace offered by the God revealed in Jesus Christ.

questions.question: How does the Church understand human reason and its role in knowing God?
questions.answer:
Marcionites affirm that reason is a limited but valuable faculty for understanding the world, yet it cannot unveil the true God. Human philosophies, though often admired, fall into grave error when they attempt to define the divine through material elements or natural forces. As Marcion’s Homily to Diognetus warns: “Or dost thou receive the vain and foolish sayings of those pretentious philosophers, of whom some said that God was fire, calling that God whereunto they themselves shall go; and others water; and others some other of the elements formed by God?” (Diognetus 8:2).

Marcionites, though conversant with the language of philosophy, reject its claim to divine knowledge apart from revelation. Revelation from God the Father is not the product of contemplation or debate, but an act of divine initiative, breaking into history through Christ. Though reason cannot discover God the Father on its own, it may serve the faithful in expressing and defending the truth once revealed. Reason may illuminate, but it can never originate the truth revealed by God the Father through Christ.

questions.question: Does the Marcionite Church believe the body is evil?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church does not teach that the body is evil in the sense that human flesh is morally guilty by mere existence. The body is weak, mortal, corruptible, and bound to the physical, fleshly, and visible world, but sin belongs to the will and to the passions that enslave the soul, not to the body as though the body alone were responsible for evil.

The Dialogue of Megethius strengthens this teaching by rejecting the claim that matter is evil in itself. Since matter is not an evil substance, the body is not evil merely because it is material. The body is weak, mortal, and corruptible, but it is not intrinsically sinful by mere existence.

The Apostle teaches that “the flesh lusteth against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh” (Galatians 5:17). He also says, “O wretched man that I am! who shall deliver me from the body of this death?” (Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 6:24). These sayings show the misery of embodied life under the powers of this world. The flesh is not the soul’s true home. It is the place of temptation, decay, suffering, and death.

Yet the Marcionite Church also rejects the claim that the body should be hated, abused, or despised as though cruelty toward the flesh were holiness. The faithful are called to chastity, fasting, temperance, modesty, and discipline, not because the body is to be mutilated or loathed, but because the soul must not be ruled by fleshly passions. The body is to be governed, not worshipped; disciplined, not adored; restrained, not destroyed.

The body also has a temporary sacred dignity because it may be used in obedience to Christ. The body receives Holy Baptism. The body partakes of Holy Communion. The body kneels in prayer, fasts in repentance, gives alms in charity, and serves the brethren in love. Therefore, although the body belongs to the lower order and shall not inherit the Kingdom in its corruptible form, it can still be made an instrument of righteousness while the soul remains in this world.

The same is true of the world. The Marcionite Church does not hate the world as though every creature, every act of love, or every experience of beauty were evil. We reject worship of the world and bondage to the world-order, not the recognition that created life contains real goods. We do not think the flesh or the world is superior to what is to come. We discipline the body and use the world without making either our god.

The Marcionite Church therefore distinguishes between the body as created under the order of corruption and the soul as called to salvation by the Father. The body is not man’s final destiny. It is a mortal vessel, a place of trial, and a sign of bondage to the present world. But it is not to be treated as an excuse for sin, nor as an object of hatred. The Christian must bring the body into subjection, that the soul may be freed for God.

Thus the Marcionite Church does not simply say, “the body is evil.” Rather, it teaches that the body is mortal, fleshly, corruptible, and subject to the powers of this world, while the soul is called by Christ to liberation, purification, and entrance into the Kingdom of God.

Bodily weakness and suffering may therefore become occasions for patience, discipline, and growth in the spirit. The Christian neither worships bodily beauty nor despises bodily frailty, but uses every condition of embodied life in the service of faith and charity.

See also: Question 9.09, “What is the difference between matter, the world, and the world-order, and are they evil?”; Question 9.19, “Why do beauty, love, and goodness exist in this world?”; Question 14.03, “What is Holy Baptism?”; Question 14.08, “What is Holy Communion?”; Dialogue of Megethius 9:1-18; Dialogue of Megethius 10:16-24.

questions.question: What is salvation?
questions.answer:
Salvation is deliverance from the law of sin and death, from bondage to the corruptible world-order, and from the powers that hold the soul beneath them. The soteriology of Marcionite Christians, particularly as expressed in the Psalmicon, is deeply rooted in the doctrine of grace and conditional election. Salvation, in the Marcionite view, is entirely the work of God and not something earned or merited by human effort. However, this election is not arbitrary or unconditional—it is based on God’s foreknowledge of those who would respond in faith to His revelation through Christ.

“For I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ: for it is the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth; to the Jew, and also to the Greek.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 1:12

“For God hath not appointed us to wrath, but to obtain salvation by our Lord Jesus Christ.”

I Thessalonians 5:9

Imputed righteousness teaches that the righteousness of Jesus is credited to believers through faith, meaning they are regarded by God as righteous not because of their own deeds, but because of Christ’s perfection. It is on the basis of this righteousness—not personal merit—that God the Father accepts and justifies the believer.

See also: Question 10.06, “How does the Marcionite Church understand original sin?”

questions.question: From what are human souls saved?
questions.answer:
Human souls are saved from ignorance of God the Father, bondage to sin and death, enslavement to the powers of the present world-order, and the cycle of judgment that follows unbelief and disobedience. Salvation is therefore both revelation and liberation.

Christ does not merely improve the conditions of the lower world. He makes the Father known, breaks the claims of the hostile powers, forgives sins, and gathers the faithful into the true covenant of grace.

questions.question: How does Christ save?
questions.answer:
Christ saves by revealing God the Father, proclaiming the Gospel of grace, giving Himself as a ransom, descending into hell, overcoming death, and liberating souls from the dominion of the present world-order. His saving work is completed through His true suffering, death, burial, and resurrection.

The faithful receive this salvation by grace through faith, are incorporated into the Church through Holy Baptism, and are strengthened in obedience through the Holy Mysteries, prayer, charity, and the law of Christ.

questions.question: What is the ransom theory of atonement?
questions.answer:
The Dialogue of Adamantius preserves precisely the sort of ransom, purchase, and liberation logic that undergirds Marcionite soteriology: salvation as deliverance from the lower powers through the redeeming work of Christ.

In the disputation, the Marcionite spokesman Megethius frames the advent of Christ as a rescue operation aimed at the tyrannical spiritual ruler. Within the Lucanist framework, Megethius distinguishes between the good, the just, and the evil: God the Father above all, the just “god of this world” associated with the making of the physical world, and Satan as the evil adversary. Christ comes from God the Father to conquer the devil and break the hostile dominion that held mankind. That victory language is already the backbone of ransom theology: humanity is held under alien lordship, namely sin, death, and the devil, and the Son is sent to break that lordship and release the captives.

The same dialogue then makes the transactional aspect explicit by leaning on Paul’s redemption language. Megethius appeals to the apostolic claim that Christ “purchased” us out of bondage and insists that this implies Christ cannot be identical with the maker of the physical world, because no one purchases what is already his. Adamantius presses the argument in overtly ransom-like terms: if Christ is the purchaser, who then is the party from whom the purchase is made? Is it the wicked devil who held mankind, or the maker of the physical world himself?

This purchase language is not incidental. It expresses the moral character of God the Father. A wholly good God does not steal, seize, or violate justice, even when rescuing those held in bondage. If mankind was under the dominion of lower powers, then the Father’s rescue is accomplished not by theft but by righteous purchase. Christ buys back the captives so that liberation itself is just, good, and holy. The ransom therefore reveals not only the defeat of the adversary, but the perfect goodness of the Father who rescues without wrongdoing.

The Apellean and Lucanist distinction matters chiefly in how one describes the party from whom mankind is ransomed, but not in whether ransom itself is true. Traditional Apelleans, preserving the original teaching of Marcion, identified the lower maker of the physical world with Satan, the same being called in the Apostolicon “the god of this world” and called in the Evangelicon “the prince of this world.” In that framework, the ransom is directed against the dominion of Satan, who is also the lower maker of the physical world. The Lucanists, by contrast, distinguished the just “god of this world” from Satan. In their framework, the ransom concerns liberation from the bondage of the lower order, sin, death, and Satan, while also separating the just maker of the physical world from the evil adversary. Yet both schools preserved the same fundamental Marcionite soteriology: Christ purchases souls out of bondage and transfers them from the dominion of lower powers into the liberty of God the Father.

Even when Megethius answers in the idiom of being purchased “from sin itself,” the structure remains the same. Redemption is conceived as manumission from slavery by a costly act that transfers humanity out of one dominion and into another. In short, Adamantius’ record shows Marcionites arguing for salvation as purchase-from-bondage in Pauline terms, not merely as moral improvement or ethical instruction.

This ransom logic fits the broader Marcionite distinction between God the Father and the lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions beneath him. Christ is not sent by the maker of the physical world to recover what already belongs to that lower maker. Rather, Christ is sent by God the Father to rescue souls from bondage under sin, death, the devil, and the lower order of the present age. The language of purchase therefore reinforces the Marcionite claim that salvation is deliverance from an alien dominion into the liberty of Christ.

That Pauline purchase motif also explains the later Marcionite price-formula often attributed to Marcion:

“We are the price of the blood of Jesus.”

The statement is a compressed ransom confession. The redeemed community is what Christ’s blood has bought. Those redeemed by Christ now belong to God the Father rather than to the old bondage. They have been transferred from the dominion of the lower powers into the kingdom of the Son.

From there it is a short step to the more developed patristic ransom idiom. Irenaeus speaks in the same broad register of liberation from captivity through the Lord’s self-gift, emphasizing that deliverance is accomplished not by the tyranny of a stronger power but by a righteous rescue. Origen, in the later tradition that summarizes his comments on Romans, sharpens the framework by naming the captor: the devil held humanity through sin and therefore demanded a price, but was outwitted because he could not hold the sinless one.

That dovetails with the classic hidden-divinity elaboration. Christ enters the lower world under the appearance of weakness. The adversary overreaches. In attempting to seize Christ, he loses his claim over those held in bondage. The death of Christ becomes the very means by which Christ shatters the adversary’s dominion and frees the captives.

Taken together, the Dialogue of Adamantius corroborates a recognizably Marcionite ransom logic: humanity is held under hostile dominion; Christ intervenes as liberator; redemption is described as a costly purchase out of bondage; and the defeat of the adversary is accomplished through Christ’s death, which becomes the instrument of emancipation rather than a mere example or moral exhortation. This remains fully compatible with Marcionite cosmology: not dualism, not two co-equal gods, but deliverance from lower powers through the saving act of Christ sent by God the Father. Most importantly, the ransom reveals the character of the Father himself: he is so wholly good that even his rescue of mankind is accomplished righteously, as purchase rather than theft, liberation rather than seizure, and mercy without injustice.

See also: Question 9.12, “Who is called the ‘god of this world’?”; Question 9.22, “How does the Church understand Satan?”; Question 12.04, “What is the harrowing of hell?”

questions.question: How does the Church explain moral evil?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christianity affirms that moral evil arises from sinful will and action, not from matter itself and not from a dualistic cosmos of co-equal gods. Evil is not a substance created by God, nor is matter an evil nature. Moral evil appears when rational creatures turn from love, truth, and mercy into disobedience, cruelty, idolatry, and bondage under the lower powers.

The Dialogue of Megethius distinguishes evil from substance. Evil is not a thing created by God, nor is it matter itself. Moral evil arises from will, passion, action, disorder, and bondage under lower powers.

Natural evil belongs to the instability and corruption of the lower world-order; moral evil belongs to will, intention, action, and omission. The two are related within the present evil age, but they should not be collapsed into one cause. The lower powers corrupt and enslave; human beings also sin by freely serving that corruption rather than the Gospel.

Moral evil consists of the actions, thoughts, and omissions of individuals that violate the law of love – the divine will revealed in the Gospel. Acts such as murder, theft, hatred, cruelty, or the refusal to show mercy are all expressions of moral evil. It also includes sins of omission: to ignore a plea for help or to remain indifferent in the face of injustice is itself a form of moral failure.

As the Apostle Paul writes:

“Wherefore, as by one man sin entered into the world, and death by sin; and so death passed upon all men, for that all have sinned.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 4:12

The Apostle does not teach that matter itself is sinful, but that sin and death reign within the present age through disobedience. The brokenness and suffering we now witness therefore arise from sinful wills, lower bondage, and corrupt rule, not from the direct intent of God the Father or from matter as such.

Violence, cruelty, hatred, and refusal of mercy arise from hearts turned away from God and from actions committed under bondage to the lower powers. Trace moral evil to its source, and one finds disordered will: the refusal to love God the Father revealed by Jesus Christ and to love one’s neighbour.

God does not forcibly eliminate moral evil, because to do so would mean abolishing human freedom. And without freedom, there is no personhood, no capacity for genuine love, no ability to reflect the image of God. A being without free will is not made in God’s image. Free will is necessary for eternal existence, for worship, for obedience, and for love. Worship that is coerced or mechanical is not worship at all – it is an insult to God.

Therefore, God permits moral evil to exist within the bounds of human freedom, using even suffering and consequence as tools to teach mankind the goodness it once knew but lost through sin.

The problem of moral evil was addressed at the cross, where Christ bore the weight of human sin. The power of death was overcome in His resurrection. Though evil remains present in the world and continues to cause suffering and death, God has acted decisively in Christ to redeem what man has ruined. He offers forgiveness for our sins and the promise of life beyond death – a life without evil or sorrow.

Imagine a world without the possibility of forgiveness for moral evil. In Christ, we have been spared such hopelessness. God has given us His Word, His Spirit, and His Gospel as a sustaining source of truth and strength through the trials of this life. The resurrection of Jesus stands as the pledge of our own, and the hope of restoration beyond the reach of evil.

Though we may feel powerless to change the world at large, each of us has the capacity to overcome the dominion of moral evil in our own lives through Jesus Christ. In doing so, we diminish its power – not only in ourselves but in every soul we help lead to Christ. Through the Gospel, we are not passive witnesses to the world’s decay, but active participants in its redemption.

See also: Question 9.08, “Is evil a substance?”; Question 9.20, “How does the Church explain natural evil?”; Question 10.07, “How does the Marcionite Church understand free will?”; Dialogue of Megethius 9:1-18; Dialogue of Megethius 10:16-24.

questions.question: How should Christians respond to suffering?
questions.answer:
Christians should respond to suffering through faith, prayer, endurance, charity, and practical care for those who are afflicted.

The Apostle Paul, who endured profound affliction for the sake of Christ, offers a powerful model for how believers should approach suffering. He did not resist it in bitterness but embraced it as a meaningful part of his calling. From his example, we learn that suffering is not meaningless. It reveals much about our relationship to God—its purpose, its effects, and the promise it carries.

Suffering affirms that we belong to God. Suffering produces endurance and, ultimately, hope. Suffering leads others to glorify God. Suffering is permitted under God’s sovereign care. And most of all, we are never alone in our suffering.

Though suffering is a reality in this world, its fruit is lasting joy and eternal reward.

First, Paul teaches that suffering cultivates hope—a hope not based on the seen or temporary, but on the eternal:

“Therefore being justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ: By whom also we have access by faith into this grace wherein we stand, and rejoice in hope of the glory of God. And not only so, but we glory in tribulations also: knowing that tribulation worketh patience; And patience, experience; and experience, hope: And hope maketh not ashamed; because the love of God is shed abroad in our hearts by the Holy Ghost which is given unto us.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 4:1–5

Paul also acknowledged that his afflictions brought him to the limits of human strength so that he would learn to rely fully on God:

“For we would not, brethren, have you ignorant of our trouble which came to us in Asia, that we were pressed out of measure, above strength, insomuch that we despaired even of life: But we had the sentence of death in ourselves, that we should not trust in ourselves, but in God which raiseth the dead: Who delivered us from so great a death, and doth deliver: in whom we trust that he will yet deliver us; Ye also helping together by prayer for us…”

II Corinthians 1:8–11

This trust in God’s sovereign care is echoed in Paul’s well-known reassurance:

“And we know that all things work together for good to them that love God, to them who are the called according to his purpose.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 7:28

Yet we must remember: what is “good” in God’s eyes may not align with what we naturally desire. When Paul pleaded with God to remove a persistent suffering—his “thorn in the flesh”—God refused, but not without purpose. He responded:

“My grace is sufficient for thee: for my strength is made perfect in weakness.”

II Corinthians 12:9

God did not remove Paul’s suffering because that very weakness revealed God’s strength—and drew others to glorify Him.

Paul also taught that the comfort we receive in our own trials equips us to comfort others:

“Who comforteth us in all our tribulation, that we may be able to comfort them which are in any trouble, by the comfort wherewith we ourselves are comforted of God.”

II Corinthians 1:4

Those who have walked through the valley of suffering are uniquely qualified to guide others through it. They become vessels of God’s grace, ministering to others with the same compassion they themselves received.

Even creation, Paul reminds us, suffers alongside humanity:

“For we know that the whole creation groaneth and travaileth in pain together until now.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 7:22

Disease, death, and decay are all part of the current age. Yet this age is not the end.

The hope of the believer lies in Christ’s return. As Paul gently reminds us:

“Let your moderation be known unto all men. The Lord is at hand.”

Philippians 4:5

The Lord is near. He is close to the brokenhearted and soon to return to restore all things. In our present trials, we are not abandoned. We wait, with hope, for the fulfillment of what God has promised: a new creation, free of suffering, where every tear is wiped away and every sorrow redeemed.

questions.question: Why is the Church not antinomian?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians believe that salvation comes by faith and through divine grace, not through adherence to the law of Moses. They affirm that the law of Moses belongs to the false covenant and has no binding authority over Christians. Yet freedom from the law of Moses is not freedom from obedience: the Church rejects the law of Moses while affirming the law of Christ, by which the faithful bear one another’s burdens, walk in love, and fulfil the commandment of the Lord.

This position is grounded firmly in the writings of the Apostle Paul, who writes:

“For sin shall not have dominion over you: for ye are not under the law, but under grace. What then? shall we sin, because we are not under the law, but under grace? God forbid.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 5:14-15

Paul continues:

“For Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 8:7

While rejecting the Mosaic Law, Marcionite Christians recognize that moral truths are accessible through natural law—what Paul refers to when speaking of Gentiles:

“For when the Gentiles, which have not the law, do by nature the things contained in the law, these, having not the law, are a law unto themselves: Which shew the work of the law written in their hearts, their conscience also bearing witness, and their thoughts the mean while accusing or else excusing one another.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 2:5-6

Paul reprimands the Galatians for returning to the law of Moses, calling it a regression:

“O foolish Galatians, who hath bewitched you, that ye should not obey the truth, before whose eyes Jesus Christ hath been evidently set forth, crucified among you? Are ye so foolish? having begun in the Spirit, are ye now made perfect by the flesh?”

Galatians 3:1-3

Marcionite Christians follow Paul’s emphasis on salvation by grace alone:

“For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God: Not of works, lest any man should boast.”

Laodiceans 2:8-9

Paul also affirms Christian liberty from the law:

“And that because of false brethren unawares brought in, who came in privily to spy out our liberty which we have in Christ Jesus, that they might bring us into bondage.”

Galatians 2:4

Christian liberty is therefore governed by the law of Christ, as Paul writes:

“Bear ye one another’s burdens, and so fulfil the law of Christ.”

Galatians 6:2

Finally, Paul declares the law of Moses abolished in Christ’s crucifixion:

“And you, being dead in your sins and the uncircumcision of your flesh, hath he quickened together with him, having forgiven you all trespasses; Blotting out the handwriting of ordinances that was against us… and took it out of the way, nailing it to his cross.”

Colossians 2:13-14

In every respect, Marcionite Christianity rejects antinomian lawlessness. Salvation is a gift received by faith, not a prize earned through legal obedience; yet those who are saved are called to live under the law of Christ through love, charity, holiness, and mutual service.

questions.question: How does the Church understand faith and works?
questions.answer:
Salvation is received through faith and grace, not earned through observance of the law of Moses or through human merit. Good works are the fruit, evidence, and discipline of living faith under the law of Christ, not the legal price paid or justification for salvation.

The Evangelicon expresses this principle directly: “And he said to the woman, Thy faith hath saved thee; go in peace.”

Evangelicon 5:50

Marcion of Sinope likewise teaches in Marcion’s Homily to Diognetus that human effort cannot earn divine favor. Salvation is the gift of God, received through faith; works follow as the grateful expression of a life already turned toward Christ.

See also: Question 11.09, “What place do good works have in salvation?”; Question 17.02, “What is the law of Christ?”

questions.question: What place do good works have in salvation?
questions.answer:
Ancient Marcionite testimony shows that good works were regarded as the necessary evidence or fruit of faith rather than the meritorious price or justification of salvation. Rhodo’s account, preserved by Eusebius, reports Apelles teaching that “those who place their hope in the Crucified will be saved if they persist in good works.”

Apelles emphasized concrete deeds of charity, purity, mercy, and self-denial as the visible evidence that faith is genuine. His teaching reflects a practical emphasis within the Marcionite tradition: faith saves, while a life transformed by the Holy Spirit manifests that faith through obedience to the law of Christ.

The present Marcionite Church therefore affirms that good works neither purchase salvation nor replace faith. They are the consequence, sign, and discipline of a sincere faith that has received grace.

See also: Question 11.08, “How does the Church understand faith and works?”; Question 17.03, “How does Christian liberty differ from lawlessness?”

questions.question: How do faith, good works, the Holy Mysteries, and Holy Rites relate?
questions.answer:
Faith receives the grace of God revealed in Jesus Christ. Good works are the fruit and evidence of living faith, not a legal price paid or justification for salvation. The three Holy Mysteries initiate and nourish the faithful within the true covenant, while the Holy Rites order repentance, healing, ministry, marriage, worship, and charity in the life of the Church.

These realities are therefore neither rivals nor interchangeable. Salvation is grounded in grace and received through faith; good works manifest obedience to the law of Christ; the Holy Mysteries incorporate and nourish the faithful; and the Holy Rites support the ordered common life by which believers are formed in holiness and love.

See also: Question 11.08, “How does the Church understand faith and works?”; Question 14.01, “Which Holy Mysteries does the Church recognize?”; Question 14.02, “Which Holy Rites does the Church recognize?”

questions.question: What is sin?
questions.answer:
Sin is the willful turning of the soul away from God the Father and the law of Christ through thought, word, deed, or omission. It includes unbelief, false worship, hatred, cruelty, impurity, dishonesty, injustice, refusal of mercy, and every act contrary to faith working through love.

Sin is not the mere possession of a material body, inherited guilt from Adam, ritual impurity, or failure to observe the law of Moses. The body is corruptible and subject to temptation, but moral guilt belongs to the person who knowingly misuses freedom. Neither food, marriage, nationality, bodily weakness, nor ceremonial condition makes a person sinful in itself.

“For whatsoever is not of faith is sin.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 12:23

Ordinary sins may be forgiven through grace, repentance, confession, amendment of life, and Holy Reconciliation. They must be distinguished from the Eternal Sin: the settled and knowing blasphemy against the Holy Spirit by which the soul repudiates the very grace through which forgiveness is received.

questions.question: What is repentance?
questions.answer:
Repentance is the turning of the soul away from sin, false worship, and disobedience toward God the Father through Jesus Christ. It includes sorrow for wrongdoing, confession, amendment of life, restitution where possible, and renewed obedience to the law of Christ.

Repentance is not an attempt to purchase forgiveness. It is the faithful response to grace and the continuing discipline by which the baptized return to the way of Christ.

questions.question: What is grace?
questions.answer:
Grace is the free and undeserved favor of God the Father revealed and bestowed through Jesus Christ. It is not a wage earned by observance of the law of Moses or by human merit, but the divine gift by which sins are forgiven, souls are liberated, and the faithful are strengthened for holy living.

“Being justified freely by his grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 3:24

Grace does not abolish obedience. It establishes the faithful in the law of Christ, whose fulfillment is love, charity, holiness, and service to one another.

questions.question: What are the Church’s eschatological beliefs?
questions.answer:
Marcion, following the eschatological teachings of the Apostle Paul, believed that a time of persecution and opposition awaited the faithful, led by a sinister figure Paul referred to as the “man of sin” or “son of perdition”:

“Let no man deceive you by any means: for that day shall not come, except there come a falling away first, and that man of sin be revealed, the son of perdition.”

II Thessalonians 2:3

In the Psalmicon, this adversary appears in symbolic form, where the redeemer triumphs over a great and terrifying beast:

“He overthrew by my hands the seven-headed dragon, and set me over its root, that I might destroy its offspring.” Psalmicon 22:5

Despite these references to evil and judgment, Marcion did not teach a visible return of Christ, nor was he consumed by apocalyptic fervor like many early Christians. His eschatology, much like that of Paul, centered on spiritual truths rather than literal, physical end-times scenarios. Paul makes this clear:

“Now this I say, brethren, that flesh and blood cannot inherit the kingdom of God; neither doth corruption inherit incorruption.”

I Corinthians 15:50

Paul taught that the resurrection would not involve earthly, perishable bodies, but that the saved would receive spiritual bodies fit for the eternal kingdom. Marcion agreed with this view and rejected any notion of bodily resurrection.

Apelles, Marcion’s disciple, reaffirmed this interpretation. He taught that salvation was reserved for the soul alone, and that the physical body would not be raised. The soul, once redeemed, would ascend to heaven, freed from the corrupt and dying flesh.

Both Marcion and Apelles derived their views entirely from the Pauline epistles. Marcion’s soteriology, centered on the boundless grace of God, naturally extended into an eschatology in which all believers would be spiritually delivered and made incorruptible in the presence of the true God.

A later and unusually explicit witness to this “non-bodily” reading appears in the Dialogue of Adamantius, where the Marcionite Megethius answers that Christ came to save “only the soul,” and the Marcionite Marcus presses the point even further by refusing salvation-language for “body” or even “soul,” insisting instead upon the saving of the “spirit.”

questions.question: What is perfect amillennialism?
questions.answer:
Marcion upheld the doctrine of perfect amillennialism, the belief that there would be no literal thousand-year reign of Christ on Earth. Instead, he taught that Christ’s kingdom is spiritual and heavenly, not political or earthly.

He stands among the earliest Christians known to have explicitly opposed an earthly millennial kingdom. For Marcion, the expectation that Christ would return physically to Earth to inaugurate a temporal kingdom before the final judgment contradicted the spiritual nature of Christ’s kingdom.

His amillennial position finds strong support in scripture. Jesus himself told the Pharisees that the coming of God’s kingdom would not be an observable event, but a spiritual reality already present among them:

“And when he was questioned by the Pharisees, when the kingdom of God cometh, he answered them and said, The kingdom of God cometh not with observation: Neither shall they say, Lo here! or, lo there! for, behold, the kingdom of God is within you.”

Evangelicon 15:36–37

The Apostle Paul also spoke of the kingdom of God not in material terms, but in moral and spiritual language:

“For the kingdom of God is not meat and drink; but righteousness, and peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 12:17

Paul further affirmed that the new dispensation began with the resurrection of Christ, describing him as the firstborn from the dead:

“And he is the head of the body, the church: who is the beginning, the firstborn from the dead; that in all things he might have the preeminence.”

Colossians 1:18

Marcion believed that Christ now reigns from heaven, seated at the right hand of the Father, and that this reign is ongoing through the Church. According to this view, the gospel age is the present manifestation of Christ’s kingdom on earth. Good and evil continue to coexist in the world, including within the Church itself, as illustrated in the parable of the wheat and tares. Marcion understood the Church’s mission of proclaiming the gospel as the true and final expression of Christ’s reign.

Ironically, many later Catholic theologians, such as Clement of Alexandria, Origen, Dionysius of Alexandria, and Eusebius of Caesarea, would come to share Marcion’s amillennial convictions. Even some of Marcion’s fiercest opponents, including Epiphanius of Salamis and Ephrem the Syrian, advocated for what might be described as an imperfect form of amillennialism, coming close to Marcion’s own teaching.

questions.question: What is Hell?
questions.answer:
Hell was the prison of condemned souls held under death, Satan, and the powers and principalities of this world before the descent of Christ. It was not the Father’s everlasting torture chamber, but a realm of captivity, darkness, accusation, and bondage beneath the dominion of the lower powers.

Christ destroyed Hell in the Harrowing of Hell. By descending into the lower parts of the earth and rising again as the firstborn from the dead, He broke the gates of the prison-house, freed the righteous dead, and ended Hell’s universal claim over mankind.

“Wherefore he saith, When he ascended up on high, he led captivity captive, and gave gifts unto men. Now that he ascended, what is it but that he also descended first into the lower parts of the earth?”

Laodiceans 4:8–9

Therefore Hell no longer exists as the unbroken prison of mankind. If the name is still used after Christ’s victory, it refers to the grim realm of Satan, devils, and fallen lower powers awaiting final defeat. It is not the destiny of ordinary ignorant souls, nor is it a place where God the Father preserves souls in endless torment.

When the Church speaks of Hell after the harrowing of hell, it does not mean a continuing prison for ordinary human souls, but either the defeated realm of fallen powers, the abyss of devils, the grave, or a figure of final destruction.

See also: Question 12.04, “What is the harrowing of hell?”; Question 12.06, “What is annihilationism?”; Question 12.05, “Does the Church believe in eternal damnation?”

questions.question: What is the harrowing of hell?
questions.answer:
Marcionites affirmed the harrowing of hell, an event also reported by Tertullian, Irenaeus, and Epiphanius, grounding the doctrine in Laodiceans 4:8–9 and Colossians 1:18. These verses teach that Christ first “descended into the lower parts of the earth” and then rose as “the firstborn from the dead.” This descent belongs to the same ransom and liberation logic central to Marcionite soteriology: mankind was held in bondage under sin, death, Satan, and the lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions of the present age. Christ, sent by God the Father, freely offered his own life as the ransom, for, as Marcion proclaims, “We are the price of the blood of Jesus.”

The distinction between the Apellean and Lucanist schools affects how this captivity is described, but not the truth of the ransom itself. Traditional Apelleans, preserving the original teaching of Marcion, identified Satan with the lower maker of the physical world, the being called in the Apostolicon “the god of this world” and called in the Evangelicon “the prince of this world.” In this framework, Christ’s descent shattered the dominion of Satan, who held mankind under the bondage of the lower order. Lucanists, by contrast, distinguished the just “god of this world” from Satan as an evil third power. In their framework, Christ’s descent liberated souls from the lower order, sin, death, and Satan, while preserving the distinction between the just maker of the physical world and the evil adversary. Yet both schools confessed the same essential doctrine: Christ descended as liberator and purchased souls out of bondage.

When Christ entered Hell, he broke its gates and destroyed its claim over mankind. This descent was essential in two respects. First, it secured the freedom of all future believers from the dominion of Satan, death, and the lower powers. Second, it rescued the righteous dead who had already perished before his Advent, liberating them from darkness and bringing them into the life revealed by God the Father.

This should not be misunderstood as a denial of the Marcionite doctrine of transmigration of souls. The Harrowing does not mean that every soul in every condition was finally and immediately translated into the Father’s kingdom. Rather, it means that Christ broke the universal prison-house of death and ended Hell’s absolute dominion over human souls. The righteous dead were liberated. Future believers were secured. Souls capable of repentance and reception were no longer abandoned to darkness without hope, but placed within the economy of Christ’s victory.

Marcionite teaching on transmigration clarifies this point. Natural souls that never truly heard the Gospel, or that remained imperfect and bound to the world-order without committing the Eternal Sin, may pass through remedial embodiment until they encounter the Gospel in due season. For such souls, Christ’s Harrowing does not abolish the need for repentance, awakening, and deliverance. Rather, it opens the way by which such deliverance becomes possible. The descent of Christ breaks the tyranny of death; transmigration explains the remedial path by which certain souls may still be brought to the light of the Gospel.

In this sense, the Harrowing and transmigration are not contradictory doctrines. They answer different parts of the same problem. The Harrowing proclaims Christ’s objective victory over Hell, death, Satan, and the lower powers. Transmigration explains how souls who had not yet truly received the Gospel may continue through the present world-order until their debt is exhausted, their bondage exposed, and the light of Christ is encountered. The one is Christ’s cosmic victory; the other is the soul’s remedial journey under that victory.

This is why the Evangelicon’s language of prison and release is so important:

“When thou goest with thine adversary to the magistrate, as thou art in the way, give diligence that thou mayest be delivered from him; lest he hale thee to the judge, and the judge deliver thee to the officer, and the officer cast thee into prison. I tell thee, thou shalt not depart thence, till thou hast paid the very last mite.”

Evangelicon 11:70–71

In Marcionite reading, this “prison” may describe embodiment within the lower world-order and the soul’s remedial passage through judgment until what is lacking has been completed. Christ’s Harrowing breaks the finality of that prison. It ensures that bondage is not absolute, death is not sovereign, and Hell cannot hold those whom Christ liberates. Yet it does not erase the distinction between spiritual souls awakened by the Gospel, natural souls still passing through remedial bondage, and those who have committed the Eternal Sin.

Those who willfully reject the Spirit in the manner Christ declares unforgivable are not included within remedial transmigration. They pass into destruction, in accordance with Marcionite annihilationism. Marcion echoes the principle in Marcion’s Epistle to the Magnesians: “For if He should imitate us according to our deeds, we are lost.” The Apostle Paul likewise warns of those “who shall be punished with everlasting destruction from the presence of the Lord, and from the glory of his power” (II Thessalonians 1:9). The punishment is not eternal conscious torment under the rule of Satan, nor endless transmigration without limit, but destruction, exclusion from life, and final non-being.

Since the Harrowing, Hell is no longer the unbroken prison of mankind. It is the grim domain of Satan and his devils, fallen lower powers destined to be vanquished when the Son’s victory is complete. Satan remains real, hostile, and active in the present age, but his dominion has been broken. He is not a second co-eternal, co-equal god. He is a fallen angel, a defeated adversary, and a lower power beneath God the Father.

The Psalmicon enshrines this triumph in song, praising Christ before whom “The abysses were dissolved before the Lord: and darkness was destroyed by His appearance” (Psalmicon 31:1) and who declares, “And I made a congregation of living men among his dead, and spake with them by living lips” (Psalmicon 42:19). These images do not require the denial of transmigration. They proclaim that darkness has been broken, the captives have been gathered, and the dead are no longer beyond the reach of Christ’s victory.

Thus, the Harrowing secures Marcionite confidence in total liberation without collapsing all souls into the same immediate destiny. The righteous dead are freed. Future believers are safe in Christ. Natural souls capable of repentance may still pass through remedial transmigration until they encounter the Gospel and are awakened. Those guilty of the Eternal Sin pass into destruction. Satan’s tyranny is broken, Hell has lost its absolute claim, and the lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions now await their final defeat when the victory of the Son is complete.

See also: Question 11.04, “What is the ransom theory of atonement?”; Question 12.06, “What is annihilationism?”

questions.question: Does the Church believe in eternal damnation?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church denies the doctrine of eternal conscious torment. We do not believe that God the Father created an everlasting torture chamber, nor do we believe that any soul is condemned to suffer without end. Hell, as commonly preached, is not the gospel of Christ or Paul. It is a later doctrine built chiefly from writings outside the original Marcionite canon, especially Revelation, II Peter, Jude, and the hellfire passages of Matthew.

Within the Evangelicon and Apostolicon, the fate of the soul is not eternal torture, but bondage, corruption, death, purification through suffering, transmigration, and final deliverance by the grace of God the Father. The physical, fleshly, and visible world is real, and its suffering is real, but its punishments are not endless. They belong to the present age, the realm of flesh, decay, and the powers that rule this world.

Paul teaches that creation itself is subject to corruption, but that this bondage will not last forever:

“Because the creature itself also shall be delivered from the bondage of corruption into the glorious liberty of the children of God.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 7:21

The Marcionite Church therefore rejects eternal damnation, but it does not reject every form of destruction. We believe annihilation applies only to those who commit the Eternal Sin, the settled blasphemy against the Holy Spirit described in Question 12.07.

Even here, however, annihilation is not eternal torment. It is not endless conscious suffering, nor a soul preserved forever in fire. It is final destruction. All other souls remain within the physical, fleshly, and visible world and transmigrate until they are saved. Epiphanius, though hostile to Marcion, preserves this point when he says that Marcion taught the salvation of souls rather than bodies and spoke of reincarnations and transmigrations from body to body.

This also explains the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus in the Evangelicon. The parable is not a map of the Father’s kingdom, nor a proof of eternal hell. Christ uses the imagery of Abraham, Moses, the prophets, the place of the dead, torment, and the great divide to expose the failure of the false covenant. Abraham cannot save. Moses cannot persuade. The prophets cannot deliver. Even within the physical, fleshly, and visible world, there is no true rescue. The Father’s salvation must come from beyond that world entirely.

Therefore, we deny eternal damnation because it contradicts the gospel of grace. There is judgment, suffering, bondage, death, corruption, and, for the eternal sin alone, annihilation. But there is no eternal conscious torment. The Father does not preserve souls forever in pain. He saves, and the final word for all who can be saved is not hell, wrath, or endless punishment, but deliverance from corruption into the liberty of the children of God.

questions.question: What is annihilationism?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians affirm the doctrine of annihilationism—the belief that condemned souls will ultimately be destroyed and cease to exist, rather than endure eternal conscious torment in Hell. This applies, in the strictest and most final sense, to those who commit the Eternal Sin: the settled blasphemy against the Holy Spirit for which Christ declares there is no forgiveness. See Question 12.07, “What is the eternal sin?”

Thus, for the unforgivable and finally hardened—those who blaspheme the Holy Spirit and therefore remain outside mercy—judgment is not remedial, and it is not endless torture, but final extinction. This view stands in contrast both to the doctrine of eternal damnation and to universalism, which claims that all souls will eventually be saved.

Annihilationism is rooted in the principle of conditional immortality—the belief that the human soul is not inherently immortal but receives eternal life only as a gift from God. In this view, the wicked do not continue in endless suffering but are completely extinguished, their being consumed because of their incompatibility with God’s holiness and justice. God grants immortality only to those who receive it through faith and righteousness; those who persist into the Eternal Sin remain unforgiven, and therefore do not receive life.

Marcion of Sinope alludes to this doctrine in Marcion’s Epistle to the Magnesians, writing:

For if He should imitate us according to our deeds, we are lost. (Magnesians 8:2)

This reflects the Marcionite understanding that judgment is real and deserved, yet not vindictive or eternal in nature—rather, it is final in its destruction, especially where forgiveness is explicitly excluded.

The Apostle Paul supports this perspective, teaching that the end result of judgment upon the wicked is their utter destruction:

Who shall be punished with everlasting destruction from the presence of the Lord, and from the glory of his power. (II Thessalonians 1:9)

Paul also declares that death itself—the final enemy—will be abolished:

The last enemy that shall be destroyed is death. (I Corinthians 15:26)

If death is destroyed, it cannot be perpetuated endlessly in conscious form; it must be fully removed, which entails the cessation of existence for those who remain under its power—preeminently those who remain unforgiven by committing the Eternal Sin.

Christ’s own teaching in the Evangelicon uses vivid parables to convey this truth. In the parable of the wheat and the tares, the weeds sown by the enemy are gathered at the harvest, bound, and burned—an image of complete eradication rather than ongoing torment:

Another parable put he forth unto them, saying, The kingdom of heaven is likened unto a man which sowed good seed in his field: But while men slept, his enemy came and sowed tares among the wheat, and went his way. But when the blade was sprung up, and brought forth fruit, then appeared the tares also. So the servants of the householder came and said unto him, Sir, didst not thou sow good seed in thy field? from whence then hath it tares? He said unto them, An enemy hath done this. The servants said unto him, Wilt thou then that we go and gather them up? But he said, Nay; lest while ye gather up the tares, ye root up also the wheat with them. Let both grow together until the harvest: and in the time of harvest I will say to the reapers, Gather ye together first the tares, and bind them in bundles to burn them: but gather the wheat into my barn. (Evangelicon 12:13-19)

The tares are consumed completely, just as chaff is destroyed in flames without remainder; they do not continue in existence once the field is cleansed.

In Luke, Christ gives an unambiguous warning of God’s power to end life entirely:

Fear him, which after he hath killed hath power to cast into hell; yea, I say unto you, Fear him. (Evangelicon 11:5)

This is the authority not merely to kill the body but to remove the whole person from existence—an authority that bears its most fearful weight where forgiveness is denied.

Paul echoes the same finality when speaking of those who reject the Gospel:

For the preaching of the cross is to them that perish foolishness; but unto us which are saved it is the power of God. (I Corinthians 1:18)

And again:

For the wages of sin is death; but the gift of God is life through Jesus Christ our Lord. (Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 5:23)

The contrast is absolute—death for the wicked, eternal life for the righteous. Death is not redefined as “life in torment,” but remains the absence of life itself.

In this way, annihilationism affirms both divine justice and divine mercy. Justice is upheld in the certain and final judgment of the wicked, above all, of those who persist in the Eternal Sin for which Christ declares there is no forgiveness. Mercy is upheld in that God does not sustain their existence forever in misery. This is neither the cruelty of endless punishment nor the compromise of universal restoration, but the just and final removal of all that is incompatible with the kingdom of the Father.

See also: Question 10.05, “What is the transmigration of souls?”; Question 12.07, “What is the eternal sin?”

questions.question: What is the eternal sin?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians affirm the reality of the eternal sin—the one sin Christ Himself declares to be unforgivable.

In the Evangelicon, the Lord draws a clear distinction between sins that may be remitted and a final blasphemy that remains without pardon:

“And every one, who shall speak against the Son of man, it shall be forgiven him: but unto him that speak against the Holy Ghost it shall not be forgiven.” (Evangelicon 11:9)

For this reason, we confess that there exists a genuine “sin unto death”: a willful and settled opposition to the Holy Spirit’s witness to Christ. It is not a moment of weakness, confusion, or ignorance, but a hardened repudiation of the very grace by which forgiveness is given. Where forgiveness is refused at the root, forgiveness cannot be received.

Therefore, the Eternal Sin stands as Christ’s own warning that divine mercy is not mocked: God the Father freely forgives all who turn to Him through the Gospel, yet He does not absolve those who knowingly blaspheme the Holy Spirit and persist in that defiance.

See also: Question 10.05, “What is the transmigration of souls?”; Question 12.06, “What is annihilationism?”

questions.question: What becomes of the soul after death?
questions.answer:
After bodily death, the soul continues under the judgment and providence of God. The faithful are received into rest and drawn toward the Kingdom of God. Souls not yet reconciled to the Gospel continue according to their condition: the unlearned transmigrate without penal torment until they encounter the Gospel, while souls requiring correction remain subject to judgment and transmigration until they are saved or, in the case of the eternal sin, destroyed.

“For I am in a strait betwixt two, having a desire to depart, and to be with Christ; which is far better.”

Philippians 1:23

The death of the body therefore does not end personal existence or divine judgment. It reveals more fully the soul’s dependence upon Christ and the necessity of salvation.

questions.question: What is the fate of the unlearned?
questions.answer:
The unlearned are those souls who never truly heard, understood, or received the Gospel of Jesus Christ, and who did not knowingly blaspheme the Holy Spirit. Their condition is ignorance and incompleteness, not final condemnation.

The Marcionite Church teaches that such souls transmigrate through remedial embodiment until they encounter the light of the Gospel in due season. They do not suffer penal torment merely for what they never knew. Their passage is one of continued mercy and instruction under the victory of Christ, not the cruelty of endless punishment.

Christ Himself prayed for the ignorant from the cross:

“Then said Jesus, Father, forgive them; for they know not what they do.”

Evangelicon 22:49

This prayer does not make ignorance saving by itself; salvation is still through Christ. But it shows that the Father’s mercy distinguishes ignorance from hardened rebellion. The unlearned therefore remain within the remedial order of transmigration until they are awakened by the Gospel, rather than being consigned to Hell or torment.

See also: Question 10.05, “What is the transmigration of souls?”; Question 12.08, “What becomes of the soul after death?”; Question 12.10, “What is the fate of children?”

questions.question: What is the fate of children?
questions.answer:
Children are born sinless. Until they can understand right from wrong, they continue to be sinless and are not morally accountable for unbelief, transgression, or ignorance. Therefore, if tragedy befalls infants, babies, or young children before such understanding, they are received directly by God the Father and do not transmigrate.

The Church does not infer hidden guilt in infants or young children from the preexistence or transmigration of souls. In this present life, before the child can discern good from evil and personally consent to sin, the child is regarded as innocent before God.

The Evangelicon bears witness to the innocence and nearness of children to the Kingdom:

“And they were bringing unto him also the infants, that he should touch them: but when the disciples saw it, they rebuked them. But Jesus called them unto him, and said, Suffer the little children to come unto me, and hinder them not: for of such is the kingdom of God. Verily I say unto you, Whosoever shall not receive the kingdom of God as a little child shall in no wise enter into it.”

Evangelicon 16:15–17

The Church therefore rejects the claim that infants and young children are guilty by birth or in need of punishment after death. Their innocence is not earned by works, nor supplied by ritual apart from faith; it belongs to their condition before moral accountability. Such little ones belong to the mercy of the Father revealed by Christ.

See also: Question 10.06, “How does the Marcionite Church understand original sin?”; Question 14.03, “What is Holy Baptism?”

questions.question: What is the final judgment?
questions.answer:
The final judgment is the definitive manifestation of Christ’s victory and the righteous determination of the destiny of souls. It is not the establishment of an earthly millennium, but the completion of the Kingdom of God and the end of the present world-order.

Those saved through Christ enter the life of the Kingdom. Those who remain under correction are judged according to truth, while those guilty of the eternal sin are annihilated rather than preserved in everlasting torment.

Immortality is not the natural possession of every soul but the gift of God bestowed through Christ. The final judgment manifests this distinction by granting incorruptible life to the saved and bringing the finally unforgiven to an end rather than sustaining them eternally in torment.

See also: Question 12.06, “What is annihilationism?”; Question 12.07, “What is the eternal sin?”

questions.question: How should Christians pray?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians pray not as servants of the false covenant, nor as worshippers bound to the prayer-forms of the ‘god of this world’, but as children of God the Father. The Holy Spirit helps our weakness and intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words. Prayer is therefore both a discipline commanded of the faithful and a grace already working within them.

“Pray without ceasing.”

I Thessalonians 5:17

“Be careful for nothing; but in every thing by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known unto God.”

Philippians 4:6

The ordinary daily rule of Marcionite prayer is the Lord’s Prayer at the third hour, 9:00 A.M.; the sixth hour, Noon; and the ninth hour, 3:00 P.M. These hours recall the Passion of Jesus Christ and order the day around remembrance of the Lord. In preparation for the Sunday Eucharist, the celebrant and the communicants recite the Lord’s Prayer three times at the ninth hour on Saturday, after which the Eucharistic fast begins and continues until Holy Communion.

This rule is also illuminated by the prayer of Christ upon the cross: “Then said Jesus, Father, forgive them; for they know not what they do.” The Lord addresses the Father directly and asks forgiveness rather than vengeance. In this He teaches the Church to pray beyond accusation, retaliation, and the law, appealing to the mercy of God the Father.

Marcionite Christians reject the seven daily fixed prayer times as a Judaizing discipline rooted in the false covenant. This does not diminish the apostolic command to pray without ceasing, to make requests known to God with thanksgiving, and to persevere in prayer through the Spirit. The Church’s fixed daily form is the Lord’s Prayer; other personal prayers are offered freely and sincerely rather than recited from an imposed script.

Prayer is also offered before meals so that food may be received with thanksgiving. Before Divine Liturgy, the celebrant and the communicants wash their hands and faces, and the celebrant washes the feet of the communicants, who may also wash one another’s feet. These acts are not legal purifications but signs of humility, service, and reverence before the common worship of the Church.

Marcionite Christians ordinarily pray facing west. When space and circumstance permit, they stand with their arms extended in the ancient orans posture. They may also kneel or bow according to the appointed rubric, the character of the prayer, or personal devotion. No single bodily posture is required for every private prayer.

Apart from the Lord’s Prayer and the prayers appointed within the Litourgicon, Christian prayer should be personal rather than the mechanical repetition of a prepared text. The believer should speak truthfully to the Father in his or her own words, naming sin, need, gratitude, suffering, captivity, hope, and intercession without trusting in eloquence or formula.

At times the Spirit does not merely carry the believer’s words but prays through the believer beyond words. Such prayer cannot be manufactured, staged, or commanded. It is not spectacle or public performance, but the Spirit of God the Father helping human weakness and carrying prayer beyond the accusation of this world.

When a believer is oppressed by fear, shame, appetite, debt, decay, accusation, grief, bondage, or any other captivity of this world, that captivity should be named plainly before the Father in personal prayer. The believer may then remain silent, receiving quietness, tears, groaning, or wordless intercession without performance or fear.

Thus Marcionite prayer has both order and freedom. The faithful pray the Lord’s Prayer at the appointed hours, pray before meals, face west, observe the bodily disciplines appropriate to worship, intercede for others, and pray without ceasing. Above all, they trust the Holy Spirit to carry their personal prayers to the Father through Jesus Christ.

See also: Question 13.02, “What form of the Lord’s Prayer does the Church use?”; Question 13.04, “Why does the Church pray westward?”

questions.question: What form of the Lord’s Prayer does the Church use?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite version of the Lord’s Prayer, preserved in Evangelicon 10:2–4, opens with the striking invocation, “Let Thy Holy Spirit come upon us, and cleanse us.”

The closing petition also reveals a key theological distinction. The Marcionite version reads, “suffer us not to be brought into temptation,” rather than “lead us not,” softening the tone and avoiding any suggestion that God initiates trials. Tertullian preserves this permissive reading in Latin, and it aligns with the passive Greek verb used. This phrasing fits Marcion’s vision of the true God—who never tests, tempts, or harms—unlike Yahweh, who in the Hebrew Bible hardens hearts, creates evil (Isaiah 45:7), and commands destruction. The shift preserves the Father’s moral purity and aligns the prayer with the Gospel Paul preached. Modern scholars such as Harnack, BeDuhn, Vinzent, and Klinghardt affirm the priority of the Marcionite version. Rome’s discomfort with the older language resurfaced in modern times: in 2008, the Vatican banned the use of the name “Yahweh” in hymns, and in 2019, Bishop Francis revised the Lord’s Prayer to read, “do not let us fall into temptation”—a move that echoes Marcion’s ancient insight. The full version of the Lord’s Prayer, as preserved in the Evangelicon, the Gospel-book of the original Testamentum, is:

Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be Thy name. Let Thy Holy Spirit come upon us, and cleanse us; Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done, as in heaven, so on earth. Give us day by day our daily bread. And forgive us our sins; for we also forgive every one that is indebted to us. And suffer us not to be brought into temptation; but deliver us from evil. Amen.

The invocation of the Holy Spirit is not supported by patristic testimony alone. It survived in the writings of Gregory of Nyssa and Maximus the Confessor and in Greek manuscripts 162 and 700, providing further witness to an early form of the prayer preserved by the Marcionite Church.

This same form is used in the Divine Liturgy and in the threefold recitation at the ninth hour on Saturday. It is the Church’s principal fixed prayer; prayers outside the appointed rites are ordinarily offered personally and in the believer’s own words.

questions.question: How does the Church conclude prayers?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians have historically ended their prayers with the word Amen, much like other Christian traditions. The Apostle Paul concluded all his epistles with Amen, and it also appears in several doxologies throughout his writings.

The term Amen means “truly,” “verily,” “so be it,” or “it is true.” Though it has Semitic roots and entered Greek from Aramaic and Hebrew-speaking Christians, its meaning is universal and does not carry any inherent theological association with the false covenant. It simply affirms the truth and sincerity of what has been spoken in prayer.

In contrast, the use of Hallelujah is strictly forbidden in Marcionite Christian prayer and liturgy. Hallelujah translates directly to “Praise Yahweh” in Hebrew and bears an explicit connection to the false god of the Hebrew Bible. Since Marcionite Christians reject the worship of Yahweh, the term is considered theologically offensive and must not be used.

questions.question: Why does the Church pray westward?
questions.answer:
Ephrem the Syrian attested that Marcionite Christians prayed facing west, a practice known as Ad occidentem. While this custom is not explicitly commanded in the canonical scriptures of the Testamentum, it does not conflict with any scriptural teaching. Rather, it appears to have developed organically as a distinctive tradition within Marcionite Christian communities.

The westward orientation likely arose to set Marcionite Christians apart from their Judeo-Christian counterparts, who preserved the Jewish custom of praying toward the east—Ad orientem—a practice rooted in the Hebrew Bible’s creation narrative, which situates the Garden of Eden in the east.

In deliberate contrast, Marcionite Christians rejected these eastward associations tied to the false covenant and instead instituted their own westward prayer direction as a unique expression of their faith and separation from Judaism.

questions.question: How does the Church understand intercession?
questions.answer:
The Apostle Paul presents intercession—prayer to God on behalf of others—as a necessary part of Christian life. He regularly prayed for the churches and asked the faithful to pray for him, showing that intercession is mutual, communal, and directed to God the Father through Jesus Christ.

The Divine Liturgy gives intercession an ecclesiastical form. The Church prays for the Marcionite Church, her bishops and presbyters, all communicants, and those who preach the Gospel; for rulers and civil authorities; for the sick, afflicted, poor, imprisoned, and all who suffer; and for the strengthening of the faithful in faith, hope, and charity. The communicants answer the appointed petitions, “O God our Father, hear our prayer.”

Outside the Divine Liturgy, Christians should intercede in their own words according to the actual needs of the persons and communities for whom they pray. The Church does not require a separate scripted form for private intercession. What matters is that the prayer be truthful, charitable, persevering, and addressed to God the Father through Christ in the Holy Spirit.

Intercession is offered for the living and for the unity, endurance, healing, repentance, and salvation of the Church. It is not prayer directed to saints or other intermediary beings, for petitions and spiritual appeals belong to God alone.

questions.question: How is Divine Liturgy conducted?
questions.answer:
The Divine Liturgy is the common Eucharistic worship of the Marcionite Church. Because no complete ancient Marcionite order has survived, the present rite is a reconstruction designed as a simple and basic Pauline liturgy in keeping with the earliest surviving pre-Nicene forms of Christian worship. It is arranged according to the Evangelicon and Apostolicon, the Psalmicon, ancient Christian liturgical practice, and Marcionite teaching concerning the Holy Mysteries and Holy Rites.

The rite may be celebrated in a church, chapel, or private home by a bishop or presbyter serving as celebrant, together with at least two communicants. Its simplicity allows the Church to worship without requiring an elaborate building, choir, or ceremonial establishment.

After the preparation for the service, the Divine Liturgy follows a seven-part order: the Introductory Rites; the Liturgy of the Word; the Preparatory Rites; the Liturgy of the Eucharist; the Communion Rites; the Concluding Rites; and the Rites of the Holy Lovefeast. Within this order the Church proclaims the Evangelicon and Apostolicon, sings or reads psalms from the Psalmicon, receives a homily, offers intercession, confesses the Pauline faith, exchanges the Holy Kiss, presents the bread and mixed chalice, gives thanks to God the Father, invokes the Holy Spirit, prays the Lord’s Prayer, receives Holy Communion, and shares the Holy Lovefeast, the Church’s Holy Rite of charity.

Cyril of Jerusalem warned Christians traveling among rival congregations not to mistake a Marcionite assembly for a Catholic one, so similar were their outward prayers, rites, and forms of worship. The reconstructed rite preserves this ancient liturgical seriousness while ordering every element according to Marcionite doctrine.

Before the service, the celebrant and the communicants wash their hands and faces, and the celebrant washes the feet of the communicants; the communicants may also wash one another’s feet. Men worship with their heads uncovered, and women cover their heads. The liturgy is celebrated in a language understood by the communicants. The celebrant and communicants pray facing west and, when space permits, stand with their arms extended. At every mention of the name of Jesus during the service, all present bow their heads in reverence.

The Divine Liturgy is ordinarily celebrated on the Lord’s Day in remembrance of the resurrection of Jesus Christ. At its conclusion, the celebrant and communicants participate in the Holy Lovefeast, contributing according to ability and sharing without preference for the wealthy over the poor. A portion shall be reserved for an absent communicant prevented from attending by sickness or infirmity. When appropriate, a portion may also be reserved for one prevented by imprisonment, distance, or another serious necessity.

The Divine Liturgy is not intended to be the longest or maximum possible form of Marcionite worship. It is the foundational order upon which the Church may build. Suitable psalms, hymns, prayers, intercessions, readings, and ceremonial actions may be added for feasts, seasons, pastoral needs, or local custom, provided that they remain scriptural, Pauline, Marcionite, orderly, reverent, and directed toward the proclamation of Christ and the edification and unity of the communicants.

questions.question: Why are three Christians required for Divine Liturgy?
questions.answer:
The Divine Liturgy requires one celebrant and at least two communicants, making a minimum assembly of three Christians. This rule expresses that the Eucharist is an act of the gathered Church rather than a private devotion or a solitary action of the clergy.

Christ joins agreement in prayer with His presence among the gathered faithful:

“Again I say unto you, That if two of you shall agree on earth as touching any thing that they shall ask, it shall be done for them of my Father which is in heaven. For where two or three are gathered together in my name, there am I in the midst of them.”

Evangelicon 15:5–6

The Church applies these words by requiring two communicants to agree and participate together under the presidency of the celebrant. The minimum assembly can therefore make the congregational responses, exchange the Holy Kiss, receive from the one bread and cup, and wait for one another at the Holy Lovefeast. As the Apostle teaches:

“For we being many are one bread, and one body: for we are all partakers of that one bread.”

I Corinthians 10:17

The requirement is therefore a rule of ecclesiastical order grounded in the scriptural character of gathered worship. It does not mean that Christ is absent from the prayer of one or two believers; it means that the Church’s celebration of the Holy Mystery should visibly include both an ordained celebrant and a congregation.

questions.question: In what language is Divine Liturgy conducted?
questions.answer: Divine Liturgy and prayers within the Marcionite Church are to be conducted in a language fully understood by those participating—typically the vernacular. However, the Church places no restriction on the use of traditional liturgical languages such as Latin or Koine Greek, provided the congregation can comprehend the words being spoken. What matters most is not the language itself, but the clarity of understanding, so that all may participate fully in the worship and meaning of the Holy Rites.

questions.question: How does the Church honor the saints?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians reject the veneration of saints, regarding it as a form of idolatry, and view the practice of canonization as a form of deification. Marcionite theology holds that no meaningful distinction can be made between veneration and worship; both ultimately divert the soul’s attention from its rightful focus—the worship of God alone.

In Marcionite belief, the term “saint” refers broadly to any true follower of Jesus Christ. This usage reflects how the Apostle Paul frequently addresses believers in his letters, identifying all members of the Body of Christ as saints by virtue of their relationship with the Lord. Accordingly, intercessory prayers directed to saints—whether living or deceased—are viewed as improper, since they attribute to others a form of honor and appeal that should be reserved for God alone.

That said, it is considered acceptable to admire and honor biblical saints—those specifically mentioned or alluded to in the scriptural books of the Testamentum—such as the Apostles and early Disciples of Jesus Christ. Marcionite churches and institutions may be named in their honor, provided it remains clear that reverence is not worship, and that no prayer or spiritual petition is ever directed to them.

questions.question: How does the Church regard icons and religious images?
questions.answer:
From a Marcionite perspective, the veneration of sacred images—iconodulism—is appropriate when directed toward the true God revealed through Jesus Christ, not the false god of the Hebrew Bible. Icons serve as visual testimonies to the goodness, mercy, and love of the Father made known through His Son.

Christ appeared truly among men—not as an illusion, but as the perfect image of the Father’s goodness and grace. Depicting Him in sacred art affirms His manifestation in history and His work of deliverance. Icons are not worshiped, but serve as visual proclamations of the Gospel and reminders of the true God’s revelation through Christ.

In honoring the image of Christ, we honor the revelation of the good God, made known in love and truth—not in fear or wrath.

questions.question: What is the Holy Sign?
questions.answer:
The Holy Sign, commonly called the Sign of the Cross, is one of the Church’s Holy Rites. According to Tertullian, Marcionite Christians were among the earliest Christians to employ it as a regular devotional practice.

The Litourgicon appoints the Holy Sign at the beginning and conclusion of Divine Liturgy; before and after the Lord’s Prayer; before and after receiving Holy Communion; before the Holy Lovefeast; and within the Liturgies of Holy Baptism and Holy Chrismation. Marcionite Christians may also make the Sign of the Cross when entering or passing a church, following Holy Reconciliation, and at other suitable moments of personal devotion.

The gesture is performed by joining the first three fingers together to represent the Holy Trinity, while folding the remaining two into the palm as a sign of Christ’s dual nature. The hand is then moved from the forehead, to below the chest, to the left shoulder, then to the right shoulder, and finally placed over the heart with a bow of the head. The invocation accompanying the motion is Trinitarian: at the forehead, “In the name of the Father,” at the chest, “and of the Son,” across the shoulders, “and of the Holy Spirit,” and finally, “Amen.”

questions.question: Does the Church pray before meals?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians should pray before meals, giving thanks to God the Father and receiving food as a gift to be used with gratitude, moderation, and charity. Jesus Himself blessed bread before giving it to those who ate with Him:

“And it came to pass, as he sat at meat with them, he took bread, and blessed it, and brake, and gave to them.”

Evangelicon 23:29

The Church does not prescribe a fixed grace for ordinary meals. The prayer should be personal and offered in the believer’s own words, thanking the Father through Jesus Christ and remembering those who lack food. The appointed blessing of the Holy Lovefeast is different because it forms part of the Divine Liturgy and is therefore prayed according to the Litourgicon.

questions.question: Which Holy Mysteries does the Church recognize?
questions.answer:
Tertullian acknowledged that the worship of the Holy Mysteries practiced by Marcionite Christians bore notable similarity to that observed within his own Catholic tradition. Likewise, Cyril of Jerusalem warned Christians to take care not to mistake a Marcionite church for a Catholic one while traveling, further attesting to the resemblance in worship and liturgical structure between the two communities.

The Marcionite Church recognizes three Holy Mysteries of Christian initiation: Holy Baptism, Holy Chrismation, and Holy Communion.

Holy Baptism joins the believer to the death and resurrection of Christ; Holy Chrismation seals the baptized with the Holy Spirit; and Holy Communion completes the believer’s incorporation into the one bread and body of the Church. Upon receiving all three, a person is a fully initiated member of the Marcionite Church and is expected to wear the Chi Rho as a visible confession of allegiance to Christ.

Holy Baptism, the foundational Holy Mystery, is repeatedly affirmed in the canonical scriptures of the Testamentum:

“Know ye not, that so many of us as were baptized into Jesus Christ were baptized into his death? Therefore we are buried with him by baptism into death: that like as Christ was raised up from the dead by the glory of the Father, even so we also should walk in newness of life.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 5:3–4
