“For by one Spirit are we all baptized into one body… and have been all made to drink into one Spirit.”

I Corinthians 12:13

“Buried with him in baptism, wherein also ye are risen with him through the faith of the operation of God, who hath raised him from the dead.”

Colossians 2:12

Holy Chrismation, the sealing with the Holy Spirit following baptism, is reflected in the Apostle Paul’s words:

“In whom ye also trusted… after that ye believed, ye were sealed with that Holy Ghost of promise.”

Laodiceans 1:13

“And grieve not the Holy Ghost of God, whereby ye are sealed unto the day of redemption.”

Laodiceans 4:30

Holy Communion was instituted by Christ at the Last Supper and is affirmed both in the Evangelicon and the Apostolicon:

“And they rehearsed… how he became known to them in the breaking of the bread.”

Evangelicon 23:34

“This is my body, which is broken for you… For as often as ye eat this bread, and drink this cup, ye do shew the Lord’s death till he come.”

I Corinthians 11:24–27

The Church therefore does not extend the name Holy Mystery beyond these three. Other scriptural and ecclesiastical actions that support, order, and express the life of grace without themselves completing Christian initiation are received as Holy Rites. See also Question 14.02, “Which Holy Rites does the Church recognize?”

questions.question: Which Holy Rites does the Church recognize?
questions.answer:
Alongside the three Holy Mysteries, the Marcionite Church recognizes nine principal Holy Rites: Holy Reconciliation, Holy Matrimony, Holy Anointing, Holy Ordination, Holy Footwashing, Holy Veiling, the Holy Lovefeast, the Holy Kiss, and the Holy Sign.

Holy Rites are blessings, disciplines, and ecclesiastical actions grounded in scripture and received tradition. They dispose the faithful to grace, strengthen the Church’s common life, and express repentance, healing, ministry, charity, and worship, but they are not Holy Mysteries and do not complete Christian initiation.

The authority to forgive sins, passed from Christ to the Apostles, is the basis of Holy Reconciliation, the Holy Rite of confession and absolution:

“For with the heart man believeth unto righteousness; and with the mouth confession is made unto salvation.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 8:13

Holy Anointing, the Holy Rite of anointing the sick, continues the healing ministry of Jesus:

“To another faith by the same Spirit; to another the gifts of healing by the same Spirit.”

I Corinthians 12:9

Holy Ordination, the Holy Rite by which clergy are set apart for ecclesiastical office, was instituted by Christ and affirmed by Paul:

“…That repentance and remission of sins should be preached in his name among all nations.”

Evangelicon 23:43–45

“Now then we are ambassadors for Christ… be ye reconciled to God.”

II Corinthians 5:20

Holy Matrimony, the Holy Rite of covenant between man and woman, symbolizes the bond between Christ and the Church:

“For this cause shall a man leave his father and mother… This is a great mystery: but I speak concerning Christ and the church.”

Laodiceans 5:31–32

Holy Footwashing is a Holy Rite of humility and service instituted by Christ:

“He riseth from supper… and began to wash the disciples’ feet.”

Evangelicon 20:10–11

“Thou gavest me no water for my feet… Wherefore I say unto thee, Her sins, which are many, are forgiven; for she loved much.”

Evangelicon 5:45–49

The Holy Kiss is a Holy Rite and sign of peace and fellowship commanded by the Apostle Paul:

“All the brethren greet you. Greet ye one another with an holy kiss.”

I Corinthians 16:20

The Holy Sign, or Sign of the Cross, is a Holy Rite of confession and devotion by which the faithful mark themselves with the cross of the Lord Jesus Christ:

“But God forbid that I should glory, save in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ.”

Galatians 6:14

Holy Veiling is a Holy Rite and discipline required of women in worship, based on Paul’s directive:

“Every woman that prayeth or prophesieth with her head uncovered dishonoureth her head… But if it be a shame for a woman to be shorn or shaven, let her be covered.”

I Corinthians 11:3–15

Lastly, the Holy Lovefeast is one of the Church’s Holy Rites: a communal meal following Holy Communion and referenced in the Apostle’s instructions:

“Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together to eat, tarry one for another… And the rest will I set in order when I come.”

I Corinthians 11:33–34

These nine Holy Rites form an important part of Marcionite liturgical and ecclesiastical life. They are rooted in the teachings of Christ and Paul and in the received worship of the Church, but remain distinct in rank and purpose from the three Holy Mysteries.

See also: Question 13.11, “What is the Holy Sign?”; Question 14.01, “Which Holy Mysteries does the Church recognize?”; Question 14.16, “What is the Holy Lovefeast?”; Question 17.07, “What is the Church’s teaching on marriage?”

questions.question: What is Holy Baptism?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians do not practice infant baptism, as infants are incapable of repentance or belief—both of which are clear prerequisites for baptism according to Scripture. Infants are born without sin and are not morally accountable until they reach an age where they can understand the difference between good and evil. Until such time, they have no need for baptism; and if they die before moral discernment, they are not condemned for lack of baptism, but belong to the kingdom of God. Nowhere in the canonical scriptures of the Testamentum is infant baptism explicitly described or commanded.

For this reason, the Marcionite Church adheres to the practice of ‘Credobaptism,’ or ‘Believer’s Baptism.’ This is administered exclusively by full triple immersion—also known as trine immersion—in the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. The triple immersion recalls both the trinitarian formula and the three days and three nights our Lord Jesus Christ lay in the tomb.

Historical testimony supports this practice. Both Epiphanius and Eznik of Kolb confirm that Marcionite Christians observed trine immersion and permitted Deaconesses to baptize other women, in keeping with apostolic-era customs of modesty and propriety.

The Marcionite Church recognizes as valid only those baptisms that are performed in the trinitarian formula and by full triple immersion. Baptisms that do not meet these criteria require proper rebaptism upon entry into the Church.

In accordance with the practice of the earliest Christians, catechumens preparing for baptism are required to fast for the full day preceding their reception of Holy Baptism. This is an act of purification and solemn anticipation.

Tertullian further records that Marcionite Christians anointed the baptized with Holy Oil and gave them milk and honey as part of the complete post-baptismal initiation. In the present Litourgicon, the anointing and the offering of milk and honey are administered in the Liturgy of Holy Chrismation immediately following Holy Baptism. These signs express the sealing of the baptized with the Holy Spirit and the sweetness and incorruptibility of the true covenant in Christ, in contrast to the false Abrahamic and Mosaic covenants.

As Christ himself commanded:

“Go ye therefore, and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost.”

Evangelicon 24:73

This command, coupled with the apostolic pattern of repentance and faith preceding baptism, guides the Marcionite Church in its discipline concerning the Holy Mysteries and preserves the purity of Christ’s ordinance.

See also: Question 14.07, “What is Holy Chrismation?”; Question 14.08, “What is Holy Communion?”

questions.question: May infants be blessed if they are not baptized?
questions.answer:
The Church does not baptize infants, for Holy Baptism belongs to those who believe, repent, and confess Jesus Christ as Lord. Yet infants and young children may be brought before the Church for naming, prayer, and blessing.

In this rite the Church gives thanks for the child, prays that the child may be preserved in innocence, raised in the faith, and brought in due time to Holy Baptism by personal confession. This blessing is not baptism, does not regenerate, and does not admit the child to Holy Communion. It is an act of charity, thanksgiving, and pastoral care.

See also: Question 14.03, “What is Holy Baptism?”; Question 12.10, “What is the fate of children?”; Question 14.08, “What is Holy Communion?”.

questions.question: Does marriage or non-celibacy prevent Holy Baptism?
questions.answer:
Marriage and non-celibacy do not prevent Holy Baptism. A lawful marriage need not be dissolved before baptism, and the Church does not require celibacy as a condition of baptism.

Tertullian incorrectly alleged that Marcionite Christians refused baptism to those who were married, requiring spouses to divorce before receiving Holy Baptism. This accusation misrepresents the discipline of the Church.

The accusation also exploited a category error. Because Marcionites did not number marriage among the Holy Mysteries, heresiologists portrayed them as rejecting marriage itself. In fact, the Church receives Holy Matrimony as a Holy Rite and covenant, distinct from the three Holy Mysteries of initiation, while fully affirming lawful Christian marriage.

The Pauline privilege, derived from the Apostle Paul’s instructions in the First Epistle to the Corinthians, teaches that if one spouse remains outside the faith and refuses peace, the baptized spouse is not bound in bondage. This principle does not mean that baptism itself requires the dissolution of a lawful marriage.

This discipline must be read narrowly. It does not authorize the dissolution of a marriage between baptized Christians, nor does it require the baptized to abandon a peaceful spouse. The Marcionite Church does not permit the dissolution of a marriage between two baptized Christians. Such a separation is viewed as adultery and is strictly forbidden.

Tertullian also wrongly asserted that candidates for baptism in the Marcionite Church were required to take vows of celibacy. This claim mischaracterizes the Church’s ascetical discipline, its refusal to bless irregular unions, and its distinction between lawful Christian marriage and sexual bondage under the world-order.

Marcionite Christianity places no restrictions on lawful Christian marriage, nor does it mandate celibacy. These false accusations were propagated by Catholic heresiologists writing long after the death of Marcion of Sinope and without firsthand knowledge of Marcionite practice.

Indeed, Marcion of Sinope emphasized that Christian marriages should be conducted with ecclesiastical approval, underscoring the moral and pastoral integrity of the Church’s leadership. As he taught:

“It becometh men and women too, when they marry, to unite themselves with the consent of the bishop, that the marriage may be after the Lord and not after concupiscence.” —Metrodorus 5:7

It is possible that bishops within the Marcionite Church—including Marcion himself—declined to sanction certain marriages that appeared to be driven solely by lust rather than genuine Christian commitment. This pastoral discretion may have contributed to the false impression that Marcionite Christians rejected marriage altogether.

In truth, the Marcionite Church affirms the sanctity of Christian marriage, upholds the Pauline privilege as scripturally warranted, and rejects the imposition of celibacy as a prerequisite for baptism.

questions.question: Does the Church believe in baptism for the dead?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church does not believe in baptism for the dead.

This accusation comes from hostile heresiologists, not from Marcionite doctrine itself. The clearest form of the charge appears in John Chrysostom, who says that those “infected with the Marcionite heresy” hid “the living man under the couch of the dead” and then baptized him in place of the departed catechumen (John Chrysostom, Homily 40 on First Corinthians). A fragment attributed to Didymus the Blind makes the same charge more briefly: “The Marcionites baptize the living on behalf of dead unbelievers” (Didymus the Blind, Fragment on I Corinthians 15:29).

This is a late and absurd heresiological accusation. Tertullian, who is the earliest and closest of these witnesses to Marcion, discusses I Corinthians 15:29 directly in Against Marcion, yet he does not accuse Marcionites of performing any such proxy rite. Instead, he gives his own interpretation of the verse: “To be ‘baptized for the dead’ therefore means, in fact, to be baptized for the body” (Tertullian, Against Marcion 5.10). This matters. If such a grotesque practice had actually belonged to Marcionite religion in the second century, Tertullian would have had every polemical reason to mention it. He does not.

The Marcionite Church therefore rejects baptism for the dead as a made-up charge of the heresiologists. It belongs to the same class of hostile caricatures by which the enemies of Marcion tried to make Marcionite doctrine appear foolish, impious, or corrupt in its Holy Mysteries and Holy Rites.

Baptism for the dead is also contrary to Marcionite theology. Holy Baptism is not a magical act performed upon one body for the benefit of another. It is the living soul’s renunciation of the lower powers, its confession of God the Father and His Son Jesus Christ, and its entrance into the life of the Gospel. A corpse cannot confess, repent, believe, or receive illumination; neither can another man do these things in its stead.

Moreover, the Marcionite teaching on the transmigration of souls makes baptism for the dead a silly and useless concept. The departed soul is not trapped in dead flesh, waiting for another person to receive water on its behalf. The soul continues under divine judgment and correction until it is saved. Therefore, to baptize a living man for a dead man would accomplish nothing. It would neither cleanse the departed soul, nor change its course, nor supply faith where faith is absent.

See also: Question 10.05, “What is the transmigration of souls?”; Question 12.07, “What is the eternal sin?”

When the Apostle speaks of those “baptized for the dead” in I Corinthians 15:29, he is not establishing a Marcionite rite of proxy baptism. At most, he refers to a practice known among others in order to make an argument about resurrection and hope. The Marcionite Church receives Holy Baptism as a Holy Mystery for the living, not as a vain ceremony for the dead.

questions.question: What is Holy Chrismation?
questions.answer:
Chrismation is the Marcionite Christian term for what is commonly referred to as Confirmation. It is administered after Holy Baptism and is the second of the three Holy Mysteries of Christian initiation. Chrismation seals the baptized with the Holy Spirit and prepares the believer to receive Holy Communion, by which initiation is completed.

The Marcionite Church holds that individuals who have received a trinitarian credobaptism by full triple immersion—but who have not undergone Chrismation—must receive this Holy Mystery before proceeding to Holy Communion. Chrismation serves as the spiritual sealing of the believer and affirms the indwelling of the Holy Spirit, while full initiation into the visible communion of the Church is completed through reception of Holy Communion.

Tertullian attests that after baptism, Marcionite Christians were given a mixture of milk and honey to drink. This act likely formed part of the Chrismation rite. The consumption of milk and honey symbolized the believer’s entrance into the spiritual Promised Land of the Christian covenant—superior to the false covenant, which forbade the use of honey in sacrificial offerings due to its tendency to corrupt.

This symbolism is reflected in the Psalmicon, which contains the line:

“Distil thy dew upon us, and open thy rich fountains, which pour forth unto us milk and honey.” —Psalmicon 4:10

This practice underscores the Marcionite emphasis on the grace-filled nature of the true covenant in contrast to the legalism, corruption, and fleshly rituals of the false covenant of the Hebrew Bible.

questions.question: What is Holy Communion?
questions.answer:
Holy Communion is the Holy Mystery in which the faithful receive the consecrated bread and mixed chalice as participation in the body and blood of Jesus Christ. It proclaims His true suffering and death, unites the Church in one fellowship, and nourishes the baptized in the covenant of grace.

“The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not the communion of the blood of Christ?”

I Corinthians 10:16

Holy Communion is neither a bare memorial nor a repetition of Christ’s sacrifice. It is the Church’s mystical participation in the one saving work of the Lord and a pledge of fellowship with Him and with one another.

Holy Communion also requires unity, equality, and self-examination. The Apostle condemns divisions at the common table and the humiliation of the poor, for the one bread cannot rightly be received while the members of Christ despise one another. The Holy Mystery therefore joins remembrance of the Lord’s death to charity within His body, the Church.

questions.question: How is Holy Communion received?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians receive Holy Communion under both kinds, partaking of the consecrated bread and mixed chalice as the body and blood of Christ. Communion in one kind alone is considered incomplete.

The celebrant receives first. The communicants then approach in order and kneel before the celebrant. A portion of the consecrated bread is dipped into the chalice and placed directly upon the communicant’s tongue. The celebrant identifies the gift as the body of Christ given and the blood of Christ shed for the communicant, who answers, “Amen,” and reverently returns to his or her place.

This manner of reception preserves the ancient Christian practice of Communion upon the tongue, ensures reception from both the bread and chalice, and visibly expresses that the Holy Mystery is received rather than taken by private possession. After all have communicated, the service continues with the thanksgiving, hymn, prayer for perseverance, blessing, and dismissal appointed in the Divine Liturgy. No additional fixed private Thanksgiving Prayer is required.

questions.question: Why does the Church use a mixed chalice?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church prepares the chalice with wine mixed with a few drops of water. Tertullian, Ephrem the Syrian, and Eznik of Kolb attest the use of wine among Marcionites, while Epiphanius’s accusation concerning water is best understood alongside the ancient Christian custom of mingling the two.

The Litourgicon expressly directs the celebrant to prepare the mixed chalice in remembrance of the blood and water that came forth from the side of the Lord. The words over the chalice likewise declare that wine and water were mingled.

“But one of the soldiers with a spear pierced his side, and forthwith came there out blood and water.”

Evangelicon 22:68

The mixed chalice therefore preserves an early Christian practice and bears sacred witness to Christ’s true suffering, the blood He shed, and the life and cleansing bestowed through Him.

questions.question: How does the Church understand the Eucharistic presence?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians hold that, in the Eucharist, the entire substance of the bread becomes the Body of Christ, and the entire substance of the wine becomes the Blood of Christ. This transformation occurs through the words of Christ and the operation of the Holy Spirit during the Eucharistic prayer. The outward appearance and physical qualities of bread and wine—the Eucharistic species—remain.

The Litourgicon makes this faith explicit in the epiclesis. The celebrant asks God the Father to send down the Holy Spirit upon the communicants and the offering, to make the bread the holy body of Christ and the cup His precious blood, and to grant that the cup and bread be communion in Christ’s blood and body. The prayer asks the Father to unite the many as one bread and one body through the Holy Spirit, fill them with the Spirit, strengthen them in faith, establish them in the truth, and confirm their reconciliation through Jesus Christ.

“They abstain from eucharist and prayer, because they allow not that the eucharist is the flesh of our Saviour Jesus Christ, which flesh suffered for our sins, and which the Father of His goodness raised up.” Smyrnaeans 6:8

The reality of Christ’s presence must never be separated from the purposes for which the Holy Mystery was given: remembrance of His saving death, self-examination, charity, sanctification, and the unity of the faithful. A metaphysical confession without these fruits contradicts the communion it professes.

questions.question: How does the Church view the validity of the mysteries and sacraments of other churches?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church does not teach that every sacred act performed outside formal Marcionite jurisdiction is automatically empty. The Church judges mysteries and sacraments by apostolic form, intention, and doctrine, receiving what accords with the Testamentum and correcting what is deficient.

For Holy Baptism, the rule is the rule already stated by the Church: baptism is valid only when performed in the trinitarian formula and by full triple immersion. Those who received a merely symbolic washing, infant baptism, sprinkling, pouring, a non-trinitarian formula, or a baptism without faith and repentance require proper Holy Baptism upon entry into the Church.

For Holy Communion, the Church recognizes the sacramental validity of a Eucharist celebrated with bread and a mixed chalice of wine and water, when the church celebrating it confesses the real presence of Christ in the Holy Mystery. A rite that denies the real presence or lacks the mixed chalice is deficient and is not received as the full Eucharistic Mystery recognized by the Marcionite Church.

This judgment does not make the Marcionite Church indifferent to doctrine, communion, or discipline. Validity is not the same as full ecclesiastical unity. The faithful may recognize a true sacramental form where it exists while still confessing the superior purity of the Gospel, canon, and liturgical order preserved by the Marcionite Church.

See also: Question 14.03, “What is Holy Baptism?”; Question 14.10, “Why does the Church use a mixed chalice?”; Question 14.11, “How does the Church understand the Eucharistic presence?”; Question 14.13, “Why does the Church practice open communion?”

questions.question: Why does the Church practice open communion?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Divine Liturgy practices open communion by welcoming Christians who have received Holy Baptism, Holy Chrismation or Confirmation, and Holy Communion, even when they are not formal members of the Marcionite Church. Properly baptized and chrismated candidates may also be admitted to their first Holy Communion, by which their Christian initiation is completed. Open communion therefore means openness beyond denominational membership, not reception without Baptism, sealing, and ecclesiastical admission to the holy table.

“Him that is weak in the faith receive ye, but not to doubtful disputations.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 12:1

Catechumens, non-Christians, and other visitors may remain and observe the Eucharistic rite, hear the Gospel and Apostle, and witness the worship of the Church. They are not numbered among the communicants until they have completed catechesis, received Holy Baptism and Holy Chrismation, and been admitted to Holy Communion, through which their initiation is completed.

Historical accounts from Tertullian, Epiphanius, and Jerome likewise attest that Marcionite congregations permitted outsiders and catechumens to enter and observe their worship. The Church therefore combines hospitality toward those who seek the Gospel with disciplined admission to the holy table.

questions.question: How is Holy Reconciliation practiced?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians maintain that the confession and absolution involved in Holy Reconciliation must take place openly before the gathered faithful. As a Holy Rite, Holy Reconciliation is ecclesial and public rather than a purely private transaction.

“To whom ye forgive any thing, I forgive also: for if I forgave any thing, to whom I forgave it, for your sakes forgave I it in the person of Christ.”

II Corinthians 2:10

questions.question: Does the Church practice fermentum?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church practices fermentum as an expression of ecclesiastical and Eucharistic communion. By this ancient Christian practice, bishops demonstrate and maintain communion with one another, as well as with their subordinate presbyters, by sending a portion of the consecrated bread from the bishop’s Eucharist to another altar.

“For we being many are one bread, and one body: for we are all partakers of that one bread.”

I Corinthians 10:17

As part of this practice, a fragment of the consecrated Eucharistic bread from one bishop’s celebration was sent—often by a presbyter or deacon—to another bishop of a neighboring or affiliated jurisdiction. The receiving bishop would then consume the fragment during his next liturgical celebration, symbolizing the unity and shared faith between the churches.

The term fermentum likely refers to the Eucharist as the leaven of Christian life—an emblem of how the faithful, though dispersed across many places, are united through the one Body of Christ to act as leaven within the world. Fermentum therefore expresses visibly that separated congregations remain one Church, one body, and one Eucharistic communion in Christ.

See also: Question 16.01, “What is the Church?”

questions.question: What is the Holy Lovefeast?
questions.answer:
The Holy Lovefeast is the communal meal shared by the celebrant and communicants after the Divine Liturgy and is one of the Church’s principal Holy Rites. It continues the fellowship expressed in Holy Communion by joining worship to practical charity, equality, and the common life of the Church.

Each household or communicant contributes according to ability. The food is gathered and shared without preference for the wealthy over the poor. No one begins before the others are ready, for the Apostle commands:

“Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together to eat, tarry one for another.”

I Corinthians 11:33

The meal is blessed according to the Divine Liturgy and shared in charity and peace. It is not merely a social reception after worship, but a sacred sign that the many are one body in Christ and that no communicant should hunger while another possesses abundance.

A portion of the Holy Lovefeast shall be reserved for an absent communicant prevented from attending by sickness or infirmity. When appropriate, a portion may also be reserved for one prevented by imprisonment, distance, or another serious necessity. The celebrant or an appointed communicant may carry it to that person as a sign that he or she remains joined to the fellowship of the Church.

See also: Question 14.01, “Which Holy Mysteries does the Church recognize?”; Question 14.02, “Which Holy Rites does the Church recognize?”; Question 14.08, “What is Holy Communion?”

questions.question: What is the Menologion?
questions.answer:
The Menologion is the official liturgical calendar of the Marcionite Church.

Menologion derives from the Greek words μήν (mēn, “month”) and λόγιον (logion, “register, catalogue”), literally “the monthly catalogue,” a term the early Church used for its liturgical calendar of feasts and saints.

It consists of designated Holy Days, which are organized into two main categories: Holy Week and Holy Feasts. The Holy Feasts are further divided into Major Feasts and Minor Feasts.

On all Holy Days, the faithful are expected to attend Mass and refrain from work and leisure activities as an act of reverence and devotion.

Each Holy Day in the Menologion corresponds to a significant historical or scriptural event. Dates are never chosen arbitrarily; every observance must be grounded in canonical or deuterocanonical scripture contained in the Testamentum, or in authentic historical tradition, preserving the integrity and meaning behind each commemoration.

See also: Question 15.02, “Why does the Church observe the Lord’s Day rather than the Jewish Sabbath?”

questions.question: Why does the Church observe the Lord’s Day rather than the Jewish Sabbath?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church observes the Lord’s Day on Sunday because it commemorates the resurrection of Jesus Christ and the new life revealed through Him. The Jewish Sabbath belongs to the law of Moses and to the false covenant; the Lord’s Day belongs to the Gospel, grace, and the newness of Christian life.

Marcion’s Epistle to the Magnesians teaches that Christians are “no longer observing sabbaths but fashioning their lives after the Lord’s day, on which our life also arose through Him and through His death.”

Marcion’s Epistle to the Magnesians 7:1

Sunday observance is therefore not the transfer of the Mosaic Sabbath to another day. It is a distinctively Christian commemoration of the Lord’s victory over death and the beginning of the new creation. The faithful gather for prayer, instruction, fellowship, and Holy Communion, ordering their lives according to Christ rather than the ordinances of the Hebrew Bible.

The Church does not condemn necessary works of mercy, protection, or service performed on the Lord’s Day. Its observance is evangelical rather than legalistic: the day is sanctified by worship, charity, rest from ordinary labor where possible, and joyful remembrance of the risen Lord.

See also: Question 3.24, “What is the Synaxicon?”; Question 15.01, “What is the Menologion?”; Question 17.02, “What is the law of Christ?”

questions.question: When does the Church celebrate Holy Week?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians were the first to break with the tradition of celebrating Easter on the same day as the Jewish Passover. Instead, they established the custom of observing it on a Sunday, the day marking Christ’s resurrection. This deliberate departure emphasized the distinction between the false covenant and the true Gospel revealed through Jesus.

In the Marcionite tradition, Holy Week is a cycle of moveable feasts and fasts, unlike the fixed-date Holy Feasts found in the Menologion. The foundation for determining the yearly observance of Holy Week is Good Friday, which Marcionite Christians fix as the first Friday of April. This date is historically anchored to April 3, 33 C.E.—the day Jesus was crucified. In the Jewish reckoning of that year, April 3 falls within the Passover observance, and this Passover dating aligns perfectly with the chronological order of the passion events as recorded in the Gospel. On that day, a notable lunar eclipse, often referred to as a “Blood Moon,” began at 2:01 P.M., roughly an hour before Christ’s death, and ended at 7:34 P.M., lasting 333 minutes.

Later Christian chronographers report that the pagan historian Thallus attempted to explain the darkness of the crucifixion as an “eclipse of the sun,” treating the event as a natural phenomenon rather than a confession of faith.

Origen further appeals to the pagan chronicler Phlegon, stating that Phlegon wrote of an “eclipse” and major earthquakes in the reign of Tiberius Caesar—again, not as Christian testimony, but as an external chronicle invoked for corroboration.

A fuller fragment attributed to Phlegon, as preserved in Jerome’s rendering of Eusebius’ Chronicle, describes an extraordinary eclipse “at the sixth hour,” accompanied by a great earthquake, with damage reported in Bithynia and Nicaea—an entry frequently connected, in later Christian argument, to the Gospel’s three-hour darkness.

“And it was about the sixth hour, and darkness came over the whole land until the ninth hour. And the sun was darkened, and the veil of the sanctuary was rent in the midst. And when Jesus had cried with a loud voice, he said, Father, into thy hands I commend my spirit: and having said this, he expired.” Evangelicon 22:59–61

Using this date as a reference point, Marcionite Christians can determine the precise dates of Holy Week each year, beginning with the first Friday of April:

Holy Sunday – Jesus’ arrival in Jerusalem (Sunday, March 29, 33 C.E.)

Holy Monday – The Parable of the Minas (Monday, March 30, 33 C.E.)

Holy Tuesday – The cleansing of the Temple and Jesus’ response to the questioning of His authority (Tuesday, March 31, 33 C.E.)

Spy Wednesday – The betrayal of Jesus by Judas (Wednesday, April 1, 33 C.E.)

Holy Thursday – The Last Supper (Thursday, April 2, 33 C.E.)

Good Friday – The Crucifixion of Jesus (Friday, April 3, 33 C.E.)

Black Saturday – Jesus laid in the sepulcher (Saturday, April 4, 33 C.E.)

Easter Sunday – The Resurrection (Sunday, April 5, 33 C.E.)

Ascension Monday – Jesus’ Ascension (Monday, April 6, 33 C.E.)

Marcionite Christians fast on Spy Wednesday in remembrance of Judas’ betrayal, and again on Good Friday in solemn mourning for the Lord’s crucifixion.

It is also a long-held tradition among Marcionite Christians to eat broiled fish and honeycomb on Easter Sunday, in memory of the meal Christ shared after His resurrection:

“And they gave him a piece of a broiled fish, and of an honeycomb. And he took it, and did eat before them.” Evangelicon 23:41–42

questions.question: Why was the Last Supper on Thursday and not Friday?
questions.answer:
In Marcionite Christianity, the question is best answered by paying close attention to how the Gospel itself reckons days and how it frames the Passion in relation to the Sabbath. The day that moderns call “Thursday night” is, in the ancient Hebrew manner of counting, already the beginning of the next day, for the day turns at sundown, so that what is Thursday evening by common modern reckoning is already the next day by ancient Hebrew reckoning. Thus, the Supper, though spoken of in common speech as Thursday evening, stands at the threshold of the day upon which the Passion unfolds. For this reason, the Church commemorates the Last Supper on Thursday, April 2, 33 C.E.

The Gospel also makes clear that the crucifixion and burial were carried out with urgency as the Sabbath approached. Jesus is taken, examined, condemned, and delivered up, and He must be laid in the tomb before sundown, for the Sabbath rest was at hand. This is the controlling fact of the chronology: the death and burial are set before the Sabbath, not within it. If one were to move the Supper to Friday night, then the arrest, judgment, crucifixion, and burial would be forced into the Sabbath itself, overturning the Gospel’s insistence that these things were accomplished before the Sabbath began.

For this cause, the Church confesses that the Supper stood on the evening before the day of the Cross. It is not that the Gospel requires us to imagine a long span of days between the table and the tree, but rather that it preserves a sober and consistent order: the Supper first, then the seizure in the night, then the condemnation and crucifixion by day, and then the burial before the Sabbath. In this way, the Gospel keeps the Passion free from contradiction and preserves the sacred sequence by which the Lord gave bread and cup, and afterward gave His life, and then rested in the tomb as the Sabbath approached.

questions.question: When and why does the Church celebrate Christmas?
questions.answer:
Christmas, also known as the Feast of the Descent, is a major feast of the Marcionite Church. It is observed as a movable feast kept on the Saturday nearest to New Year’s Day. It therefore falls either in the last week of December or the first week of January, depending on which Saturday lies closest to January 1.

This dating reflects the ancient Marcionite conviction—drawn from the received chronology of the Evangelicon—that Christ’s descent into Capernaum occurred at the beginning of the fifteenth year of Tiberius Caesar and that His first public appearing was bound to the Jewish Sabbath, for He went directly to the synagogue of Capernaum.

“In the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar, Pontius Pilate being governor of Judaea, and Herod being tetrarch of Galilee, and his brother Philip tetrarch of Ituraea and of the region of Trachonitis, and Lysanias the tetrarch of Abilene, Annas and Caiaphas being the high priests, the word of God came unto John the son of Zacharias in the wilderness. Jesus descended out of heaven into the Galilean city of Capernaum, a city near the sea, in the borders of Zabulon and Nephthalim. Having taken on the appearance of a man, he appeared thirty years of age and was teaching in the synagogue on the Sabbath days.”

Evangelicon 2:1-4

Even Tertullian, while disputing Marcion, preserves this Marcionite sequence with the striking phrase: “From heaven straightway into the synagogue” (Latin: De caelo statim ad synagogam; Tertullian, Against Marcion IV.7).

In civil-calendar terms, the threshold dates December 31, 28 C.E. (Friday) and January 1, 29 C.E. (Saturday) align naturally with the Sabbath boundary: the Jewish Sabbath begins at sundown on Friday and continues through Saturday. Thus, either a Friday-evening descent (already Sabbath) or a Saturday descent coheres with the Marcionite insistence that Christ appeared on the Sabbath and went “straightway” to the synagogue. For that reason, Marcionite Christians commemorate the Descent on the nearest Jewish Sabbath to New Year’s Day, preserving both the event’s historic calendrical roots and its placement at the turning of the year.

This practice also finds a notable historical echo in the Paulician adjurations, which include the explicit condemnation: “[Anathema] to those who on the first of January assemble, as they say for a feast …” (Anathema 7). Many have regarded the Paulicians as Neo-Marcionites or at least as inheritors of Marcionite-adjacent traditions; on that reading, a New-Year feast is plausibly a late survival or distortion of an earlier Descent commemoration.

Ironically, this Marcionite moveable feast typically falls between the dominant ecclesiastical calendars: the Western Church’s fixed December 25 (a later convention often explained in relation to the Roman midwinter calendrical milieu), and the cluster of Orthodox and Oriental observances in early January—most prominently January 6 in the Armenian tradition (Nativity and Theophany together), January 7 for churches following the Julian-calendar December 25, and the wider Theophany season that, in many Orthodox usages, culminates on January 19.

questions.question: When and why does the Church celebrate Hallowmas?
questions.answer: Hallowmas, also known as the Feast of the Martyrs, is a major feast of the Marcionite Church. It is observed on January 10, marking the exact date on which Asclepius, the Marcionite bishop of Eleutheropolis, was martyred in 310 C.E.

questions.question: What minor feasts does the Church observe?
questions.answer:
The Feast of Marcion and the Feast of the Cross are the two minor feasts of the Marcionite Church. Marcionite Christians commemorate the Feast of Marcion on July 15, the Ides of July, as a sacred observance honoring the life and enduring legacy of Marcion of Sinope. This date reflects the early Marcionite Christian phrase, “115 years and six and a half months between Christ and Marcion,” a calculation cited by Tertullian. It spans from the descent of Jesus in the fifteenth year of Tiberius Caesar (29 C.E.) to the excommunication of Marcion by the Roman Church and his formation of his own Pauline church, the Marcionite Church in July of 144 C.E. Marcion’s unwavering boldness, empowered by the Holy Spirit, enabled him to stand firm against opposition and proclaim that the true Christian God was made known solely through Jesus Christ.

On this day, Marcionite Christians read Marcion’s Homily to Diognetus as the appointed homily during the Divine Liturgy, reflecting the theological convictions and spiritual clarity Marcion brought to the early Church.

Marcionite Christians also observe the Feast of the Cross on September 14. According to Christian tradition, the True Cross was discovered on this date in the year 326 C.E. by Helena of Constantinople, mother of Emperor Constantine the Great, during a pilgrimage to Jerusalem. At the site of the discovery, the Church of the Holy Sepulchre was commissioned and later dedicated. The cross was divided into three parts: one remained in Jerusalem, another was brought to Rome and enshrined in the Basilica of Santa Croce in Gerusalemme, and the third was sent to Constantinople to serve as a symbol of divine protection for the city.

questions.question: Why does the Church reject Lent?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christianity does not observe Lent, because the scriptural basis commonly appealed to for Lent is not present in the Evangelicon as a binding rule of Christian practice.

It is also notable that certain neo-Marcionite movements appear, in hostile sources, as explicitly anti-Lenten by design. In the Byzantine abjuration formulae directed against the Paulicians, converts are made to anathematize those who refuse Christian fasts and deliberately eat dairy during Lent. The text reads:

“Anathema to those who reject all Christian fasts, and at the season of what they think is Lent enjoy cheese and milk.”

A parallel abjuration formula expands the charge:

“Anathema to those who avoid all Christian fasts, but who at the time which they think is Lent enjoy meat, cheese and milk.”

This practice coheres with Marcionite antithetical tradition: where later convention imposes a fast without Gospel warrant, the instinct is not merely to ignore it, but to invert it—so that what others mark as a time of compulsory abstinence becomes, in effect, a time of open eating and feasting as a visible contradiction.

questions.question: When does the Church fast?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christians observe fasting on Spy Wednesday to commemorate the betrayal of Christ by Judas, and again on Good Friday in solemn remembrance of the crucifixion of Jesus.

Additionally, Marcionite Christians are required to fast from their third recitation of the Lord’s Prayer at the ninth hour (3:00 P.M.) on Saturday until they receive Holy Communion on Sunday. A full day of fasting is also mandated prior to baptism as a sign of spiritual preparation and devotion.

Epiphanius noted that the Marcionite practice of a Saturday Eucharistic fast arose in deliberate contrast to Judaism, which regarded the Sabbath as a time of joy and feasting. Interestingly, this same tradition of Saturday fasting was later adopted in the Western Catholic Church, further underscoring its early Christian roots.

questions.question: What is the Church?
questions.answer:
The Church is the assembly of those called by the Gospel and united in the confession that Jesus is Lord. A believer becomes a fully initiated member of the Church through Holy Baptism, Holy Chrismation, and Holy Communion. Within this body the Evangelicon and Apostolicon are proclaimed as canonical scripture, the other books of the Testamentum are used according to their proper rank, the Holy Mysteries are celebrated, the Holy Rites are administered, and the faithful are formed in worship, charity, and obedience.

“Now ye are the body of Christ, and members in particular.”

I Corinthians 12:27

“Know ye not that ye are the temple of God, and that the Spirit of God dwelleth in you?”

I Corinthians 3:16

The Church is more than an institution or historical association. It is the visible community of the saved and the appointed instrument through which the Gospel is preserved and proclaimed in the world.

See also: Question 16.03, “What is the Church’s ecclesiastical polity?”; Question 16.05, “How does the Church understand apostolic succession?”; Question 14.01, “Which Holy Mysteries does the Church recognize?”

questions.question: What is the mission of the Church?
questions.answer:
The mission of the Church is to proclaim the Gospel of Jesus Christ, make God the Father known, call souls to faith and repentance, celebrate the Holy Mysteries, administer the Holy Rites, instruct the faithful, care for the suffering, and preserve the apostolic doctrine received through Paul.

“For from you sounded out the word of the Lord.”

I Thessalonians 1:8

“For though I preach the gospel, I have nothing to glory of: for necessity is laid upon me; yea, woe is unto me, if I preach not the gospel!”

I Corinthians 9:16

The Church fulfills this mission through preaching, catechesis, worship, discipline, charity, and the common witness of holy lives.

questions.question: What is the Church’s ecclesiastical polity?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church follows an episcopal form of governance, specifically a monoepiscopal or monarchic episcopate, wherein each jurisdiction is overseen by a single bishop. This structure is rooted in the earliest traditions of the Church.

Adamantius attests to an unbroken succession of Marcionite bishops beginning with Marcion of Sinope himself. Marcion served as the original Archbishop of the Marcionite community and passed this role on to his disciple and chosen successor, Apelles of Alexandria. The title of Archbishop, denoting the chief spiritual leader of the Church, has been preserved as the exclusive designation for the head of the Marcionite Church. Tertullian, one of the ancient Catholic heresiologists, mockingly referred to Marcion as the first “Arch-heretic,” inadvertently acknowledging the prominence of his episcopal authority.

The Archbishop alone holds the authority to ordain new bishops, in accordance with ancient Christian practice. The Marcionite Church also maintains the apostolic custom of episcopal succession, whereby bishops are appointed by other bishops. This requires the laying on of hands by three bishops in the ordination of a new bishop who has previously served as a presbyter. Bishops may also ordain presbyters who have served as deacons, and presbyters may, in turn, ordain deacons and deaconesses.

This model emphasizes continuity, unity, and apostolic order in the governance and liturgical life of the Church.

questions.question: What are the orders of clergy?
questions.answer: The Marcionite Church recognizes three major Holy Orders—bishop, presbyter, and deacon—and one minor order, the acolyte. The major orders are the episcopate, presbyterate, and diaconate; the acolyte serves in support of the major orders.

questions.question: How does the Church understand apostolic succession?
questions.answer:
The Church understands apostolic succession as continuity in apostolic doctrine, ministry, ordination, the Holy Mysteries, the Holy Rites, and ecclesiastical office. Doctrinal succession through fidelity to the Gospel revealed by Christ and preached by Paul is the higher measure, because an outward chain of ordination without Pauline truth is not genuine apostolic succession.

Marcionite tradition also preserves a historical episcopal foundation. Philologus of Sinope is remembered as a disciple connected with Paul and Andrew, and Marcion as his successor in the episcopal ministry of Sinope. On this basis, the ancient Marcionite Church claimed both doctrinal and historical continuity with the apostolic age.

Centuries of persecution and discontinuity interrupted the visible historical chain between the ancient Church and its modern revival. The modern Marcionite Church therefore does not claim an unbroken episcopal lineage from antiquity. It honors historical succession where it can be recovered or restored, but it does not make such a lineage the sole foundation of ordination, the validity of the Holy Mysteries, or ecclesiastical legitimacy.

See also: Question 16.06, “How does the Church regard traditional episcopal succession lists?”

questions.question: How does the Church regard traditional episcopal succession lists?
questions.answer:
The Church respects traditional episcopal succession lists as historical witnesses, but it does not regard them as infallible, complete, or the sole foundation of ecclesiastical legitimacy. Such lists must be judged by chronology, surviving evidence, and fidelity to the apostolic Gospel.

Marcionite tradition honors the ancient successions of Rome, Antioch, Alexandria, Ephesus, and other early centers up to the rupture of 144 C.E., when Bishop Pius of Rome excommunicated Marcion and the Pauline faction. In Antioch, Paul is remembered as having ordained Ignatius, while in Rome the earliest succession through Linus and Anacletus is understood as Pauline in character before the later Petrine realignment.

Beginning with Pius, the Roman succession no longer represents the Pauline ecclesial continuity claimed by the Marcionite Church. These lists remain valuable for reconstructing early Christian ministry, but apostolic legitimacy ultimately depends upon fidelity to Christ and Paul rather than upon an uninterrupted list alone.

See also: Question 16.05, “How does the Church understand apostolic succession?”

questions.question: Does the Church require clerical celibacy?
questions.answer:
Marriage and raising a family are both virtuous and honorable. The Marcionite Church does not prohibit clergy from marrying or having children; in fact, being married and raising children can demonstrate the capacity to govern one’s own household well.

“A bishop then must be blameless, the husband of one wife, vigilant, sober, of good behaviour, given to hospitality, apt to teach; Not given to wine, no striker, not greedy of filthy lucre; but patient, not a brawler, not covetous; One that ruleth well his own house, having his children in subjection with all gravity; For if a man know not how to rule his own house, how shall he take care of the church of God?” —I Timothy 3:2–5

questions.question: Does the Church ordain women?
questions.answer: The Church permits the ordination of women as deaconesses. In accordance with the customs of the early Church, they are permitted to assist in the administration of baptism for women, perform charitable service, and minister to women in circumstances where modesty and propriety require female assistance.

questions.question: Who are the Father and Doctors of the Church?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church honors Marcion of Sinope as Father of the Church. He preserved the Gospel revealed by Jesus Christ and preached by Paul, compiled the original Christian canon, established the Marcionite ecclesiastical tradition, and became the decisive witness by whom the Church’s doctrine, scripture, and discipline are known.

The Church also honors Apelles of Alexandria, Lucanus of Rome, Megethius, and Prepon the Assyrian as Doctors of the Church. They are so called because each either wrote or taught something of lasting value for the advancement of Marcionite theology, or stood as a disciple of Marcion who founded or preserved a distinct school within the broader Marcionite tradition.

Apelles is honored for his theological defense of one supreme God, his refinement of Marcionite cosmology, and his witness to the soul’s descent and salvation. Lucanus of Rome is honored as the founder and teacher of the Lucanist school. Megethius is honored as a Marcionite teacher and disputant whose witness is connected with the Church’s theological defense against false accounts of matter. Prepon the Assyrian is honored for his remembered role in the Marcionite theological tradition, even though his work is now lost.

The loss of Prepon’s writing does not erase his honor. The Church remembers not only surviving books, but also faithful teachers whose works, schools, or testimony helped preserve and advance the doctrine of Christ and Paul within the ancient Marcionite communion.

See also: Question 1.02, “Who was Marcion of Sinope?”; Question 6.07, “Who were the disciples of Marcion?”; Question 6.08, “Who were the notable Marcionite Christian martyrs?”; Question 6.17, “How were the Marcionite schools theologically distinguished?”

questions.question: Who are the biblical saints?
questions.answer:
Marcionite Christianity recognizes as saints all Apostles and Disciples of Jesus Christ, together with every Christian mentioned—directly or indirectly—within the scriptural books of the Testamentum. They are honored not through veneration or intercession, but as examples of faith and devotion. The following are named biblical saints (their names appearing either in Scripture or in received tradition) acknowledged by the Marcionite Church:

1) The Twelve Apostles

2) The Seventy Disciples

3) The Four Myrrhbearers

4) The Other Saints

questions.question: What is the communion of saints?
questions.answer:
The communion of saints is the spiritual fellowship of all who belong to Jesus Christ: the faithful living upon earth and the souls of the saved received into the Kingdom of God. All are united in one Lord, one faith, one baptism, and one spiritual body.

This communion does not mean that departed saints are invoked, worshipped, or treated as mediators alongside Christ. The Church honors the saints as examples of faith, remembers them in its calendar and worship, and gives thanks for their witness, but directs prayer to God the Father through Jesus Christ.

The communion of saints is therefore a communion in Christ rather than a cult of the departed. Death does not overthrow the unity of those who belong to the Lord, for whether living or departed, they remain His.

“For as the body is one, and hath many members, and all the members of that one body, being many, are one body: so also is Christ. Now ye are the body of Christ, and members in particular.”

I Corinthians 12:12, 27

“There is one body, and one Spirit, even as ye are called in one hope of your calling; One Lord, one faith, one baptism.”

Laodiceans 4:4–5

questions.question: Who are the prophets?
questions.answer:
We reject all prophets of the Law and the Hebrew Scriptures. Yet we affirm that Christ’s assemblies truly receive the gift of prophecy: “Follow after charity, and desire spiritual gifts, but rather that ye may prophesy” (I Corinthians 14:1). “He that prophesieth speaketh unto men to edification, and exhortation, and comfort” (I Corinthians 14:3). “Despise not prophesyings. Prove all things; hold fast that which is good” (I Thessalonians 5:20–21).

We recognize prophets only if Scripture explicitly names them a prophet, or if they are among the Seventy Disciples and tradition explicitly ascribes prophetic status. We also hold that Paul, the chief Apostle of the true Gospel and the only apostle endowed with the gift of prophecy, received his commission directly from Christ: “Paul, an apostle, not of men, neither by man, but by Jesus Christ, and God the Father” (Galatians 1:1). “I certify you, brethren, that the gospel which was preached of me is not after man. For I neither received it of man, neither was I taught it, but by the revelation of Jesus Christ” (Galatians 1:11–12).

Recognized prophets (by Scripture and tradition):

John the Baptist. Forerunner named in Scripture as a prophet who testified to Christ.

Paul the Apostle. The chief Apostle of the true Gospel and the only apostle endowed with the gift of prophecy.

Barnabas of Milan. Traditionally listed as one of the Seventy Disciples and an early herald of the gospel.

Lucius of Cyrene. Prophet and teacher at Antioch, included among the Seventy Disciples in tradition.

Silvanus of Thessalonica. Included among the Seventy Disciples in tradition and esteemed as a prophet.

Timotheus of Ephesus. Disciple and coworker of Paul; Scripture speaks of prophecies concerning him and a gift imparted “by prophecy.”

Hermas of Philippopolis. Traditionally listed among the Seventy Disciples; remembered as a visionary whose revelations were received and recorded in the early Church.

Philomena the Prophetess. Her gifts were confirmed by Marcion’s disciple Apelles, and her revelations were recorded in his now-lost Manifestations. She went to Rome before Marcion to proclaim his arrival and served as a Marcionite Deaconess. Her remains were discovered on May 24, 1802, in the Catacomb of Priscilla. Three tiles sealing the tomb read Pax tecum Filumena (“Peace be unto you, Philomena”), from which her name (Filumena, Greek φιλουμένη (philouménē), “beloved”) is rendered in English as Philomena. The two anchors, three arrows, the palm, and the ivy leaf on the tiles were interpreted as symbols of her martyrdom. We maintain that, after miracles were associated with the tomb and remains, the Roman Catholic Church originally attributed a constructed history unrelated to her true identity; later, on February 14, 1961, the Roman Catholic Church ordered her name removed from all liturgical calendars, most likely when it was recognized that she was the Marcionite Philomena.

questions.question: How does the Church understand prophecy and revelation?
questions.answer:
We affirm the gift of prophecy, yet we do not accept “continuous revelation” as the addition of new doctrine or new scripture. By “continuous revelation,” we mean any claim that adds to the Church’s rule beyond the Evangelicon and the Apostolicon. These writings are the Church’s rule, and they judge all prophetic claims.

Paul requires both discernment and order in the exercise of prophecy: “to another discerning of spirits” (I Corinthians 12:10); “Let the prophets speak two or three, and let the other judge” (I Corinthians 14:29); and “the spirits of the prophets are subject to the prophets” (I Corinthians 14:32).

This guarded openness to prophecy is not foreign to ancient Marcionite controversy. Tertullian, writing against Marcion, challenged Marcionite Christians to produce the signs of spiritual gifts: prophets speaking by the Spirit, predictions of things to come, the manifestation of the secrets of the heart, psalms, visions, prayers, ecstatic utterance, interpretation of tongues, and even women prophets among their holy sisters. His testimony is hostile, and he presents the challenge in order to deny Marcionite claims, not to commend them. Nevertheless, the passage shows that prophecy, visions, psalms, spiritual prayer, tongues, discernment, and women prophets were all recognized categories in the second-century debate over true apostolic assembly life.

Our guardrails are Pauline. “Though we, or an angel from heaven, preach any other gospel unto you… let him be accursed” (Galatians 1:8–9). “If any man think himself to be a prophet, or spiritual, let him acknowledge that the things that I write unto you are the commandments of the Lord” (I Corinthians 14:35). We hold that Paul is the chief Apostle of the true Gospel, the only apostle endowed with the gift of prophecy, and the authoritative herald of the revelation of Jesus Christ.

Accordingly, we reject any claim that revises Paul’s gospel or expands the canon, and we reject treating private revelations as universally binding doctrine. We likewise reject the false prophets of the Law and the Hebrew Bible.

In the apostolic era, the Apostles received revelation directly from God the Father or from God the Son (Galatians 1:12; I Corinthians 11:23; II Corinthians 12:1). After the Apostles, authentic revelation is mediated: the Father grants illumination through the Holy Spirit and, at times, by his holy angels (I Corinthians 2:10).

Because mediation can be counterfeited, discernment is necessary. Paul warns that “Satan himself is transformed into an angel of light” (II Corinthians 11:14). For this reason, Paul’s apostolic Scripture always stands above all later revelations and judges them.

We acknowledge that the Father may grant revelation through the Holy Spirit and at times by his holy angels, yet we also heed the warning that there are deceiving spirits (I Timothy 4:1). A basic confession test remains: “No man can say that Jesus is the Lord, but by the Holy Spirit” (I Corinthians 12:3).

In practice, we distinguish between general or public prophecy and direct or private revelation. General prophecy is given in the assembly for the edification of the body. It may warn, console, direct, or present interpreted visions, but it cannot create new doctrine, new Holy Mysteries or Holy Rites, or new Scripture. It is delivered under judgment and in ecclesiastical order. Private revelation is granted to an individual for personal direction, correction, consolation, intercession, or vocation. It is not binding on the whole Church, must come under pastoral oversight if it touches others, and is tested against the Evangelicon and the Apostolicon.

Claims are tested by content, authority, purpose, and process. Their content must conform to the Evangelicon and the Apostolicon. Their authority is examined by Marcionite bishops with their presbyters and deacons, together with the assembly. Their purpose must be to edify, exhort, or comfort, not to sow confusion, for “God is not the author of confusion, but of peace” (I Corinthians 14:33). Their process must be orderly, and “all things” must be done decently and in order (I Corinthians 14:38).

Today, revelation may enrich and support the faith, but it is acceptable only insofar as it remains in complete harmony with the written word, that is, the Evangelicon and the Apostolicon. Prophecy is pastoral and local in scope. It may correct, console, warn, or call to repentance. It cannot create new doctrine, new Holy Mysteries or Holy Rites, new canon, a new creed, or a binding timetable for the whole Church, nor may it set dates for eschatological events.

The Church has historically received a small number of prophets through Scripture and tradition, yet even then, their words did not add to the Evangelicon or the Apostolicon, nor did they alter Paul’s gospel. Philumena the Prophetess is the last figure our tradition recognizes in connection with the ancient Marcionite schools; her revelations, preserved in Apelles’ now-lost Manifestations, were never added to the canon and do not stand above the Apostolicon.

We believe the Holy Spirit still grants prophecy, and we welcome what truly builds up the faithful. We do not believe in ongoing doctrinal revelation. The gospel delivered through Paul is complete, and all things are proved and held fast only if good.

See also: Question 2.03, “What is the hierarchy of authority in the Marcionite Church?”; Question 2.04, “What is prima scriptura?”

questions.question: What is the Christian moral life?
questions.answer:
The Christian moral life is the life of faith working through love under the law of Christ. It requires truthfulness, chastity, mercy, charity, self-control, forgiveness, care for the poor, and faithful service to God and neighbor.

“For, brethren, ye have been called unto liberty; only use not liberty for an occasion to the flesh, but by love serve one another.”

Galatians 5:13

Christian morality is not obedience to the law of Moses and is not lawlessness. It is the grateful response of souls liberated by grace and called to reflect the goodness revealed by Jesus Christ.

questions.question: What is the law of Christ?
questions.answer:
The law of Christ is the commandment of love and the pattern of life taught and embodied by Jesus Christ. It is fulfilled by bearing one another’s burdens, showing mercy, practicing charity, forgiving enemies, preserving holiness, and serving the brethren.

The Church therefore rejects the law of Moses as a covenantal authority while affirming the binding moral authority of Christ. Grace frees the faithful from the former law so that they may obey the Lord in love rather than descend into lawlessness.

See also: Question 11.07, “Why is the Church not antinomian?”; Question 17.01, “What is the Christian moral life?”

questions.question: How does Christian liberty differ from lawlessness?
questions.answer:
Christian liberty is freedom from the law of Moses, condemnation, false worship, and bondage to the powers of the present world-order. Lawlessness is the rejection of moral responsibility and the use of grace as an excuse for sin.

The faithful are free in Christ so that they may obey the law of Christ. Liberty therefore produces holiness, charity, discipline, and service rather than self-indulgence.

questions.question: How does the Church understand peace, non-retaliation, and righteous resistance?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church commands Christians to seek peace, love their enemies, reject vengeance, and overcome evil with good. It does not, however, teach an absolute pacifism that requires moral paralysis before grave injustice or forbids the protection of the innocent.

When the Lord commands the faithful to offer the other cheek, He forbids retaliation born of hatred and teaches a courageous refusal to be mastered by humiliation. Righteous resistance preserves dignity without imitating the cruelty of the aggressor:

“And unto him that smiteth thee on the one cheek offer also the other; and him that taketh away thy cloak forbid not to take thy coat also.”

Evangelicon 4:30

The Apostle likewise forbids private vengeance and teaches personal peace:

“Recompense to no man evil for evil. Provide things honest in the sight of all men. If it be possible, as much as lieth in you, live peaceably with all men. Dearly beloved, avenge not yourselves, but rather give place unto wrath.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 10:9–11

This rule governs personal conflict, private retaliation, and the ordinary conduct of Christians. It does not abolish civil authority, public justice, or the duty of lawful rulers to restrain violent evil. The Church’s teaching on public force and war is therefore treated separately from personal vengeance.

Christians must neither worship force nor abandon those entrusted to their care. They are called to act with courage while remaining under the law of Christ, using no more force than protection and justice require and seeking reconciliation whenever it is possible.

See also: Question 11.06, “How should Christians respond to suffering?”; Question 17.02, “What is the law of Christ?”; Question 17.03, “How does Christian liberty differ from lawlessness?”; Question 17.05, “Does the Church support just war theory?”

questions.question: Does the Church support just war theory?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church recognizes the principles of just war as a moral exception under lawful public authority, not as a celebration of war or violence. It bases this judgment on Pauline theology rather than on Augustine. The Church begins from the Apostle’s distinction between private vengeance and public authority.

In personal conflict, Christians are commanded not to avenge themselves, but to live peaceably as far as possible, feed the enemy, and overcome evil with good. This governs the conduct of the faithful as private persons:

“Recompense to no man evil for evil. Provide things honest in the sight of all men. If it be possible, as much as lieth in you, live peaceably with all men. Dearly beloved, avenge not yourselves, but rather give place unto wrath.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 10:9–11

The Apostle then turns immediately to civil authority and teaches that rulers bear a real public office for the restraint of evil:

“Let every soul be subject unto the higher powers. For there is no power but of God: the powers that be are ordained of God. For rulers are not a terror to good works, but to the evil. Wilt thou then not be afraid of the power? do that which is good, and thou shalt have praise of the same: For he is the minister of God to thee for good. But if thou do that which is evil, be afraid; for he beareth not the sword in vain.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 11:1–4

Therefore the Church distinguishes the Christian’s duty to renounce private revenge from the civil authority’s duty to restrain violent evil. A war may be just only when undertaken by lawful authority, for a just cause, with right intention, proportionate force, discrimination between combatants and noncombatants, and a sincere aim at restoring peace. War may never be waged for conquest, cruelty, hatred, vengeance, persecution, or forced conversion.

Just war is therefore an exception under public justice, not a license for bloodshed. The Christian must never worship the sword; yet neither may he deny that civil authority can bear the sword for the protection of the innocent and the restraint of wickedness.

See also: Question 17.04, “How does the Church understand peace, non-retaliation, and righteous resistance?”

questions.question: What is the Church’s teaching on sexual morality?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church teaches that sexual relations belong within Holy Matrimony between one man and one woman, and that all Christians are called to chastity, fidelity, self-control, and holiness.

The Marcionite Church fears God alone, and we do not hate our neighbors. The Church receives all who come to hear the Gospel, to pray, and to seek the mercy of God through Jesus Christ.

If someone experiences same-sex attractions, those thoughts are to be brought to God in prayer, as with any other temptation. Temptation itself is not sin. Sin is the willful consent to, and acting upon, what God forbids. The Apostle teaches that believers are called to self-control and holiness, not to be driven by passions: “This is the will of God, even your sanctification, that ye should abstain from fornication… that every one of you should know how to possess his vessel in sanctification and honour; not in the lust of concupiscence” (I Thessalonians 4:3–5).

The Apostolicon does not present same-sex sexual behavior as something to be blessed or affirmed. Paul is explicit that same-sex acts fall under disordered passions and are contrary to nature: “For even their women did change the natural use into that which is against nature: and likewise also the men… burned in their lust one toward another” (Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 1:22–23). This is not framed as a “special sin” singled out for unique hatred; it is included within Paul’s broader indictment of human rebellion and the need for grace. The point is moral clarity, not personal contempt.

Likewise, Paul warns that unrepented sexual wrongdoing excludes a person from the inheritance of the kingdom—while immediately holding out the promise of cleansing and restoration for those who turn to God: “Be not deceived… [the sexually immoral]… shall not inherit the kingdom of God. And such were some of you: but ye are washed, but ye are sanctified” (I Corinthians 6:9–11). The Church’s message is therefore twofold: (1) certain behaviors are sin, and (2) sinners can be forgiven, changed, and made clean through Christ.

For that reason, our teaching about same-sex behavior sits inside a consistent standard applied to everyone. Paul does not permit believers to redefine holiness around desire—whether heterosexual or homosexual. He calls the faithful to flee “fornication” and every form of sexual uncleanness (Galatians 5:19–21), and he affirms chastity as the expectation outside of marriage: “To avoid fornication, let every man have his own wife, and let every woman have her own husband” (I Corinthians 7:2). The same Gospel that forbids sin also provides strength to resist it, forgiveness when one falls, and a new life ordered by faith rather than appetite.

So, no—we are not “homophobic.” We do not teach fear, ridicule, or mistreatment of anyone. We do teach repentance, self-denial, and purity, as Paul delivered to the churches. Anyone who desires to follow Christ is invited to do so, to pray, to seek counsel, and to pursue holiness—trusting that God is able to grant both mercy and power to obey.

See also: Question 17.07, “What is the Church’s teaching on marriage?”; Question 17.08, “What is the Church’s teaching on divorce?”; Question 17.10, “Does the Church mandate celibacy?”

questions.question: What is the Church’s teaching on marriage?
questions.answer:
Marriage, also called Holy Matrimony, is a sacred covenant and Holy Rite ordained solely between one man and one woman. The Marcionite Church firmly rejects and prohibits both same-sex marriage and all forms of polygamy.

Ancient Catholic heresiologists attacked the Marcionites by claiming that they did not believe in marriage. This was a purposeful distortion of the Church’s refusal to number Holy Matrimony among the Holy Mysteries. The Marcionite Church has always been able to honor, solemnize, and defend marriage as a holy covenant and Holy Rite without treating it as one of the three Holy Mysteries.

Many Protestant denominations likewise practice and affirm Christian marriage while declining to classify it among their sacraments. Their position is not ordinarily taken to mean that they reject marriage; neither should the same distinction be misrepresented when applied to the Marcionite Church.

“Marriage is honourable in all, and the bed undefiled: but whoremongers and adulterers God will judge.” —Alexandrians 14:2

questions.question: What is the Church’s teaching on divorce?
questions.answer:
We do not permit or recognize divorce between properly baptized Christians. However, we uphold the Pauline privilege, which allows for the dissolution of a marriage between two individuals who were not validly baptized at the time of their union—meaning they had not received credobaptism by triple immersion, nor had they been sealed by Chrismation or Confirmation. Outside of this circumstance, divorce is regarded as a form of adultery and is strictly prohibited.

“And unto the married I command, yet not I, but the Lord, Let not the wife depart from her husband: But and if she depart, let her remain unmarried, or be reconciled to her husband: and let not the husband put away his wife. But to the rest speak I, not the Lord: If any brother hath a wife that believeth not, and she be pleased to dwell with him, let him not put her away. And the woman which hath an husband that believeth not, and if he be pleased to dwell with her, let her not leave him. For the unbelieving husband is sanctified by the wife, and the unbelieving wife is sanctified by the husband: else were your children unclean; but now are they holy. But if the unbelieving depart, let him depart. A brother or a sister is not under bondage in such cases: but God hath called us to peace.”

I Corinthians 7:10–15

questions.question: What is the Church’s teaching on abortion?
questions.answer:
The womb is sacred, and the taking of innocent life within it is murder.

The Homily to Diognetus, traditionally attributed by the Church to Marcion, identifies the refusal to destroy or abandon offspring as a distinguishing mark of Christian life:

“They marry as all other men, and they beget children; But they cast not away their offspring.” —Diognetus 5:8–9

“Blessed is the womb that bare thee, and the breasts which thou didst suck.”

Evangelicon 10:27

questions.question: Does the Church mandate celibacy?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church does not mandate celibacy, although it honors voluntary celibacy and temporary abstinence as spiritual disciplines.

This accusation—like many others made by the ancient Catholic heresiologists—was a false and misleading claim designed to discredit Marcionite Christians and portray them as enemies of marriage and family life.

Nowhere in the canonical scriptures of the Testamentum is there a commandment requiring followers of Jesus Christ to live in celibacy. The Apostle Paul affirmed the value of celibacy for those called to it, especially in service to the Gospel, but he never forbade marriage. Rather, he upheld marriage as honorable and drew rich theological parallels between the union of husband and wife and the relationship between Christ and the Church:

“Husbands, love your wives, even as Christ also loved the church, and gave himself for it; That he might sanctify and cleanse it with the washing of water by the word, That he might present it to himself a glorious church, not having spot, or wrinkle, or any such thing; but that it should be holy and without blemish. So ought men to love their wives as their own bodies. He that loveth his wife loveth himself.”

Laodiceans 5:25–28

While it is true that Marcion himself embraced celibacy as a personal spiritual discipline, he never imposed it as a requirement upon his followers. Rather, he emphasized the importance of Eucharistic discipline and encouraged married couples to observe periodic abstinence in preparation for receiving Holy Communion.

This practice aligns with the Marcionite Church’s tradition of the Saturday Eucharistic fast, in which believers abstain from food and drink beginning at the ninth hour (3:00 P.M.) following their third recitation of the Lord’s Prayer. This fast continues until the faithful receive the Holy Communion on Sunday. During this time, abstention from marital relations is also expected—again, not as a blanket prohibition on sexuality, but as a devotional preparation for the Holy Mystery.

This discipline reflects the Apostle Paul’s own guidance regarding married life, in which he encourages temporary abstinence by mutual consent for the purpose of prayer and spiritual focus:

“Nevertheless, to avoid fornication, let every man have his own wife, and let every woman have her own husband. Let the husband render unto the wife due benevolence: and likewise also the wife unto the husband. The wife hath not power of her own body, but the husband: and likewise also the husband hath not power of his own body, but the wife. Defraud ye not one the other, except it be with consent for a time, that ye may give yourselves to fasting and prayer; and come together again, that Satan tempt you not for your incontinency.”

I Corinthians 7:2–5

In keeping with this instruction, Marcionite Christians also observe abstinence from marital relations on Spy Wednesday, in remembrance of Christ’s betrayal, and on Good Friday, in mourning of His crucifixion.

These devout practices of Eucharistic discipline and temporary abstinence were not unique to the Marcionite Church. They were later adopted by the emerging Catholic Church and continue to be observed in various forms by many Christian traditions today. Far from forbidding marriage, the Marcionite Church affirms it as a holy covenant while also upholding the value of spiritual discipline in preparation for Holy Rites.

questions.question: Does the Church prohibit circumcision?
questions.answer: Circumcision is viewed by the Marcionite Church as a form of carnal mutilation and an outdated, barbaric practice. In keeping with the teachings of the Apostle Paul, we reject circumcision as a religious obligation and refuse to bind Christians to the mark of the false covenant.

questions.question: Does the Church impose dietary restrictions?
questions.answer:
The only dietary restrictions observed by Marcionite Christians are those outlined in the Apostolic Decree. These include abstaining from food offered to idols, from blood, from meat containing blood, and from the flesh of animals that have been strangled.

“Speaking lies in hypocrisy; having their conscience seared with a hot iron; Forbidding to marry, and commanding to abstain from meats, which God hath created to be received with thanksgiving of them which believe and know the truth. For every creature of God is good, and nothing to be refused, if it be received with thanksgiving: For it is sanctified by the word of God and prayer.” I Timothy 4:2–5
