Outside of these restrictions, Marcionite Christians are free to eat all foods with thanksgiving, as they are sanctified through prayer and the word of God.

questions.question: Does the Church require vegetarianism, pescatarianism, or veganism?
questions.answer:
There is no scriptural basis for mandating a vegetarian, pescatarian, or vegan lifestyle for Marcionite Christians.

The Apostle Paul plainly teaches that abstaining from meat is not a sign of spiritual strength, but rather a sign of weaker faith:

“Him that is weak in the faith receive ye, but not to doubtful disputations. For one believeth that he may eat all things: another, who is weak, eateth herbs.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 12:1–2

At the same time, Paul warns believers not to pass judgment on each other regarding food, emphasizing that diet should not be a source of division within the Christian community:

“Let us not therefore judge one another any more: but judge this rather, that no man put a stumblingblock or an occasion to fall in his brother’s way.”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 12:13

Paul also suggests that refraining from meat in certain contexts—such as when dining with those who are sensitive or concerned about food sacrificed to idols—is an act of charity, not a commandment:

“But if thy brother be grieved with thy meat, now walkest thou not charitably. Destroy not him with thy meat…”

Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 12:15

Paul addresses concerns about food sacrificed to idols, explaining that while idols are nothing, Christians must remain sensitive to the consciences of others:

“What say I then? that the idol is any thing, or that which is offered in sacrifice to idols is any thing? But I say, that the things which the Gentiles sacrifice, they sacrifice to devils, and not to God… Whatsoever is sold in the shambles, that eat, asking no question for conscience sake: For the earth is the Lord’s, and the fulness thereof… But if any man say unto you, This is offered in sacrifice unto idols, eat not for his sake that shewed it, and for conscience sake…”

I Corinthians 10:19–29

Dietary practices are therefore matters of Christian liberty and conscience, provided they do not violate the Apostolic Decree or become a cause of spiritual harm to others.

questions.question: How does the Church understand asceticism?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church practices disciplined and voluntary asceticism but does not impose extreme renunciation as a universal condition of salvation. Fasting, celibacy, temporary abstinence, prayer, and bodily discipline may serve the spiritual life when freely undertaken in faith and charity.

“But I keep under my body, and bring it into subjection.”

I Corinthians 9:27

Marcion of Sinope personally embraced an ascetic life, including celibacy, but Marcionite tradition does not treat his personal discipline as a command binding every Christian. Marriage, family life, and ordinary labor remain honorable Christian vocations.

Apelles likewise illustrates the voluntary character of ascetic discipline. He did not impose universal celibacy, and the diversity preserved within the early Marcionite tradition shows that ascetic practices were understood as callings and disciplines rather than as prerequisites for salvation.

See also: Question 15.09, “When does the Church fast?”; Question 16.07, “Does the Church require clerical celibacy?”; Question 17.10, “Does the Church mandate celibacy?”

questions.question: How should Christians live among those outside the Church?
questions.answer:
Christians should live among those outside the Church with truth, peace, patience, charity, and fidelity to the Gospel. They must neither conceal their confession nor treat unbelievers with hatred or contempt.

“Walk in wisdom toward them that are without, redeeming the time. Let your speech be alway with grace, seasoned with salt, that ye may know how ye ought to answer every man.”

Colossians 4:5–6

The faithful bear witness through honest speech, moral discipline, works of mercy, and readiness to explain their hope. They should cooperate in what is good while refusing idolatry, injustice, and participation in practices contrary to the law of Christ.

questions.question: What duties do Christians owe to the poor, the suffering, and one another?
questions.answer:
Christians owe practical charity to the poor, comfort to the suffering, hospitality to the stranger, forgiveness to the repentant, and mutual service within the Church. These duties are not optional ornaments of faith but direct expressions of the law of Christ.

“Only they would that we should remember the poor; the same which I also was forward to do.”

Galatians 2:10

The faithful must therefore give according to their means, defend those who are wronged, visit the sick, sustain the bereaved, and bear one another’s burdens in love.

questions.question: Why does the Marcionite Church reject the charge of heresy?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church rejects the charge of heresy because the Roman Catholic Church, as it exists today, did not yet exist at the time the Marcionite Christians established their own church. In fact, the Marcionite Church predates the formal organization of Catholicism by several generations. Many of the attacks against Marcion came later and were often written by critics with vested interests in discrediting his legacy. One of the most vocal among them was Tertullian. Yet even Jerome, a prominent figure in the early Catholic tradition, offered the following assessment:

“As to Tertullian, I have nothing else to say except that he was not a man of the Church.” — Jerome, De Viris Illustribus

On this point, we are in full agreement with our Catholic counterparts.

Moreover, the most common accusations of heresy against the Marcionite Christians are either mistaken or entirely unfounded. Marcionites are not dualists. We are not docetists. We do not forbid marriage, impose celibacy, or prohibit the consumption of wine or meat. These accusations belong more to the polemical imagination of later heresiologists than to the actual faith and practice of the Marcionite Church.

Cerdo, a Syrian Christian teacher active in Rome before Marcion, was part of the same wider Pauline current that had already taken root in Rome before 144 C.E. His excommunication around 138 C.E. appears to have been an early stage in a broader purge of Pauline Christians from the Roman church, a purge that culminated several years later in the rejection of Marcion himself.

Unlike Marcion, Cerdo did not immediately establish a separate church. He sought to regain communion, returning repeatedly to the Roman assembly even after being condemned. Later heresiologists mocked this pattern, portraying him as unstable or evasive. Yet a more charitable reading is possible: Cerdo still hoped that the Roman church could be corrected from within. Marcion, by contrast, would later recognize that the conflict had become irreconcilable and would give the Pauline Gospel its independent ecclesial form.

Many charges later directed against Marcion were first attached to Cerdo and then blurred together by hostile writers who wished to make Marcionite Christianity appear derivative, confused, or tainted by association. Tertullian and other polemicists depicted Cerdo as Marcion’s teacher rather than as a related predecessor within the same Pauline movement. This conflation served a polemical purpose: it reduced Marcion’s public witness to a mere continuation of an already-condemned figure and obscured the independent authority of the Marcionite Church.

It is notable, however, that Cerdo and the Cerdonians appear to have used the original Testamentum, or an early form of the same Gospel-and-Apostle scriptural foundation later received and ordered by the Marcionites. Like the Marcionites, they rejected the Hebrew Bible as Christian scripture and did not identify Jesus with the expected deliverer of the Hebrew prophets. This indicates that Marcion was not alone in his rejection of the Hebrew scriptures. The Pauline Gospel had already produced communities and teachers who distinguished God the Father revealed by Christ from the lower ‘god of this world’ proclaimed in the Law and judgment.

Under Bishops Hyginus and Pius I, the Roman church excommunicated first Cerdo and then Marcion. These were not isolated disciplinary incidents. They reflect an escalating struggle over the identity of Christianity itself: whether the Church would be governed by the Gospel revealed through Christ and preached by Paul, or whether it would be reattached to the Hebrew Bible, the Law, and the authority of the lower world-ruling power. Cerdo’s condemnation in 138 C.E. and Marcion’s rejection in 144 C.E. therefore belong to the same historical sequence: the Roman suppression of a strong Pauline Christianity already present in the city.

It is highly plausible that the original Testamentum, later associated exclusively with the Marcionite Church, was at one point known among Roman Christians before 144 C.E. The existence of Cerdo and the Cerdonians shows that Marcion did not invent this scriptural and theological current out of nothing. He inherited, clarified, and organized a living Pauline movement already present in Rome, with roots also reaching back to Cerdo’s native Syria.

At the conclusion of his excommunication trial, Marcion is said to have turned to the assembled bishops and presbyters of Rome and declared:

“I will divide your Church and cause within her a division, which will last forever.”

History suggests he was right.

questions.question: How did the Catholic Church respond to Marcionite Christianity?
questions.answer:
Following Marcion’s evangelistic mission across the Roman Empire in the second century, several energetic responses emerged—many of which can be seen as indirect reactions to his foundational work. Among these were the expansion of the New Testament canon, the development of apostolic tradition, the formulation of the rule of faith, and the doctrine of apostolic succession. Each of these innovations served to counter Marcion’s uncompromising commitment to prima scriptura—the principle that scripture alone, not tradition or hierarchy, is the foundation of Christian belief.

The doctrine of apostolic succession enabled emerging Catholic authorities to claim divine legitimacy in determining which texts were to be regarded as “authentic” and which were “spurious.” This conferred on Church leadership the power to enforce doctrinal conformity, often by retroactively assigning apostolic origin to texts that aligned with their theological agenda. As a result, a flood of gospels, interpolated epistles, and forged writings began to circulate—many bearing false claims of apostolic authorship. Criteria for canonization were arbitrarily established: a text had to claim apostolic origin, or at least connection to an apostle, and it had to reflect beliefs already widespread within the Catholic faction. This framework, known as “apostolic tradition,” effectively institutionalized the attribution of Catholic customs and doctrines to the Apostles—regardless of historical authenticity.

Because many of these writings lacked legitimate claims to apostolic origin, elaborate legends and theological rationales were constructed to justify their inclusion in the canon. This is a particularly sobering realization for modern Protestants who adhere to sola scriptura, often unaware that several New Testament books owe their canonical status solely to assertions of tradition rather than demonstrable apostolic authorship. As Pastor Dietrich Bonhoeffer aptly observed, “Protestants, in denying the authority of tradition, have cut off the branch on which they sit.”

Marcion’s missionary advance also triggered a flood of apologetic and polemical writing. Pseudepigraphal texts expanded in the second century with explicit anti-Marcionite polemics, such as the forged Third Epistle to the Corinthians, and even the Apostles’ Creed itself was crafted or edited to counter Marcionite theology. Scholars such as Arthur C. McGiffert view the creed precisely as a reaction meant to counter Marcion’s radical theology.

The renowned scholar Adolf von Harnack went so far as to describe Marcion as the “father of the Catholic Church”—not because Marcion founded it, but because the emerging Catholic Church largely defined itself in opposition to his teachings. The Roman Church’s reaction to the rapid spread of Marcionite congregations across the Empire during the early second century compelled it to construct a more centralized hierarchy and formalize its canon, doctrine, and institutional presence.

Marcion’s introduction of a clearly defined and limited scriptural canon presented a direct challenge to the developing Catholic tradition. His original Testamentum, containing only the Gospel of the Lord and the ten original Pauline epistles, forced the emerging Catholic Church to confront a difficult question: if Marcion’s canon was not the true one, then what was? In response, the Church began the long and contested process of expanding the New Testament—a process rooted not in original apostolic witness, but in theological reaction to Marcion’s radical clarity.

questions.question: Why is Marcionism not Gnosticism?
questions.answer:
Marcionism does not involve any form of secret knowledge. Its doctrine, worship, history, and discipline are openly set forth in the books gathered within the modern Testamentum, above all in the canonical Evangelicon and Apostolicon. There are no hidden doctrines, secret initiations, or esoteric revelations reserved for an elite inner circle.

“For in him dwelleth all the fulness of the Godhead bodily.”

Colossians 2:9

The confusion between Marcionism and Gnosticism arose because hostile writers often collapsed many different movements into one convenient category. Irenaeus, Tertullian, Hippolytus, and later heresiologists frequently treated Valentinians, Sethians, Basilideans, Carpocratians, Marcionites, and other non-Catholic Christians as though they belonged to one broad “Gnostic” tendency. This was polemically useful, but historically imprecise. These movements differed sharply in canon, worship, theology, cosmology, ecclesiology, and soteriology.

Classical Gnostic systems, especially the Valentinian and Sethian traditions, developed elaborate mythologies of Aeons, Pleroma, Sophia’s fall, an ignorant Demiurge, and salvation through esoteric knowledge. Marcionite Christianity has none of this. It has no Pleroma, no Aeon-pairs, no Sophia myth, no secret initiatory curriculum, and no hidden saving knowledge reserved for an elite. Marcionite Christianity teaches the public Gospel of Christ, proclaimed by the Apostle Paul and preserved in the original Testamentum.

Marcionite cosmology is also distinct from Gnostic mythology. Marcionite Christianity teaches deferred demiurgy: the physical world was made mediately through lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions beneath God the Father. In the traditional Apellean reading, which preserves the original teaching of Marcion, the lower maker of the physical world is the being called in the Apostolicon “the god of this world” and called in the Evangelicon “the prince of this world.” But this lower maker is not a second co-eternal, co-equal god, nor is he the ignorant offspring of Sophia. He is subordinate, begotten, limited, and derivative.

Salvation in Marcionite Christianity is not gnosis. It is rescue. Christ descends into the lower world-order to liberate souls from bondage to sin, death, Satan, and the lower powers. The Gospel is not an esoteric map through the Aeons, but the public proclamation of deliverance through Jesus Christ. The Father is not discovered through secret initiation; he is revealed by Christ and proclaimed by Paul.

Marcionite anthropology is likewise different from Gnostic caste systems. Some Gnostic schools divided humanity into fixed classes, such as pneumatic, psychic, and hylic. Marcionite Christianity does recognize the distinction between natural and spiritual souls, and it affirms the preexistence and transmigration of souls. But this is not a metaphysical caste system of secret initiates. Natural souls may be awakened through the Gospel in due season, so long as they have not committed the Eternal Sin, while spiritual souls ascend through Christ into the Father’s kingdom.

Marcion’s commitment to the authority of a defined Christian canon placed him far closer to scripture-oriented Christianity than to the speculative and myth-driven systems of the Gnostics. Unlike Gnostic groups, which often relied on esoteric writings, hidden revelations, and mythological expansions, Marcion grounded his teaching in public scripture: the Gospel of the Lord Jesus Christ and the epistles of the Apostle Paul. His project was not the invention of a secret mythology, but the recovery of the pure Gospel from Jewish corruption and proto-Catholic harmonization.

Marcion also fostered an open and public Christian faith. Marcionite Christians organized congregations, built churches, ordained clergy, celebrated the Holy Mysteries and Holy Rites, and proclaimed a public canon. This differs sharply from Gnostic circles that often emphasized private instruction, graded initiation, and spiritual elitism. The Marcionite Church was not a mystery cult for the learned few. It was a public Christian communion centered on the Gospel, the Apostle, and the worship of God the Father revealed by Christ.

While Gnosticism tended toward abstract mythology and metaphysical speculation, Marcion’s theology was pastoral, scriptural, and ethically clear. Central to Marcion’s message was Christ’s command to love even one’s enemies, a command that revealed the moral superiority of God the Father over the law, violence, jealousy, tribal election, and vengeance associated with the Hebrew Bible. Marcionite Christianity was therefore not an escape into secret speculation, but a public mission of compassion, liberation, and Gospel proclamation.

Even secular and academic authorities have long distinguished Marcionite Christianity from Gnosticism. The 1911 Encyclopaedia Britannica states:

“It was no mere school for the learned, disclosed no mysteries for the privileged, but sought to lay the foundation of the Christian community on the pure gospel, the authentic institutes of Christ. The pure gospel, however, Marcion found to be everywhere more or less corrupted and mutilated in the Christian circles of his time. His undertaking thus resolved itself into a reformation of Christendom. This reformation was to deliver Christendom from false Jewish doctrines by restoring the Pauline conception of the gospel, Paul being, according to Marcion, the only apostle who had rightly understood the new message of salvation as delivered by Christ. In Marcion’s own view, therefore, the founding of his church—to which he was first driven by opposition—amounts to a reformation of Christendom through a return to the gospel of Christ and to Paul; nothing was to be accepted beyond that. This of itself shows that it is a mistake to reckon Marcion among the Gnostics.”

Similarly, The Oxford Dictionary of the Christian Church affirms:

“It is clear that he would have had little sympathy with their mythological speculations.”

Adolf von Harnack likewise distinguished Marcion from the Gnostics, even while later popular summaries often blurred that distinction again. The distinction remains essential. Marcion was not offering a hidden cosmological curriculum. He was proclaiming the Gospel.

That said, it is true that Marcionites and Gnostics shared certain points of contact. Both rejected the authority of the Hebrew Bible. Both denied that the deity depicted in the Hebrew Bible was the Father revealed by Christ. Both held Paul in high regard. Both discussed the soul’s bondage in the material order. Some Gnostic groups may even have drawn from the Evangelicon, the Apostolicon, or related Gospel-and-Apostle traditions. But shared opposition to the Hebrew Bible and shared persecution by proto-Catholic authorities do not make the two movements identical.

Solidarity in suffering is not theological identity. The Valentinians, Sethians, and other Gnostics taught mythological systems of hidden knowledge, Aeons, emanations, and esoteric ascent. Marcionite Christianity taught the public Gospel, the Apostle Paul, the canonical Evangelicon and Apostolicon, deferred demiurgy, the rescue of souls through Christ, and the open worship of God the Father. We are not Gnostics. We are Marcionite Christians.

See also: Question 9.03, “What is matter?”; Question 9.10, “What is deferred demiurgy?”; Question 10.04, “What is the preexistence of souls?”

questions.question: How does the Church understand Gnosis?
questions.answer:
The word gnosis means knowledge. The Marcionite Church affirms the true knowledge of God the Father revealed through Jesus Christ and communicated by the Holy Spirit, but it rejects the claim that salvation is obtained through secret doctrines, hidden passwords, esoteric initiations, or knowledge reserved for a spiritual elite.

Before Christ, God the Father was unknown to the world. Christ descended to reveal Him, and the Apostle Paul was entrusted with the full proclamation of that revelation. True Christian knowledge is therefore not discovered by speculation or recovered from pagan mysteries; it is received through the Gospel revealed by Christ and preached by Paul.

Knowledge alone does not save. A person may possess learning, understand mysteries, and yet remain without charity. Saving knowledge is inseparable from faith, grace, repentance, and love. It brings the soul into obedience to Christ rather than exalting the knower above the faithful.

“And though I have the gift of prophecy, and understand all mysteries, and all knowledge; and though I have all faith, so that I could remove mountains, and have not charity, I am nothing.”

I Corinthians 13:2

The Church therefore receives gnosis when the word signifies the knowledge of God given openly in Christ. It rejects Gnosticism when gnosis is made a secret means of salvation, separated from faith, charity, the Gospel, and the common life of the Church.

questions.question: Why is Marcionism not dualism?
questions.answer:
Marcionism is not metaphysical dualism. It recognizes a real moral and cosmic opposition between God the Father and the powers governing the present world, but it does not teach two co-eternal, co-equal, or independent gods. The Marcionite Church confesses one supreme God, the Father, above every lower ruler, principality, power, throne, and dominion.

When hostile writers speak of “principles,” the Church does not receive that language as meaning three co-eternal first principles. It means that Marcionite theology distinguished God the Father, the lower just ruler, and matter as different realities within the account of the visible world.

Classical dualism means belief in two equal and opposing divine beings or eternal principles, often one good and one evil, coexisting in cosmic balance. That is not the Marcionite view. Marcionite Christianity does not teach two co-eternal, co-equal gods. It does not teach that Satan is equal to God. It does not teach that the ‘prince of this world’ is an eternal rival to God the Father.

Marcion’s foremost disciple, Apelles of Alexandria, directly rejected the accusation of dualism leveled against Marcionite Christians. Apelles preserved the original teaching of Marcion and reaffirmed belief in one supreme God above all lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions. Ancient witnesses say that Apelles confessed one first principle and described the ‘prince of this world’ not as a second god, but as an angelic power beneath the higher God. Tertullian says that the Apelleans spoke of “a certain angel of great renown” who had “created this world of ours,” and Pseudo-Tertullian says Apelles taught that powers and angels in the lower regions formed this world.

Apelles declared:

“One good God, one Beginning, and one Power beyond all description.”

The Apellean school, founded by Apelles, was the direct continuation of Marcion’s original teaching. Apelles did not create a foreign system or abandon Marcionite Christianity. He taught, as Marcion did, that there is God the Father, revealed by Jesus Christ and proclaimed by Paul. Beneath him are lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions. These lower powers are best understood not as rival deities, but as angelic powers: subordinate, begotten, limited, and beneath the sovereignty of God.

This is how Apelles clarifies the issue. When the ‘prince of this world’ is described as an angel, the charge of strict dualism collapses. An angel may rule, fashion, accuse, judge, or rebel, but an angel is not God. An angel is not unbegotten. An angel is not co-eternal or co-equal with the Father. The lower power may be called “the god of this world” in the Apostolicon because he governs this present age, blinds the unbelieving, and exercises dominion over the visible order, but this title does not make him truly divine.

The traditional Apelleans identified the ‘prince of this world’ with Satan, but not as a second co-eternal, co-equal god. He was a begotten and subordinate angelic power. The ‘prince of this world’ was therefore inferior to God, dependent upon God, and unable to reveal the alien mercy of God the Father. But traditional Apelleans, like Marcion, did not regard the physical world as evil in itself, nor did they regard the ‘prince of this world’ as evil in the absolute sense. They regarded him as just, limited, and inferior.

The Lucanists, founded by Lucanus or Lucianus of Rome, developed the cosmology differently but still did not teach dualism. They distinguished between the “god of this world” and Satan. For them, the “god of this world” was the just lower angelic power directly responsible for fashioning the physical world, while Satan was an evil lower angelic power. This produced a threefold hierarchy: God the Father, above all; the just lower angelic power beneath him; and Satan as an evil angelic adversary.

The Severians, a later development associated with Severus, took a stricter and more ascetic position. Like the Apelleans, they identified Satan with the being called in the Apostolicon “the god of this world.” Unlike traditional Apelleans, however, they treated the ‘prince of this world’ as evil rather than merely just, limited, and inferior. This led them toward a more ascetic and world-denying approach. Yet even the Severians did not teach that Satan was equal to God the Father. Their doctrine remained hierarchical rather than dualist in the strict sense.

This is the key distinction: Marcionite Christianity teaches cosmological hierarchy, not dualism. The lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions may govern, blind, accuse, judge, or oppose mankind, but they are not equal to God. They are beneath him. They are not eternal rivals. They are not coequal divine beings. They are angelic powers, not unbegotten gods. All reality remains ultimately beneath the sovereignty of God the Father.

Marcionite Christianity therefore does not teach that there is one god of the Hebrew Bible and another god of the Christian Testament locked in equal opposition. Rather, it teaches that the deity portrayed in the Hebrew Bible is a false and counterfeit deity, unworthy of Christian worship, and that the Hebrew scriptures are neither divinely inspired nor religiously binding upon Christians. They are viewed as filled with contradictions, failed prophecies, and fables that may have limited historical or moral value but no spiritual authority.

Notably, even the heresiologists are inconsistent in how they frame the charge. Some accuse Marcionites of teaching two principles; others describe three; still others multiply the accusation further. This instability shows that “dualism” was often used as a loose polemical label rather than as a precise description of Marcionite doctrine. Hostile writers regularly confused a hierarchy of lower angelic powers with a doctrine of rival eternal gods.

This same problem appears in the treatment of later movements often labeled “neo-Marcionite,” especially the Paulicians. Modern reassessments have increasingly challenged the older claim that Paulicians were absolute dualists, arguing that hostile Byzantine sources used “Manichaean” and “dualistic” labels as polemical and juridical weapons rather than as accurate descriptions of Paulician self-confession. The same caution should be applied to the Marcionites. A hostile source calling a movement “dualistic” does not prove that the movement taught two co-eternal, co-equal gods.

The canonical Evangelicon and Apostolicon affirm that the one true God was revealed through Jesus Christ. The Apostle Paul makes this clear:

“As concerning therefore the eating of those things that are offered in sacrifice unto idols, we know that an idol is nothing in the world, and that there is none other God but one.”

I Corinthians 8:4

Thus, the Marcionite Church upholds strict monotheism. There is only God the Father, revealed by Christ and proclaimed by Paul. Beneath him are lower powers, principalities, thrones, and dominions, including the ‘prince of this world’ and Satan. But none of these is a second co-eternal, co-equal god. None is worthy of worship. None rivals the Father. They are lower angelic powers, not rival deities. Marcionite Christianity is therefore not dualism, but a confession of one supreme God over all.

See also: Question 9.03, “What is matter?”; Question 9.14, “How does the Church distinguish physical and visible creation from spiritual and invisible creation?”; Question 9.04, “What is the origin of matter?”

questions.question: How does Marcionism differ from Protestantism?
questions.answer:
Marcionism differs fundamentally from Protestantism, although certain ideas associated with the Marcionite tradition later appeared in Protestant thought. Throughout the twentieth century, renewed scholarly attention to Marcion led many Christian thinkers to recognize him as a foundational figure in the development of several concepts now associated with modern Christianity, particularly within Protestantism. Marcion has been credited with articulating early forms of sola fide, dispensational distinction, “New Testament Christianity” as distinct from the Hebrew tradition, and the very concept of a New Testament as a separate and authoritative body of inspired writings. He also advanced a form of prima scriptura: the principle that Christian doctrine must be grounded first and foremost in scripture. An additional similarity is that Marcionism recognizes only three Holy Mysteries—Holy Baptism, Holy Chrismation, and Holy Communion—while some Protestant denominations likewise recognize only three sacraments. Yet Marcionism is not a branch of Protestantism, for it predates the Reformation by more than a millennium and possesses its own canon, ecclesiology, order of the Holy Mysteries and liturgical worship, and theological tradition. While the Marcionite Church affirms that tradition, reason, and experience may support the faith, it maintains that these must never contradict or supersede the authority of scripture.

It is for these reasons that the prominent scholar Adolf von Harnack famously referred to Marcion as “the first Protestant.”

questions.question: How does the Church confess the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit?
questions.answer:
The Marcionite Church confesses God the Father, Jesus Christ his Son, and the Holy Spirit. In this essential and practical sense, Marcionite Christianity is effectively Trinitarian, even though it does not derive this confession from the metaphysical definitions or authority of specific ecumenical councils. We receive baptism into the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit, yet we do not bind ourselves to the later conciliar formulations of the Catholic churches.

Early evidence suggests that Marcion of Sinope and the first-generation Marcionite Church practiced a simple Christological baptismal formula. Cyprian of Carthage reports that Marcionite converts had been baptized solely “in the name of Jesus Christ.” This reflects the earliest Marcionite emphasis on Christ as the revealer of God the Father, and on baptism as entrance into the Gospel proclaimed by Paul.

Despite this, in the 250s C.E., Stephen I, Bishop of Rome, held that Marcionite baptisms were valid. This position sits at the heart of the third-century disputes over rebaptism, which were opposed by Cyprian of Carthage and Firmilian of Caesarea. The controversy shows that Marcionite baptism was recognized by some outside the Marcionite Church as a real Christian baptism, even when its formula and theology differed from Catholic practice.

By the third and fourth centuries C.E., Marcionite language concerning the Holy Mysteries, like that of other early Christians, developed toward more explicit triadic expression. This does not mean that the Marcionite Church abandoned its original confession of God the Father. Rather, it means that Marcionite Christians came to articulate more clearly the relationship of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit in teaching, worship, and the Holy Mysteries.

This development followed the same broad historical pattern seen elsewhere in early Christianity. The earliest Christian communities often used simpler Christological or binitarian language, especially in baptismal and confessional contexts. Over time, as theological questions became more precise and liturgical forms became more fixed, Christians increasingly used triadic formulas naming the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Marcionite Christianity participated in this broader development while retaining its distinctive canon, Gospel, Apostle, and rejection of the Hebrew Bible as Christian scripture.

By the fourth century C.E., this triadic baptismal practice was explicit. Augustine of Hippo notes that the Marcionites of his day baptized “in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit.” This shows that Marcionite communities had moved from the earlier Christological baptismal formula into a fuller baptismal confession of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.

The Evangelicon itself preserves the baptismal command:

“Go ye therefore, and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost.”

Evangelicon 24:73

The Apostle Paul also witnesses to the same triadic order:

“The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Spirit, be with you all. Amen.”

II Corinthians 13:14

The Psalmicon likewise echoes this confession without any hint of subordinationism:

“And upon it was the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit, to rule for ever and ever. Amen.” Psalmicon 23:20

Therefore, the Marcionite Church affirms the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit in worship and in the life of the Holy Mysteries, but rejects the authority of later ecumenical councils to define the faith over against the Evangelicon and Apostolicon. The movement from baptism in the name of Jesus Christ to baptism in the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit was not a rejection of Marcionite Christianity. It was the maturation of its language concerning the Holy Mysteries and confession.

Marcionite Christians continue to confess God the Father, revealed through Jesus Christ his Son, with the Holy Spirit given to the Church in worship, communion, and the Holy Mysteries.

questions.question: Does the Marcionite Church recognize the Holy Spirit as feminine?
questions.answer:
Not universally. The Marcionite Church confesses the Holy Spirit as divine and as one with God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ in the trinitarian life of the Church, but it does not require every Marcionite Christian to understand the Holy Spirit as literally female.

The Apostle Paul speaks of “the same Spirit,” “the same Lord,” and “the same God,” and concludes that all spiritual gifts are wrought by “that one and the selfsame Spirit” (I Corinthians 12:4–11). He likewise gives the trinitarian blessing: “The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Ghost, be with you all” (II Corinthians 13:14). Paul identifies the Spirit by divine activity, communion, adoption, intercession, and sanctification, but does not assign the Spirit a universally binding male or female sex. Indeed, the Apostolicon says that “the Spirit itself beareth witness with our spirit” and that “the Spirit itself maketh intercession for us” (Paul’s Epistle to the Romans 7:16, 26).

The Psalmicon, however, sometimes employs explicitly feminine grammar and imagery for the Holy Spirit. It says that the Holy Spirit “opened her bosom, and mingled the milk of the two breasts of the Father” (Psalmicon 19:4). Elsewhere, “The Dove fluttered over the Christ, because He was her Head; and she sang over Him” (Psalmicon 24:1), and Psalmicon 28:7 declares of the Spirit within the believer, “she cannot die, for she is life.” Such language permits a Marcionite Christian to speak poetically—and, where conscience and theological conviction allow, maternally—of the Holy Spirit.

This language is partly explained by the history of the Psalmicon. The Marcionite Church understands Marcion to have compiled these psalms from hymns circulating among several ancient Pauline churches rather than to have composed every psalm himself. Some arose within Syriac-speaking communities, while others reflect Greek forms or were transmitted through Greek, Syriac, and Coptic witnesses. In Syriac, the word for spirit, ruḥā, is grammatically feminine. The feminine pronouns of the Psalmicon therefore need not always signify a doctrine that the Holy Spirit is a female being; they may preserve the natural grammar and poetic imagination of eastern Semitic Christianity.

Nevertheless, some eastern Marcionites may have developed this grammatical and poetic femininity into a more definite theological belief. Early Syriac Christians frequently used feminine and maternal language for the Holy Spirit, and similar conceptions appeared among other eastern Semitic and Gnostic Christian communities. Certain believers may therefore have understood the Trinity as possessing a harmonious relation of Father, Son, and maternal Spirit, with the feminine Spirit completing or balancing the divine triad.

Such a belief may have been especially plausible among eastern Marcionite circles associated with Prepon the Assyrian, whose teaching arose in a Syriac environment, or among earlier Syriac Christians associated with Cerdo, a proto-Marcionite teacher, and the Cerdonians, a proto-Marcionite community. It is possible that members of these communities interpreted the Holy Spirit not merely as grammatically feminine but as the maternal divine presence through whom the life of the Father is communicated to the children of God. No surviving testimony, however, expressly attributes this doctrine to Prepon, Cerdo, or the Cerdonians; it remains a reasonable historical possibility rather than an established fact.

The Marcionite Church therefore allows both readings. A believer may understand the Psalmicon’s language as grammatical and poetic, without declaring the Holy Spirit literally female. Another may receive its maternal imagery more theologically and confess the Holy Spirit as the feminine or motherly person of the Trinity. Neither interpretation changes the essential confession that the Holy Spirit proceeds from God the Father, bears witness to the Lord Jesus Christ, gives life, distributes spiritual gifts, and unites the faithful in one body.

Accordingly, the femininity of the Holy Spirit is an ancient and permissible Marcionite interpretation, particularly within Syriac forms of the tradition, but it is not a universal dogma required of every member of the Marcionite Church.

questions.question: Why does the Church reject the label “Judeo-Christian”?
questions.answer:
The Church rejects the label “Judeo-Christian.” The doctrine of Christian independence from Judaism found its earliest and most decisive advocate in Marcion of Sinope. It was Marcion who first made explicit the radical distinction between the God revealed in Jesus Christ and the false deity depicted in the Hebrew Bible.

By preserving Jewish monotheism as its foundational dogma—as reflected in later creeds—the emerging Catholic Church left open the door for a reabsorption of Hebrew Bible traditions. Over time, this led to a gradual adoption of many of the same features found in ancient Israel’s religious system, including an increasingly complex clerical hierarchy, formalized ritualism, and a hostility toward theological dissent. In this way, the trajectory of Catholicism moved away from the liberating message of the Gospel and toward the legalism Marcion opposed.

Before Marcion, Christianity was widely perceived by Pagans as little more than a sect within Judaism. Marcion’s proclamation of the original Testamentum marked a turning point—establishing Christianity as a distinct faith with its own canon, doctrine, and God. Following this, the Judaizing factions within the early church, whose beliefs and practices aligned more closely with Mosaic law, became increasingly marginalized and eventually formed separate sects. This was inevitable, for the covenant of Moses was incompatible with the revelation brought by Jesus Christ and proclaimed by Paul.

Prior to Marcion’s influence, many Christians had begun reverting to Hebrew customs and using the Hebrew scriptures as authoritative. But with the publication of the original Testamentum, the landscape of Christianity shifted, and four main currents emerged: the Gnostics, who pursued hidden wisdom; the Catholics, who sought a synthesis with Judaism; the Judaizers, who remained loyal to Mosaic tradition; and the Marcionites, who proclaimed the new and true covenant revealed by Christ and preserved in Paul’s Gospel.

“Not giving heed to Jewish fables, and commandments of men, that turn from the truth.”

Titus 1:13

questions.question: Why does the Church reject the category of Abrahamic religion?
questions.answer: Marcionite Christianity does not claim any lineage from Abraham nor any connection to his so-called covenant. It recognizes no spiritual or theological inheritance from Abraham, Moses, or the Hebrew patriarchs, because the true covenant was revealed only through Jesus Christ and proclaimed by the Apostle Paul.

questions.question: How does the Church regard Hinduism, Islam, and Judaism?
questions.answer:
The doctrine set forth in the modern Testamentum contains no hatred of Hindus, Muslims, or Jews. The Marcionite Church rejects the scriptures, theologies, and deities of false religions as sources of Christian doctrine, but rejection of false religion is not hatred of persons.

The Evangelicon and Apostolicon alone constitute the supreme written authority of the Marcionite Church. They reveal God the Father through Jesus Christ and preserve the apostolic witness of Paul. We therefore do not draw Christian doctrine from the scriptures of other religions, nor do we recognize their deities as true gods.

This must not be confused with hostility toward the people who belong to those religions. Rejecting a scripture is not rejecting a people. Rejecting a covenant is not rejecting a people. Rejecting a deity is not rejecting a people. A god is not a people, and a religious text is not a human body. Marcionite Christianity refuses to collapse theology into ethnicity.

For that reason, our rejection of the Hebrew Bible as Christian scripture is not a rejection of Jewish people. Our rejection of Yahweh as God the Father is not hatred of Jews. The same principle applies universally: rejecting Zeus is not hatred of Greeks; rejecting Thor is not hatred of Scandinavians; rejecting the Qur’an as Christian scripture is not hatred of Muslims. These are theological judgments, not ethnic or personal condemnations.

Marcionite Christians pray for all people to turn to Christ and receive salvation. We hold no doctrine that treats any person as cursed by birth, ranked by ethnicity, or condemned by ancestry. The Gospel is offered to Jew and Gentile alike, not through the laws or scriptures of other religions, but through Jesus Christ, who reveals God the Father.

Thus, while we respect the dignity of all people, we do not affirm all religions as true. We have no interest in incorporating their theologies, acknowledging their deities, or treating their scriptures as part of Christian revelation. The Marcionite faith is public, clear, and canonically defined: one Gospel, one Apostle, one canonical rule, gathered within one ordered Testamentum, and God the Father revealed through Jesus Christ.

CONCLUSION

The purpose of the Didascalicon is not knowledge alone, but faithful confession and life in Jesus Christ. Doctrine must lead to worship, prayer, charity, discipline, and obedience to the law of Christ, through which the faithful bear witness to the Gospel revealed by the Lord and preached by Paul.

Those who receive this instruction are called to remain united with the Marcionite Christian community, strengthening one another through fellowship, encouragement, common prayer, participation in the Holy Mysteries, and the liturgical life of the Church. May they hold fast to the canon, discern every subordinate witness according to it, and preserve the faith in truth and love until every soul called by the Gospel is brought into the Kingdom of God.

Here endeth the Didascalicon.

Info@MarcioniteChurchofChrist.org

“O wonder beyond wonders, rapture, power, and amazement is it, that one can say nothing at all about the gospel, nor even conceive of it, nor compare it with anything.”

Marcion of Sinope
